definition: An argument is valid if and only if (i) the premises logically support the conclusion, (ii) the conclusion follows from the premises by recognised rules of inference, and (iii) there is no assignment of truth-values that makes the premises true and the conclusion false.
conditions: premises support conclusion
conclusion follows by recognised rules
no counterexample assignment
counterexample: Consider an argument with necessarily true conclusion, such as 'The cat is on the mat; therefore, either it is raining or it is not raining.' There is no assignment making the premises true and the conclusion false, since the conclusion is a tautology. The argument is valid by the third condition, yet the premises are irrelevant to the conclusion.
missing_condition: Validity in the formal sense does not require relevance of premises to conclusion; one may wish to add a relevance condition, but this goes beyond classical validity.
passage: One naturally says that an argument is valid if and only if the premises logically support the conclusion, the conclusion follows from the premises by recognised rules of inference, and there is no assignment of truth-values that makes the premises true and the conclusion false. The third condition is the precise one, and it admits of a surprising consequence. Consider the argument: 'The cat is on the mat; therefore, either it is raining or it is not raining.' The conclusion is a tautology — it is true under every assignment of truth-values. There is therefore no assignment that makes the premise true and the conclusion false. By the third condition, the argument is valid. Yet the premise is entirely irrelevant to the conclusion: the fact that the cat is on the mat has nothing whatever to do with the weather. The definition, faithfully applied, pronounces the argument valid, though many would feel that an argument whose premises are irrelevant to its conclusion is defective in some important sense. The classical notion of validity, it appears, is indifferent to relevance — a feature which some logicians have regarded as a deficiency in the classical account.

definition: An argument is sound if and only if (i) it is valid, (ii) all its premises are true, and (iii) it employs no informal fallacies.
conditions: valid
all premises true
no informal fallacies
counterexample: An argument may be valid and have all true premises, and employ no informal fallacy, yet be utterly unpersuasive because the premises, though true, are less certain than the conclusion. Such an argument is sound by the definition, yet worthless as a piece of reasoning.
missing_condition: Soundness in the logical sense does not guarantee intellectual value; the premises must also be more evident than the conclusion if the argument is to serve as a justification.
passage: The standard definition holds that an argument is sound if and only if it is valid, all its premises are true, and it employs no informal fallacies. Yet one may construct a case in which these conditions are satisfied and the argument is nevertheless wholly without value as a piece of reasoning. Suppose one argues: 'All events that will occur in the year 2030 are events that will occur; the next solar eclipse visible from London will occur in the year 2030; therefore, the next solar eclipse visible from London will occur.' This argument is valid: the conclusion follows from the premises. The premises are, we may suppose, true. And no informal fallacy is committed. The argument is sound. Yet it is entirely useless, for the conclusion is at least as certain as the premises, and arguably more so. The premises do not illuminate the conclusion; they merely restate it in a more complex form. The definition of soundness, it appears, captures the logical correctness of an argument but not its value as a justification. One may wish to add a requirement that the premises be more evident than the conclusion, but this goes beyond what soundness, strictly defined, provides.

definition: A proposition Q is a logical consequence of a set of propositions P if and only if (i) in every model where all members of P are true, Q is true, (ii) the inference from P to Q preserves truth, and (iii) the connection holds in virtue of the logical form of P and Q alone.
conditions: true in every model where P is true
truth-preserving inference
holds in virtue of logical form
counterexample: Consider the inference from 'x is red' to 'x is coloured.' In every model where 'x is red' is true, 'x is coloured' is also true, and truth is preserved. Yet the connection does not hold in virtue of logical form alone — it depends on the meaning of 'red' and 'coloured.' This is a material consequence, not a formal one.
missing_condition: The definition must distinguish formal logical consequence from material consequence, which depends on the non-logical content of the terms involved.
passage: It is held that a proposition Q is a logical consequence of a set of propositions P if and only if in every model where all members of P are true, Q is true, the inference preserves truth, and the connection holds in virtue of the logical form of P and Q alone. This third condition is meant to exclude inferences that depend on the specific content of the terms involved. Yet the boundary between logical form and content is not always clear. Consider the inference from 'this object is red' to 'this object is coloured.' In every possible circumstance where the premise is true, the conclusion is true; the inference preserves truth without exception. The first two conditions are satisfied. But the third condition fails: the connection between 'red' and 'coloured' is a matter of meaning, not of logical form. If we substitute other predicates of the same logical form — say, 'this object is heavy' and 'this object is fragile' — the inference fails. The definition correctly excludes such material consequences, but in doing so it reveals that the notion of 'logical form' upon which it relies is itself in need of clarification.

definition: A statement is paradoxical if and only if (i) it leads by valid reasoning to a contradiction, (ii) the reasoning employs only apparently acceptable premises, and (iii) the statement involves self-reference.
conditions: leads to contradiction by valid reasoning
employs acceptable premises
involves self-reference
counterexample: The Sorites paradox — if one grain of sand is not a heap, and adding one grain to a non-heap cannot make it a heap, then no amount of sand constitutes a heap — leads by valid reasoning to a contradiction with our intuitions, employs apparently acceptable premises, but involves no self-reference.
missing_condition: Self-reference is characteristic of some paradoxes (the Liar, Russell's) but not all; the definition should not require it as a necessary condition.
passage: A tempting definition holds that a statement is paradoxical if and only if it leads by valid reasoning to a contradiction, the reasoning employs only apparently acceptable premises, and the statement involves self-reference. This definition captures the structure of familiar paradoxes such as the Liar ('this statement is false') and Russell's paradox — cases which Stebbing analysed with characteristic precision in her work on logical thinking (the set of all sets that do not contain themselves). But it excludes a class of paradoxes that have no element of self-reference whatever. Consider the Sorites paradox. One grain of sand, we agree, does not constitute a heap. And we accept, as apparently obvious, that adding a single grain to a collection that is not a heap cannot transform it into a heap. By repeated application of this principle, we conclude that no number of grains of sand can constitute a heap — which contradicts our plain experience of heaps. The reasoning is valid, the premises are apparently acceptable, and the conclusion is contradictory. Yet there is no self-reference in the argument. The definition has identified a feature that is common to some paradoxes but is by no means essential to all.

definition: A set of propositions is consistent if and only if (i) no contradiction can be derived from them, (ii) each proposition is individually possible, and (iii) the propositions can all be simultaneously true.
conditions: no contradiction derivable
each individually possible
can all be simultaneously true
counterexample: Consider three propositions: 'A is taller than B,' 'B is taller than C,' and 'C is taller than A.' Each is individually possible, and no contradiction can be derived from any pair. Yet the three cannot all be simultaneously true, for the taller-than relation is transitive and antisymmetric.
missing_condition: Individual possibility does not guarantee joint consistency; the definition must attend to the logical relations among the propositions taken as a whole.
passage: One might define consistency by saying that a set of propositions is consistent if and only if no contradiction can be derived from them, each proposition is individually possible, and the propositions can all be simultaneously true. The third condition is the crucial one, and it is worth seeing how the second condition, taken alone, is insufficient. Consider the three propositions: 'A is taller than B,' 'B is taller than C,' and 'C is taller than A.' Each of these propositions, taken by itself, is perfectly possible — there is nothing self-contradictory about any one of them. And from any pair of them, no contradiction follows. Yet the three cannot all be simultaneously true, for the relation 'taller than' is transitive: if A is taller than B and B is taller than C, then A is taller than C, which contradicts the third proposition. The set is inconsistent, though each member is individually possible. The definition correctly requires simultaneous truth, but this requirement cannot be verified by examining the propositions individually — one must consider their logical interrelations as a whole.

definition: A proposition is a tautology if and only if (i) it is a well-formed formula of propositional logic, (ii) it is true under every assignment of truth-values to its constituent variables, and (iii) its truth can be established by the truth-table method alone.
conditions: well-formed formula of propositional logic
true under every assignment
truth establishable by truth-table method
counterexample: The proposition 'For all x, x equals x' is true under every interpretation and cannot be falsified by any assignment. Yet it is not a well-formed formula of propositional logic; it belongs to predicate logic. One might hesitate to call it a tautology in the strict propositional sense, though it is a logical truth. The third condition fails as well, for its truth cannot be established by the truth-table method, which applies only to propositional formulae.
missing_condition: The definition restricts tautologies to propositional logic, but there are logical truths in predicate logic and higher-order logic that are necessarily true yet fall outside the scope of the truth-table method.
passage: It is commonly held that a proposition is a tautology if and only if it is a well-formed formula of propositional logic, it is true under every assignment of truth-values to its constituent variables, and its truth can be established by the truth-table method alone. This definition works admirably for the familiar cases: the law of excluded middle, the principle of non-contradiction, and the various tautological schemata of the propositional calculus. Yet consider the proposition that for all x, x is identical with x. This is true under every interpretation of the variable and cannot be falsified. It seems, in an important sense, to be a logical truth. But it is not a well-formed formula of propositional logic at all; it belongs to the predicate calculus. Nor can its truth be established by the truth-table method, which presupposes a finite number of propositional variables. The definition, faithfully applied, excludes this proposition from the class of tautologies, though many logicians would wish to regard it as a logical truth of the most fundamental kind. The definition has confused the narrower notion of propositional tautology with the broader notion of logical truth, and it is perhaps the broader notion that better captures what we mean when we say that a proposition is true in virtue of its form alone.

definition: A rule of inference is valid if and only if (i) it is a syntactic transformation from premises to conclusion, (ii) it preserves truth in every model, and (iii) it is derivable from the basic axioms of the logical system in question.
conditions: syntactic transformation from premises to conclusion
preserves truth in every model
derivable from basic axioms
counterexample: Modus ponens is typically taken as a primitive rule: from 'if P then Q' together with 'P,' one may infer 'Q.' It is a syntactic transformation and preserves truth in every model. Yet in many formalisations of logic, modus ponens is not derived from the axioms but is itself one of the primitive rules of inference upon which the system rests. The third condition fails, for it cannot be derived from anything more basic within its own system.
missing_condition: Some rules of inference are primitive rather than derived; the definition ought to allow that a valid rule may be either a primitive stipulation of the system or derivable from other such stipulations.
passage: One might define a valid rule of inference as one that is a syntactic transformation from premises to conclusion, preserves truth in every model, and is derivable from the basic axioms of the logical system in question. The first two conditions are widely accepted: a rule must operate on the syntactic form of propositions, and it must not lead from truth to falsehood. The third condition, however, proves too demanding. Consider modus ponens, perhaps the most fundamental of all rules of inference. From the proposition 'if P then Q' and the proposition 'P,' one infers 'Q.' This is a syntactic transformation, and it preserves truth in every model. But in a great many formalisations of logic, modus ponens is not derived from the axioms; it is itself one of the primitive rules upon which the entire system is erected. It is not derived from anything more basic, for there is nothing more basic from which it could be derived. The third condition, requiring derivability, would exclude the very rules that make derivation possible in the first instance. The definition has demanded that all rules be derived, forgetting that some must be assumed as starting points if any derivation is to take place at all.

definition: A proposition is necessarily true if and only if (i) it is true in the actual world, (ii) it is true in every possible world, and (iii) its negation is logically self-contradictory.
conditions: true in the actual world
true in every possible world
negation is logically self-contradictory
counterexample: The proposition that water is H2O is, on many accounts, necessarily true: it is true in the actual world and in every possible world in which water exists. Yet its negation, 'water is not H2O,' is not logically self-contradictory in the formal sense. One can state it without violating any rule of logic; the contradiction emerges only when one adds the empirical identification of water with H2O. The third condition is therefore not satisfied.
missing_condition: There are propositions that are metaphysically necessary yet whose negation is not a formal logical contradiction; the definition conflates logical and metaphysical necessity.
passage: It is tempting to say that a proposition is necessarily true if and only if it is true in the actual world, it is true in every possible world, and its negation is logically self-contradictory. This definition captures the paradigm cases well enough: the law of non-contradiction, the truths of pure mathematics, and the like. Their negations do indeed appear self-contradictory. Yet consider the proposition that water is H2O. If one accepts, as many philosophers of science do, that the identity of a natural kind with its microstructural constitution holds of necessity, then this proposition is true in the actual world and true in every possible world. The first two conditions are met. But the negation of the proposition, that water is not H2O, is not logically self-contradictory in the manner of 'P and not-P.' One can state it without violating any rule of syntax or formal logic. The contradiction emerges only when one conjoins the statement with the empirical discovery that water is in fact H2O. The third condition, which requires that the negation be self-contradictory purely in virtue of logical form, is not satisfied. The definition has failed to distinguish between logical necessity, whose negation is a formal contradiction, and metaphysical necessity, whose negation may be perfectly coherent from the standpoint of logic alone.

definition: A universal generalisation is true if and only if (i) it quantifies over a non-empty domain, (ii) every individual in the domain satisfies the predicate, and (iii) the generalisation has been confirmed by examination of instances.
conditions: quantifies over a non-empty domain
every individual satisfies the predicate
confirmed by examination of instances
counterexample: The proposition 'All unicorns are herbivores' quantifies over the domain of unicorns. If we take the domain to be empty, then, by the standard semantics of the universal quantifier, the proposition is vacuously true: there is no unicorn that fails to be a herbivore. Yet the domain is empty, and no instances have been examined. The first condition arguably fails, and the third certainly does.
missing_condition: Vacuous truth poses a difficulty: in classical logic a universal generalisation over an empty domain is true by convention, without any need for confirmation by instances.
passage: A natural account of the universal generalisation holds that it is true if and only if it quantifies over a non-empty domain, every individual in the domain satisfies the predicate, and the generalisation has been confirmed by examination of instances. This account captures our ordinary practice of confirming generalisations by checking particular cases. Yet consider the proposition 'All unicorns are herbivores.' There are no unicorns. The domain of quantification is empty. In classical logic, a universal statement whose domain is empty is true vacuously: there is no unicorn that fails to be a herbivore, simply because there are no unicorns at all. The first condition, requiring a non-empty domain, is not met, and yet the standard semantics pronounces the statement true. Furthermore, no instances have been examined, for there are none to examine, so the third condition fails as well. The definition, it appears, has tacitly assumed that every universal generalisation ranges over existing things and is supported by empirical evidence. Classical logic, however, treats vacuous truth as a limiting case, and the definition must accommodate this if it is to agree with the received semantics of the universal quantifier.

definition: Two objects are identical if and only if (i) they share all their properties, (ii) they are indiscernible with respect to every predicate of the language, and (iii) substitution of one for the other preserves the truth-value of every sentence.
conditions: share all properties
indiscernible with respect to every predicate
substitution preserves truth-value of every sentence
counterexample: The morning star and the evening star are identical: both are the planet Venus. They share all their physical properties and are indiscernible with respect to every astronomical predicate. Yet the substitution of 'the morning star' for 'the evening star' in the sentence 'The ancient Babylonians believed that the evening star appears at dusk' may alter its truth-value, for the Babylonians may not have known that the two are the same object.
missing_condition: Substitution in intensional contexts (such as belief reports) may fail to preserve truth-value even for genuinely identical objects; the definition must restrict the substitution condition to extensional contexts.
passage: It is commonly supposed that two objects are identical if and only if they share all their properties, they are indiscernible with respect to every predicate of the language, and substitution of one name for the other preserves the truth-value of every sentence. The first two conditions express different facets of Leibniz's principle: if a and b are identical, then whatever is true of a is true of b, and no predicate can distinguish them. The third condition adds a linguistic test: wherever one name occurs, the other may be substituted without altering truth-value. Yet consider the morning star and the evening star, which are, as a matter of astronomical fact, one and the same body — the planet Venus. They share every physical property and are indiscernible with respect to every astronomical predicate. But the substitution condition encounters a difficulty. The sentence 'The ancient Babylonians believed that the evening star appears at dusk' is, presumably, true. If we substitute 'the morning star' for 'the evening star,' we obtain 'The ancient Babylonians believed that the morning star appears at dusk,' which may well be false, for the Babylonians may not have recognised the identity of the two celestial appearances. The third condition fails in what logicians call intensional contexts — contexts involving belief, knowledge, or supposition. The definition, in its unrestricted form, confuses extensional and intensional identity and must be qualified accordingly.

definition: A sentential connective is truth-functional if and only if (i) it combines one or more propositions to form a new proposition, (ii) the truth-value of the resulting proposition is wholly determined by the truth-values of the component propositions, and (iii) it can be represented by a finite truth-table.
conditions: combines propositions to form a new proposition
truth-value determined by component truth-values
representable by finite truth-table
counterexample: The connective 'because' combines two propositions: 'The bridge collapsed because the supports were corroded.' It forms a new proposition from two components. Yet the truth-value of the whole is not determined by the truth-values of the parts alone. Both 'the bridge collapsed' and 'the supports were corroded' may be true, yet the bridge may have collapsed for an entirely different reason, making the 'because' statement false. The second condition fails.
missing_condition: Many connectives of natural language, such as 'because,' 'although,' and 'before,' are not truth-functional: their contribution to truth-value depends on more than the truth-values of the propositions they connect.
passage: A familiar definition holds that a sentential connective is truth-functional if and only if it combines one or more propositions to form a new proposition, the truth-value of the resulting proposition is wholly determined by the truth-values of the component propositions, and it can be represented by a finite truth-table. This definition correctly characterises the standard connectives of propositional logic: negation, conjunction, disjunction, the conditional, and the biconditional. Each of these satisfies all three conditions. Yet natural language furnishes connectives that combine propositions in ways that resist this analysis. Consider the connective 'because,' as in 'The bridge collapsed because the supports were corroded.' This sentence combines two propositions to form a third, so the first condition is met. But the truth-value of the compound is not determined by the truth-values of the components alone. Both component propositions may be true — the bridge did collapse, and the supports were corroded — and yet the 'because' statement may be false, if in fact the bridge collapsed for an entirely different reason. The second condition therefore fails, and no finite truth-table can represent the behaviour of 'because.' The definition correctly identifies truth-functionality as a property of the classical connectives but shows, by contrast, that natural language is replete with connectives whose semantic contribution exceeds what truth-values alone can capture.

definition: A formal system is complete if and only if (i) every well-formed formula is either provable or refutable within the system, (ii) the system contains no contradictions, and (iii) every truth expressible in the language of the system is a theorem of the system.
conditions: every formula provable or refutable
system contains no contradictions
every expressible truth is a theorem
counterexample: Goedel's first incompleteness theorem demonstrates that any consistent formal system capable of expressing elementary arithmetic contains a well-formed sentence that is true but not provable within the system. The second condition is satisfied — the system is consistent — and many truths are indeed theorems. Yet there exists at least one truth expressible in the language that is not a theorem, and correspondingly a formula that is neither provable nor refutable. The first and third conditions are violated together.
missing_condition: For sufficiently powerful formal systems, completeness and consistency cannot both be achieved; the definition implicitly assumes that both are simultaneously attainable, which Goedel showed to be impossible.
passage: One might define a formal system as complete if and only if every well-formed formula is either provable or refutable within the system, the system contains no contradictions, and every truth expressible in the language of the system is a theorem of the system. This definition appears to describe the ideal towards which logicians have long aspired: a system in which every meaningful question receives an answer and no falsehood is asserted. Yet the work of Goedel has shown that this ideal is unattainable for any formal system of sufficient power. Goedel's first incompleteness theorem establishes that any consistent formal system capable of expressing the elementary arithmetic of the natural numbers contains a sentence that is true but not provable within the system. The system is consistent, so the second condition is met. But there is a truth, expressible in the language of the system, that is not among its theorems; the third condition is violated. Equally, there is a well-formed formula that is neither provable nor refutable; the first condition fails as well. The definition implicitly presupposes that completeness and consistency may be simultaneously achieved, but for any system rich enough to capture arithmetic, this combination is impossible. The lesson is not that the definition is wrong in its aspirations, but that the three conditions cannot be jointly satisfied by any sufficiently expressive formal system.

definition: A set of axioms is adequate for a theory if and only if (i) the axioms are mutually independent, (ii) every theorem of the theory is derivable from the axioms, and (iii) the axioms are self-evident truths.
conditions: axioms are mutually independent
every theorem derivable from axioms
axioms are self-evident truths
counterexample: The axioms of Euclidean geometry were long regarded as self-evident. The fifth postulate, concerning parallel lines, was accepted as true though many found it less obvious than the others. When non-Euclidean geometries were discovered, it became clear that one could deny the fifth postulate without contradiction. The axiom is not self-evident in the requisite sense, for its negation yields a perfectly consistent alternative geometry. Yet Euclid's axiomatisation remains adequate for Euclidean geometry.
missing_condition: Self-evidence is not a necessary feature of axioms; an axiom may be adopted for its fruitfulness or its role in generating a coherent body of theorems, regardless of its intuitive obviousness.
passage: It is sometimes supposed that a set of axioms is adequate for a theory if and only if the axioms are mutually independent, every theorem of the theory is derivable from them, and the axioms are self-evident truths. The first two conditions are standard requirements of formal elegance and deductive power: no axiom should be redundant, and the axiom set should generate the full body of theorems. The third condition, however, raises a philosophical difficulty. The axioms of Euclidean geometry were for centuries regarded as self-evident. Euclid's fifth postulate, the parallel postulate, was accepted as true, though geometers from antiquity onwards found it less intuitively compelling than the other four. The discovery of non-Euclidean geometries in the nineteenth century revealed that one could deny the fifth postulate without falling into contradiction. The resulting geometries are internally consistent and, in the case of Riemannian geometry, have found application in the physics of general relativity. The fifth postulate is not self-evident in any strong sense, for its negation is perfectly coherent. Yet Euclid's axiomatisation remains adequate for Euclidean geometry: the axioms are independent, and every theorem of the Euclidean theory follows from them. The demand for self-evidence, it appears, is too stringent. An axiom may be adopted not because it is obvious, but because it is fruitful — because the body of theorems it generates is rich, coherent, and useful.

definition: An argument is deductive if and only if (i) the conclusion is claimed to follow necessarily from the premises, (ii) the argument proceeds from general principles to particular instances, and (iii) the truth of the premises guarantees the truth of the conclusion.
conditions: conclusion claimed to follow necessarily
proceeds from general to particular
truth of premises guarantees truth of conclusion
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'This figure has three sides; therefore, this figure has three angles.' The conclusion follows necessarily from the premise, and the truth of the premise guarantees the truth of the conclusion. The argument is plainly deductive. Yet it does not proceed from a general principle to a particular instance; both premise and conclusion concern the same particular figure. The second condition is not met.
missing_condition: Deduction need not proceed from general to particular; it may proceed from particular to particular, or from particular to general, provided the conclusion follows necessarily from the premises.
passage: A standard characterisation of deduction holds that an argument is deductive if and only if the conclusion is claimed to follow necessarily from the premises, the argument proceeds from general principles to particular instances, and the truth of the premises guarantees the truth of the conclusion. This characterisation is often contrasted with induction, which is said to proceed from particular instances to general principles. Yet the association of deduction with the general-to-particular direction proves too restrictive. Consider the argument: 'This figure has three sides; therefore, this figure has three angles.' The conclusion follows necessarily from the premise, given the geometrical fact that any three-sided plane figure has three angles. The truth of the premise guarantees the truth of the conclusion. The argument is, by any reasonable reckoning, deductive. Yet it does not proceed from a general principle to a particular instance. Both premise and conclusion concern the same particular figure. The second condition — that the argument proceed from general to particular — is not satisfied. Equally, one may construct deductive arguments that proceed from particular facts to a general conclusion, as when one argues from the properties of an arbitrary triangle to a theorem about all triangles. The definition has confounded the direction of an argument with its logical character, and it is the latter, not the former, that distinguishes deduction from other forms of reasoning.

definition: An inductive argument is strong if and only if (i) the premises are true, (ii) the premises make the conclusion probable, and (iii) additional true premises would not diminish the probability of the conclusion.
conditions: premises are true
premises make conclusion probable
additional true premises would not diminish probability
counterexample: Suppose one observes that every raven examined thus far has been black, and concludes that all ravens are black. The premises are true and make the conclusion probable. Yet consider the additional true premise that the ornithological survey was conducted only in Northern Europe. This additional true premise does diminish the probability of the conclusion, for it reveals that the evidence base was geographically restricted. The third condition is violated, though the original argument seemed strong.
missing_condition: The requirement that no additional true premise diminish the probability is too demanding; almost any inductive argument is susceptible to defeat by new information, and this susceptibility is a feature, not a defect, of inductive reasoning.
passage: One might say that an inductive argument is strong if and only if the premises are true, the premises make the conclusion probable, and additional true premises would not diminish the probability of the conclusion. This third condition is meant to capture a kind of robustness: a strong inductive argument, one might think, should not be weakened by further evidence. Yet this requirement proves too stringent. Suppose one has observed that every raven examined thus far has been black. The premises are true — one has indeed observed only black ravens — and they render the conclusion, that all ravens are black, quite probable. The argument appears strong. But now consider the additional true premise that the survey was conducted exclusively in Northern Europe. This information, though true, diminishes the probability of the conclusion, for it reveals that the evidence is geographically parochial. The third condition is violated. Yet the original argument was not therefore weak; it was simply incomplete. The difficulty is that inductive arguments are, by their nature, defeasible: new evidence can always alter the probability of the conclusion. The definition has confused strength with invulnerability, demanding of induction a property that belongs only to deduction — namely, that no addition of premises can undermine the inference.

definition: A set of propositions is contradictory if and only if (i) it contains at least two propositions, (ii) the propositions cannot all be true simultaneously, and (iii) the conjunction of the propositions is logically false.
conditions: contains at least two propositions
cannot all be true simultaneously
conjunction is logically false
counterexample: Consider the single proposition 'It is raining and it is not raining.' This is a contradiction — its conjunction with itself is logically false — yet the set containing only this proposition has just one member. The first condition requires at least two propositions, but a single self-contradictory proposition suffices for contradiction.
missing_condition: A contradiction may reside within a single proposition as well as between two or more; the definition ought to allow for self-contradictory propositions that require no partner to generate an inconsistency.
passage: A familiar definition holds that a set of propositions is contradictory if and only if it contains at least two propositions, the propositions cannot all be true simultaneously, and the conjunction of the propositions is logically false. This definition captures the paradigm case of contradiction: two propositions, one affirming what the other denies, whose conjunction is necessarily false. Yet it is too narrow in at least one respect. Consider the single proposition 'It is raining and it is not raining.' This proposition is, by itself, a contradiction. Its conjunction with itself, trivially, is logically false. Yet the set containing only this proposition has but one member, and the first condition requires at least two. The definition therefore refuses to call this set contradictory, though it contains a patent contradiction. The difficulty arises because the definition locates contradiction in the relationship between propositions rather than in the content of propositions. A self-contradictory proposition needs no partner to generate an inconsistency; it is contradictory in its own right. One might repair the definition by dropping the requirement that the set contain at least two members, or by adding a disjunctive condition allowing for self-contradictory individual propositions.

definition: A proposition P materially implies a proposition Q if and only if (i) it is not the case that P is true and Q is false, (ii) whenever P is true, Q is true, and (iii) there is a genuine connection of content between P and Q.
conditions: not the case that P is true and Q is false
whenever P is true Q is true
genuine connection of content between P and Q
counterexample: The material conditional 'If the moon is made of cheese, then two plus two equals four' satisfies the first two conditions: it is not the case that the antecedent is true and the consequent false (the antecedent is false), and whenever the antecedent is true (which it never is) the consequent is true. Yet there is no genuine connection of content between lunar composition and arithmetic. By the standard account, this counts as a true material conditional.
missing_condition: Material implication, as defined in classical logic, does not require any connection of content between antecedent and consequent; the third condition belongs to a stricter notion of implication, such as relevant implication.
passage: It is natural to say that a proposition P materially implies a proposition Q if and only if it is not the case that P is true and Q is false, whenever P is true Q is true, and there is a genuine connection of content between P and Q. The first two conditions express the truth-functional core of the material conditional: the only way for 'if P then Q' to be false is for P to be true and Q false. The third condition adds a requirement of relevance. Yet consider the conditional 'If the moon is made of cheese, then two plus two equals four.' The antecedent is false; hence it is not the case that the antecedent is true and the consequent false. The first condition is satisfied. Equally, there is no instance in which the antecedent is true and the consequent false, so the second condition is met. But there is plainly no connection of content between the composition of the moon and the truths of arithmetic. The third condition fails. Classical logic, however, pronounces this conditional true: a material conditional with a false antecedent is true regardless of the consequent. This is one of the so-called paradoxes of material implication. The definition has conflated two distinct notions: material implication, which is purely truth-functional, and a richer notion of implication that demands relevance of content. The classical account dispenses with the third condition entirely.

definition: An argument from analogy is valid if and only if (i) two cases share a set of relevant properties, (ii) one case possesses a further property, and (iii) the shared properties are sufficient to warrant attributing the further property to the other case.
conditions: two cases share relevant properties
one case possesses a further property
shared properties sufficient to warrant attribution
counterexample: The Earth and Mars share several properties: both are rocky planets, both orbit the same star, and both have atmospheres. The Earth supports life. One might argue by analogy that Mars supports life. Yet the shared properties, whilst real, are not sufficient to warrant the conclusion, since the critical differences — atmospheric composition, surface temperature, liquid water — are decisive. The analogy meets the structural conditions yet the argument is plainly weak.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the shared properties be relevant to the further property in question and that the disanalogies not be decisive; structural similarity alone does not warrant the inference.
passage: An argument from analogy proceeds by noting that two cases share a set of properties and inferring that a further property of one must belong to the other. One might formalise this: such an argument is valid if and only if the two cases share relevant properties, one case possesses a further property, and the shared properties are sufficient to warrant attributing the further property to the other. This account appears to capture the logic of analogical reasoning. Yet consider the following argument. The Earth and Mars are both rocky planets orbiting the Sun. Both have atmospheres. Both experience seasons. The Earth supports life. Therefore Mars supports life. The two cases share several properties, one possesses a further property, and the shared properties might seem, at first blush, sufficient to warrant the inference. Yet the argument is plainly weak, because the differences between the two planets — the thinness and composition of Mars's atmosphere, the absence of liquid water on its surface, the extreme cold — are far more relevant to the question of life than the similarities. The definition provides no means of distinguishing strong from weak analogies, because it does not require that the shared properties be relevant to the inferred property, nor does it account for the possibility that critical disanalogies may undermine the inference even when the structural conditions are met.

definition: A concept is arrived at by abstraction if and only if (i) it is derived from particular instances by the removal of individual features, (ii) what remains after abstraction is common to all the instances, and (iii) the abstract concept is applicable to every instance from which it was derived.
conditions: derived from particulars by removing individual features
what remains is common to all instances
abstract concept applicable to every instance
counterexample: The concept of number is arrived at by abstraction from collections of objects, yet zero — the number of objects in an empty collection — was not derived from particular instances by the removal of features. There is no collection from which one abstracts zero by removing individual features; zero represents the absence of any instance. The first condition fails for this case, yet zero is a genuine number and a product of mathematical abstraction.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that abstraction always proceeds from positive instances, but some abstract concepts — such as zero, the null set, or the void — are reached by extending the process of abstraction beyond the particular, to encompass absence itself.
passage: A traditional account holds that a concept is arrived at by abstraction if and only if it is derived from particular instances by the removal of individual features, what remains after abstraction is common to all the instances, and the abstract concept is applicable to every instance from which it was derived. This account captures many cases of abstraction. The concept of redness is derived from red objects by setting aside their individual features — shape, size, texture — and attending only to the colour they share. The concept of triangle is derived from particular triangles by removing the specific measurements and retaining only the three-sided figure. Yet consider the concept of zero. Zero is a number, and the concept of number is paradigmatically abstract. But zero was not derived from particular instances by the removal of features. One does not arrive at zero by observing a collection of objects and stripping away their individual characteristics; zero represents the absence of any collection, the number of objects where there are none. There is no positive instance from which zero is abstracted. The first condition fails. The definition has assumed that abstraction always begins with particular, positive instances, but the history of mathematics shows that the process of abstraction may be extended beyond the given, generating concepts that correspond to the absence or negation of the very instances from which the process began.

definition: A proposition is logically necessary if and only if (i) its denial is self-contradictory, (ii) it is provable from the axioms of logic alone, and (iii) its truth is independent of any contingent fact about the world.
conditions: denial is self-contradictory
provable from axioms of logic alone
truth independent of contingent facts
counterexample: The proposition that every natural number has a successor is widely regarded as necessarily true, and its truth is independent of any contingent fact. Yet its denial is not obviously self-contradictory in the sense of formal logic — one must assume the Peano axioms to derive a contradiction. And it is not provable from the axioms of logic alone; it requires the axioms of arithmetic. The first and second conditions fail, yet the proposition is a paradigm of necessary truth in mathematics.
missing_condition: The definition restricts logical necessity to truths provable by logic alone, but mathematical necessities — which depend upon axioms beyond pure logic — are nonetheless necessary in a sense the definition fails to capture.
passage: One might define a logically necessary proposition as one whose denial is self-contradictory, which is provable from the axioms of logic alone, and whose truth is independent of any contingent fact about the world. This definition captures the truths of propositional logic: the law of non-contradiction, the law of excluded middle, the elementary tautologies. Each can be proved from the axioms of logic, each has a self-contradictory denial, and none depends upon how the world happens to be. Yet consider the proposition that every natural number has a successor. This proposition is widely regarded as necessarily true — it is not a contingent matter that five is followed by six. Its truth is independent of any empirical fact. Yet its denial is not self-contradictory in the strict sense of formal logic; one can consistently deny it unless one has already adopted the Peano axioms of arithmetic. And it is not provable from the axioms of logic alone: one requires axioms that go beyond pure logic, namely the axioms of number theory. The first and second conditions fail. The definition has identified logical necessity with provability in pure logic, but the necessary truths of mathematics, though they depend upon axioms beyond logic, are no less necessary for that. The concept of necessity, it would seem, is broader than the definition allows.

definition: A proposition is contingent if and only if (i) it is neither necessarily true nor necessarily false, (ii) its truth-value can be determined by observation, and (iii) it describes a state of affairs that might have been otherwise.
conditions: neither necessarily true nor necessarily false
truth-value determinable by observation
describes a state of affairs that might have been otherwise
counterexample: The proposition that there are infinitely many twin primes is neither necessarily true (it has not been proved) nor is it thought to be necessarily false. It describes a mathematical state of affairs that, if true, could not have been otherwise. Moreover, its truth-value cannot be determined by observation. The second and third conditions fail, yet the proposition appears to be contingent in the sense that we do not know whether it is true or false.
missing_condition: The definition conflates contingency with empirical decidability, but some propositions whose truth-value is undetermined are mathematical rather than empirical, and their contingency (in the sense of our ignorance) does not rest upon observational access.
passage: A standard account holds that a proposition is contingent if and only if it is neither necessarily true nor necessarily false, its truth-value can be determined by observation, and it describes a state of affairs that might have been otherwise. This definition captures the paradigm cases of contingency: that it is raining in London, that the cat is upon the mat, that the battle of Waterloo was fought in 1815. Each might have been otherwise, and each can be verified or falsified by observation. Yet consider the proposition that there are infinitely many twin primes — pairs of prime numbers differing by two. This proposition has not been proved, and it is not known to be necessarily true. Nor has it been shown to be false. It is neither necessarily true nor necessarily false, so far as our present knowledge extends. But its truth-value cannot be determined by observation; one cannot inspect the infinite sequence of natural numbers and count the twin primes. And if the proposition is true, the state of affairs it describes could not have been otherwise — mathematical truths, if they obtain, obtain of necessity. The second and third conditions fail. The definition has identified contingency with the empirical, but there exist propositions whose status is uncertain for reasons that have nothing to do with observation, and the concept of contingency, at least in one important sense, extends beyond the empirical domain.

definition: A possible world is a complete and consistent description of a way things might have been, such that (i) every proposition is either true or false in it, (ii) no proposition is both true and false in it, and (iii) it is conceivable by a rational mind.
conditions: every proposition true or false in it
no proposition both true and false
conceivable by a rational mind
counterexample: A possible world in which the continuum hypothesis is true and a possible world in which it is false are both consistent, yet neither is straightforwardly conceivable in the way that, say, a world in which Napoleon won at Waterloo is conceivable. The mathematical content is too remote from ordinary thought for conceivability to serve as a useful criterion. The third condition imposes a psychological constraint that is inappropriate to the logical notion.
missing_condition: The definition ties possibility to conceivability, but logical possibility outstrips what a finite rational mind can conceive, and a possible world need only be internally consistent, not imaginable.
passage: One might define a possible world as a complete and consistent description of a way things might have been, such that every proposition is either true or false in it, no proposition is both true and false in it, and it is conceivable by a rational mind. This definition captures the intuitive idea: a possible world in which Napoleon won at Waterloo is one we can readily conceive; we can describe the battle going differently and trace the consequences. Yet the third condition proves troublesome. Consider a possible world in which the continuum hypothesis holds — a world in which there is no set whose cardinality lies strictly between that of the natural numbers and that of the real numbers. And consider another world in which the continuum hypothesis fails. Both are consistent with the standard axioms of set theory. Both are possible worlds in the logical sense. But neither is conceivable in the way that the Napoleon world is conceivable. The mathematical content is too abstract, too far removed from sensory experience and ordinary thought, for the imagination to get a purchase on it. If conceivability is required, then these worlds fail to qualify, yet they are logically possible. The definition has imported a psychological criterion — conceivability — into a domain that requires only logical consistency. A possible world need not be one that a human mind can picture; it need only be one that harbours no contradiction.

definition: Every meaningful proposition has a truth-value if and only if (i) it is either true or false, (ii) its truth-value is determinate independently of our ability to ascertain it, and (iii) its truth-value is unchanging.
conditions: either true or false
truth-value determinate independently of ascertainment
truth-value unchanging
counterexample: The proposition 'It is raining' is meaningful and has a truth-value, but its truth-value is not unchanging — it may be true at noon and false at three o'clock. The third condition fails, yet the proposition is meaningful and possesses a determinate truth-value at each moment.
missing_condition: The definition treats truth-values as timeless, but many meaningful propositions are tensed, and their truth-values vary with time, place, or context of utterance.
passage: A natural account holds that every meaningful proposition has a truth-value if and only if it is either true or false, its truth-value is determinate independently of our ability to ascertain it, and its truth-value is unchanging. This account fits the propositions of mathematics and logic well: the proposition that two plus two equals four is true, determinately so, and its truth does not change. But consider the proposition 'It is raining.' This is a perfectly meaningful proposition, and at any given moment it has a determinate truth-value — it is either raining or it is not. But its truth-value is not unchanging. At noon in London it may be true; by three o'clock the rain may have ceased and the proposition may be false. The third condition fails. Yet no one would deny that the proposition is meaningful or that it possesses a truth-value at each moment of its utterance. The definition has taken the timeless truths of logic and mathematics as the paradigm and imposed their immutability upon all meaningful propositions. But ordinary empirical propositions are tensed — their truth-values depend upon when and where they are asserted — and this temporal variability does not deprive them of meaning or of determinacy.

definition: An expression is a predicate if and only if (i) it is an open sentence containing one or more free variables, (ii) it expresses a property or relation, and (iii) substitution of a name for the variable yields a proposition with a determinate truth-value.
conditions: open sentence with free variables
expresses a property or relation
substitution yields proposition with determinate truth-value
counterexample: The expression 'x is a heap' functions as a predicate in ordinary language and in many logical analyses, yet substitution of a name for the variable may yield a proposition without a determinate truth-value. Is a collection of fifty grains of sand a heap? The answer is indeterminate. The third condition fails for vague predicates, yet they are genuine predicates.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that every predicate yields sharp truth-values upon substitution, but vague predicates — 'tall', 'bald', 'heap' — are genuine predicates that admit of borderline cases where the truth-value is indeterminate.
passage: One might define a predicate as an expression that is an open sentence containing one or more free variables, that expresses a property or relation, and that yields a proposition with a determinate truth-value when a name is substituted for the variable. This definition captures the predicates of classical logic: 'x is even', 'x is greater than y', 'x is a prime number'. In each case, substitution of a numeral yields a proposition that is determinately true or false. Yet consider the predicate 'x is a heap'. It is an open sentence with a free variable, and it expresses a property. But upon substitution it may fail to yield a determinate truth-value. Is a collection of ten thousand grains of sand a heap? Plainly yes. Is a single grain a heap? Plainly no. But what of fifty grains, or a hundred? Here the truth-value is genuinely indeterminate — the predicate is vague, and no sharp boundary separates heaps from non-heaps. The third condition fails for such borderline cases. The definition has been framed with the crisp predicates of mathematics in mind, where every substitution yields a determinate answer. But the predicates of natural language and of much philosophical reasoning are vague, and their vagueness does not disqualify them from being predicates. A satisfactory account must accommodate the phenomenon of borderline cases.

definition: An utterance expresses a proposition if and only if (i) it is a declarative sentence, (ii) it is either true or false, and (iii) it has the same truth-value on every occasion of its utterance.
conditions: declarative sentence
either true or false
same truth-value on every occasion of utterance
counterexample: The utterance 'I am hungry' is a declarative sentence that is either true or false on each occasion of its use. Yet its truth-value varies from one occasion to another — it may be true when spoken at noon and false when spoken after dinner. The third condition fails, yet the utterance expresses a proposition on each occasion.
missing_condition: The definition conflates the proposition expressed with the sentence that expresses it; a single sentence may express different propositions on different occasions of utterance owing to the presence of indexical elements such as 'I', 'here', and 'now'.
passage: A familiar account holds that an utterance expresses a proposition if and only if it is a declarative sentence, it is either true or false, and it has the same truth-value on every occasion of its utterance. This definition fits the sentences of mathematics: 'Two plus two equals four' is declarative, true, and true whenever and wherever it is uttered. Yet consider the sentence 'I am hungry.' It is declarative, and on each occasion of its utterance it is either true or false. But its truth-value varies from occasion to occasion. When Smith says it at noon, it may be true; when he says it after a large dinner, it may be false. When Jones says it, its truth-value depends upon Jones's condition, not Smith's. The third condition fails. Yet on each occasion the sentence expresses a genuine proposition — a claim about a particular person at a particular time that is determinately true or false. The definition has confused the sentence with the proposition it expresses. A single sentence containing indexical words — 'I', 'here', 'now' — may express a different proposition on each occasion of its utterance, and the proposition, once fixed by context, has a determinate truth-value, even though the sentence does not.

definition: An argument constitutes a demonstration if and only if (i) its premisses are self-evident, (ii) each step follows deductively from the preceding steps, and (iii) it proceeds from premisses to conclusion in a finite number of steps.
conditions: premisses are self-evident
each step follows deductively
proceeds in a finite number of steps
counterexample: Euclid's proof that there are infinitely many prime numbers proceeds deductively in a finite number of steps, yet its premisses — the definitions and postulates of number theory — are not self-evident in the way that is commonly supposed. The parallel postulate, for instance, has been questioned since antiquity. The first condition fails, yet Euclid's proof is a paradigmatic demonstration.
missing_condition: The definition requires self-evidence of premisses, but many of the most important demonstrations proceed from axioms whose status is stipulative, conventional, or accepted for their fruitfulness, rather than from premisses whose truth is self-evident.
passage: An influential account holds that an argument constitutes a demonstration if and only if its premisses are self-evident, each step follows deductively from the preceding steps, and it proceeds from premisses to conclusion in a finite number of steps. This definition captures the ideal of demonstrative reasoning: one begins with truths so manifest that they require no further justification, proceeds by impeccable logical steps, and arrives at a conclusion that inherits the certainty of its starting points. Yet consider Euclid's celebrated proof that there are infinitely many prime numbers. The proof proceeds deductively and in a finite number of steps: assume there are only finitely many primes, form their product, add one, observe that the result is either itself prime or has a prime factor not on the original list, and conclude by contradiction. The proof is universally admired as a demonstration. Yet its premisses are not self-evident in any straightforward sense. The axioms of number theory upon which it rests were later formalised by Peano, and their status has been debated: are they self-evident truths about the natural numbers, or are they stipulations that define a formal system? The first condition is at best doubtful. The definition has imposed a requirement of self-evidence that many genuine demonstrations fail to satisfy, proceeding instead from axioms whose authority rests on convention, fruitfulness, or reflective equilibrium rather than on luminous self-evidence.

definition: An argument is formally valid if and only if (i) its conclusion follows from its premisses by virtue of logical form alone, (ii) every argument of the same form is truth-preserving, and (iii) the form can be expressed in the notation of first-order predicate logic.
conditions: conclusion follows by logical form alone
every argument of same form is truth-preserving
form expressible in first-order predicate logic
counterexample: The argument 'If it is necessary that P, then P; it is necessary that P; therefore P' is formally valid, and every argument of the same form is truth-preserving. But the form involves the modal operator 'it is necessary that', which cannot be expressed in the notation of first-order predicate logic. The third condition fails, yet the argument is valid by any reasonable standard.
missing_condition: The definition restricts formal validity to arguments expressible in first-order logic, but valid arguments may involve modal, temporal, or higher-order operators that exceed the expressive resources of first-order notation.
passage: One might define formal validity as follows: an argument is formally valid if and only if its conclusion follows from its premisses by virtue of logical form alone, every argument of the same form is truth-preserving, and the form can be expressed in the notation of first-order predicate logic. This definition captures the classical arguments: modus ponens, modus tollens, universal instantiation, and the like. In each case the form is expressible in first-order notation and every instance is truth-preserving. Yet consider the argument: 'If it is necessary that every planet moves in an ellipse, then every planet moves in an ellipse; it is necessary that every planet moves in an ellipse; therefore every planet moves in an ellipse.' This argument is plainly valid. Its conclusion follows from its premisses by virtue of logical form — it is an instance of the schema 'If necessarily P, then P; necessarily P; therefore P.' And every argument of the same form is truth-preserving. But the form involves the modal operator 'necessarily', which is not part of the vocabulary of first-order predicate logic. First-order logic has no means of expressing necessity. The third condition fails. The definition has identified formal validity with expressibility in one particular logical system, when in truth valid arguments may be framed in modal, temporal, and higher-order logics that go beyond the resources of first-order notation.

definition: An argument commits a fallacy if and only if (i) its conclusion does not follow from its premisses, (ii) the error can be identified as an instance of a catalogued fallacy type, and (iii) the argument appears persuasive to the uncritical mind.
conditions: conclusion does not follow from premisses
error is an instance of catalogued type
argument appears persuasive to uncritical mind
counterexample: A novel and subtle error in reasoning may appear highly persuasive and its conclusion may indeed fail to follow from its premisses, yet the error may not correspond to any fallacy in the traditional catalogue — it may be entirely new. The second condition fails, yet the argument is fallacious.
missing_condition: The definition ties fallaciousness to membership in a pre-existing catalogue, but new fallacies may be discovered that do not fit any established category, and the catalogue is an open, evolving list rather than a closed inventory.
passage: A traditional account holds that an argument commits a fallacy if and only if its conclusion does not follow from its premisses, the error can be identified as an instance of a catalogued fallacy type, and the argument appears persuasive to the uncritical mind. This definition captures the familiar fallacies: the ad hominem, the straw man, the appeal to authority, the affirming of the consequent. Each is catalogued, each involves a non sequitur, and each can deceive the unwary. Yet consider a novel error of reasoning that no logician has previously identified and catalogued. Suppose a philosopher constructs an argument that is subtly invalid — its conclusion does not follow from its premisses — and the argument is highly persuasive: it has deceived not merely the uncritical but several careful thinkers as well. The first and third conditions are met. But the error does not correspond to any established fallacy in the traditional catalogue; it is a new species of mistake. The second condition fails. Yet the argument is plainly fallacious — it contains an error of reasoning, and that error can be exposed by careful analysis. The definition has confused being a fallacy with being a recognised fallacy. The catalogue of fallacies is not a closed list; it is an evolving inventory to which new entries may always be added, and an argument may be fallacious whether or not its particular species of error has yet been named.

definition: A conditional proposition 'if P then Q' is true if and only if (i) the truth of P is sufficient for the truth of Q, (ii) there is a connexion of relevance between P and Q, and (iii) Q can be inferred from P without additional premisses.
conditions: truth of P sufficient for truth of Q
connexion of relevance between P and Q
Q inferable from P without additional premisses
counterexample: The conditional 'If this match is struck, it will light' is ordinarily accepted as true, yet Q cannot be inferred from P without additional premisses — one must also assume that the match is dry, that there is oxygen present, and that the match is not defective. The third condition fails, yet the conditional is true in its ordinary meaning.
missing_condition: The definition demands that Q follow from P alone, but most ordinary conditionals are implicitly conditional upon background conditions that are assumed but not stated, and their truth does not require that Q be derivable from P in isolation.
passage: One might hold that a conditional proposition 'if P then Q' is true if and only if the truth of P is sufficient for the truth of Q, there is a connexion of relevance between P and Q, and Q can be inferred from P without additional premisses. This definition attempts to capture the intuitive meaning of the conditional: P brings Q about, the two are relevant to each other, and P alone entails Q. It fits the purely logical conditional well: 'If x is greater than 5 and 5 is greater than 3, then x is greater than 3' — here the conclusion follows from the premiss without further assumptions. Yet consider the ordinary conditional 'If this match is struck, it will light.' We accept this as true. The striking is relevant to the lighting, and the striking is, in the appropriate sense, sufficient for the lighting. But Q cannot be inferred from P without additional premisses. One must also assume that the match is dry, that sufficient oxygen is present, that the match head is not defective, and that no extraordinary circumstances intervene. The third condition fails. The definition has demanded a standard of inferential self-sufficiency that ordinary conditionals do not meet. Most everyday conditionals depend upon background conditions that are taken for granted but not stated, and their truth consists in the sufficiency of P for Q given those conditions, not in the derivability of Q from P alone.

definition: A disjunctive proposition 'P or Q' is true if and only if (i) at least one of P and Q is true, (ii) the disjuncts are mutually exclusive, and (iii) both disjuncts are genuinely possible.
conditions: at least one of P and Q is true
disjuncts mutually exclusive
both disjuncts genuinely possible
counterexample: The proposition 'She is a poet or a philosopher' is a true disjunction when spoken of a woman who is both a poet and a philosopher. The disjuncts are not mutually exclusive — both are true — and the inclusive 'or' of ordinary language permits this. The second condition fails, yet the disjunction is true.
missing_condition: The definition imposes exclusivity, but the logical disjunction 'or' is standardly inclusive, requiring only that at least one disjunct be true, and permitting both to be true simultaneously.
passage: A natural account holds that a disjunctive proposition 'P or Q' is true if and only if at least one of P and Q is true, the disjuncts are mutually exclusive, and both disjuncts are genuinely possible. This definition captures the exclusive sense of 'or' that is sometimes intended in ordinary discourse: 'You may have soup or salad' typically implies that you may not have both. Yet consider the proposition 'She is a poet or a philosopher,' spoken of a woman who is in fact both a poet and a philosopher. The proposition is true — at least one of the disjuncts is true (indeed, both are). But the disjuncts are not mutually exclusive: there is nothing contradictory in being both a poet and a philosopher, and in this case the woman is both. The second condition fails. The definition has built exclusivity into the disjunction, when in fact the standard logical sense of 'or' is inclusive: 'P or Q' is true when at least one disjunct is true, and it is true a fortiori when both are true. The exclusive reading is a special case, sometimes conveyed by context, but the fundamental logical operation of disjunction does not require it.

definition: Two propositions are logically independent if and only if (i) neither entails the other, (ii) neither entails the negation of the other, and (iii) they concern entirely different subject matters.
conditions: neither entails the other
neither entails negation of the other
concern entirely different subject matters
counterexample: The proposition 'It is raining' and the proposition 'The streets are wet' concern the same subject matter — the weather and its effects — yet they are logically independent: neither entails the other (the streets might be wet from a burst water main, and rain might fall on covered streets). The third condition fails, yet the propositions are logically independent.
missing_condition: The definition conflates logical independence with topical unrelatedness, but two propositions may concern the same subject and yet be logically independent if neither's truth or falsity determines the other's.
passage: One might propose that two propositions are logically independent if and only if neither entails the other, neither entails the negation of the other, and they concern entirely different subject matters. This definition captures clear cases of independence: the proposition that Paris is the capital of France and the proposition that seven is a prime number concern entirely different matters, and neither has any logical bearing upon the other. Yet consider the propositions 'It is raining' and 'The streets are wet.' They concern closely related subject matters — the weather and its terrestrial effects. But neither entails the other. It might rain without the streets becoming wet (if they are covered), and the streets might be wet without rain (from a burst water main or a fire hose). Neither entails the negation of the other, either. The propositions are logically independent by the first two conditions, yet they fail the third — they concern overlapping subject matters. The definition has confused logical independence with topical unrelatedness. Two propositions may be about the very same phenomenon and still be logically independent, if the truth of one does not determine the truth or falsity of the other.

definition: A formal system is complete if and only if (i) every true proposition expressible in the system is provable within it, (ii) the system is consistent, and (iii) the addition of any unprovable proposition would render the system inconsistent.
conditions: every true proposition expressible in system is provable
system is consistent
addition of any unprovable proposition renders system inconsistent
counterexample: Propositional logic is complete in the standard sense: every tautology is provable, and the system is consistent. Yet one may consistently add propositions to propositional logic — for instance, axioms extending it to predicate logic — without rendering it inconsistent. The third condition fails, yet propositional logic is complete.
missing_condition: The definition conflates completeness with maximal consistency, but a system may be complete — all truths of its intended domain provable — without being maximal, since it may be consistently extended to a richer language or domain.
passage: A natural account holds that a formal system is complete if and only if every true proposition expressible in the system is provable within it, the system is consistent, and the addition of any unprovable proposition would render the system inconsistent. This definition combines two ideas: that the system proves all the truths it can express, and that it is, in a sense, maximally informative — admitting no further independent truths. The first two conditions capture the standard notion of completeness well. Yet the third condition is too strong. Consider propositional logic, which is complete in the standard sense: every tautology is provable, and the system is consistent. But propositional logic is not maximally consistent in the sense required by the third condition. One may consistently add to it propositions that go beyond its expressive resources — the axioms of predicate logic, for instance — without generating a contradiction. The third condition fails. Propositional logic is complete with respect to propositional truth but not maximal with respect to all possible extensions. The definition has conflated completeness with maximal consistency, when in truth a system may be complete for its intended domain whilst remaining consistently extendable to richer domains.

definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A has at least two premisses, (ii) the conclusion of A follows from the premisses by a recognised rule of inference, and (iii) the premisses of A are expressible in a formal language.
conditions: A has at least two premisses
the conclusion of A follows from the premisses by a recognised rule of inference
the premisses of A are expressible in a formal language
counterexample: Consider the argument consisting of the single premiss 'All swans are birds' and the conclusion 'All swans are birds.' The conclusion follows from the premiss by the rule of identity — it is, trivially, a consequence of itself. The premiss is expressible in first-order predicate logic. Yet the argument has only one premiss, so the first condition fails. The argument is nonetheless valid in the standard sense, for there is no possible circumstance in which the premiss is true and the conclusion false.
missing_condition: The definition requires at least two premisses, but an argument may be valid with only a single premiss, or indeed with no premisses at all, as in the case of a logical theorem derived from the empty set. The number of premisses is irrelevant to whether the conclusion follows necessarily from whatever premisses are given.
passage: One might propose that an argument is valid if and only if it has at least two premisses, its conclusion follows from the premisses by a recognised rule of inference, and its premisses are expressible in a formal language. The definition has a certain initial plausibility, for many of the arguments one first encounters in the study of logic do possess two or more premisses — the standard syllogism, for instance, comprises a major premiss, a minor premiss, and a conclusion. One might naturally suppose that this multiplicity of premisses is part of what it is for an argument to be an argument, and hence part of what it is for an argument to be valid. Yet one must consider the case of an argument with a single premiss: 'All swans are birds; therefore, all swans are birds.' The conclusion follows from the premiss by the simplest of logical principles — the principle of identity. There is no possible circumstance in which the premiss is true and the conclusion false. The premiss is perfectly expressible in a formal language. The argument is valid by any standard account of validity. Yet it has only one premiss, and the definition excludes it. The same difficulty arises, perhaps more strikingly, in the case of a logical theorem — a proposition that is derivable from no premisses at all, being a consequence of the logical rules alone. The definition has confused a contingent feature of many valid arguments — plurality of premisses — with a necessary condition of validity itself.

definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) the premisses of A are consistent with one another, (ii) the conclusion of A is not already contained among the premisses, and (iii) it is impossible for the premisses to be true and the conclusion false.
conditions: the premisses of A are consistent with one another
the conclusion of A is not already contained among the premisses
it is impossible for the premisses to be true and the conclusion false
counterexample: Consider an argument whose premisses are 'It is raining' and 'It is not raining,' with the conclusion 'The moon is made of green cheese.' The premisses are inconsistent — they cannot both be true. Yet the argument is valid, for there is no possible circumstance in which both premisses are true and the conclusion false; indeed, there is no circumstance in which both premisses are true at all. The first condition fails, but the argument satisfies the standard definition of validity.
missing_condition: The definition requires consistent premisses, but an argument with inconsistent premisses is trivially valid, since the impossibility of the premisses being jointly true ensures that there is no situation in which the premisses are true and the conclusion false. Consistency of premisses is not a necessary condition of validity; it is, rather, a condition one might impose for the separate purpose of ensuring that the argument is also sound or informative.
passage: Suppose one defines a valid argument as one whose premisses are consistent with one another, whose conclusion is not already contained among the premisses, and for which it is impossible that the premisses be true and the conclusion false. The motivation for the first condition is understandable: one might think that an argument from contradictory premisses is defective in a way that disqualifies it from counting as valid.
