The second condition reflects the intuition that a valid argument ought to take us somewhere new — that the conclusion ought to be a genuine consequence rather than a mere repetition. Yet the standard accounts of deductive validity in the logical literature impose neither requirement. Consider the argument: premiss one, 'It is raining'; premiss two, 'It is not raining'; conclusion, 'The moon is made of green cheese.' The premisses are manifestly inconsistent. But there is no possible situation in which both premisses are true and the conclusion false, for the simple reason that there is no possible situation in which both premisses are true. The argument is valid — trivially so, by explosion. The first condition fails. Likewise, the argument 'P; therefore P' has its conclusion contained in its premisses, yet it is valid. The second condition fails. The definition has imported conditions — consistency and non-circularity — that are desiderata for good reasoning but not necessary conditions of validity as such.

definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) each premiss of A is a declarative sentence, (ii) the conclusion of A can be derived from the premisses in finitely many steps, and (iii) no premiss of A is logically equivalent to the conclusion.
conditions: each premiss of A is a declarative sentence
the conclusion of A can be derived from the premisses in finitely many steps
no premiss of A is logically equivalent to the conclusion
counterexample: Consider the argument 'Either it will rain or it will not rain; therefore, it will rain or it will not rain.' The premiss is logically equivalent to the conclusion — indeed, it is the very same sentence. The derivation requires but a single step. Yet the argument is valid in the fullest sense: there is no possible circumstance in which the premiss is true and the conclusion false. The third condition excludes it, but the argument is undeniably valid.
missing_condition: The definition prohibits logical equivalence between premiss and conclusion, but an argument whose conclusion is logically equivalent to one of its premisses is trivially valid. The non-equivalence condition confuses a desideratum of informative reasoning with a necessary condition of validity.
passage: One might propose that an argument is valid if and only if each of its premisses is a declarative sentence, the conclusion can be derived from the premisses in finitely many steps, and no premiss is logically equivalent to the conclusion. The first two conditions seem uncontroversial; indeed, they capture something important about the nature of deductive reasoning. The third condition is motivated by the thought that a valid argument ought to accomplish something — that the conclusion ought not merely to restate what is already given. There is a venerable tradition in logic that distinguishes between trivial and non-trivial consequences, and one might suppose that only non-trivial derivations deserve the name of validity. Yet consider the argument: 'Either it will rain or it will not rain; therefore, it will rain or it will not rain.' The premiss and the conclusion are one and the same tautology. The derivation is immediate. And the argument is valid — there is no interpretation under which the premiss is true and the conclusion false. The definition, however, excludes it, since the premiss is logically equivalent to the conclusion. The difficulty is that validity is a structural property of the relationship between premisses and conclusion — it concerns the impossibility of a certain truth-value combination — and it is entirely indifferent to whether the conclusion says something new. The definition has conflated logical consequence with informational or cognitive advance.

definition: An argument A is sound if and only if (i) A is valid, (ii) all premisses of A are empirically verifiable, and (iii) the conclusion of A is true.
conditions: A is valid
all premisses of A are empirically verifiable
the conclusion of A is true
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'All bachelors are unmarried; John is a bachelor; therefore, John is unmarried.' This is a valid argument with true premisses and a true conclusion. It is sound by any standard account. Yet the premiss 'All bachelors are unmarried' is not empirically verifiable — it is true in virtue of the meanings of the words, not in virtue of any observation. The second condition fails, yet the argument is paradigmatically sound.
missing_condition: The definition requires empirical verifiability of the premisses, but soundness requires only that the premisses be true and the argument valid. Many sound arguments proceed from premisses that are true a priori — definitions, logical truths, mathematical axioms — which are not empirically verifiable in any straightforward sense.
passage: Suppose one proposes that an argument is sound if and only if it is valid, all of its premisses are empirically verifiable, and its conclusion is true. The inclusion of empirical verifiability reflects the thought, prominent in certain philosophical traditions, that the premisses of a respectable argument ought to be answerable to experience — that one ought to be able, at least in principle, to determine their truth by observation or experiment. This is an appealing requirement for arguments in the natural sciences. Yet consider a simple syllogism drawn from the domain of conceptual analysis: 'All bachelors are unmarried; John is a bachelor; therefore, John is unmarried.' The argument is valid — the conclusion follows from the premisses by the rules of the syllogism. The premisses are true, and the conclusion is true. The argument is sound by any standard account. Yet the major premiss, 'All bachelors are unmarried,' is not empirically verifiable. One does not verify it by surveying bachelors and checking their marital status; it is true in virtue of the meaning of the word 'bachelor.' It is, as one might say, an analytic truth, knowable a priori. The second condition of the proposed definition fails. The same difficulty would arise for any sound argument whose premisses include mathematical truths, logical laws, or definitions. The definition has confused a property that is desirable in certain domains of inquiry — empirical verifiability — with a condition necessary for soundness as such.

definition: An argument A is sound if and only if (i) A is valid, (ii) every premiss of A is accepted by all competent speakers of the language, and (iii) every premiss of A is true.
conditions: A is valid
every premiss of A is accepted by all competent speakers of the language
every premiss of A is true
counterexample: Consider a valid argument in number theory whose premisses include the axiom of choice. The axiom of choice is not accepted by all competent speakers — indeed, it is rejected by constructivist mathematicians who are perfectly competent in the language of set theory. Yet if the axiom is true, and the argument is valid, the argument is sound regardless of whether every competent speaker endorses its premisses. The second condition fails, but the argument may still be sound.
missing_condition: The definition requires universal acceptance by competent speakers, but soundness depends on the truth of the premisses and the validity of the argument, not on universal assent. A premiss may be true even though reasonable and competent persons disagree about its truth.
passage: One might hold that an argument is sound if and only if it is valid, every premiss is accepted by all competent speakers of the language, and every premiss is true. The second condition reflects a natural thought: if an argument is truly sound, one might suppose, its premisses ought to be beyond dispute — they ought to command the assent of every rational person who understands the language in which they are expressed. The condition is motivated by the idea that soundness is, in some sense, a public or intersubjective property. Yet the history of logic and mathematics furnishes difficulties. Consider a valid argument in set theory whose premisses include the axiom of choice. This axiom — that for any collection of non-empty sets, there exists a function selecting one element from each — has been the subject of sustained disagreement among mathematicians of the highest competence. Constructivists reject it; classical mathematicians accept it. If the axiom of choice is true, then a valid argument from premisses that include it is sound, regardless of the fact that some competent mathematicians refuse to accept it. The second condition fails. The difficulty is general: many truths are controversial, and many sound arguments proceed from premisses that are not universally accepted. Soundness is a matter of the truth of the premisses and the validity of the argument; it is indifferent to the sociology of assent.

definition: A proposition Q is a logical consequence of a set of propositions S if and only if (i) Q can be derived from S by successive applications of modus ponens, (ii) every member of S is expressible in propositional logic, and (iii) Q is distinct from every member of S.
conditions: Q can be derived from S by successive applications of modus ponens
every member of S is expressible in propositional logic
Q is distinct from every member of S
counterexample: Consider the set S containing the single proposition 'All men are mortal and Socrates is a man,' and let Q be 'Socrates is mortal.' Q is a logical consequence of S in the standard sense — in every model in which S is satisfied, Q is satisfied also. Yet the derivation requires not modus ponens but universal instantiation, a rule of predicate logic. The first condition fails, for modus ponens alone cannot extract the conclusion. The second condition fails also, for the premiss involves quantification and is not expressible in propositional logic. Yet Q undeniably follows from S.
missing_condition: The definition restricts logical consequence to derivability by modus ponens within propositional logic, but logical consequence is a broader notion encompassing all of deductive logic, including predicate logic, modal logic, and other formal systems. The definition has confused one particular rule and one particular formal language with the general concept of logical consequence.
passage: Suppose one defines logical consequence as follows: a proposition Q is a logical consequence of a set S if and only if Q can be derived from S by successive applications of modus ponens, every member of S is expressible in propositional logic, and Q is distinct from every member of S. The definition captures something genuine, for modus ponens is perhaps the most fundamental rule of inference, and propositional logic is the simplest formal language in which deductive reasoning can be conducted. A great many logical consequences can be established by repeated application of modus ponens to propositional formulae. Yet consider the inference from 'All men are mortal and Socrates is a man' to 'Socrates is mortal.' This is among the oldest and most celebrated examples of logical consequence in the philosophical tradition. The conclusion follows from the premiss with absolute necessity. Yet the derivation cannot be carried out by modus ponens alone; it requires universal instantiation — a rule that belongs to predicate logic, not propositional logic. The premiss itself involves quantification over individuals and is not expressible in propositional logic. The first and second conditions both fail. The definition has taken one prominent rule and one elementary formal language and elevated them to necessary conditions of logical consequence, when in truth the concept of logical consequence is far broader, encompassing the full resources of predicate logic, higher-order logic, and indeed any sound deductive system.

definition: A statement S is paradoxical if and only if (i) S is self-referential, (ii) S leads to a contradiction when one attempts to assign it a truth value, and (iii) S is expressible in natural language.
conditions: S is self-referential
S leads to a contradiction when one attempts to assign it a truth value
S is expressible in natural language
counterexample: Consider the Banach-Tarski paradox: a solid ball in three-dimensional space can be decomposed into finitely many pieces and reassembled, using only rotations and translations, into two solid balls each identical in size to the original. This result is paradoxical — it conflicts violently with our ordinary spatial intuitions and is standardly called a paradox in the mathematical literature. Yet it is not self-referential; it concerns geometrical objects. It does not lead to a contradiction; it is a theorem of Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory with the axiom of choice. All three conditions fail, yet the result is paradigmatically paradoxical.
missing_condition: The definition restricts paradoxes to self-referential, contradictory statements in natural language, but many paradoxes — including the Banach-Tarski paradox, Skolem's paradox, and the paradox of the heap — are not self-referential, do not involve outright contradiction, or are framed in formal rather than natural language. A paradox is better understood as any result or situation that conflicts sharply with well-entrenched expectations, whether or not it involves self-reference or contradiction.
passage: One might propose that a statement is paradoxical if and only if it is self-referential, it leads to a contradiction when one attempts to assign it a truth value, and it is expressible in natural language. This definition fits the liar paradox admirably — the sentence 'This sentence is false' refers to itself, generates a contradiction whether one assigns it truth or falsity, and is expressed in ordinary English. One might suppose that all paradoxes share these features. Yet one must consider the Banach-Tarski paradox: the theorem, proved rigorously within Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory with the axiom of choice, that a solid ball in three-dimensional Euclidean space can be decomposed into a finite number of disjoint subsets and reassembled, by rigid motions alone, into two solid balls each of the same volume as the original. This result is called a paradox by mathematicians and philosophers alike, and it is paradoxical in the most vivid sense — it affronts our deepest spatial intuitions. Yet it is not self-referential; it is a statement about geometrical objects, not about itself. It does not lead to a contradiction; it is a proved theorem, consistent with the axioms of the theory. And while it can be stated informally, its rigorous formulation belongs to formal mathematics. All three conditions fail. The definition has modelled the concept of paradox upon a single, albeit famous, exemplar — the liar — and has mistaken features peculiar to semantic paradoxes for necessary conditions of paradoxicality as such.

definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) P and Q share at least one non-logical term, (ii) the conditional 'if P then Q' is a logical truth, and (iii) Q is not a logical truth.
conditions: P and Q share at least one non-logical term
the conditional 'if P then Q' is a logical truth
Q is not a logical truth
counterexample: Consider the proposition P, 'It is raining and it is not raining,' and the proposition Q, 'Two plus two equals five.' The conditional 'if P then Q' is a logical truth — an impossible antecedent entails any consequent whatsoever. Yet P and Q share no non-logical terms: P concerns rain and Q concerns arithmetic. The first condition fails. Nevertheless, P entails Q in the classical sense, for there is no possible circumstance in which P is true and Q false.
missing_condition: The definition requires a shared non-logical term, but classical entailment places no such relevance condition on the relationship between premiss and conclusion. A contradiction entails every proposition, regardless of subject matter. The shared-term requirement confuses relevance logic with classical entailment.
passage: One might define entailment as follows: P entails Q if and only if P and Q share at least one non-logical term, the conditional 'if P then Q' is a logical truth, and Q is not a logical truth. The first condition reflects the intuition that if P genuinely entails Q, then P and Q must be about the same subject matter — they must, at a minimum, share some content. The third condition reflects the thought that a logical truth is not entailed by anything in particular, since it is true in virtue of logic alone. Together, the three conditions yield a notion of entailment that is both formally rigorous and intuitively satisfying. Yet consider a contradictory proposition — 'It is raining and it is not raining' — and an arbitrary proposition — 'Two plus two equals five.' In classical logic, the conditional from a contradiction to any proposition is a logical truth; a contradiction entails every proposition. This is the principle of explosion, and it is a cornerstone of classical logic. But 'It is raining and it is not raining' and 'Two plus two equals five' share no non-logical vocabulary whatsoever. The first condition fails. The definition excludes what classical logic affirms. The difficulty is that the shared-term condition, whilst capturing a genuine intuition about relevance, belongs to a different conception of entailment — one associated with relevance logic — and is not a necessary condition of entailment as classically understood.

definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) every model that satisfies P also satisfies Q, (ii) P is not itself a contradiction, and (iii) Q is not itself a tautology.
conditions: every model that satisfies P also satisfies Q
P is not itself a contradiction
Q is not itself a tautology
counterexample: Consider the tautology 'Either it is raining or it is not raining.' Every proposition entails a tautology, for a tautology is true in every model. The proposition 'Snow is white' satisfies the first condition with respect to this tautology: every model satisfying 'Snow is white' also satisfies 'Either it is raining or it is not raining.' Yet the definition excludes this case because Q is a tautology. The third condition fails, but the entailment holds under the standard semantic account.
missing_condition: The definition excludes tautologies from being entailed, but every proposition entails every tautology, and a contradiction entails every proposition. These are features, not defects, of classical entailment. The definition conflates a substantive or interesting entailment relation with the formal notion of entailment itself.
passage: Suppose one defines entailment thus: P entails Q if and only if every model satisfying P also satisfies Q, P is not a contradiction, and Q is not a tautology. The second and third conditions are motivated by the desire to exclude degenerate cases. If P is a contradiction, it has no models, so every model satisfying P trivially satisfies Q; and if Q is a tautology, it is satisfied by every model, so every model satisfying P satisfies Q regardless of what P says. In both cases, one might feel, the entailment is vacuous and uninteresting. Yet the standard semantic definition of entailment places no such restrictions. Consider the proposition 'Either it is raining or it is not raining' — a tautology, true in every model. The proposition 'Snow is white' entails it, for every model in which 'Snow is white' is true is also a model in which the tautology holds. This is not controversial; it is a straightforward consequence of the semantic account. But the definition excludes it because Q is a tautology. The third condition fails. Similarly, a contradiction does entail every proposition — this is the principle of explosion, and it is accepted in classical logic as a limiting case of the entailment relation, not as an error. The definition has attempted to restrict entailment to its substantive and interesting instances, but in so doing it has altered the concept itself, excluding cases that the standard account unambiguously includes.

definition: A set of propositions S entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) Q can be derived from S by a sequence of valid inferences, (ii) the derivation is unique, and (iii) each inference in the derivation introduces exactly one new proposition.
conditions: Q can be derived from S by a sequence of valid inferences
the derivation is unique
each inference in the derivation introduces exactly one new proposition
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'P and (P implies Q)' entailing Q. One derivation proceeds by conjunction elimination to obtain P and 'P implies Q' separately, then applies modus ponens. Another derivation might first derive 'P implies Q' by conjunction elimination, then derive P by conjunction elimination, then apply modus ponens in the reverse order. Both derivations are valid; both establish the same entailment. The second condition — uniqueness of derivation — fails, yet the entailment certainly holds.
missing_condition: The definition requires a unique derivation, but for any non-trivial entailment there will typically be multiple distinct proofs. Entailment is a semantic relation between propositions; it does not depend on there being exactly one way to demonstrate it. The uniqueness condition confuses entailment with some notion of canonical proof.
passage: One might hold that a set of propositions S entails Q if and only if Q can be derived from S by valid inferences, the derivation is unique, and each step introduces exactly one new proposition. The uniqueness condition might be motivated by an analogy with computation: just as one might wish a function to have a unique output for each input, one might wish an entailment to have a unique proof. The third condition imposes a certain tidiness upon the derivation. Yet consider a simple case. Let S contain 'P and (P implies Q),' and let Q be the proposition Q. One may derive Q as follows: first, extract P from the conjunction; second, extract 'P implies Q'; third, apply modus ponens to obtain Q. But one may equally derive Q by extracting 'P implies Q' first, then P, then applying modus ponens. These are distinct derivations — the order of the intermediate steps differs — yet both are valid, and both establish the same entailment. For any entailment of even modest complexity, the number of distinct derivations will be large, and in many cases infinite. The uniqueness condition fails. Entailment is a relation that holds between propositions in virtue of their logical structure; it is not a relation that depends on there being a single canonical way of demonstrating it. The definition has confused the semantic notion of entailment with a proof-theoretic notion that is, in any case, too restrictive to be faithful even to the proof-theoretic tradition.

definition: A set of propositions S is consistent if and only if (i) every proposition in S is individually possible, (ii) no two propositions in S are negations of one another, and (iii) S is finite.
conditions: every proposition in S is individually possible
no two propositions in S are negations of one another
S is finite
counterexample: Consider the infinite set of propositions comprising all true statements of elementary arithmetic: '1+1=2,' '2+2=4,' '3+3=6,' and so forth, without end. This set is consistent — no contradiction can be derived from it. Each member is individually possible; no two members are negations of one another. Yet the set is infinite. The third condition fails, but the set is paradigmatically consistent.
missing_condition: The definition requires finiteness, but consistency is a property that applies equally to infinite sets of propositions. Many of the most important consistent theories in mathematics and logic — Peano arithmetic, Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory — have infinitely many axioms or infinitely many theorems. Finiteness is not a necessary condition of consistency.
passage: Suppose one defines consistency as follows: a set of propositions is consistent if and only if every proposition in the set is individually possible, no two propositions are negations of one another, and the set is finite. The finiteness condition might be motivated by the thought that one can only survey a finite collection, and that one needs to survey a set to determine its consistency. The condition on negation pairs captures the most obvious source of inconsistency. Yet the definition is inadequate. Consider the infinite set of all true arithmetical statements — the set containing '1+1=2,' '2+2=4,' '7 is prime,' and every other true proposition of elementary number theory. This set is consistent; indeed, it is the set of truths of a particular mathematical structure and therefore cannot contain a contradiction. Every member is individually possible, and no two members are negations of one another. But the set is infinite — it contains infinitely many distinct arithmetical truths. The third condition fails. More generally, the axiom systems of the most important mathematical theories — Peano arithmetic, Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory — give rise to infinite sets of theorems, and the consistency of such theories is of the greatest logical significance. A definition that restricts consistency to finite sets excludes the very cases in which the concept is most important and most deeply studied.

definition: A set of propositions S is consistent if and only if (i) no contradiction can be derived from S, (ii) S has at least one model, and (iii) every proper subset of S is also consistent.
conditions: no contradiction can be derived from S
S has at least one model
every proper subset of S is also consistent
counterexample: Consider the set S = {P, Q, P implies not-Q}. This set is inconsistent — from P and 'P implies not-Q' one derives not-Q, which contradicts Q. Yet the proper subset {P, Q} is consistent, and the proper subset {P, P implies not-Q} is consistent, and the proper subset {Q, P implies not-Q} is consistent. Each proper subset is consistent, but S itself is not. This shows that the third condition is not sufficient for consistency. Conversely, consider any consistent set that contains an inconsistent proper subset — but no such set can exist, since if a proper subset yields a contradiction, the whole set does as well. The point is rather that the third condition is automatically satisfied by certain inconsistent sets when we reverse the direction: the set {P, Q, P implies not-Q} fails the first two conditions whilst satisfying the third, showing that the three conditions are not jointly sufficient; but more importantly, they exclude consistent sets unnecessarily when read as individually necessary, since some might question whether every proper subset's consistency is a meaningful requirement. Consider instead the set S = {P or not-P, Q, R}. Every proper subset is consistent, and S itself is consistent. The third condition holds but adds nothing to the first two. It is not that the condition is too strong — it is that it is either trivially satisfied or irrelevant to what it is for a set to be consistent.
passage: One might propose that a set of propositions is consistent if and only if no contradiction can be derived from it, it has at least one model, and every proper subset of it is also consistent. The first two conditions, taken together, constitute the standard account — they are, in the presence of a complete proof system, equivalent. The third condition is novel and merits examination. It might be motivated by the thought that genuine consistency ought to be robust — that a set ought to be consistent not merely as a whole but in every part. This is a species of structural requirement: the consistency of the whole should be grounded in the consistency of each portion. Yet the condition is redundant. If a set S is consistent, then every proper subset of S is necessarily consistent; one cannot derive a contradiction from fewer premisses if one cannot derive a contradiction from more premisses (since anything derivable from a subset is derivable from the whole). The third condition follows logically from the first and, whilst true of every consistent set, adds nothing to the characterisation. It is satisfied trivially. More interestingly, it can be satisfied by inconsistent sets as well: the set containing P, Q, and 'P implies not-Q' is inconsistent, yet every two-element proper subset is consistent. The three conditions are thus not jointly sufficient for consistency, because the third condition does not prevent the kind of inconsistency that arises only when all members of the set are taken together. The definition is flawed in its attempt to strengthen the standard account with a structural requirement that is simultaneously redundant and insufficient.

definition: A formal theory T is consistent if and only if (i) T has a finite axiomatisation, (ii) no theorem of T is the negation of another theorem of T, and (iii) T is complete.
conditions: T has a finite axiomatisation
no theorem of T is the negation of another theorem of T
T is complete
counterexample: Peano arithmetic is widely held to be consistent. It can be given a finite axiomatisation (with axiom schemata). No theorem of Peano arithmetic is the negation of another theorem, assuming its consistency. Yet Peano arithmetic is incomplete — by Goedel's first incompleteness theorem, there are sentences of arithmetic that are neither provable nor refutable within the theory. The third condition fails, but Peano arithmetic is nonetheless consistent.
missing_condition: The definition requires completeness, but by Goedel's incompleteness theorems, any sufficiently strong consistent theory is incomplete. Requiring completeness as a condition of consistency would render inconsistent all of the most important formal theories in mathematics, which is absurd. Completeness and consistency are independent properties.
passage: Suppose one proposes that a formal theory is consistent if and only if it has a finite axiomatisation, no theorem is the negation of another theorem, and the theory is complete. The completeness condition might be motivated by the thought that a genuinely consistent theory ought to settle every question in its domain — that for every sentence in the language of the theory, either the sentence or its negation should be provable. This would ensure that the theory leaves no gaps, no undecidable propositions, no lacunae. Yet Goedel's first incompleteness theorem, published in 1931, demonstrates that any consistent formal theory that is sufficiently strong to express elementary arithmetic is incomplete: there exist sentences in the language of the theory that are neither provable nor refutable within the theory. Peano arithmetic is the paradigmatic example. It is finitely axiomatisable in the relevant sense, and — assuming its consistency — no theorem is the negation of another. Yet it is incomplete. The third condition fails. If completeness were a necessary condition of consistency, then Peano arithmetic would be inconsistent, which contradicts our best understanding of the theory. The definition has conflated two distinct desiderata — consistency and completeness — that are, as Goedel showed, in tension with one another for any theory of sufficient expressive power. A theory may be consistent without being complete, and indeed for the most important mathematical theories, incompleteness is an unavoidable consequence of consistency itself.

definition: A property F is definable in a formal language L if and only if (i) there exists a formula phi of L such that phi is true of all and only the objects with property F, (ii) phi contains no free variables other than the variable ranging over the objects in question, and (iii) phi is logically equivalent to a formula in prenex normal form with at most one quantifier.
conditions: there exists a formula phi of L such that phi is true of all and only the objects with property F
phi contains no free variables other than the variable ranging over the objects in question
phi is logically equivalent to a formula in prenex normal form with at most one quantifier
counterexample: Consider the property of being a natural number n such that for every prime p dividing n, p squared also divides n. This property — the property of being a powerful number — is definable in the language of arithmetic. The defining formula requires quantification over divisors and their squares, which involves multiple nested quantifiers. It is not logically equivalent to any formula with at most one quantifier. The third condition fails, yet the property is perfectly definable in the language.
missing_condition: The definition restricts definability to properties expressible with at most one quantifier, but many perfectly definable properties require multiple nested quantifiers. The quantifier complexity of a defining formula is a measure of the complexity of the property, not a condition on its definability. Definability requires only the existence of a suitable formula, with no restriction on quantifier depth.
passage: One might propose that a property is definable in a formal language if and only if there is a formula of the language that is true of all and only the objects possessing the property, the formula contains no free variables beyond the relevant individual variable, and the formula is logically equivalent to one in prenex normal form with at most one quantifier. The third condition imposes a simplicity requirement: only properties that can be expressed with minimal quantificational complexity count as definable. This might be motivated by the thought that a genuinely definable property ought to be simply expressible — that a definition ought not to require layer upon layer of quantification. Yet many properties of the greatest mathematical interest require multiple quantifiers for their expression. Consider the property of being a powerful number — a natural number n such that for every prime p dividing n, the square of p also divides n. The defining formula requires at least two quantifiers: one ranging over prime divisors, another expressing the divisibility condition for their squares. No equivalent formula with a single quantifier exists. The third condition fails, yet the property is unambiguously definable in the language of arithmetic; one can write down a precise formula that is true of exactly the powerful numbers and no others. The definition has confused a desideratum of expressive simplicity with a condition on definability itself. The concept of definability requires only that a suitable formula exist; it places no bound on the formula's quantificational complexity.

definition: A concept C is definable if and only if (i) C can be expressed by a finite conjunction of necessary and sufficient conditions, (ii) the conditions employ only primitive terms of the language, and (iii) the definition is non-circular — no condition mentions C itself.
conditions: C can be expressed by a finite conjunction of necessary and sufficient conditions
the conditions employ only primitive terms of the language
the definition is non-circular — no condition mentions C itself
counterexample: Consider the concept of a well-formed formula in the language of propositional logic. The standard definition is recursive: an atomic proposition is a well-formed formula; if phi is a well-formed formula, then its negation is a well-formed formula; if phi and psi are well-formed formulae, then their conjunction is a well-formed formula; and so forth. The definition is inductive — it mentions the concept being defined in its own clauses. The third condition fails; the definition is circular by the proposed standard. Yet the concept of a well-formed formula is perfectly definable, and the recursive definition is the standard means of defining it.
missing_condition: The definition prohibits circularity, but recursive and inductive definitions — which are essential in logic and mathematics — are circular in form whilst being perfectly well-grounded. Recursive definitions define a concept by specifying base cases and inductive clauses, and they are a paradigm of rigorous definability. The ban on circularity excludes the very mode of definition most characteristic of formal logic.
passage: Suppose one proposes that a concept is definable if and only if it can be expressed as a finite conjunction of necessary and sufficient conditions, the conditions employ only primitive terms, and the definition is non-circular. The non-circularity condition is motivated by the thought that a definition ought to elucidate a concept by reducing it to terms that are already understood; a circular definition, by this reasoning, fails to elucidate because it presupposes the very concept it purports to define. This is a venerable requirement, traceable to Aristotle's strictures on definition. Yet one of the most fundamental concepts in logic — the concept of a well-formed formula — is standardly defined by recursion. The definition proceeds: an atomic proposition is well-formed; if phi is well-formed, then the negation of phi is well-formed; if phi and psi are well-formed, then the conjunction of phi and psi is well-formed; nothing else is well-formed. The concept of a well-formed formula appears in its own definition — the inductive clauses mention 'well-formed formula' — and the definition is therefore circular by the standard of the proposed account. Yet it is among the most successful and universally accepted definitions in all of logic. The recursive definition is well-grounded; it permits one to determine, for any string of symbols, whether it is well-formed, by tracing the construction back to atomic propositions. The definition has confused viciously circular definitions — which genuinely fail to define — with inductive definitions, which are a paradigm of rigorous concept formation in mathematics and logic.

definition: A reasoning pattern P is paradoxical if and only if (i) P proceeds from premisses that are individually obvious, (ii) P employs only valid deductive steps, and (iii) the conclusion of P is obviously false.
conditions: P proceeds from premisses that are individually obvious
P employs only valid deductive steps
the conclusion of P is obviously false
counterexample: Consider the paradox of the preface. An author writes a book containing many assertions, each of which she has checked carefully and holds to be true. In the preface, she acknowledges that the book almost certainly contains at least one error. She thus holds each assertion to be true whilst also holding that at least one assertion is false. This is paradoxical — it seems to show that rational persons can have inconsistent sets of commitments. Yet the conclusion — that the author's total set of commitments is inconsistent — is not obviously false; it is, rather, puzzling and thought-provoking. One might even regard it as true. The third condition fails, yet the preface paradox is a genuine paradox.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the conclusion be obviously false, but some paradoxes conclude with propositions that are merely surprising, puzzling, or counterintuitive rather than obviously false. The preface paradox and similar cases show that paradoxicality may consist in the tension between plausible premisses and an unsettling conclusion, even when the conclusion is not straightforwardly false.
passage: One might hold that a reasoning pattern is paradoxical if and only if it proceeds from individually obvious premisses, employs only valid deductive steps, and arrives at an obviously false conclusion. This definition captures an important class of paradoxes admirably. The Russell paradox, for instance, proceeds from the seemingly obvious principle that for any property there is a set of all objects having that property, employs straightforward set-theoretic reasoning, and arrives at the obviously false conclusion that a certain set both does and does not contain itself. The definition identifies the characteristic tension — between the plausibility of the premisses and the absurdity of the conclusion — that makes paradoxes philosophically significant. Yet one must consider the paradox of the preface. An author writes a book of many chapters, each containing assertions she has carefully verified and holds to be true. In the preface, she writes that the book almost certainly contains at least one error — a modest and eminently reasonable acknowledgement. She thus holds each assertion in the book to be true, and simultaneously holds that at least one of those assertions is false. The situation is paradoxical; it seems to show that a perfectly rational person can have a set of commitments that is, taken collectively, inconsistent. But the conclusion is not obviously false. One might judge it surprising, or philosophically troubling, but it is not in the same category as the conclusion that a set both belongs and does not belong to itself. Indeed, some philosophers accept the conclusion and take the paradox to reveal something important about rational commitment. The third condition fails. The definition has modelled paradoxicality on the most dramatic examples and has overlooked the more subtle paradoxes whose force lies not in an obviously false conclusion but in an unsettling or counterintuitive one.

definition: Q is a logical consequence of P if and only if (i) Q is true in every possible world in which P is true, (ii) there is a possible world in which P is true, and (iii) there is a possible world in which Q is false.
conditions: Q is true in every possible world in which P is true
there is a possible world in which P is true
there is a possible world in which Q is false
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'Either it is raining or it is not raining.' This is a logical truth — it is true in every possible world. Any proposition P whatsoever has this tautology as a logical consequence, since every world in which P is true is a world in which the tautology is true. Yet there is no possible world in which the tautology is false. The third condition fails, but the tautology is a logical consequence of every proposition.
missing_condition: The definition requires that Q be false in some possible world, but logical truths are consequences of every proposition and are false in no possible world. The condition was intended to exclude trivial cases but in fact excludes the very propositions that are most universally entailed.
passage: Suppose one defines logical consequence as follows: Q is a logical consequence of P if and only if Q is true in every possible world in which P is true, there is a possible world in which P is true, and there is a possible world in which Q is false. The second and third conditions are designed to exclude degenerate cases — the second ensures that P is not impossible, the third ensures that Q is not trivially necessary. Together, they restrict logical consequence to cases in which the relationship between P and Q is substantive: P must be possibly true, and Q must be possibly false, so that the preservation of truth from P to Q is a genuine logical achievement rather than a vacuous artefact. Yet one must consider the case of a tautology — say, 'Either it is raining or it is not raining.' This proposition is true in every possible world. Every proposition P is such that in every world where P is true, the tautology is true also. The tautology is a logical consequence of every proposition whatsoever. But there is no possible world in which the tautology is false. The third condition fails. The definition excludes tautologies from being logical consequences of anything, which contradicts the standard account. The difficulty is that the third condition, whilst motivated by the desire to exclude trivial cases, in fact excludes the most fundamental case of all: the logical truth, which follows from everything precisely because it is true everywhere.

definition: A deductive system D is valid if and only if (i) every theorem of D is true under the intended interpretation, (ii) every rule of inference in D preserves truth, and (iii) D has only finitely many rules of inference.
conditions: every theorem of D is true under the intended interpretation
every rule of inference in D preserves truth
D has only finitely many rules of inference
counterexample: Consider a deductive system for first-order logic that includes an omega-rule: from the premisses 'phi(0),' 'phi(1),' 'phi(2),' and so on for every natural number, one may infer 'for all n, phi(n).' This rule has infinitely many premisses, and a system employing it alongside the standard rules has infinitely many rules if one counts each instance of the omega-rule as a separate rule, or it has a rule that is not finitary in character. Yet the system is valid — every theorem it produces is true under the intended interpretation. The third condition fails, but the system is valid.
missing_condition: The definition requires finitely many rules, but validity of a deductive system depends on the soundness of its rules and the truth of its axioms, not on the number of rules. Systems with infinitely many rules or with infinitary rules — such as the omega-rule — may be perfectly valid.
passage: One might propose that a deductive system is valid if and only if every theorem is true under the intended interpretation, every rule preserves truth, and the system has only finitely many rules of inference. The finitude condition reflects a natural thought: a deductive system ought to be surveyable, and surveyability seems to require that the rules be finite in number and each rule finite in form. Many standard presentations of logic do, in fact, employ finitely many rules — modus ponens, universal generalisation, and a handful of others suffice for a complete axiomatisation of first-order logic. Yet one must consider the omega-rule, employed in certain infinitary proof systems for arithmetic. The omega-rule permits one to infer 'for all n, phi(n)' from the infinite collection of premisses 'phi(0),' 'phi(1),' 'phi(2),' and so on without end. A deductive system that includes the omega-rule is valid — every theorem it produces is true in the standard model of arithmetic. Indeed, the omega-rule is truth-preserving in the clearest possible sense: if phi holds of every natural number individually, then it holds of all natural numbers universally. But the system has an infinitary rule, and one might count infinitely many instances. The third condition fails. The definition has confused a practical desideratum — finitude of the rule set, which is important for effective computability and human surveyability — with a condition on validity itself. A system may be valid even if its rules are infinite in number or infinitary in character, provided every rule preserves truth.

definition: A deductive system D is sound if and only if (i) every axiom of D is a logical truth, (ii) every rule of inference of D preserves logical truth, and (iii) D is complete — every logical truth is a theorem of D.
conditions: every axiom of D is a logical truth
every rule of inference of D preserves logical truth
D is complete — every logical truth is a theorem of D
counterexample: Consider a deductive system for propositional logic that includes modus ponens and a proper subset of the standard axioms — enough to derive some, but not all, tautologies. Every axiom is a logical truth; modus ponens preserves logical truth. The system is sound: it proves only truths. But it is not complete — there exist tautologies that it cannot derive. The third condition fails, yet the system is sound.
missing_condition: The definition conflates soundness with completeness. Soundness requires only that everything the system proves is true, not that the system proves everything that is true. Completeness is a separate and independent virtue. A system may be sound without being complete.
passage: Suppose one defines soundness as follows: a deductive system is sound if and only if every axiom is a logical truth, every rule preserves logical truth, and the system is complete. The first two conditions are standard; they ensure that the system never leads from truth to falsehood. The third condition — completeness — requires that every logical truth be derivable within the system. One might motivate the inclusion of completeness by the thought that a sound system ought to be adequate to its subject matter — that it ought to capture all the truths of the domain, not merely some of them. Yet soundness and completeness are distinct and independent properties. Consider a deductive system for propositional logic that has been deliberately weakened: it includes modus ponens and a restricted set of axiom schemata that are all tautologies, but the axiom schemata are insufficient to derive every tautology. Such a system is sound — every theorem it produces is a logical truth, since the axioms are tautologies and modus ponens preserves tautologousness. But it is incomplete; there are tautologies that lie beyond its reach. The third condition fails. The definition has yoked together two properties that logic carefully distinguishes. Soundness is the property of proving only truths; completeness is the property of proving all truths. A system may enjoy the former without the latter, and the proposed definition, by requiring both, has redefined soundness in a way that conflicts with standard usage and obscures a fundamental logical distinction.

definition: A relation R on a domain D is definable in a language L if and only if (i) there is a formula of L that is satisfied by exactly the tuples in R, (ii) the formula uses only the connectives of first-order logic, and (iii) the domain D is countable.
conditions: there is a formula of L that is satisfied by exactly the tuples in R
the formula uses only the connectives of first-order logic
the domain D is countable
counterexample: Consider the real number line — an uncountable domain — and the relation 'x is less than y.' This relation is definable in the first-order language of ordered fields: the formula 'x < y' is satisfied by exactly those pairs (x, y) where x is less than y. The formula uses only first-order connectives. Yet the domain is uncountable. The third condition fails, but the relation is perfectly definable.
missing_condition: The definition requires a countable domain, but definability is a feature of the relationship between a language and a structure, and it applies to uncountable domains no less than to countable ones. Many of the most important examples of definability — in the real numbers, in the complex numbers, in arbitrary set-theoretic structures — involve uncountable domains.
passage: One might propose that a relation is definable in a language if and only if there is a formula satisfied by exactly the tuples in the relation, the formula uses only first-order connectives, and the domain is countable. The countability condition might be motivated by the Loewenheim-Skolem theorem, which guarantees that any satisfiable first-order theory has a countable model. One might infer from this that the natural setting for first-order definability is the countable domain. Yet the inference is mistaken. The Loewenheim-Skolem theorem shows that countable models exist; it does not show that definability is restricted to countable domains. Consider the real number line, an uncountable set equipped with the ordering relation. The relation 'x < y' is definable in the first-order language of ordered fields — the formula 'x < y' captures exactly the intended pairs. The language is first-order; the connectives are standard. But the domain is uncountable: the real numbers form a continuum. The third condition fails. The definition excludes definability over the real numbers, the complex numbers, and indeed over any uncountable structure, which would render the concept of definability useless for a vast portion of mathematics. The difficulty is that countability of the domain is irrelevant to whether a formula of the language picks out a particular relation; definability depends on the expressive resources of the language and the structure of the domain, not on the domain's cardinality.

definition: P entails Q if and only if (i) the conjunction of P and the negation of Q is unsatisfiable, (ii) P is contingent, and (iii) Q is contingent.
conditions: the conjunction of P and the negation of Q is unsatisfiable
P is contingent
Q is contingent
counterexample: Consider the logical truth 'For all x, x equals x.' Every proposition entails this truth, and it entails itself. The conjunction of any proposition P with the negation of this truth is unsatisfiable, since the negation of a logical truth is itself unsatisfiable. Yet 'For all x, x equals x' is not contingent — it is necessarily true. Both the second and third conditions may fail depending on the case, yet the entailment holds.
missing_condition: The definition requires both P and Q to be contingent, but necessary truths are entailed by every proposition, and contradictions entail every proposition, regardless of contingency. The contingency condition excludes the limiting cases that are central to the logical analysis of entailment.
passage: Suppose one defines entailment thus: P entails Q if and only if the conjunction of P with the negation of Q is unsatisfiable, P is contingent, and Q is contingent. The contingency conditions are motivated by the desire to restrict entailment to relationships between propositions that are genuinely dependent on how the world is — propositions that might be true and might be false. If P is necessarily true, it seems trivial that it entails Q; if Q is necessarily true, it seems trivial that P entails it. One might wish to reserve the concept of entailment for the non-trivial cases. Yet the logical tradition does not impose this restriction. Consider the proposition 'For all x, x equals x' — a logical truth, true in every structure and under every interpretation. This proposition is entailed by every proposition whatsoever: the conjunction of any proposition with the negation of a logical truth is unsatisfiable, since the negation of a logical truth is itself unsatisfiable. The first condition is met. But 'For all x, x equals x' is not contingent; it is necessarily true. The third condition fails. The definition excludes the entailment of logical truths, which is one of the most fundamental features of the entailment relation. Similarly, a contradiction — being true in no possible world — entails every proposition, regardless of whether the conclusion is contingent. The contingency conditions, whilst capturing a reasonable pre-theoretic intuition about substantive entailment, are too restrictive to serve as conditions on entailment itself.

definition: A set-theoretic construction C is paradoxical if and only if (i) C involves the unrestricted application of the comprehension principle, (ii) C produces a set that both possesses and lacks a given property, and (iii) C can be formulated in the language of naive set theory.
conditions: C involves the unrestricted application of the comprehension principle
C produces a set that both possesses and lacks a given property
C can be formulated in the language of naive set theory
counterexample: Consider Curry's paradox. Let S be the set of all x such that if x is a member of x, then Q, where Q is any arbitrary proposition. Asking whether S is a member of itself leads, by conditional reasoning, to the conclusion that Q is true — for any Q whatsoever. This is paradoxical: from a seemingly innocent set-theoretic definition, one can derive any proposition at all. Yet Curry's paradox does not produce a set that both possesses and lacks a property; there is no direct contradiction of the form 'S has property F and S lacks property F.' The second condition fails, yet the result is undeniably paradoxical.
missing_condition: The definition requires a property that is both possessed and lacked, but Curry's paradox derives an arbitrary proposition without producing a straightforward property-contradiction. The paradox exploits the interaction of self-reference with the conditional, not the comprehension principle in its standard contradictory form. Paradoxicality in set theory encompasses more than the simple pattern of a set that both has and lacks a property.
passage: One might define a set-theoretic paradox as a construction that involves unrestricted comprehension, produces a set that both possesses and lacks a given property, and can be formulated in naive set theory. This definition fits Russell's paradox: the set of all sets that are not members of themselves, formed by unrestricted comprehension, both is and is not a member of itself, and the construction is carried out in naive set theory. The definition captures the archetype admirably. Yet one must consider Curry's paradox. Let S be the set of all x such that if x is a member of x, then two plus two equals five. If S is a member of S, then by the definition of S, if S is a member of S then two plus two equals five; by modus ponens, two plus two equals five. If S is not a member of S, then the defining condition is vacuously satisfied, so S is a member of S — a contradiction. The net result is that one can derive the proposition that two plus two equals five. Indeed, substituting any proposition for Q, one can derive any proposition whatsoever from the existence of S. This is paradoxical in the most alarming sense. Yet the paradox does not produce a set that both possesses and lacks a property in the straightforward way that Russell's paradox does. There is no property F such that S has F and S lacks F; the paradox instead derives an arbitrary falsehood through conditional reasoning. The second condition fails. The definition has modelled set-theoretic paradox on its most famous exemplar and has overlooked the subtler paradoxes that arise from the interaction of self-reference with other logical connectives.

definition: Q is a logical consequence of a set S if and only if (i) there is no interpretation under which all members of S are true and Q is false, (ii) Q is not a member of S, and (iii) S is satisfiable.
conditions: there is no interpretation under which all members of S are true and Q is false
Q is not a member of S
S is satisfiable
counterexample: Consider any unsatisfiable set of propositions — for instance, the set {P, not-P}. There is no interpretation under which all members are true (since P and not-P cannot both be true), so a fortiori there is no interpretation under which all members are true and Q is false, for any Q. In classical logic, an unsatisfiable set has every proposition as a logical consequence. Yet the third condition requires S to be satisfiable. It fails, and the definition excludes the case, despite the fact that the logical consequence relation holds.
missing_condition: The definition requires the premiss set to be satisfiable, but in classical logic an unsatisfiable set entails every proposition — this is the principle of explosion. The satisfiability condition excludes a fundamental feature of the consequence relation, one that is central to the classical account.
passage: One might propose that Q is a logical consequence of S if and only if there is no interpretation under which all members of S are true and Q is false, Q is not a member of S, and S is satisfiable. The satisfiability condition is motivated by the thought that the consequence relation ought to be informative: if S is unsatisfiable, then the first condition is trivially met for any Q, and the consequence relation becomes vacuously universal. One might feel that genuine logical consequence requires the premisses to be jointly satisfiable, so that the truth-preservation at the heart of the concept is a real achievement rather than a trivial artefact. Yet the classical account of logical consequence includes the vacuous case. If S is the set {P, not-P}, then there is no interpretation satisfying all members of S, and hence no interpretation satisfying all members of S whilst falsifying Q, for any proposition Q. The consequence relation holds universally: every proposition is a consequence of an unsatisfiable set. This is the principle of explosion, and it is a theorem of classical logic. The third condition fails for every unsatisfiable set, and the definition therefore excludes every instance of explosion from the consequence relation. The exclusion is not merely a matter of a few exotic cases; the behaviour of the consequence relation in the presence of inconsistent premisses is one of the central topics of logical theory, and the principle of explosion is a defining feature of classical logic. The definition has attempted to restrict the consequence relation to its non-trivial instances, but in so doing it has departed from the classical account in a way that is both substantive and, for most logicians, unacceptable.

definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) the premisses of A are fewer in number than the conclusion requires for its support, (ii) the conclusion of A is a universal generalisation, and (iii) no premiss of A is a tautology.
conditions: the premisses of A are fewer in number than the conclusion requires for its support
the conclusion of A is a universal generalisation
no premiss of A is a tautology
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'Socrates is mortal; therefore, Socrates is mortal.' This argument is trivially valid — there is no circumstance in which the premiss is true and the conclusion false. Yet the conclusion is not a universal generalisation; it is a singular statement about a particular individual. The second condition fails, but the argument is valid nonetheless.
missing_condition: The definition restricts valid arguments to those with universally generalised conclusions, but validity is a property of the logical relationship between premisses and conclusion regardless of the logical form of the conclusion. Singular, existential, and disjunctive conclusions may all figure in valid arguments.
passage: One might propose that an argument is valid if and only if its premisses are fewer in number than the conclusion requires for its support, the conclusion is a universal generalisation, and no premiss is a tautology. The motivation for the second condition is perhaps this: one might suppose that the proper business of deductive logic is the establishment of universal truths, and that an argument whose conclusion merely asserts something about a particular individual falls short of the generality that validity demands. One thinks of the classic syllogism, whose conclusion — 'All men are mortal,' or 'No reptiles are mammals' — is a universal statement. Yet one must consider the simplest possible valid argument: 'Socrates is mortal; therefore, Socrates is mortal.' The premiss is true, the conclusion is true, and there is no possible interpretation under which the premiss holds and the conclusion does not. The argument is valid in the strictest sense. But its conclusion is not a universal generalisation; it is a singular proposition about a particular individual. The second condition fails. The same difficulty would arise for any valid argument whose conclusion is existential ('There exists at least one even prime'), disjunctive ('Either it is raining or it is not'), or conditional. The definition has confused a feature of one traditional form of deductive argument — the syllogism with a universal conclusion — with a necessary condition of validity as such. Validity is indifferent to the logical form of the conclusion; it concerns only whether the truth of the premisses guarantees the truth of the conclusion.

definition: A proposition Q is a logical consequence of a set of propositions P if and only if (i) Q can be derived from P by a finite sequence of syntactic transformations according to the rules of a given formal system, (ii) the derivation has been actually carried out and written down, and (iii) no step in the derivation appeals to any semantic consideration such as truth or meaning.
conditions: Q can be derived from P by a finite sequence of syntactic transformations according to the rules of a given formal system
the derivation has been actually carried out and written down
no step in the derivation appeals to any semantic consideration such as truth or meaning
counterexample: Consider a proposition that is a logical consequence of a set of axioms in first-order arithmetic but whose derivation has never been carried out by any human being — a theorem, that is, whose truth follows from the axioms but which no one has yet proved. By the completeness theorem for first-order logic, such a proposition is derivable; a proof exists in principle. Yet the second condition fails, for the derivation has not been actually carried out. The proposition is nonetheless a logical consequence of the axioms.
missing_condition: The definition conflates the existence of a derivation with its actual execution. Logical consequence is a relation that holds independently of whether any person has produced the derivation. What matters is the existence in principle of a derivation, not its actual inscription upon paper.
passage: One might propose that a proposition Q is a logical consequence of a set of propositions P if and only if Q can be derived from P by a finite sequence of syntactic transformations, the derivation has been actually carried out and written down, and no step appeals to semantic considerations such as truth or meaning. The definition reflects a strongly syntactic and constructivist conception of logical consequence. One might suppose that consequence is not merely a relationship that holds in the abstract but something that must be exhibited, demonstrated, realised in a concrete chain of formal steps. Until the proof is written, one might argue, the consequence does not obtain. Yet one must consider the vast domain of unproved theorems in first-order arithmetic. The completeness theorem for first-order logic, established by Goedel in 1930, guarantees that every proposition that is true in all models of first-order logic is derivable from its axioms. There are, accordingly, propositions that are logical consequences of the Peano axioms — propositions whose derivations exist in principle — but whose proofs have never been carried out by any mathematician. They are consequences of the axioms in the precise technical sense, for a derivation could be produced, and the semantic relationship that guarantees this is well understood. The second condition of the definition excludes them, yet they are logical consequences nonetheless. The definition has confused the ontological status of the consequence relation — which holds whether or not it has been exhibited — with the cognitive act of producing a proof. Logical consequence is a relation between propositions, not an event in the biography of a logician.

definition: A statement S is paradoxical if and only if (i) S is self-referential — that is, S refers to itself, (ii) S leads to an explicit logical contradiction of the form 'P and not-P,' and (iii) S can be formulated in ordinary natural language without recourse to technical notation.
conditions: S is self-referential — that is, S refers to itself
S leads to an explicit logical contradiction of the form 'P and not-P'
S can be formulated in ordinary natural language without recourse to technical notation
counterexample: Consider the Banach-Tarski paradox, which demonstrates that a solid sphere can be decomposed into finitely many pieces and reassembled into two spheres, each identical in size to the original. This result is widely described as paradoxical, for it offends against the most basic spatial intuitions. Yet it is not self-referential; it makes no reference to itself. It does not lead to an explicit contradiction; the result is a theorem of set theory, provable from the axiom of choice, and is perfectly consistent. And it is not naturally formulated in ordinary language but requires the apparatus of set theory and measure theory. All three conditions fail, yet the Banach-Tarski result is paradigmatically paradoxical.
missing_condition: The definition restricts paradox to self-referential contradictions expressible in natural language, but paradoxes may arise from non-self-referential sources — from counterintuitive but consistent mathematical results, from conflicts between well-supported principles, or from situations that confound expectations without producing formal contradiction. What makes a result paradoxical is that it violates deeply held rational expectations, not that it is self-referential or contradictory.
passage: One might propose that a statement is paradoxical if and only if it is self-referential, it leads to an explicit logical contradiction of the form 'P and not-P,' and it can be formulated in ordinary natural language. The definition fits the liar paradox admirably. 'This sentence is false' refers to itself, generates a contradiction (if true then false, if false then true), and is expressed in plain English. One might suppose that self-reference and contradiction are the essence of paradox. Yet one must consider the Banach-Tarski paradox, one of the most celebrated results in twentieth-century mathematics. This theorem, proved by Stefan Banach and Alfred Tarski in 1924, demonstrates that a solid sphere in three-dimensional space can be decomposed into a finite number of non-measurable pieces and reassembled, using only rigid motions, into two solid spheres, each the same size as the original. The result is deeply paradoxical — it seems to violate the conservation of volume, a principle so basic that its denial appears absurd. Yet the Banach-Tarski paradox is not self-referential; the theorem makes no reference to itself. It does not produce an explicit contradiction; the result is a theorem, proved rigorously from the axioms of Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory with the axiom of choice, and it is perfectly consistent within that framework. And its formulation requires the technical apparatus of measure theory, group theory, and point-set topology; it cannot be stated precisely in ordinary language. All three conditions of the definition fail. The definition has taken the liar paradox as the paradigm of paradoxicality and generalised from its features, but paradoxes come in many forms, and the Banach-Tarski result shows that a result may be profoundly paradoxical without being self-referential, contradictory, or expressible in natural language.

definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) P and Q share at least one non-logical term, (ii) the truth of P provides a reason for believing Q, and (iii) P and Q belong to the same domain of discourse.
conditions: P and Q share at least one non-logical term
the truth of P provides a reason for believing Q
P and Q belong to the same domain of discourse
counterexample: Consider the entailment from the conjunction 'It is raining and it is Tuesday' to the disjunction 'Either snow is white or grass is purple.' The conjunction entails the disjunction, for the conjunction entails any proposition whatsoever — or more precisely, the conjunction entails 'It is raining,' which together with the logical truth 'If it is raining then either snow is white or grass is purple or anything else' yields the disjunction. But more simply, any proposition entails any tautological disjunction. The premiss and conclusion need share no non-logical term, and the truth of the premiss provides no evidential reason for the conclusion; the entailment is purely structural. The first and second conditions fail.
missing_condition: The definition requires shared non-logical vocabulary and evidential relevance between premiss and conclusion, but classical entailment is a purely structural relation: P entails Q if and only if it is impossible for P to be true and Q false, regardless of whether the propositions share vocabulary or whether the truth of the one provides reason for believing the other.
passage: One might propose that a proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if P and Q share at least one non-logical term, the truth of P provides a reason for believing Q, and P and Q belong to the same domain of discourse. The first condition reflects the intuition that entailment ought to involve a connection of content between the propositions: that the premiss and conclusion ought to be, in some sense, about the same thing. The second condition captures the idea that entailment is a relation of evidential support. The third ensures topical coherence. Yet classical logic recognises entailments that violate all three conditions. Consider: any necessarily true proposition — a logical truth, a tautology — is entailed by every proposition whatsoever. The proposition 'Roses are red' entails 'Either it is snowing or it is not snowing,' for there is no possible circumstance in which the former is true and the latter false. The two propositions share no non-logical term; the truth of the first provides no evidential reason for believing the second; and the propositions arguably belong to different domains of discourse — one concerns botany, the other concerns meteorology or logic. All three conditions fail. The definition has imported requirements of relevance and topical connection that are characteristic of certain non-classical logics — relevance logic, for instance — but that do not belong to the classical concept of entailment. Classical entailment is a matter of the impossibility of a certain truth-value assignment, and it is indifferent to whether the propositions involved share subject matter or evidential connection.

definition: A set of propositions S is consistent if and only if (i) every proposition in S is individually true, (ii) the propositions in S are mutually independent — that is, no proposition in S is derivable from the others, and (iii) the propositions in S belong to a single, unified theory.
conditions: every proposition in S is individually true
the propositions in S are mutually independent — that is, no proposition in S is derivable from the others
the propositions in S belong to a single, unified theory
counterexample: Consider the set containing the propositions 'All whales are mammals' and 'All mammals are warm-blooded.' Each is true, they belong to the same zoological domain, and the second is not derivable from the first alone. The set is consistent. But now consider the set {'Snow is white', 'Grass is green'}. These propositions are individually true but belong to quite different domains — one concerns meteorology, the other botany. The third condition might be taken to exclude this set, yet the set is perfectly consistent: there is no contradiction between its members. More tellingly, the set {'Unicorns have one horn', 'Dragons breathe fire'} contains propositions that are both false, yet the set is consistent — its members do not contradict one another. The first condition fails, but the set is consistent nonetheless.
missing_condition: The definition conflates consistency with truth and theoretical unity, but consistency is a purely logical property: a set of propositions is consistent if and only if the propositions can all be true simultaneously — not if they are all actually true, nor if they belong to the same theory. False propositions may be mutually consistent, and propositions from disparate domains may be jointly consistent.
passage: One might propose that a set of propositions is consistent if and only if every proposition in the set is individually true, the propositions are mutually independent, and they belong to a single, unified theory. The definition has a certain tidiness: a consistent set, on this account, is a collection of true, independent propositions forming part of a coherent theoretical framework. One might think that consistency is, at bottom, a matter of truth — that propositions are consistent because they are all true and therefore cannot contradict one another. Yet one must consider the following. Take the set containing the two propositions 'Unicorns have one horn' and 'Dragons breathe fire.' Neither proposition is true; unicorns and dragons do not exist. The first condition of the definition fails for both propositions. Yet the set is consistent in the perfectly clear logical sense that its members do not contradict one another. There is no derivation from one to the negation of the other; they are, as logicians say, jointly satisfiable — there is a possible state of affairs in which both are true, even if the actual state of affairs is not among them. The definition has confused consistency with truth. Consistency is a matter of the possibility of joint truth, not of actual truth. A set of false propositions may be consistent, and a set of true propositions drawn from disparate and unrelated domains is consistent without belonging to any single theory. The second condition, requiring independence, is likewise too strong: the set {'P', 'If P then Q', 'Q'} is consistent even though Q is derivable from the other two. The definition imposes conditions — truth, independence, theoretical unity — that are desirable features of well-constructed theories but not necessary conditions of the purely logical property of consistency.

definition: A concept C is definable if and only if (i) C can be stated in terms of a finite list of necessary and sufficient conditions, (ii) the conditions are jointly precise enough to determine, for any given case, whether it falls under C or not, and (iii) the definition of C is non-circular — that is, it does not employ C itself or any synonym of C in the definiens.
conditions: C can be stated in terms of a finite list of necessary and sufficient conditions
the conditions are jointly precise enough to determine, for any given case, whether it falls under C or not
the definition of C is non-circular — that is, it does not employ C itself or any synonym of C in the definiens
counterexample: Consider the concept of a natural number, as characterised by the Peano axioms. The axioms employ the concept of a successor function, and the natural numbers are defined recursively: zero is a natural number, and the successor of any natural number is a natural number. This definition is circular in the relevant sense — the concept of a natural number appears in its own characterisation. Yet the natural numbers are eminently definable; the Peano axioms characterise them with perfect precision, and the recursive definition determines membership unambiguously. The third condition fails, but the concept is defined with exemplary rigour.
missing_condition: The definition prohibits circularity, but recursive and inductive definitions — which are the standard means of defining the natural numbers, well-formed formulae, and many other mathematical concepts — are inherently circular in the sense that the definiendum appears in the definiens. This circularity is not vicious but productive; it is the means by which infinite domains are finitely characterised. A proper account of definability must accommodate recursive and inductive specifications.
passage: One might propose that a concept is definable if and only if it can be stated in terms of a finite list of necessary and sufficient conditions, the conditions determine membership for any given case, and the definition is non-circular.
