The third condition reflects a venerable prohibition: a definition, it is held, must not employ the very concept it seeks to define, on pain of uninformativeness. If one defines 'red' as 'the colour red,' one has said nothing. The prohibition against circularity seems well-motivated. Yet one must consider how the most fundamental concepts of mathematics are in fact defined. The natural numbers are characterised by the Peano axioms, which state that zero is a natural number and that the successor of any natural number is a natural number. The concept of a natural number appears in its own characterisation: one cannot state what a natural number is without using the concept 'natural number' in the definiens. The same structure appears in the definition of a well-formed formula in predicate logic, in the definition of a proof, and in countless other mathematical contexts. These are recursive or inductive definitions, and they are the standard and accepted means of defining concepts with infinite extensions. They are circular in the sense that the definiendum appears in the definiens, but the circularity is not vicious; it is the engine by which a finite specification generates an infinite domain. The third condition of the proposed definition would exclude all such characterisations, yet the natural numbers are among the most precisely and satisfactorily defined concepts in the whole of human thought. The definition has mistaken a legitimate concern about uninformative circularity for a blanket prohibition that would exclude the most powerful definitional technique in mathematics.

definition: A formal system F is sound if and only if (i) every theorem of F is a truth of classical propositional logic, (ii) F has been shown to be sound by a finitary proof, and (iii) the soundness of F has been acknowledged by the community of working logicians.
conditions: every theorem of F is a truth of classical propositional logic
F has been shown to be sound by a finitary proof
the soundness of F has been acknowledged by the community of working logicians
counterexample: Consider a formal system for first-order arithmetic whose theorems are all true statements about the natural numbers. Such a system is sound — its theorems are truths — but its theorems are not truths of classical propositional logic; they are truths about numbers, a domain that lies beyond the scope of propositional logic. The first condition fails. Moreover, by Goedel's second incompleteness theorem, the consistency and hence the soundness of any sufficiently strong system of arithmetic cannot be proved within the system itself by finitary means, so the second condition is at best problematic. Yet the system is sound.
missing_condition: The definition restricts soundness to propositional logic, but soundness is a property that any formal system may possess with respect to its intended semantics. A sound system of arithmetic has true theorems about numbers; a sound modal logic has theorems true in all possible worlds. Soundness is relative to a semantics, not confined to one particular logical framework.
passage: One might propose that a formal system is sound if and only if every theorem of the system is a truth of classical propositional logic, the soundness has been established by a finitary proof, and the soundness has been acknowledged by the community of working logicians. The definition appears to set a rigorous standard: a sound system is one whose theorems are truths, whose soundness can be demonstrated, and whose credentials are recognised by the relevant experts. Yet one must consider a formal system for first-order arithmetic — a system whose axioms describe the properties of the natural numbers and whose rules of inference are designed to derive truths about addition, multiplication, and order. If the system is sound, then every theorem it derives is true — true, that is, about the natural numbers. But the theorems of such a system are not truths of classical propositional logic. They are statements about specific mathematical objects: that every number has a successor, that addition is commutative, that there is no largest prime. Propositional logic is too impoverished to express such truths. The first condition fails. Furthermore, by Goedel's celebrated second incompleteness theorem, any consistent formal system strong enough to encode basic arithmetic cannot prove its own consistency by finitary means. Since soundness implies consistency, the second condition — that soundness be demonstrated by a finitary proof — is unattainable for precisely the systems of greatest mathematical interest. The definition has conflated soundness with a particular logical framework and a particular proof-theoretic standard, when in truth soundness is a relation between a formal system and its intended interpretation, a relation that transcends any single logic and that may resist finitary demonstration.

definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) the premisses of A are jointly consistent, (ii) the conclusion of A is not a logical truth, and (iii) there is no possible interpretation under which the premisses are all true and the conclusion is false.
conditions: the premisses of A are jointly consistent
the conclusion of A is not a logical truth
there is no possible interpretation under which the premisses are all true and the conclusion is false
counterexample: Consider the argument whose sole premiss is 'Either it is raining or it is not raining' and whose conclusion is 'Either snow is white or snow is not white.' Both premiss and conclusion are logical truths — tautologies — and the conclusion is certainly not false under any interpretation. The argument is valid. But the conclusion is a logical truth, so the second condition fails. Yet the argument satisfies the standard definition of validity: there is no interpretation under which the premiss is true and the conclusion false.
missing_condition: The definition excludes arguments whose conclusions are logical truths, but such arguments are trivially valid precisely because the conclusion cannot be false under any interpretation. What is wanted is the recognition that validity concerns solely the impossibility of true premisses with a false conclusion, regardless of the logical status of the conclusion.
passage: One might propose that an argument is valid if and only if its premisses are jointly consistent, its conclusion is not a logical truth, and there is no possible interpretation under which the premisses are all true and the conclusion is false. The first condition is motivated by the thought that an argument from contradictory premisses is defective and ought not to count as valid. The second condition reflects the intuition that an argument to a tautological conclusion is trivial and uninformative. The third condition captures the standard semantic notion of validity. Yet one must consider the argument from the premiss 'Either it is raining or it is not raining' to the conclusion 'Either snow is white or snow is not white.' The premiss is a tautology, the conclusion is a tautology, and the argument is valid in the most straightforward sense: there is no interpretation under which the premiss is true and the conclusion false. But the second condition excludes it, for the conclusion is a logical truth. The definition has confused validity with a notion of substantive or informative inference, when in truth validity is a formal property that is entirely indifferent to whether the conclusion is a tautology, a contingent proposition, or a deep philosophical insight. Arguments to logical truths are among the most trivially valid of all arguments.

definition: An argument A is sound if and only if (i) A is valid, (ii) the premisses of A are true, and (iii) the conclusion of A is not already known to the person evaluating the argument.
conditions: A is valid
the premisses of A are true
the conclusion of A is not already known to the person evaluating the argument
counterexample: Consider the argument 'All men are mortal; Socrates is a man; therefore Socrates is mortal.' Suppose this argument is evaluated by a professor of logic who has known the conclusion since childhood. The argument is valid, and its premisses are true. The third condition fails, since the evaluator already knows that Socrates is mortal. Yet the argument is sound — its soundness does not depend upon whether any particular person happens already to know its conclusion.
missing_condition: The definition conflates soundness, which is an objective property of arguments, with the epistemic state of the evaluator. An argument is sound if it is valid and its premisses are true, regardless of whether its conclusion is novel to anyone. The definition has imported a pragmatic consideration — the informativeness of the argument to a particular audience — into a purely logical concept.
passage: Suppose one holds that an argument is sound if and only if it is valid, its premisses are true, and its conclusion is not already known to the person evaluating it. The first two conditions are standard: validity ensures that the conclusion follows from the premisses, and truth of the premisses ensures that the argument transmits truth to the conclusion. The third condition is motivated by the thought that a sound argument ought to accomplish something — it ought to extend the evaluator's stock of knowledge rather than merely rehearse what is already known. Yet one must consider the venerable syllogism about Socrates and mortality. The argument is valid, and both premisses — that all men are mortal and that Socrates is a man — are true. But the conclusion, that Socrates is mortal, is known to virtually everyone. A professor of logic who evaluates this argument has known its conclusion for decades. By the proposed definition, the argument is not sound when evaluated by such a person, even though it is sound when evaluated by a child encountering it for the first time. Soundness would thus be a property that varies from evaluator to evaluator, which is absurd. The definition has introduced a psychological condition into what ought to be a purely logical concept. Whether an argument is sound depends upon validity and the truth of its premisses, not upon whether its conclusion is news to anyone.

definition: A proposition Q is a logical consequence of a set of propositions P1, ..., Pn if and only if (i) Q can be derived from P1, ..., Pn by a finite sequence of applications of the rules of a given formal system S, (ii) S is consistent, and (iii) Q is not derivable from the empty set of premisses in S.
conditions: Q can be derived from P1, ..., Pn by a finite sequence of rule applications in S
S is consistent
Q is not derivable from the empty set of premisses in S
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'Either it is raining or it is not raining.' In standard propositional logic, this proposition is derivable from the empty set of premisses — it is a theorem, a logical truth. It is therefore a logical consequence of any set of premisses whatsoever, for whatever follows from nothing follows a fortiori from something. The third condition excludes it from being a consequence of any particular premiss set, yet it is a logical consequence of every premiss set.
missing_condition: The definition excludes theorems — propositions derivable from no premisses at all — from counting as logical consequences of any premiss set. But a theorem is a consequence of every set of premisses, since adding premisses cannot remove a derivation that was already available. What is wanted is a definition that recognises theorems as limiting cases of logical consequence rather than excluding them.
passage: One might define logical consequence as follows: a proposition is a logical consequence of a set of propositions if and only if it can be derived from them by a finite sequence of rule applications in a given formal system, the system is consistent, and the proposition is not derivable from the empty set of premisses. The third condition is intended to ensure that logical consequence is a substantive relation between premisses and conclusion — that the conclusion genuinely depends upon the premisses rather than being derivable independently. The motivation is understandable: if a proposition can be proved without any premisses at all, it might seem misleading to call it a consequence of this or that particular set of propositions. Yet one must consider the law of excluded middle, or any other theorem of classical logic. The proposition 'Either it is raining or it is not raining' is derivable from the empty set of premisses. It is a logical truth. By the proposed definition, it is not a logical consequence of any premiss set, since it is derivable from no premisses. But this is plainly wrong. A logical truth follows from every set of premisses: if one can derive it from nothing, one can certainly derive it from something. The definition has confused a desideratum of informative reasoning — that the conclusion should depend upon the premisses in some non-trivial way — with a necessary condition of logical consequence itself.

definition: A sentence S is paradoxical if and only if (i) S leads, by apparently valid reasoning, to a contradiction, (ii) S employs self-reference — that is, S refers to itself or to a class of which it is a member, and (iii) S is formulated within a single language without appeal to a metalanguage.
conditions: S leads, by apparently valid reasoning, to a contradiction
S employs self-reference
S is formulated within a single language without appeal to a metalanguage
counterexample: Consider the paradox of the heap (sorites). Beginning from the apparently true premiss that a heap of sand with one grain removed is still a heap, and iterating, one is led to the conclusion that a single grain of sand is a heap — a contradiction of common sense, and indeed a contradiction if one adds the premiss that a single grain is not a heap. The reasoning appears valid at each step. Yet the sorites paradox involves no self-reference whatsoever; the sentence 'If a heap of n grains has one grain removed, the result is still a heap' does not refer to itself or to any class of which it is a member. The second condition fails, yet the sorites is one of the oldest and most discussed paradoxes in the history of logic.
missing_condition: The definition restricts paradoxes to those involving self-reference, but many genuine paradoxes — including the sorites, the paradox of the preface, and others — arise from sources other than self-reference. What is wanted is a broader characterisation that recognises paradox as arising from any combination of apparently true premisses and apparently valid reasoning that leads to an unacceptable conclusion, regardless of whether self-reference is involved.
passage: One might propose that a sentence is paradoxical if and only if it leads by apparently valid reasoning to a contradiction, it employs self-reference, and it is formulated within a single language. The definition is tailored to the most famous paradoxes: the liar sentence 'This sentence is false' refers to itself and engenders a contradiction, and Russell's paradox concerns the set of all sets that are not members of themselves — a self-referential construction. One might naturally suppose that self-reference is the essential mechanism of paradox and that all genuine paradoxes share this feature. Yet one must consider the sorites paradox, known since antiquity. A man has a heap of ten thousand grains of sand. He removes one grain; what remains is still a heap. He removes another; still a heap. The reasoning seems impeccable at each step — the removal of a single grain cannot transform a heap into a non-heap. But after ten thousand iterations, he is left with no grains at all, and no grains is not a heap. The premisses are apparently true, the reasoning apparently valid, and the conclusion contradictory. The sorites is a paradox by any reasonable standard. Yet it involves no self-reference. The premiss does not refer to itself; the heap does not refer to the heap. The paradox arises from the vagueness of the predicate 'heap' and the apparent validity of each small step, not from any reflexive structure. The second condition of the definition fails. The definition has identified self-reference as the source of paradox because the most conspicuous examples happen to exhibit it, but paradox is a broader phenomenon than self-reference.

definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) P and Q are both contingent propositions, (ii) the truth of P guarantees the truth of Q in every possible circumstance, and (iii) P and Q share at least one non-logical term.
conditions: P and Q are both contingent propositions
the truth of P guarantees the truth of Q in every possible circumstance
P and Q share at least one non-logical term
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'It is raining and it is not raining.' This is a contradiction — a necessarily false proposition, not a contingent one. Yet it entails every proposition whatsoever, including 'The moon is made of cheese,' by the principle of explosion. The truth of the contradiction guarantees the truth of every proposition (vacuously, since the contradiction is never true). The first condition fails, since the contradiction is not contingent, and the third condition fails, since 'It is raining and it is not raining' and 'The moon is made of cheese' share no non-logical terms. Yet the entailment holds.
missing_condition: The definition restricts entailment to contingent propositions that share non-logical vocabulary, but entailment as standardly understood holds between any propositions where the truth of the first guarantees the truth of the second. Contradictions entail everything, and necessary truths are entailed by everything, regardless of shared vocabulary. What is wanted is a purely structural account of entailment, free of restrictions on the modal or lexical properties of the propositions involved.
passage: One might propose that a proposition entails another if and only if both are contingent, the truth of the first guarantees the truth of the second in every possible circumstance, and the two propositions share at least one non-logical term. The first condition is intended to exclude degenerate cases involving necessary truths and necessary falsehoods; the third condition is intended to ensure a substantive connection of subject-matter between the entailing and entailed propositions. Yet one must consider the proposition 'It is raining and it is not raining.' This proposition is a contradiction; it is necessarily false. It is not contingent, so the first condition fails. Nonetheless, it entails every proposition whatsoever, by the principle of explosion or ex falso quodlibet. Since there is no possible circumstance in which the contradiction is true, there is no possible circumstance in which it is true and some other proposition is false. The entailment holds vacuously but genuinely. Moreover, the contradiction 'It is raining and it is not raining' entails 'The moon is made of cheese,' yet these two propositions share no non-logical terms; the third condition fails as well. The definition has sought to restrict entailment to cases that feel substantive and informative, but entailment is a formal relation that holds wherever the truth of one proposition guarantees the truth of another, regardless of whether the propositions are contingent, necessary, or impossible, and regardless of whether they share subject-matter.

definition: A set of propositions Gamma is consistent if and only if (i) every proposition in Gamma is individually possible — that is, no member of Gamma is a contradiction, (ii) no two members of Gamma are logically contradictory, and (iii) Gamma is finite.
conditions: every member of Gamma is individually possible
no two members of Gamma are logically contradictory
Gamma is finite
counterexample: Consider the infinite set of propositions: 'There is at least one star,' 'There are at least two stars,' 'There are at least three stars,' and so on without end. Each proposition is individually possible, no two of them are contradictory, and the set as a whole is perfectly consistent — all of its members could be true simultaneously, provided the universe contains infinitely many stars. But the set is infinite, so the third condition fails. The set is nonetheless consistent.
missing_condition: The definition restricts consistency to finite sets of propositions, but consistency is a property that applies equally to infinite sets. The axioms of Peano arithmetic, for example, constitute an infinite set of propositions that is (on the standard view) consistent. What is wanted is a definition that imposes no restriction on the cardinality of the set.
passage: Suppose one defines a consistent set of propositions as one in which every member is individually possible, no two members are contradictory, and the set is finite. The first two conditions capture something important: a set containing a contradiction is inconsistent for obvious reasons, and a set containing a proposition together with its negation is likewise inconsistent. The third condition might seem a natural simplification — one might think that consistency is a property of interest only for finite collections of premisses or axioms, and that infinite sets introduce complications best avoided. Yet one must consider the set of propositions asserting the existence of ever more numerous stars: 'There is at least one star,' 'There are at least two stars,' 'There are at least three stars,' and so on without limit. This set is infinite, but it is perfectly consistent. All of its members could be true simultaneously in a universe containing infinitely many stars. Each member is individually possible, and no two members contradict one another. The third condition fails, for the set is not finite. But the set is consistent by any reasonable standard. The definition has imposed an arbitrary restriction upon the cardinality of consistent sets, when in truth some of the most important consistent sets in logic and mathematics — the axioms of arithmetic, of set theory, of geometry — are infinite.

definition: A concept C is definable within a formal language L if and only if (i) there exists a formula phi of L such that phi applies to all and only the objects falling under C, (ii) phi is finitely long, and (iii) phi uses only primitive symbols of L without the introduction of new symbols by abbreviation.
conditions: there exists a formula phi of L applying to all and only the objects falling under C
phi is finitely long
phi uses only primitive symbols of L without abbreviation
counterexample: Consider the concept of a well-ordering of the natural numbers. In first-order arithmetic, one cannot define this concept by means of any single formula that applies to all and only the well-orderings, because first-order logic lacks the expressive resources to distinguish well-orderings from non-standard orderings (by the compactness theorem, any first-order theory with an infinite model has models of every infinite cardinality, and these models may contain non-standard elements that defeat the intended characterisation). The concept is perfectly coherent and mathematically precise, yet it is not definable in first-order arithmetic. The first condition fails — not because the concept is defective, but because the language is insufficiently expressive.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that every coherent concept is definable in a given formal language, but the expressive limitations of formal languages may prevent the definition of concepts that are nevertheless perfectly well understood in a richer framework. What is wanted is a recognition that definability is relative to the resources of the language, and that failure of definability reflects the limitations of the language rather than the incoherence of the concept.
passage: One might propose that a concept is definable within a formal language if and only if there exists a formula of the language that applies to all and only the objects falling under the concept, the formula is finitely long, and it uses only the primitive symbols of the language. The proposal seems to capture what we mean by definability: to define a concept is to characterise it exactly by means of the resources available in the language. For many mathematical concepts this works admirably. The concept of an even number is definable in first-order arithmetic by the formula stating that there exists a number whose double is equal to the given number. The formula is finite, uses only primitive symbols, and applies to all and only the even numbers. Yet one must consider the concept of a well-ordering of the natural numbers. This is a perfectly precise mathematical concept: an ordering is a well-ordering if every non-empty subset has a least element. The concept is central to set theory and to the theory of ordinals. But in first-order arithmetic, no formula captures the concept exactly. By the compactness theorem, any first-order theory satisfied by the standard model of arithmetic is also satisfied by non-standard models in which there exist elements greater than every standard natural number, and these non-standard models contain orderings that are not well-orderings but that satisfy every first-order formula satisfied by the standard ordering. The first condition fails. The concept of well-ordering is not definable in first-order arithmetic. The definition has implicitly assumed that the formal language is sufficiently expressive to capture any coherent concept, but the limitations of first-order logic preclude the definition of many important mathematical concepts, which can be captured only in more powerful frameworks.

definition: A proposition Q is a logical consequence of a proposition P if and only if (i) Q can be derived from P by a sequence of truth-preserving steps, (ii) each step in the derivation is one that any rational agent would accept, and (iii) the derivation does not exceed a humanly surveyable length.
conditions: Q can be derived from P by a sequence of truth-preserving steps
each step is one that any rational agent would accept
the derivation does not exceed a humanly surveyable length
counterexample: Consider a proposition of elementary number theory whose proof exists but runs to ten thousand pages — a proof that no single human being could survey in its entirety without mechanical assistance. The conclusion follows from the axioms by truth-preserving steps, each of which is individually unimpeachable. The proposition is a logical consequence of the axioms. Yet the derivation exceeds any humanly surveyable length; the third condition fails. Logical consequence is not constrained by the cognitive limitations of human beings.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a humanly surveyable length upon derivations, but logical consequence is an objective relation between propositions that holds regardless of whether any human being can survey the derivation in its entirety. What is wanted is a definition that separates the logical relation from the contingent limitations of human cognition.
passage: Suppose one defines logical consequence as follows: a proposition is a logical consequence of another if it can be derived from it by truth-preserving steps, each step is one that any rational agent would accept, and the derivation does not exceed a humanly surveyable length. The third condition is motivated by the reasonable concern that a derivation too long for any human to follow is, in some practical sense, useless — one cannot verify it, one cannot be confident in it, and one cannot learn from it. Yet logical consequence is not a pragmatic notion but a structural one. Consider the four-colour theorem, whose proof was first given with essential reliance upon a computer verification of nearly two thousand cases, each involving extensive calculation. No human being has surveyed every step of the proof. Yet the theorem follows from the axioms of graph theory, and each step of the derivation is truth-preserving. The conclusion is a logical consequence of the premisses. One might equally consider a hypothetical theorem of number theory whose shortest proof runs to ten thousand pages of closely reasoned argument. The proof exists; each step is valid; the conclusion follows. The third condition of the definition would exclude it from counting as a logical consequence, but this is absurd. Logical consequence holds between propositions in virtue of their logical form and content, not in virtue of the capacity of human beings to survey the derivation. The definition has confused a desideratum of mathematical practice with a condition of logical reality.

definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) P and Q are about the same subject-matter, (ii) the truth of P is sufficient for the truth of Q, and (iii) P provides an explanation of why Q is true.
conditions: P and Q are about the same subject-matter
the truth of P is sufficient for the truth of Q
P provides an explanation of why Q is true
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'It is raining' and the proposition 'Either it is raining or elephants can fly.' The first entails the second by the rule of disjunction introduction: if it is raining, then it is raining or elephants can fly, regardless of whether elephants can fly. But the proposition 'It is raining' does not provide an explanation of why it is the case that either it is raining or elephants can fly; the disjunction holds simply because one of its disjuncts is true, not because the first proposition explains the second. Moreover, the two propositions are not straightforwardly about the same subject-matter, since the disjunction introduces elephants. Both the first and third conditions fail, yet the entailment holds.
missing_condition: The definition requires a shared subject-matter and an explanatory connection between the entailing and entailed propositions, but entailment is a formal relation that holds purely in virtue of the impossibility of the premiss being true and the conclusion false. Disjunction introduction generates entailments across disparate subject-matters and without any explanatory link. What is wanted is a recognition that entailment is a logical, not an explanatory, relation.
passage: One might propose that a proposition entails another if and only if the two are about the same subject-matter, the truth of the first is sufficient for the truth of the second, and the first provides an explanation of why the second is true. The proposal has an intuitive appeal, for in many paradigmatic cases of entailment the premiss does seem to explain the conclusion: 'All men are mortal and Socrates is a man' entails 'Socrates is mortal,' and the premisses explain why the conclusion holds. Yet one must consider the entailment from 'It is raining' to 'Either it is raining or elephants can fly.' This entailment is valid — it is an instance of disjunction introduction, one of the most elementary rules of logic. If it is raining, then it is raining or elephants can fly, because a disjunction is true whenever at least one of its disjuncts is true. But the first proposition does not explain the second in any illuminating sense. The fact that it is raining does not explain why it is the case that either it is raining or elephants can fly; the explanation, such as it is, appeals merely to the logical truth that a disjunction follows from either disjunct. Moreover, the two propositions are not about the same subject-matter in any natural sense: the first is about the weather, and the second introduces the topic of elephants and their capacity for flight. Both the first and third conditions fail. Entailment is a formal relation, indifferent to subject-matter and indifferent to explanatory connections; it holds wherever the truth of one proposition suffices for the truth of another, regardless of whether the propositions concern the same topic.

definition: A formal system S is sound if and only if (i) every theorem of S is true under the intended interpretation, (ii) S has only finitely many axioms, and (iii) every rule of inference of S preserves not only truth but also intuitive obviousness.
conditions: every theorem of S is true under the intended interpretation
S has only finitely many axioms
every rule of inference of S preserves intuitive obviousness
counterexample: Consider first-order Peano arithmetic. It is standardly formulated with an axiom schema of induction — one axiom for each formula of the language — yielding infinitely many axioms. The system is sound: every theorem is true in the standard model of the natural numbers. But it has infinitely many axioms, so the second condition fails. Moreover, certain theorems of Peano arithmetic, whilst true, are far from intuitively obvious; the Goedel sentence, for instance, is a true but highly non-obvious arithmetical proposition whose proof involves a subtle self-referential construction. The rules of inference preserve truth but not intuitive obviousness, for the application of valid rules to obvious premisses may yield non-obvious conclusions. The second and third conditions fail, yet the system is sound.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a finite axiomatisation requirement and a preservation-of-obviousness requirement, neither of which is necessary for soundness. Soundness requires only that the theorems be true under the intended interpretation and that the rules of inference preserve truth. Many of the most important formal systems in logic and mathematics have infinitely many axioms and yield theorems that are far from obvious.
passage: One might define a sound formal system as one in which every theorem is true under the intended interpretation, the system has only finitely many axioms, and every rule of inference preserves not merely truth but intuitive obviousness. The second condition might be motivated by a desire for economy and surveyability; the third by the thought that a truly sound system ought to produce only results that are immediately recognisable as correct. Yet one must consider first-order Peano arithmetic. This system is standardly formulated with a schema of mathematical induction, which generates one axiom for each formula of the language. The schema yields infinitely many axioms, and the system is not finitely axiomatisable — no finite set of axioms is logically equivalent to the full schema. The second condition fails. Furthermore, among the theorems of Peano arithmetic are propositions of extraordinary subtlety — the Goedel sentence, the Paris-Harrington theorem, and many results whose truth is established only through long and intricate proofs that are anything but intuitively obvious. The rules of inference — modus ponens, universal generalisation — preserve truth faithfully, but they do not preserve intuitive obviousness; the successive application of valid rules to obvious premisses may produce conclusions that are bewildering in their complexity. The third condition fails. The system is nonetheless sound: every theorem it proves is true in the standard model of the natural numbers. The definition has confused soundness with properties that are desirable for pedagogical or practical reasons but that have no bearing on the truth of the system's output.

definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) the premisses of A are relevant to the conclusion — that is, the premisses and the conclusion share subject-matter, (ii) the conclusion of A follows from the premisses by a recognised rule of inference, and (iii) the argument could in principle be used to persuade a rational person of the conclusion.
conditions: the premisses are relevant to the conclusion
the conclusion follows from the premisses by a recognised rule of inference
the argument could in principle persuade a rational person of the conclusion
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'Snow is white and snow is not white; therefore, Napoleon was Corsican.' The premiss is a contradiction, and the conclusion follows from it by the principle of explosion — from a contradiction, any proposition follows. The argument is valid in classical logic. But the premiss is not relevant to the conclusion in any ordinary sense of 'relevant'; the premiss is about snow and the conclusion is about Napoleon. Moreover, no rational person would be persuaded of a conclusion about Napoleon's birthplace by a contradictory premiss about snow. The first and third conditions fail, yet the argument is valid.
missing_condition: The definition requires relevance of premisses to conclusion and potential persuasive force, but classical validity demands neither. An argument is classically valid whenever the truth of the premisses guarantees the truth of the conclusion, even if the premisses are entirely irrelevant to the conclusion and even if the argument would persuade no one. What is wanted is either a purely formal account of validity that dispenses with relevance, or an explicit adoption of a relevance logic that rejects the principle of explosion.
passage: One might propose that an argument is valid if and only if its premisses are relevant to its conclusion, the conclusion follows by a recognised rule of inference, and the argument could in principle be used to persuade a rational person. The relevance condition is motivated by the intuition that the premisses of a valid argument ought to bear upon the conclusion — that there should be a connection of subject-matter between them. The persuasion condition reflects the thought that validity ought to have some connection to rational conviction. Yet one must consider the argument from the contradictory premiss 'Snow is white and snow is not white' to the conclusion 'Napoleon was Corsican.' In classical logic, this argument is valid. The principle of explosion — ex falso quodlibet — guarantees that any proposition follows from a contradiction. The premiss cannot be true, so there is no possible circumstance in which the premiss is true and the conclusion false. But the premiss is about snow and the conclusion is about Napoleon. There is no connection of subject-matter between them. And no rational person would be persuaded of any conclusion about Napoleon by being told that snow both is and is not white. The first and third conditions fail. The definition has imposed desiderata of relevance and persuasion that are not part of the classical concept of validity. Classical validity is a purely formal property: it holds whenever the truth of the premisses guarantees the truth of the conclusion, regardless of subject-matter or rhetorical force.

definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A consists of a finite sequence of declarative sentences, the last of which is designated as the conclusion and the rest as premisses, (ii) the premisses and conclusion are expressed in a formal language with a determinate syntax, and (iii) there is no interpretation of the non-logical vocabulary under which all premisses are true and the conclusion is false.
conditions: A consists of a finite sequence of declarative sentences with the last designated as conclusion and the rest as premisses
The premisses and conclusion are expressed in a formal language with determinate syntax
There is no interpretation of the non-logical vocabulary under which all premisses are true and the conclusion is false
counterexample: Consider the following argument conducted entirely in natural English: 'All men are mortal; Socrates is a man; therefore Socrates is mortal.' This argument is valid — it is the paradigm of a valid argument, indeed. Yet its premisses and conclusion are not expressed in a formal language with a determinate syntax; they are expressed in ordinary English, which is rife with ambiguity, vagueness, and context-dependence. Condition (ii) is not satisfied. If we insist upon it, we are forced to deny that any argument expressed in natural language is valid, which is absurd.
missing_condition: The definition restricts validity to arguments expressed in formal languages, but validity is a property that attaches to arguments in natural language as well, and the formalisation of an argument is a means of revealing its validity, not a precondition of it.
passage: One might define validity as follows: an argument is valid if and only if it consists of a finite sequence of declarative sentences with the last designated as conclusion and the rest as premisses, the premisses and conclusion are expressed in a formal language with a determinate syntax, and there is no interpretation of the non-logical vocabulary under which all premisses are true and the conclusion is false. The third clause is, of course, the essential semantic condition, and it captures what most logicians mean by validity. But one might observe that the second clause introduces a restriction that is at once unnecessary and pernicious. Consider the following argument conducted entirely in natural English: 'All men are mortal; Socrates is a man; therefore Socrates is mortal.' This is, by universal consent, a valid argument — it is the paradigm of a valid argument, the example to which one turns when introducing the very concept. Yet its premisses and conclusion are not expressed in a formal language with a determinate syntax. They are expressed in ordinary English, a language that is shot through with ambiguity, vagueness, and context-dependence. Condition (ii) is not satisfied. If we insist upon it, we must conclude that no argument conducted in natural language is valid — that Barbara in its vernacular dress is not valid until it has been translated into the notation of the predicate calculus. That conclusion is absurd. What the case reveals is that validity is a property of arguments in virtue of their logical form, and while formalisation is an invaluable tool for making that form perspicuous, it is not a precondition of validity itself. The definition has confused the instrument of analysis with the object of analysis.

definition: An argument A is sound if and only if (i) A is deductively valid — there is no possible situation in which the premisses are true and the conclusion false, (ii) each premiss of A is in fact true, and (iii) the truth of each premiss is established independently of the conclusion — that is, no premiss is accepted merely because the conclusion is antecedently believed.
conditions: A is deductively valid
Each premiss of A is in fact true
The truth of each premiss is established independently of the conclusion
counterexample: Consider a mathematical proof in which a theorem is established by reductio ad absurdum. One assumes the negation of the theorem, derives a contradiction, and concludes that the theorem is true. In such a proof, the premiss — the negation of the theorem — is not in fact true; it is false. Yet the proof is sound in every sense that matters to the mathematician: it establishes its conclusion with certainty. Condition (ii) fails, since the assumed premiss is false, but the argument is a paradigm of rigorous reasoning.
missing_condition: The definition does not accommodate arguments by reductio ad absurdum, in which the soundness of the reasoning consists not in the truth of the premisses but in the logical structure that allows one to derive a conclusion from the falsity of its negation.
passage: One might propose that an argument is sound if and only if it is deductively valid, each of its premisses is in fact true, and the truth of each premiss is established independently of the conclusion. The definition is standard in introductory textbooks, and it serves well enough for simple syllogistic reasoning. A sound argument, on this account, is one that not only preserves truth but starts from truth, and thus guarantees the truth of its conclusion. But one might observe that the definition encounters a difficulty with a certain class of arguments that are central to mathematical and philosophical practice. Consider a proof by reductio ad absurdum. One assumes the negation of the theorem one wishes to establish, derives from that assumption a contradiction, and concludes that the theorem is true. The premiss — the negation of the theorem — is not in fact true; it is false. That is precisely the point: its falsity is demonstrated by the contradiction it engenders. Yet the proof is sound in every sense that matters to the mathematician. It establishes its conclusion with the highest degree of certainty available to human reason. Condition (ii) is not satisfied, since the assumed premiss is false, and yet the argument is a paradigm of rigorous and reliable reasoning. What the case reveals is that the definition of soundness given above is too narrow. It captures soundness for direct arguments but fails to accommodate indirect proofs, in which the logical structure guarantees the conclusion not through the truth of the premisses but through the consequences of their supposed truth.

definition: A sentence C is a logical consequence of a set of sentences S if and only if (i) C can be derived from S by a finite sequence of applications of inference rules drawn from a specified formal system, (ii) each step in the derivation is mechanically checkable — that is, it can be verified by a procedure that requires no insight or judgement, and (iii) the derivation employs no premisses other than members of S and axioms of the formal system.
conditions: C can be derived from S by a finite sequence of applications of inference rules from a specified formal system
Each step is mechanically checkable
The derivation employs no premisses other than members of S and axioms of the formal system
counterexample: Consider Goedel's incompleteness result. There are sentences of arithmetic that are true in the standard model — that is, they are semantic consequences of the axioms of Peano Arithmetic — but that cannot be derived from those axioms by any finite sequence of inference rules. Such a sentence is a logical consequence of the axioms in the semantic sense: it is true in every model in which the axioms are true. Yet it fails condition (i), since it cannot be derived by any proof in the system. The concept of logical consequence applies, but the definition, being purely syntactic, does not capture it.
missing_condition: The definition identifies logical consequence with derivability in a formal system, but semantic consequence may outstrip syntactic derivability, as the incompleteness theorems demonstrate. An adequate account must allow for consequences that hold in virtue of truth-preservation across all models, even when no proof exists.
passage: One might define logical consequence as follows: a sentence is a logical consequence of a set of sentences if and only if it can be derived from that set by a finite sequence of applications of inference rules drawn from a specified formal system, each step is mechanically checkable, and the derivation employs no premisses other than members of the set and axioms of the formal system. This is the syntactic or proof-theoretic conception of consequence, and it has much to recommend it: derivability is precise, decidable in principle, and free from the ontological commitments that attend semantic accounts. Yet one might observe that the definition is too narrow. Consider the situation revealed by Goedel's first incompleteness theorem. There are sentences of arithmetic that are true in the standard model of the natural numbers — that is, they are semantic consequences of the Peano axioms — but that cannot be derived from those axioms by any finite sequence of applications of the rules of the system. Such a sentence is a logical consequence of the axioms in the sense that it is true in every model in which the axioms are true. There is no interpretation under which the axioms hold and the sentence fails. Yet condition (i) is not satisfied: no derivation exists. The concept of logical consequence applies in its fullest and most important sense, but the definition, being purely proof-theoretic, fails to capture it. What the case reveals is that derivability and consequence are not coextensive, and that a definition which identifies the two will inevitably exclude genuine consequences that lie beyond the reach of any particular formal system.

definition: A statement S is paradoxical if and only if (i) S is derived by apparently valid reasoning from apparently true premisses, (ii) S is itself apparently false or self-contradictory, and (iii) no step in the reasoning from premisses to S can be identified as fallacious upon careful inspection.
conditions: S is derived by apparently valid reasoning from apparently true premisses
S is itself apparently false or self-contradictory
No step in the reasoning can be identified as fallacious upon careful inspection
counterexample: Consider the Liar sentence: 'This sentence is false.' It is not derived from any premisses at all — it is a single, self-referential assertion. There is no chain of reasoning from premisses to conclusion that one might inspect for fallacious steps. It does not satisfy conditions (i) or (iii), since there is no argument involved. Yet it is, by universal consent, a paradox — indeed, it is the paradigm of a logical paradox. The Liar is paradoxical not because it results from apparently valid reasoning but because it resists consistent assignment of truth-value.
missing_condition: The definition conceives of paradoxes exclusively as the conclusions of arguments with apparently sound reasoning, but some paradoxes — such as semantic paradoxes involving self-reference — arise not from chains of inference but from the internal structure of single statements, and no account that requires an argumentative derivation can accommodate them.
passage: One might propose that a statement is paradoxical if and only if it is derived by apparently valid reasoning from apparently true premisses, it is itself apparently false or self-contradictory, and no step in the reasoning from premisses to conclusion can be identified as fallacious upon careful inspection. This definition captures a good deal of what we ordinarily mean by a paradox. Zeno's arguments, for instance, proceed from plausible premisses about space and motion by apparently valid steps to conclusions that seem plainly false — that Achilles can never overtake the tortoise, that the arrow does not move. In each case the difficulty consists in the combination of plausible premisses, valid reasoning, and an unacceptable conclusion. Yet one might observe that the definition does not accommodate all cases that are paradigmatically paradoxical. Consider the Liar sentence: 'This sentence is false.' It is not derived from any premisses; it is a single, self-referential assertion that stands alone. There is no chain of reasoning from premisses to conclusion that one might inspect for validity or fallacy. It does not satisfy conditions (i) or (iii), since there is no argument involved. And yet it is, by universal consent, a paradox — indeed, it is the archetype of logical paradox. The Liar is paradoxical not because it results from apparently valid reasoning applied to apparently true premisses, but because it resists consistent assignment of a truth-value: if it is true then it is false, and if it is false then it is true. What the case reveals is that the definition has conceived of paradox too narrowly, as essentially an argumentative phenomenon, when in fact some of the deepest paradoxes arise not from chains of inference but from the internal structure of single sentences.

definition: A set of sentences S entails a sentence C if and only if (i) it is impossible for all members of S to be true and C to be false, (ii) the impossibility in (i) is a matter of the logical forms of the sentences involved — that is, it holds in virtue of the meanings of the logical constants alone, and (iii) C is not already a member of S or a trivial restatement of a member of S.
conditions: It is impossible for all members of S to be true and C to be false
The impossibility is a matter of logical form — it holds in virtue of the meanings of the logical constants alone
C is not already a member of S or a trivial restatement thereof
counterexample: Consider the sentences 'John is a bachelor' and 'John is unmarried.' It is impossible for the first to be true and the second false. Yet this impossibility does not hold in virtue of the meanings of the logical constants alone; it holds in virtue of the meaning of the non-logical term 'bachelor,' which contains 'unmarried' as part of its analysis. Condition (ii) is not satisfied. Yet many logicians and philosophers would say that the first sentence entails the second. The case shows that the definition captures only formal or logical entailment and excludes analytic or meaning-based entailment.
missing_condition: The definition restricts entailment to relations that hold in virtue of logical form, but there is a broader and equally important sense of entailment in which one sentence entails another in virtue of the meanings of its non-logical terms, and a complete account must accommodate this analytic dimension.
passage: One might define entailment as follows: a set of sentences entails a sentence if and only if it is impossible for all members of the set to be true and the entailed sentence false, this impossibility is a matter of the logical forms of the sentences — holding in virtue of the meanings of the logical constants alone — and the entailed sentence is not already a member of the set or a trivial restatement of one. The definition is clean and precise, and it captures what is ordinarily meant by formal or strictly logical entailment. 'All As are Bs' and 'x is an A' entail 'x is a B' because the impossibility of the premisses being true and the conclusion false is a matter of the universal quantifier and the structure of predication. But one might observe that the definition is too restrictive. Consider the sentences 'John is a bachelor' and 'John is unmarried.' It is impossible for the first to be true and the second false; anyone who grasps the meaning of 'bachelor' will see this at once. Yet the impossibility does not hold in virtue of the meanings of the logical constants alone. It holds in virtue of the meaning of the non-logical term 'bachelor,' which contains 'unmarried' as part of its analysis. Condition (ii) is not satisfied. And yet many logicians and philosophers of language would say without hesitation that the first sentence entails the second. The entailment is analytic rather than formal — it rests on meaning rather than on logical structure — but it is entailment nonetheless. What the case reveals is that the definition captures only one species of a broader genus, and that an adequate account must recognise entailment relations that hold in virtue of the content of non-logical vocabulary as well as those that hold in virtue of logical form.

definition: A set of sentences S is consistent if and only if (i) no contradiction can be derived from S using the inference rules of classical logic, (ii) there exists at least one interpretation under which all members of S are simultaneously true, and (iii) S is finite.
conditions: No contradiction can be derived from S using the inference rules of classical logic
There exists at least one interpretation under which all members of S are simultaneously true
S is finite
counterexample: Consider the set of all sentences of the form 'There exist at least n distinct objects,' for every natural number n. This set is infinite. By the compactness theorem of first-order logic, it is consistent: every finite subset has a model, and therefore the whole set has a model — namely, any infinite domain. The set satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) but violates condition (iii), since it is not finite. Yet it is consistent. The finiteness requirement excludes perfectly consistent infinite sets of sentences.
missing_condition: The definition unnecessarily restricts consistency to finite sets of sentences, but consistency is a property that applies equally to infinite sets, and the compactness theorem shows that infinite sets may be consistent even though they cannot be surveyed in their entirety.
passage: One might propose that a set of sentences is consistent if and only if no contradiction can be derived from it using the inference rules of classical logic, there exists at least one interpretation under which all members are simultaneously true, and the set is finite. The finiteness condition may seem natural enough: we typically consider finite theories and finite collections of premisses, and the demand that a set be surveyable appears innocuous. Yet one might observe that it introduces an arbitrary limitation. Consider the set of all sentences of the form 'There exist at least n distinct objects,' where n ranges over the natural numbers: 'There exists at least one object,' 'There exist at least two distinct objects,' 'There exist at least three distinct objects,' and so on without end. This set is infinite. But it is consistent. By the compactness theorem of first-order logic, every finite subset of this set has a model — one simply takes a domain of sufficiently many objects — and the full set also has a model, namely any infinite domain. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied: no contradiction is derivable, and there exists an interpretation under which all members are true. But condition (iii) fails, since the set is not finite. If we insist upon finiteness, we are forced to deny that this set is consistent, which contradicts both the model-theoretic fact and the intuition of every working logician. What the case reveals is that consistency is not restricted to finite collections, and that the definition has introduced a condition that is not merely unnecessary but actively misleading.

definition: A concept C is definable in a formal language L if and only if (i) there exists a formula φ(x) in L such that φ(x) is true of exactly those objects that fall under C, (ii) φ(x) contains only vocabulary belonging to L — no terms from outside L are employed, and (iii) the extension of C is fixed by φ(x) in every model of L — that is, the definition is absolute across interpretations.
conditions: There exists a formula φ(x) in L true of exactly those objects falling under C
φ(x) contains only vocabulary belonging to L
The extension of C is fixed by φ(x) in every model of L — the definition is absolute across interpretations
counterexample: Consider the concept of a well-ordering of the natural numbers within the first-order language of arithmetic. There is no first-order formula that picks out exactly the well-orderings in every model of Peano Arithmetic, because first-order Peano Arithmetic has non-standard models in which there exist descending chains that do not terminate, and the formula cannot distinguish standard from non-standard models. The extension varies across interpretations: the formula captures the intended concept in the standard model but not in non-standard ones. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the concept of well-ordering is, by any reasonable standard, definable in arithmetic — it is definable in second-order arithmetic, and it is perfectly determinate. The concept is definable; it is merely not first-order definable with absolute invariance across all models.
missing_condition: The definition requires that definability be absolute across all models of the language, but many important mathematical concepts are definable in richer logical frameworks even though they resist first-order characterisation that is invariant under all interpretations.
passage: One might propose that a concept is definable in a formal language if and only if there exists a formula in that language which is true of exactly those objects falling under the concept, the formula contains only vocabulary belonging to the language, and the extension of the concept is fixed by the formula in every model — that is, the definition is absolute across interpretations. This is a strong and precise notion of definability, and it has clear applications in model theory. Yet one might observe that condition (iii) demands too much. Consider the concept of a well-ordering of the natural numbers within the first-order language of arithmetic. In the standard model, the natural numbers are well-ordered: every non-empty subset has a least element. One might attempt to express this in first-order arithmetic, and indeed one can write formulas that capture aspects of the ordering. But first-order Peano Arithmetic has non-standard models in which there exist descending chains that do not terminate, and no first-order formula can distinguish the standard model from these aberrant ones. The extension of the concept varies across interpretations: the formula captures the intended concept in the standard model but fails in non-standard models. Condition (iii) is not satisfied. Yet the concept of well-ordering is, by any reasonable standard, definable — it is definable in second-order arithmetic, where one can quantify over sets. What the case reveals is that the definition has set the bar for definability too high, demanding absolute invariance across all models when many of the most important mathematical concepts can be defined only in richer frameworks or with the recognition that definition is relative to a class of intended interpretations.

definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) the conclusion of A is true in every possible world in which the premisses are true, (ii) the connection between premisses and conclusion is a matter of necessity rather than contingent correlation, and (iii) the premisses are relevant to the conclusion — that is, there is a substantive connection of content between premisses and conclusion.
conditions: The conclusion is true in every possible world in which the premisses are true
The connection between premisses and conclusion is a matter of necessity
The premisses are relevant to the conclusion — there is a substantive connection of content
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'It is raining and it is not raining; therefore the moon is made of cheese.' The premiss is a contradiction. A contradiction is true in no possible world, so condition (i) is vacuously satisfied: there is no possible world in which the premiss is true and the conclusion false, because there is no possible world in which the premiss is true. The connection is a matter of necessity. Yet the premisses are not relevant to the conclusion — there is no substantive connection between the weather and the composition of the moon. Condition (iii) fails. And yet, by the standard account of classical logic, this argument is valid. The case shows that the relevance condition excludes arguments that classical logic counts as valid.
missing_condition: The definition adds a relevance condition to validity, but classical validity does not require relevance: an argument from contradictory premisses is classically valid regardless of the content of the conclusion, and the relevance condition confuses validity with a stronger property.
passage: One might propose that an argument is valid if and only if the conclusion is true in every possible world in which the premisses are true, the connection between premisses and conclusion is a matter of necessity, and the premisses are relevant to the conclusion — there is a substantive connection of content between them. The third clause is motivated by a natural intuition: surely a valid argument must have something to do with its conclusion; the premisses must bear upon the point at issue. Yet one might observe that this intuition, however attractive, conflicts with the standard account. Consider the argument: 'It is raining and it is not raining; therefore the moon is made of cheese.' The premiss is a contradiction. A contradiction is true in no possible world. Therefore condition (i) is vacuously satisfied: there is no possible world in which the premiss is true and the conclusion false, simply because there is no possible world in which the premiss is true at all. The connection, such as it is, holds of necessity. But condition (iii) fails conspicuously. There is no substantive connection of content between the weather and the composition of the moon. The premisses are wholly irrelevant to the conclusion. Yet by the standard account of classical logic, this argument is valid — indeed, ex falso quodlibet is a theorem of every standard system. The case reveals that the definition has confused validity with a stronger property — perhaps the property that relevance logicians call 'relevant entailment.' Classical validity is purely truth-preservational and has no relevance requirement, and a definition that imposes one will exclude arguments that are valid by the lights of the dominant logical tradition.

definition: A sentence C is a logical consequence of a set of premisses S if and only if (i) every model that satisfies all members of S also satisfies C, (ii) the vocabulary of C is a subset of the vocabulary of S — that is, C introduces no terms not already present in S, and (iii) S is a satisfiable set — that is, there exists at least one model in which all members of S are true.
conditions: Every model that satisfies all members of S also satisfies C
The vocabulary of C is a subset of the vocabulary of S
S is satisfiable — there exists at least one model in which all members of S are true
counterexample: Consider an unsatisfiable set of premisses — for instance, { P, ¬P }. There exists no model in which both members are true, so condition (iii) fails. Yet by the standard semantic account of logical consequence, every sentence is a logical consequence of an unsatisfiable set, because the universal quantification over satisfying models is vacuously true when there are no such models. The sentence '2 + 2 = 5' is, in this sense, a logical consequence of { P, ¬P }. If we insist on condition (iii), we must deny this, contradicting the standard account.
missing_condition: The definition excludes the case of unsatisfiable premiss sets, but logical consequence is standardly understood to hold vacuously when the premisses are unsatisfiable, and any account that requires satisfiability of the premiss set will fail to capture this important feature of the classical notion.
passage: One might define logical consequence as follows: a sentence is a logical consequence of a set of premisses if and only if every model satisfying all premisses also satisfies the sentence, the vocabulary of the sentence is a subset of the vocabulary of the premisses, and the premiss set is satisfiable. The second and third conditions may seem reasonable restrictions. The vocabulary condition ensures that the consequence does not smuggle in new terms; the satisfiability condition ensures that we are reasoning from premisses that are at least jointly possible. Yet one might observe that the third condition conflicts with the standard semantic account. Consider an unsatisfiable set of premisses — { P, ¬P }, for instance. There is no model in which both are true, so condition (iii) fails. But on the standard Tarskian account, every sentence is a logical consequence of an unsatisfiable set, because the relevant universal quantification — 'for every model in which the premisses are true' — is vacuously satisfied when there are no such models. The sentence '2 + 2 = 5' is, on this account, a logical consequence of { P, ¬P }. This is not a defect but a feature of the classical notion: it reflects the principle that from a contradiction anything follows. If we insist upon condition (iii), we are forced to deny this, and we thereby depart from the standard account in a way that is difficult to justify. What the case reveals is that the satisfiability requirement, however intuitively appealing, is not part of the classical concept of logical consequence, and a definition that includes it will fail to capture consequences that the standard account recognises.

definition: A situation S is paradoxical if and only if (i) S involves a set of propositions each of which is individually plausible, (ii) the conjunction of all propositions in S is logically impossible, and (iii) S can be resolved by identifying which proposition is false and providing grounds for its rejection.
conditions: S involves a set of propositions each of which is individually plausible
The conjunction of all propositions in S is logically impossible
S can be resolved by identifying which proposition is false and providing grounds for its rejection
counterexample: Consider the Sorites paradox. One grain of sand is not a heap. Adding one grain of sand to something that is not a heap does not make it a heap. Yet a million grains of sand, accumulated one by one, constitute a heap. Each proposition is individually plausible, and their conjunction leads to an impossibility. But condition (iii) is not straightforwardly satisfied: the paradox resists resolution by simple rejection of one proposition. One cannot easily identify which addition of a single grain transforms a non-heap into a heap, and the grounds for rejecting any particular instance of the inductive premiss are precisely what is at issue. The paradox is paradoxical precisely because it resists the kind of resolution the definition requires.
missing_condition: The definition requires that every paradox be resolvable by identifying and rejecting a false proposition, but some paradoxes — particularly those involving vagueness — resist this strategy, and their paradoxicality consists precisely in the absence of any satisfactory resolution of the kind the definition demands.
passage: One might propose that a situation is paradoxical if and only if it involves a set of propositions each of which is individually plausible, the conjunction of all propositions is logically impossible, and the situation can be resolved by identifying which proposition is false and providing grounds for its rejection. This is a tidy account, and it fits many familiar cases. The paradox of the ravens, for instance, involves individually plausible principles about confirmation that jointly yield an unacceptable conclusion, and it may be resolved by rejecting or qualifying one of the principles. But one might observe that condition (iii) is too optimistic. Consider the Sorites paradox. One grain of sand is not a heap. Adding one grain of sand to something that is not a heap does not make it a heap. Yet a million grains, accumulated one at a time, constitute a heap. Each proposition is individually plausible, and their conjunction generates an impossibility. But which proposition is false? One cannot easily identify the precise addition that transforms a non-heap into a heap. The inductive premiss — that adding one grain to a non-heap does not make a heap — is not straightforwardly false; it seems correct for every particular case, even though it leads to an absurd conclusion when iterated. The grounds for rejecting any particular instance are precisely what is at issue. The Sorites is paradoxical not because it awaits resolution but because it resists the kind of resolution the definition demands. What the case reveals is that the definition confuses paradoxicality with a particular mode of response to paradox, and that some of the deepest paradoxes are paradoxical precisely because they cannot be resolved by the simple expedient of locating a false premiss.

definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A consists of a set of premisses and a conclusion, (ii) it is impossible that all the premisses of A be true and the conclusion of A be false, and (iii) the conclusion of A is derived from the premisses by a finite sequence of applications of recognised rules of inference.
conditions: A consists of a set of premisses and a conclusion
It is impossible that all premisses be true and the conclusion false
The conclusion is derived by a finite sequence of recognised rules of inference
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'All mammals are warm-blooded; all whales are mammals; therefore all whales are warm-blooded.' This argument is valid in the standard sense — it is impossible for the premisses to be true and the conclusion false. But now consider a person who grasps the truth of the premisses and the truth of the conclusion, and who recognises that the conclusion cannot be false if the premisses are true, but who arrives at the conclusion not by any application of a rule of inference but by a flash of intuitive insight. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met, but condition (iii) — derivation by recognised rules — is not. Yet the argument, considered as a relation between premisses and conclusion, is valid regardless of the psychological process by which the conclusion was reached.
missing_condition: The definition conflates the semantic notion of validity — the impossibility of true premisses and a false conclusion — with the syntactic notion of derivability by rules of inference. A valid argument need not be accompanied by a formal derivation; the definition should clarify whether validity is a property of the argument's logical form or of the process by which the conclusion is obtained.
passage: One might define validity as follows: an argument is valid if and only if it consists of premisses and a conclusion, it is impossible that all premisses be true and the conclusion false, and the conclusion is derived from the premisses by a finite sequence of applications of recognised rules of inference. This definition attempts to combine two ideas that are related but distinct. The first two conditions capture what is sometimes called semantic validity — the preservation of truth from premisses to conclusion. The third condition adds a syntactic requirement — that the conclusion be obtained by a process of formal derivation. In many cases, these two requirements coincide: a valid argument can be furnished with a formal derivation, and a formally derivable conclusion is one that cannot be false if the premisses are true. But one might observe that the conjunction of these requirements is too restrictive. Consider a simple syllogism whose premisses and conclusion are such that it is plainly impossible for the premisses to be true and the conclusion false. A person might grasp this impossibility immediately, by intuitive insight, without constructing or being able to construct a formal derivation from recognised rules. The argument, considered as a relation between its premisses and its conclusion, is valid — the truth of the premisses guarantees the truth of the conclusion. Yet the third condition is not met, for no derivation has been produced. The definition, it appears, has conflated two distinct notions: the semantic property of truth-preservation and the syntactic property of formal derivability. A more satisfactory definition would either specify which of these properties it intends to capture or would explain the relation between them.

definition: An argument A is sound if and only if (i) A is valid — that is, it is impossible for the premisses of A to be true and the conclusion false, (ii) all the premisses of A are in fact true, and (iii) the premisses of A are known to be true by the person advancing the argument.
conditions: A is valid
All premisses of A are in fact true
The premisses are known to be true by the person advancing the argument
counterexample: Consider an argument whose premisses happen to be true and whose form is valid, but which was advanced two thousand years ago by a philosopher who believed the premisses on the basis of entirely mistaken reasoning. The philosopher's grounds for accepting the premisses were bad, and he cannot be said to have known them to be true in any demanding sense; he merely believed them, and they happened to be true. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met, but condition (iii) is not. Yet the argument itself — considered as a logical structure with true premisses and a valid form — is sound in the standard sense. The soundness of an argument, it appears, is independent of the cognitive state of the person who advances it.
missing_condition: The definition introduces a condition concerning the person's cognitive state that is extraneous to the logical concept of soundness. Soundness is standardly a property of the argument itself — validity plus truth of premisses — and does not require that anyone know the premisses to be true.
passage: One might propose that an argument is sound if and only if it is valid, all its premisses are true, and the premisses are known to be true by the person advancing the argument. The first two conditions capture the standard definition of soundness in logic: a sound argument is a valid argument with true premisses. The third condition adds a requirement concerning the cognitive state of the arguer — the person must not merely advance true premisses but must know them to be true. This addition has a certain appeal: one might think that an argument whose premisses are true by accident, or whose truth is unknown to the arguer, has less claim to be called sound than one whose premisses are advanced with full awareness of their truth. Yet one might observe that the third condition introduces a confusion between the logical properties of an argument and the psychological properties of the person who advances it. Consider a philosopher of antiquity who advances a valid argument with premisses that are, as it happens, true — but who believes those premisses on the basis of entirely mistaken reasoning. The philosopher does not know the premisses to be true; he merely holds them as beliefs that happen to coincide with the facts. Condition (iii) is not met. Yet the argument, considered as a logical structure, is valid and has true premisses; it is sound in the standard sense regardless of the philosopher's cognitive situation. The definition has imported into the concept of soundness a requirement that belongs not to logic but to the assessment of the arguer's position. What is needed is a recognition that soundness is a property of the argument itself, not of the person who happens to state it.

definition: A proposition Q is a logical consequence of a set of propositions P1, P2, ..., Pn if and only if (i) Q is true in every interpretation in which P1, P2, ..., Pn are all true, (ii) the connection between P1, P2, ..., Pn and Q is purely formal — that is, it depends upon the logical structure of the propositions and not upon the meanings of their non-logical terms, and (iii) Q is not already contained among P1, P2, ..., Pn — that is, Q is distinct from each of the premisses.
conditions: Q is true in every interpretation in which P1 through Pn are all true
The connection is purely formal, depending only on logical structure
Q is not already contained among the premisses
counterexample: Consider the set of premisses {P}. The proposition P is true in every interpretation in which the premisses are true — trivially so, since P is the only premiss. The connection is purely formal. But P is already contained among the premisses. Condition (iii) is violated. Yet by the standard definition of logical consequence, P is a logical consequence of any set that contains it. The requirement that Q be distinct from the premisses excludes the trivial but perfectly valid case of a proposition following from itself — a case that any adequate account of logical consequence must accommodate.
