missing_condition: The definition erroneously excludes the case in which a proposition is a logical consequence of a set containing itself. Any adequate account of logical consequence must allow that a proposition follows from any set of premisses that includes it, since this is a trivial but genuine instance of truth-preservation.
passage: One might define logical consequence by saying that a proposition Q is a logical consequence of premisses P1, P2, ..., Pn if and only if Q is true in every interpretation in which the premisses are true, the connection between premisses and conclusion is purely formal, and Q is not already contained among the premisses. The first condition captures the model-theoretic core of the concept: logical consequence is truth-preservation across all interpretations. The second condition ensures that the consequence relation holds in virtue of logical form rather than the particular meanings of the non-logical vocabulary. The third condition is apparently intended to ensure that the consequence relation is informative — that the conclusion says something the premisses do not already say. Yet this third condition, well-intentioned though it may be, is too restrictive. Consider any proposition P and the set of premisses consisting of P alone. It is trivially true that P is true in every interpretation in which the premisses are true, since P is the only premiss. The connection is formal. But P is contained among the premisses, and condition (iii) is violated. The definition thus excludes the case of a proposition following from itself — a case that is trivial but that any adequate account of logical consequence must accommodate. A proposition follows logically from any set of propositions that includes it; this is not a defect of the concept but a consequence of the very notion of truth-preservation. The definition, by adding a non-triviality requirement, has inadvertently excluded a perfectly legitimate class of logical consequences.

definition: A set of propositions constitutes a paradox if and only if (i) each proposition in the set, taken individually, appears self-evidently true or at least highly plausible, (ii) the propositions, taken jointly, entail a contradiction, and (iii) the contradiction is derivable by elementary and uncontroversial steps of reasoning.
conditions: Each proposition individually appears self-evidently true or highly plausible
The propositions jointly entail a contradiction
The contradiction is derivable by elementary, uncontroversial reasoning
counterexample: Consider the sorites paradox. The premisses — that one grain of sand does not make a heap, and that adding one grain to a non-heap does not make it a heap — are each highly plausible. And yet repeated application of the second premiss leads, by elementary reasoning, to the conclusion that no amount of sand makes a heap, which contradicts the evident truth that large collections of sand are heaps. The three conditions are met. But now consider a sophisticated mathematical result — say, the Banach-Tarski theorem, which states that a solid sphere can be decomposed into a finite number of pieces and reassembled into two spheres of the same size. The premisses — the axioms of set theory — are individually plausible, and the conclusion contradicts common sense. Yet the derivation is far from elementary; it requires advanced mathematics. Condition (iii) fails, yet many would call the result paradoxical. The definition, by requiring elementary reasoning, excludes paradoxes that arise from sophisticated derivations.
missing_condition: The definition restricts paradoxes to those arising from elementary reasoning, but paradoxical results may emerge from complex and technically demanding derivations. The condition concerning the simplicity of the reasoning should be relaxed or reformulated to accommodate paradoxes that arise at any level of logical complexity.
passage: One might define a paradox as a set of propositions each of which appears self-evidently true or at least highly plausible, which jointly entail a contradiction, and from which the contradiction is derivable by elementary and uncontroversial steps of reasoning. This definition captures many of the classical paradoxes admirably. The liar paradox, the sorites, and the paradox of the ravens all involve premisses that seem individually compelling, conclusions that are contradictory, and reasoning that proceeds by steps so simple that there appears to be no room for error. The requirement that the reasoning be elementary is intended to ensure that the paradox is genuinely surprising — that the contradiction emerges not from some abstruse technical manoeuvre but from principles and inferences that any competent reasoner would accept. Yet one might observe that some of the most striking results in the foundations of mathematics have the character of paradoxes without satisfying the third condition. Consider the Banach-Tarski theorem, which demonstrates that a solid sphere may be decomposed into finitely many pieces and reassembled — without stretching or distortion — into two spheres each equal in size to the original. The premisses are the axioms of Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory with the axiom of choice, each of which is individually plausible. The conclusion contradicts common sense in the most dramatic fashion. Yet the derivation is far from elementary; it requires a sophisticated apparatus of measure theory and group theory. The definition would exclude this result from the class of paradoxes, yet it is commonly described as paradoxical. It appears that the requirement of elementary reasoning is too restrictive; paradoxes may arise at any level of logical complexity.

definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) P and Q are well-formed propositions of the same language, (ii) there is no possible circumstance in which P is true and Q is false, and (iii) the truth of Q is a consequence of the content of P — that is, the entailment holds in virtue of the meanings of the terms in P and Q.
conditions: P and Q are well-formed propositions of the same language
There is no possible circumstance in which P is true and Q is false
The truth of Q is a consequence of the content of P
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'It is raining and it is not raining.' Since this proposition is necessarily false — true in no possible circumstance — there is no possible circumstance in which it is true and any other proposition Q is false. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met for any Q whatever. But condition (iii) — that the truth of Q be a consequence of the content of P — seems to fail when Q is, say, 'The moon is made of cheese.' There is no sense in which the content of a contradiction about rain has anything to do with the composition of the moon. Yet by the standard account of entailment, a necessarily false proposition entails every proposition. The definition's third condition attempts to block this result but does so only informally, without specifying what 'consequence of the content' means.
missing_condition: The definition introduces a relevance condition — that Q follow from the content of P — but does not provide a precise account of what content-based consequence amounts to. Without a rigorous explication of this notion, the condition is too vague to do the logical work required of it, and the definition remains vulnerable to the paradoxes of material and strict implication.
passage: One might propose that a proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if both are well-formed propositions of the same language, there is no possible circumstance in which P is true and Q is false, and the truth of Q is a consequence of the content of P. The first condition is a harmless housekeeping requirement. The second captures the modal core of entailment: the truth of P necessitates the truth of Q. The third is an attempt to impose a relevance constraint — to ensure that the entailment holds not by accident of logical form but in virtue of a genuine connection between what P says and what Q says. This third condition is motivated by the well-known difficulty that, on the standard account, a necessarily false proposition entails every proposition and every proposition entails a necessary truth. Consider the proposition that it is raining and it is not raining. This proposition is necessarily false; there is no possible circumstance in which it is true. It follows that there is no possible circumstance in which it is true and any proposition Q is false. Conditions (i) and (ii) are therefore met for any Q — including the proposition that the moon is composed of cheese. The third condition is intended to exclude such absurdities by requiring that Q follow from the content of P. But the definition does not specify what content-based consequence amounts to. Is it a matter of meaning? Of subject-matter? Of relevance? Without a precise explication, the third condition remains a gesture in the direction of a solution rather than a solution itself. The definition identifies a genuine problem — the paradoxes of material and strict implication — but the tool it offers for solving that problem is too blunt to do the required work.

definition: A set of propositions S is consistent if and only if (i) no proposition in S contradicts any other proposition in S, (ii) no contradiction is derivable from S by the application of standard rules of inference, and (iii) there exists at least one interpretation under which all propositions in S are true.
conditions: No proposition in S directly contradicts any other
No contradiction is derivable from S by standard rules
There exists an interpretation under which all propositions in S are true
counterexample: Consider a set of axioms for a mathematical theory — say, a set of axioms for arithmetic — that is in fact consistent. No axiom directly contradicts any other, no contradiction has ever been derived from the axioms, and there exist interpretations (models) in which all axioms are true. All three conditions are met. But now suppose a logician produces a new derivation — one not previously known — that derives a contradiction from the axioms using standard rules. It turns out that condition (ii) was never met, even though the contradiction had not yet been discovered. The conditions, as stated, do not distinguish between a set that is consistent and a set that merely appears consistent because no one has yet found the contradiction. The definition seems to describe consistency as an objective property, yet condition (ii) could be read as requiring that no contradiction has been derived rather than that none is derivable.
passage: One might define consistency of a set of propositions by requiring that no proposition directly contradicts any other, that no contradiction is derivable by standard rules of inference, and that there exists at least one interpretation under which all propositions are true. These three conditions correspond, roughly, to three different ways of understanding consistency: the absence of explicit contradictions, the impossibility of deriving contradictions, and the existence of a model. In simple cases, the three conditions coincide, and the definition appears to capture the concept of consistency without difficulty. Yet one might observe that the relation among the conditions is more delicate than it first appears. The first condition — no direct contradiction — is a surface requirement that can be met trivially by a set that harbours latent contradictions derivable by a chain of inferences. The second condition — no derivable contradiction — is the operative one, but it faces a peculiar difficulty. Consider a set of axioms for arithmetic that has been studied for a century without any contradiction having been derived. One might say that condition (ii) is met. But whether it is met is not a matter of what has been derived but of what is derivable — and this is an objective mathematical fact that may be unknown. If tomorrow a logician produces a derivation of a contradiction from the axioms, it will not be that the set has suddenly become inconsistent; it will be that it was inconsistent all along and that the inconsistency had simply not been detected. The third condition — the existence of a model — would settle the matter, but for sufficiently complex theories the existence of a model may itself be unprovable. The definition, it appears, states three conditions that are intended to be equivalent but that may come apart in ways that are difficult to resolve.

definition: A concept C is definable within a formal language L if and only if (i) there exists a well-formed expression E in L such that E applies to all and only those objects to which C applies, (ii) E uses only the primitive symbols and logical connectives of L, and (iii) the equivalence between C and E is provable from the axioms of the theory formulated in L.
conditions: There exists a well-formed expression E in L coextensive with C
E uses only primitive symbols and logical connectives of L
The equivalence between C and E is provable from the axioms of L
counterexample: Consider the concept of truth for sentences of a formal language L, as studied by Tarski. If one attempts to define truth for L within L itself, one confronts the requirement that there exist an expression in L coextensive with the concept of truth for L, using only the primitive symbols of L, with the equivalence provable from L's axioms. All three conditions specify what such a definition would require. Yet Tarski's theorem on the undefinability of truth shows that, for any sufficiently expressive language L, no such expression exists — the concept of truth for L is not definable within L. The conditions specify a perfectly coherent set of requirements, but they cannot be jointly satisfied in the case of truth, which reveals a deep limitation on definability within formal languages.
missing_condition: The definition does not acknowledge the possibility that certain important concepts — such as truth — are in principle undefinable within the language to which they apply, owing to the expressive limitations demonstrated by Tarski's result. A caveat concerning the boundaries of definability within a given language is needed.
passage: One might propose that a concept is definable within a formal language if and only if there exists a well-formed expression in the language coextensive with the concept, the expression uses only the primitive symbols and logical connectives of the language, and the equivalence between the concept and the expression is provable from the axioms of the relevant theory. This definition captures the standard notion of explicit definability in formal systems: a concept is definable when it can be replaced, without loss, by an expression already available in the language. The requirement that only primitive symbols be used ensures that the definition is non-circular; the provability condition ensures that the equivalence is not merely stipulated but established within the theory. Yet one might observe that the definition, precisely because it is so exacting, highlights a remarkable limitation. Consider the concept of truth for sentences of a formal language L — the concept that a sentence of L is true if and only if things are as the sentence says they are. One might expect this concept to be definable within L itself, given that L is the language in which the sentences are formulated. But Tarski's celebrated result demonstrates that, for any language of sufficient expressive power, the concept of truth for that language cannot be defined within it. No well-formed expression of L is coextensive with the truth predicate for L; no such equivalence is provable from the axioms. The three conditions specify a perfectly coherent set of requirements for definability, but in the case of truth they cannot be jointly satisfied. What the definition does not acknowledge is that there are important concepts — truth being the foremost — that are in principle beyond the reach of definition within the language to which they pertain. This is not a defect of the definition so much as a revelation of the limits of formal definability itself.

definition: A deductive system D is sound if and only if (i) D consists of a set of axioms and a set of rules of inference, (ii) every axiom of D is logically valid — that is, true in every interpretation, and (iii) every rule of inference of D preserves logical validity — that is, if the premisses of the rule are logically valid, then the conclusion is also logically valid.
conditions: D consists of axioms and rules of inference
Every axiom of D is logically valid
Every rule of inference preserves logical validity
counterexample: Consider a deductive system that includes as an axiom the proposition 'If P then P' — which is logically valid — and as a rule the rule of modus ponens — which preserves validity. Now add a further axiom: 'There exists something' (the existential claim that the domain is non-empty). In the standard semantics for first-order logic, this axiom is logically valid only if one restricts attention to non-empty domains. If one allows the empty domain as a possible interpretation, the axiom is not logically valid — it is false in the empty domain. All three conditions appear to be met under the restriction to non-empty domains, but condition (ii) fails under the broader interpretation that admits the empty domain. The soundness of the system thus depends upon a decision about the range of admissible interpretations that the definition does not explicitly address.
missing_condition: The definition does not specify the class of interpretations over which logical validity is assessed. Since whether an axiom is logically valid depends upon which interpretations are admitted — in particular, whether the empty domain is included — the definition requires a further condition specifying the intended semantics.
passage: One might define soundness of a deductive system by requiring that it consist of axioms and rules of inference, that every axiom be logically valid, and that every rule of inference preserve logical validity. This definition reflects the standard account: a sound system is one that never leads from truth to falsehood, because its starting points are guaranteed to be true in every interpretation and its rules transmit this guarantee from premisses to conclusions. The definition is elegant and, in many contexts, entirely adequate. Yet one might observe that the concept of logical validity upon which it relies — truth in every interpretation — is itself ambiguous in a way that the definition does not address. Consider the axiom that there exists something — the assertion that the domain of discourse is non-empty. In the standard semantics for first-order logic, this axiom is logically valid, because the standard semantics restricts interpretations to non-empty domains. But if one admits the empty domain as a possible interpretation — as certain logicians have proposed — the axiom fails: it is false in the interpretation with no objects. Whether the deductive system that includes this axiom is sound therefore depends upon a prior decision about which interpretations count. The definition speaks of logical validity without specifying the class of admissible interpretations, and this omission is not merely a technicality. It affects which axioms satisfy condition (ii) and therefore which systems are sound. What the definition requires is an explicit specification of the semantic framework within which validity is assessed.

definition: A formal system F is complete if and only if (i) F is consistent — no contradiction is derivable within F, (ii) for every well-formed sentence S of the language of F, either S or its negation is a theorem of F, and (iii) every theorem of F is true in the intended interpretation of F.
conditions: F is consistent
For every sentence S, either S or its negation is a theorem of F
Every theorem of F is true in the intended interpretation
counterexample: Consider Peano arithmetic — the standard formal system for the theory of the natural numbers. Peano arithmetic is consistent (assuming, as is universally believed, that the natural numbers form a consistent structure). Every theorem of Peano arithmetic is true in the intended interpretation — the standard natural numbers. But Goedel's first incompleteness theorem demonstrates that there exist well-formed sentences of the language of Peano arithmetic such that neither the sentence nor its negation is a theorem. Conditions (i) and (iii) are met, but condition (ii) is not. Peano arithmetic is incomplete, and moreover it is provably so: no consistent extension of it that is recursively axiomatisable can be complete. The definition specifies a property that certain important formal systems provably cannot possess.
missing_condition: The definition does not acknowledge the fundamental limitation established by Goedel's incompleteness theorems — that for any sufficiently powerful, consistent, and recursively axiomatisable formal system, completeness in the specified sense is unattainable. A caveat recognising this impossibility for a wide class of formal systems is needed.
passage: One might define completeness of a formal system by requiring that the system be consistent, that for every well-formed sentence either the sentence or its negation be a theorem, and that every theorem be true in the intended interpretation. This definition combines three desirable properties: freedom from contradiction, decisiveness on every question expressible in the language, and fidelity to the intended subject-matter. A complete system, so defined, would settle every question within its domain and would settle it correctly. Such a system would represent the ideal of a formal theory: a perfectly reliable oracle for its intended domain of discourse. Yet one might observe that for the most important class of formal systems — those capable of expressing the arithmetic of the natural numbers — this ideal is provably unattainable. Goedel's first incompleteness theorem demonstrates that for any consistent, recursively axiomatisable system that is capable of expressing a sufficient fragment of arithmetic, there exist well-formed sentences that are true in the intended interpretation but that are not theorems of the system. Condition (ii) fails, and it fails necessarily, not because of a deficiency in any particular axiomatisation but because of a deep structural feature of formal systems themselves. Peano arithmetic is consistent, and every theorem of Peano arithmetic is true of the natural numbers, but there are sentences about the natural numbers that Peano arithmetic can neither prove nor refute. The definition specifies a property that many formal systems of great importance provably cannot possess. This does not render the definition useless — it renders it illuminating, for it identifies a standard against which the expressive limitations of formal systems can be measured.

definition: A self-referential sentence S is paradoxical if and only if (i) S refers to itself — that is, S is about S or about a property of S, (ii) assuming S is true leads, by valid reasoning, to the conclusion that S is false, and (iii) assuming S is false leads, by valid reasoning, to the conclusion that S is true.
conditions: S refers to itself
Assuming S is true leads to the conclusion that S is false
Assuming S is false leads to the conclusion that S is true
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'This sentence has exactly five words.' The sentence refers to itself; it is about a property of itself, namely its word count. But assuming it is true does not lead to the conclusion that it is false, and assuming it is false does not lead to the conclusion that it is true. It is simply false — it has six words — and there is nothing paradoxical about it. Self-reference alone does not produce paradox. Conversely, consider Curry's paradox: 'If this sentence is true, then the moon is made of cheese.' This sentence refers to itself, and assuming it is true leads, by modus ponens, to the conclusion that the moon is made of cheese — an absurdity, but not the conclusion that the sentence is false. The standard oscillation between truth and falsity described in conditions (ii) and (iii) does not occur. Yet Curry's sentence is widely regarded as paradoxical.
missing_condition: The definition is too narrow in requiring the specific oscillation between truth and falsity characteristic of the liar paradox. Other paradoxes of self-reference, such as Curry's paradox, generate absurdity without this oscillation. A broader characterisation of the paradoxical consequence of self-reference is needed.
passage: One might define a paradoxical self-referential sentence as one that refers to itself, is such that assuming it true leads to the conclusion that it is false, and is such that assuming it false leads to the conclusion that it is true. This definition captures the structure of the classical liar paradox with admirable precision. The liar sentence — 'This sentence is false' — refers to itself, yields its own falsity upon the assumption of its truth, and yields its own truth upon the assumption of its falsity. The oscillation between truth and falsity, with no stable resting place, is the hallmark of the liar and the source of its philosophical interest. Yet one might observe that the definition is too closely modelled upon this single paradigm. Not all paradoxes of self-reference exhibit the liar's distinctive oscillation. Consider Curry's paradox: the sentence 'If this sentence is true, then the moon is made of cheese.' This sentence refers to itself. If we assume it is true, then by modus ponens we conclude that the moon is made of cheese — an absurd conclusion, but not the conclusion that the sentence is false. There is no oscillation between truth and falsity; instead, the self-referential structure generates an arbitrary and absurd consequence. Curry's sentence is widely regarded as paradoxical, yet it does not satisfy conditions (ii) and (iii) as stated. The definition, it appears, has been tailored to the liar at the expense of other equally important self-referential paradoxes. What is needed is a broader characterisation of the manner in which self-reference may produce paradoxical consequences — one that accommodates the generation of arbitrary absurdities as well as the oscillation between truth and falsity.

definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A has at least one premiss and a conclusion, (ii) the premisses of A are logically consistent with one another, and (iii) the conclusion of A is logically related to the premisses.
conditions: A has at least one premiss and a conclusion
The premisses of A are logically consistent with one another
The conclusion of A is logically related to the premisses
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'All cats are mammals; all mammals are animals; therefore, some birds can fly.' The argument has premisses and a conclusion. The premisses are logically consistent with one another. And the conclusion is logically related to the premisses in the weak sense that all the terms involved belong to the domain of zoological classification. Yet the argument is plainly invalid, for the conclusion does not follow from the premisses.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the conclusion follow necessarily from the premisses — that it be impossible for the premisses to be true and the conclusion false. The vague requirement of logical relatedness is no substitute for the strict requirement of necessary truth-preservation.
passage: A tempting but inadequate analysis of validity holds that an argument is valid if and only if it has at least one premiss and a conclusion, the premisses are logically consistent with one another, and the conclusion is logically related to the premisses. The first condition is a structural requirement — it ensures that we are dealing with an argument rather than a mere collection of propositions. The second excludes arguments whose premisses are contradictory, which one might think unworthy of assessment. The third requires some connection between premisses and conclusion, ruling out cases of sheer irrelevance. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is not far to seek. The argument 'All cats are mammals; all mammals are animals; therefore, some birds can fly' satisfies all three conditions: it has premisses and a conclusion, the premisses are consistent, and the conclusion bears a loose logical relation to the premisses inasmuch as all the terms belong to a common domain. But the argument is plainly invalid. What is missing is the requirement that the conclusion follow necessarily from the premisses — that there be no possible circumstance in which the premisses are true and the conclusion false.

definition: An argument A is sound if and only if (i) A is valid, (ii) each premiss of A is believed by the person advancing the argument, and (iii) the conclusion of A is true.
conditions: A is valid
Each premiss of A is believed by the person advancing the argument
The conclusion of A is true
counterexample: Consider a logician who, for pedagogical purposes, advances a valid argument from premisses she knows to be false, and whose conclusion happens by coincidence to be true. The argument is valid, she believes (or at least asserts) the premisses for the sake of illustration, and the conclusion is true. Yet we should not call the argument sound, for soundness requires that the premisses actually be true, not merely that the arguer believes them.
missing_condition: The definition substitutes belief in the premisses for their truth. Soundness requires not that the premisses be believed but that they be true. The second condition must be replaced by a requirement of the actual truth of each premiss.
passage: One might define a sound argument as one that is valid, whose premisses are believed by the person advancing the argument, and whose conclusion is true. The first condition ensures the proper logical structure — the conclusion must follow from the premisses. The second condition is intended to ensure that the argument is advanced in good faith. The third condition requires that the argument reach a true conclusion. Yet this definition conflates two quite different things: the sincerity of the arguer and the actual truth of what is argued. Consider a logician who, in the course of a lecture, advances a valid argument from premisses she knows to be false, for the purpose of illustrating a point about logical form. Suppose that, by coincidence, the conclusion happens to be true. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied — the argument is valid, the premisses are in some sense endorsed by the lecturer in the context of her presentation, and the conclusion is true. Yet the argument is not sound, for its premisses are false. Soundness, properly understood, requires not that the premisses be believed but that they be true. The definition has confused a psychological condition with a semantic one.

definition: A proposition Q is a logical consequence of a set of propositions S if and only if (i) Q can be derived from S by a sequence of valid inference steps, (ii) each inference step employs only the standard rules of classical logic, and (iii) the derivation contains no redundant steps.
conditions: Q can be derived from S by a sequence of valid inference steps
Each inference step employs only the standard rules of classical logic
The derivation contains no redundant steps
counterexample: Consider a derivation of Q from S that proceeds correctly through five inference steps, but could have been accomplished in three. On the proposed definition, if a logician presents the longer derivation, which contains two redundant steps, one would have to deny that Q is a logical consequence of S as demonstrated by that derivation. Yet logical consequence is a relation between propositions, not a property of particular derivations, and the presence of redundancy in a proof is irrelevant to whether the conclusion follows from the premisses.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly includes a condition pertaining to the elegance or economy of the derivation. Logical consequence is a semantic relation that holds or fails to hold independently of how — or how efficiently — the derivation is conducted. The third condition must be removed or replaced by a condition concerning the impossibility of the premisses being true while the conclusion is false.
passage: It might be supposed that a proposition is a logical consequence of a set of propositions if and only if it can be derived from that set by a sequence of valid inference steps, each step employs only the standard rules of classical logic, and the derivation contains no redundant steps. The first two conditions are unexceptionable: they characterise the syntactic notion of derivability in a standard system. The third condition, however, introduces a requirement that has nothing to do with logical consequence as such. Consider a perfectly correct derivation of Q from S that proceeds through five steps where three would have sufficed. The additional steps, though unnecessary, introduce no error. On the proposed definition, we should have to say that Q is not a logical consequence of S as demonstrated by this derivation, since it contains redundant steps. This is absurd. Logical consequence is a relation between a set of propositions and a proposition; it does not depend upon the elegance or parsimony of any particular proof. A derivation may be inelegant, circuitous, or verbose, and yet demonstrate genuine consequence. The third condition confuses a desideratum of good proof-writing with a requirement of the consequence relation itself.

definition: A set of propositions constitutes a paradox if and only if (i) each proposition in the set appears, taken individually, to be true, (ii) the propositions are jointly inconsistent, and (iii) no proposition in the set is ambiguous.
conditions: Each proposition in the set appears, taken individually, to be true
The propositions are jointly inconsistent
No proposition in the set is ambiguous
counterexample: Consider the Sorites paradox. Its premisses include a proposition such as 'If a man with n hairs is bald, then a man with n+1 hairs is bald,' which is arguably ambiguous — the predicate 'bald' admits of borderline cases, and the premiss exploits this vagueness. On the proposed definition, the Sorites would fail to qualify as a paradox because it contains an ambiguous proposition. Yet the Sorites is universally regarded as a paradigmatic paradox.
missing_condition: The definition errs in requiring that no proposition in the set be ambiguous. Many of the most important paradoxes — including Sorites-type paradoxes — trade precisely upon ambiguity or vagueness, and excluding such cases from the class of paradoxes renders the definition too restrictive. The third condition should be dropped or replaced.
passage: One might define a paradox as a set of propositions each of which appears individually to be true, which are jointly inconsistent, and none of which is ambiguous. The first two conditions capture the essential tension that characterises a paradox: we have a collection of seemingly true claims that cannot all be true together. The third condition is presumably intended to exclude cases in which the appearance of paradox dissolves once an ambiguity is detected. Yet this third condition is too strong. Consider the Sorites paradox, in which we begin with the plausible premiss that removing a single grain of sand from a heap still leaves a heap, and iterate this premiss until we are forced to conclude that a single grain of sand is a heap. The premiss arguably trades upon the vagueness of the term 'heap,' and so contains a measure of ambiguity. On the proposed definition, the Sorites would not count as a paradox. But the Sorites is universally recognised as one of the oldest and most instructive paradoxes in philosophy. The definition, by excluding paradoxes that depend upon ambiguity or vagueness, fails to capture a large and important class of cases.

definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) it is not possible for P to be true and Q false, (ii) there is a conceptual connection between the content of P and the content of Q, and (iii) the truth of P provides a reason for asserting Q.
conditions: It is not possible for P to be true and Q false
There is a conceptual connection between the content of P and the content of Q
The truth of P provides a reason for asserting Q
counterexample: Consider the proposition P: 'It is raining and 2+2=4.' P entails Q: '2+2=4,' since it is not possible for P to be true and Q false — Q is a conjunct of P. Yet the connection between the meteorological component of P and the arithmetical content of Q is purely formal; there is no conceptual connection between rain and arithmetic. One might argue that the conjunctive structure supplies a formal link, but the definition seems to demand a connection of content, which is absent between these heterogeneous subject-matters.
missing_condition: The definition's requirement of a conceptual connection between the contents of P and Q is too restrictive. Entailment is a formal relation that can hold between propositions with entirely disparate subject-matters, as when a conjunction entails its conjuncts. The second condition must be dropped or reformulated to accommodate purely formal entailments.
passage: A natural proposal is that a proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if it is not possible for P to be true and Q false, there is a conceptual connection between the content of P and the content of Q, and the truth of P provides a reason for asserting Q. The first condition is the standard modal characterisation of entailment. The second is meant to exclude so-called paradoxes of entailment — cases in which a necessary truth is entailed by every proposition, despite there being no connection of content. The third adds a pragmatic dimension, tying entailment to the practice of assertion. Yet the second condition proves too restrictive. Consider the proposition 'It is raining and two plus two equals four.' This proposition entails 'two plus two equals four,' since the latter is a conjunct of the former, and it is impossible for a conjunction to be true while one of its conjuncts is false. But there is no conceptual connection between rain and arithmetic. The entailment holds purely in virtue of the logical form of conjunction, not in virtue of any relation between the subject-matters involved. The definition, by insisting upon a conceptual connection of content, excludes genuine entailments that proceed from logical form alone.

definition: A set of propositions S is consistent if and only if (i) no proposition in S is self-contradictory, (ii) no two propositions in S are direct negations of one another, and (iii) the propositions in S can all be entertained by a single rational agent without discomfort.
conditions: No proposition in S is self-contradictory
No two propositions in S are direct negations of one another
The propositions in S can all be entertained by a single rational agent without discomfort
counterexample: Consider the set {All roses are red, All red things are fragrant, No roses are fragrant}. No proposition in this set is self-contradictory. No two propositions are direct negations of one another. And a rational agent might initially entertain all three without discomfort, since the inconsistency only emerges when one draws the intermediate conclusion that all roses are fragrant and compares it with the third proposition. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the set is logically inconsistent.
missing_condition: The definition fails to capture inconsistencies that arise only through the logical interrelations among three or more propositions, none of which directly contradicts any other. A condition requiring that there be no assignment of truth-values under which all propositions in S are simultaneously true is needed.
passage: It might be proposed that a set of propositions is consistent if and only if no proposition in the set is self-contradictory, no two propositions are direct negations of one another, and the propositions can all be entertained by a single rational agent without discomfort. The first two conditions rule out the most obvious sources of inconsistency: internal contradiction within a single proposition and overt contradiction between pairs. The third adds a psychological gloss, the thought being that a rational agent would feel discomfort upon entertaining an inconsistent set. Yet this analysis is plainly insufficient. Consider the set consisting of the following three propositions: all roses are red; all red things are fragrant; no roses are fragrant. No single proposition here is self-contradictory. No two are direct negations — 'all roses are red' is not the negation of 'no roses are fragrant.' And a rational agent might well entertain all three without immediately perceiving any tension, for the inconsistency becomes apparent only when one draws the intermediate conclusion that all roses are fragrant and notes its conflict with the third proposition. All three conditions of the definition are met, yet the set is inconsistent. The definition must be strengthened to require that there exist a possible circumstance in which all propositions of the set are true together.

definition: A concept C is definable within a formal language L if and only if (i) there exists a formula F in L such that F applies to all and only the instances of C, (ii) F is finitely expressible, and (iii) F contains no undefined primitive terms.
conditions: There exists a formula F in L such that F applies to all and only the instances of C
F is finitely expressible
F contains no undefined primitive terms
counterexample: Consider the concept of a well-ordering on the natural numbers. In first-order arithmetic, one might attempt to define this concept by a finite formula containing only previously defined terms. Yet it is well known that first-order arithmetic cannot categorically distinguish between standard and non-standard models, and hence cannot capture the concept of well-ordering in the requisite sense. A formula satisfying all three conditions may exist and yet fail to define the concept in any model of the language, because the formula's extension in non-standard models includes orderings that are not well-orderings.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the formula F capture the concept C not merely in the intended model but across all models of the language. A condition requiring that the definability be robust across model-theoretic interpretations is needed if the definition is to reflect the standard logical notion.
passage: One might propose that a concept is definable within a formal language if and only if there exists a formula in that language which applies to all and only the instances of the concept, the formula is finitely expressible, and it contains no undefined primitive terms. This appears to be a reasonable explication of the intuitive idea of definability: we want a linguistic expression that picks out exactly the right extension, is finitely statable, and does not rely upon terms that have not themselves been given a meaning within the language. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account arises in the context of first-order arithmetic. The concept of a well-ordering on the natural numbers is one that we understand clearly and can characterise in second-order logic. In first-order arithmetic, however, any formula that attempts to capture this concept will have models in which the formula's extension includes orderings that are not well-orderings — non-standard models, as they are called, in which the formula applies to structures the concept was not meant to include. All three conditions of the definition may be satisfied by such a formula, and yet we should hesitate to say that the concept has been defined, for the formula fails to track the concept across all models. The definition must incorporate a model-theoretic condition requiring that the formula capture the concept robustly.

definition: An inference from P to Q is valid if and only if (i) every competent logician would accept the inference, (ii) the inference conforms to a recognised rule of some logical system, and (iii) no one has produced a case in which P is true and Q is false.
conditions: Every competent logician would accept the inference
The inference conforms to a recognised rule of some logical system
No one has produced a case in which P is true and Q is false
counterexample: Consider an inference that conforms to a rule in a logical system that has been widely accepted but in which a subtle error has not yet been detected. Every competent logician accepts the inference, it conforms to a recognised rule, and no one has yet produced a case in which the premiss is true and the conclusion false — perhaps because the case is extremely complex and has not been investigated. All three conditions are satisfied, yet if the system contains an error, the inference may be invalid despite meeting every condition of the definition.
missing_condition: The definition grounds validity in the sociological facts of what logicians accept, what systems are recognised, and what cases have been discovered, rather than in the objective impossibility of the premiss being true and the conclusion false. Validity is not hostage to the current state of logical investigation; it must be defined in terms of what is the case, not in terms of what has been found to be the case.
passage: It might be suggested that an inference from P to Q is valid if and only if every competent logician would accept it, it conforms to a recognised rule of some logical system, and no one has produced a case in which P is true and Q is false. This definition has an empiricist appeal: it ties validity to the actual practice of the logical community. The first condition invokes a consensus criterion, the second grounds the inference in a codified system, and the third demands the absence of known cases of truth-preservation failure. Yet the definition is unsatisfactory, for it makes validity a contingent and revisable matter. Consider an inference rule in a logical system that has enjoyed wide acceptance for decades. Every competent logician endorses inferences conforming to it, and no one has found a falsifying instance. But suppose the system harbours a subtle error — an error that has simply not yet been detected because the relevant cases are fiendishly complex. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied, and yet the inference is invalid. Validity cannot depend upon what has happened to be discovered or upon the current consensus of practitioners; it must depend upon what is objectively the case — upon the impossibility, not the mere absence, of a premiss being true while the conclusion is false.

definition: P entails Q if and only if (i) the conditional 'if P then Q' is a logical truth, (ii) P and Q share at least one non-logical term, and (iii) Q is not itself a logical truth.
conditions: The conditional 'if P then Q' is a logical truth
P and Q share at least one non-logical term
Q is not itself a logical truth
counterexample: Consider any logical truth T — say, 'Either it is raining or it is not raining.' The proposition 'It is raining' entails T, since the conditional 'If it is raining, then either it is raining or it is not raining' is a logical truth, and the two propositions share the non-logical term 'it is raining.' But T is a logical truth, so condition (iii) fails. On the proposed definition, P does not entail T. Yet it is a standard result in logic that any proposition entails every logical truth.
missing_condition: The definition's third condition wrongly excludes logical truths from the class of propositions that can be entailed. Since every logical truth is entailed by every proposition, the requirement that Q not be a logical truth must be removed.
passage: One proposal for analysing entailment holds that P entails Q if and only if the conditional 'if P then Q' is a logical truth, P and Q share at least one non-logical term, and Q is not itself a logical truth. The first condition provides the modal force of entailment: a logical truth is true in every interpretation. The second condition is a relevance requirement designed to block the so-called paradoxes of material implication, in which any proposition entails any necessary truth regardless of subject-matter. The third condition is intended to exclude trivial cases in which Q's truth owes nothing to P. Yet the third condition is too restrictive. Consider any logical truth T — say, the law of excluded middle applied to some particular proposition. It is a standard and well-established result that every proposition entails every logical truth: if T is true in every interpretation, then a fortiori it is true in every interpretation in which P is true. The conditional 'if P then T' is therefore a logical truth, and if P and T share a non-logical term, conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. But condition (iii) excludes T from the class of propositions that can be entailed. This is an unacceptable restriction. The definition must be revised to accommodate the entailment of logical truths.

definition: A formal theory T is consistent if and only if (i) T has been formalised in a well-defined language, (ii) no proof of a contradiction has been found within T, and (iii) T has been applied successfully in practice without yielding absurd results.
conditions: T has been formalised in a well-defined language
No proof of a contradiction has been found within T
T has been applied successfully in practice without yielding absurd results
counterexample: Consider naive set theory as formalised by Frege. For some years after its formalisation, no proof of a contradiction had been found, and the theory was applied with apparent success in the foundations of arithmetic. All three conditions were satisfied. Yet naive set theory was inconsistent, as Russell's discovery of the paradox bearing his name demonstrated. The absence of a known contradiction is not the same as the non-existence of a contradiction.
missing_condition: The definition conflates the absence of a discovered contradiction with the absence of a contradiction. Consistency requires that no contradiction be derivable, not merely that none has yet been derived. The second condition must be strengthened from an empirical claim about the current state of proof-theoretic investigation to a claim about what is in principle derivable.
passage: It is sometimes supposed that a formal theory is consistent if and only if it has been formalised in a well-defined language, no proof of a contradiction has been found within it, and it has been applied successfully in practice without yielding absurd results. The first condition is a prerequisite — one cannot meaningfully assess the consistency of a theory that has not been given a precise formulation. The second and third conditions appeal to the evidence of experience: a theory that has resisted all attempts to derive a contradiction, and that has proved serviceable in application, is presumed to be consistent. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the history of naive set theory. Frege's Grundgesetze formalised set theory in a well-defined language. For several years, no proof of a contradiction was found. And the theory was applied with considerable success in the foundations of arithmetic. All three conditions were satisfied. But naive set theory was inconsistent, as Russell demonstrated by deriving the contradiction now known as Russell's paradox. The lesson is clear: consistency is not an empirical property that can be established by the absence of discovered contradictions or by successful application. It is a logical property — the non-derivability of any contradiction — and the definition must reflect this.

definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) the premises of A are propositions expressed in a well-formed language, (ii) the conclusion of A follows from the premises by the application of recognised rules of inference, and (iii) no step in the derivation introduces an assumption not contained in the premises.
conditions: The premises are propositions expressed in a well-formed language
The conclusion follows by recognised rules of inference
No step introduces an assumption not contained in the premises
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'All mammals are warm-blooded; all whales are mammals; therefore all whales are warm-blooded.' This argument is valid by any standard. Yet consider a second argument whose conclusion follows from its premises by a rule of inference not yet recognised — perhaps a novel rule proposed in a newly developed logical system. The argument may be truth-preserving in every possible case, and yet the second condition would exclude it from the class of valid arguments simply because the rule is not yet 'recognised.'
missing_condition: The definition ties validity to the use of recognised rules of inference, but validity is a semantic property — the impossibility of true premises and a false conclusion — which may obtain even when the derivation employs rules not yet codified or recognised.
passage: It is common to define validity by requiring that the conclusion follow from the premises through recognised rules of inference without the introduction of extraneous assumptions. This definition captures the practice of formal proof in established logical systems. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider the relationship between syntactic derivability and semantic consequence. Consider an argument whose conclusion follows from its premises by a rule of inference that is truth-preserving in every possible interpretation but that has not yet been recognised or codified. Perhaps the rule belongs to a logical system that has only recently been proposed, or perhaps it captures a pattern of reasoning that logicians have not yet formalised. The argument is such that there is no possible situation in which the premises are all true and the conclusion false. By the semantic criterion, the argument is valid. Yet the definition excludes it, because the rule of inference is not among the recognised rules. The definition thus confuses validity — which is fundamentally a matter of the preservation of truth across all possible situations — with provability in a particular formal system. A valid argument is one in which the truth of the premises guarantees the truth of the conclusion, regardless of whether the derivation employs rules that happen to be catalogued in the textbooks.

definition: An argument A is sound if and only if (i) A is valid, (ii) all premises of A are true, and (iii) the premises of A are mutually independent — that is, no premise is derivable from the others.
conditions: A is valid
All premises of A are true
The premises are mutually independent
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'All men are mortal; Socrates is a man; all men are mortal and Socrates is a man; therefore Socrates is mortal.' The argument is valid, and all its premises are true. But the third premise is merely the conjunction of the first two and is thus derivable from them, violating the independence condition. Yet the argument is manifestly sound — its conclusion follows necessarily from true premises.
missing_condition: The definition introduces a condition of mutual independence among premises that is not required for soundness as traditionally understood; an argument with redundant premises may be inelegant but is not thereby unsound.
passage: One might define a sound argument as one that is valid, has true premises, and whose premises are mutually independent. The independence condition is intended to exclude arguments burdened with redundant premises — a desideratum of logical economy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition as an analysis of soundness is easily constructed. Consider the following argument: all men are mortal; Socrates is a man; all men are mortal and Socrates is a man; therefore Socrates is mortal. The argument is valid — the conclusion follows from the premises. All three premises are true. Yet the third premise is simply the conjunction of the first two and is therefore derivable from them; it is not independent. The independence condition is violated. But it would be eccentric to deny that this argument is sound. The conclusion follows necessarily from true premises, which is all that soundness requires. The addition of a redundant premise may offend against elegance but it does not impair truth-preservation. The definition confuses a methodological preference for parsimony with a necessary condition for soundness.

definition: A proposition Q is a logical consequence of a set of propositions P1, ..., Pn if and only if (i) Q is derivable from P1, ..., Pn by a finite sequence of steps, (ii) each step employs a rule of inference that is truth-preserving, and (iii) the derivation is constructive — that is, each step produces a determinate proposition.
conditions: Q is derivable from the premises by a finite sequence of steps
Each step employs a truth-preserving rule of inference
The derivation is constructive in that each step produces a determinate proposition
counterexample: Consider a classical proof by reductio ad absurdum. One assumes the negation of the desired conclusion, derives a contradiction, and discharges the assumption to obtain the conclusion. The conclusion is a logical consequence of the premises by classical standards. Yet the derivation is not constructive in the relevant sense: the conclusion is obtained not by directly producing it but by showing that its negation leads to absurdity. The third condition is not met according to any strict reading of 'constructive,' and yet the conclusion is a logical consequence by the lights of classical logic.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a constructivist constraint on logical consequence that excludes classical indirect proofs, thereby confusing a particular philosophical stance on the nature of proof with the concept of consequence itself.
passage: One might propose that logical consequence consists in derivability through a finite sequence of truth-preserving steps, each producing a determinate proposition in a constructive fashion. This proposal reflects an important tradition in the philosophy of logic — one that insists upon the directness and transparency of deductive reasoning. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises from the widespread use of indirect proof in classical logic. Consider a proof by reductio ad absurdum: one assumes the negation of the desired conclusion, derives a contradiction from that assumption together with the given premises, and discharges the assumption to obtain the conclusion. This method of proof is accepted throughout classical mathematics and logic. The conclusion so obtained is regarded as a logical consequence of the premises by any classical standard. Yet the derivation is not constructive in the required sense. The conclusion is not directly produced; rather, its negation is shown to be untenable. If we insist upon a constructive derivation, we must deny that the conclusion of a classical reductio is a logical consequence of its premises, which is at odds with the practice of virtually every working mathematician. The definition confuses a debatable philosophical restriction on the nature of proof with the concept of logical consequence, which is broader than any particular proof method.

definition: A set of propositions constitutes a paradox if and only if (i) each proposition in the set is individually plausible, (ii) the propositions are jointly inconsistent, and (iii) there is no obvious member of the set whose rejection would resolve the inconsistency.
conditions: Each proposition is individually plausible
The propositions are jointly inconsistent
No obvious member can be rejected to resolve the inconsistency
counterexample: Consider the sorites paradox, in which the propositions are: a man with no hairs is bald; the addition of a single hair does not convert a bald man into a non-bald man; a man with a hundred thousand hairs is not bald. Each proposition is individually plausible, and the propositions are jointly inconsistent. Yet many philosophers would say that the second proposition — the tolerance principle — is the obvious candidate for rejection. It is quite clear which proposition must give way; the difficulty lies not in identifying it but in explaining why it appears so plausible. The third condition is not met, and yet the sorites is universally acknowledged as a genuine paradox.
missing_condition: The definition requires that no obvious resolution be available, but this excludes cases where the paradoxical character lies not in the intractability of the inconsistency but in the depth of the illusion that sustains the offending premise.
passage: A standard analysis of paradox requires individually plausible propositions that are jointly inconsistent, with no obvious candidate for rejection. This analysis fits the classic antinomies of set theory rather well: each axiom appears plausible, the set is inconsistent, and it is far from clear which axiom to abandon. Yet the analysis encounters difficulty with another paradigm case. Consider the sorites paradox. A heap of sand is still a heap if one grain is removed; a single grain is not a heap; a collection of a million grains is a heap. Each proposition is plausible, and the propositions are jointly inconsistent. Yet it appears that the tolerance principle — that the removal of a single grain cannot convert a heap into a non-heap — is the obvious candidate for rejection. Most philosophers agree that this principle must ultimately be given up, however difficult it may be to say precisely where the boundary lies. The third condition is therefore not met. Nevertheless, the sorites is universally regarded as a genuine paradox, and rightly so, for the difficulty lies not in identifying the offending premise but in explaining why it commands such powerful intuitive assent. The definition's insistence that no obvious resolution be available confuses the sense of paradox — the felt tension between plausible commitments — with the intractability of its resolution.

definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) it is impossible for P to be true and Q false, (ii) there is a relevant connection of meaning or content between P and Q, and (iii) the entailment can be established by purely logical means, without appeal to empirical fact.
conditions: It is impossible for P to be true and Q false
There is a relevant connection of meaning between P and Q
The entailment is establishable by purely logical means
counterexample: Consider the proposition that water is H2O and the proposition that water contains hydrogen. The former entails the latter: it is impossible for water to be H2O without containing hydrogen. There is a relevant connection of content between the two propositions. Yet the entailment cannot be established by purely logical means alone; it depends upon the empirical discovery that H2O is composed of hydrogen and oxygen atoms. The third condition is not met, and yet the entailment holds.
missing_condition: The definition restricts entailment to relations establishable by purely logical means, thereby excluding cases where the entailment depends upon necessary truths that are discoverable only a posteriori.
passage: One might define entailment as the impossibility of true premises and a false conclusion, combined with a relevant connection of meaning and the requirement that the relation be establishable by purely logical means. This definition is intended to capture the intuition that entailment is a matter of logical structure rather than empirical happenstance. The relevance condition excludes the paradoxes of strict implication, and the restriction to logical means excludes entailments that depend upon contingent facts. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises from the class of necessary a posteriori truths. Consider the proposition that water is H2O. This proposition, if true, is necessarily true — there is no possible world in which water is not H2O. It follows that water contains hydrogen: it is impossible for water to be H2O and not contain hydrogen. There is a clear connection of content between the two propositions. Yet the entailment cannot be established by purely logical means. One must know, as a matter of empirical discovery, that the molecule designated by the formula H2O contains hydrogen atoms. The entailment depends upon a necessary truth that is discoverable only through experience. The definition, in restricting entailment to what can be established by logic alone, excludes a large and important class of entailments that depend upon the necessary identities uncovered by empirical science.

definition: A set of propositions S is consistent if and only if (i) no contradiction can be derived from S by the application of the rules of classical logic, (ii) each member of S is individually free of internal contradiction, and (iii) S is satisfiable — that is, there exists an interpretation under which all members of S are true.
conditions: No contradiction is derivable from S by the rules of classical logic
Each member of S is individually consistent
S is satisfiable
counterexample: Consider a set of propositions formulated within an intuitionistic or paraconsistent logical framework, where the rules of classical logic do not all hold. There may be a set S such that under the deviant logic no contradiction is derivable and S is satisfiable within the intended non-classical semantics, yet under classical logic a contradiction would be derivable. The definition declares this set inconsistent because condition (i) refers specifically to classical logic, whereas the proponents of the alternative framework regard it as perfectly consistent.
missing_condition: The definition relativises consistency to the rules of classical logic, but fails to recognise that consistency is relative to the logical framework in which the propositions are evaluated, and that a set may be consistent under one logic while inconsistent under another.
passage: A familiar account of consistency holds that a set of propositions is consistent when no contradiction is derivable from it by classical rules, each member is internally free of contradiction, and the set is satisfiable. This account serves well within the confines of classical logic. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider propositions formulated within alternative logical frameworks. Suppose a set of propositions is advanced within a paraconsistent logic — a logic designed to tolerate certain contradictions without collapsing into triviality. Within this framework, the set is taken to be consistent: no triviality results, and the propositions are satisfiable under the intended semantics. Yet under classical logic, a contradiction would be derivable from the very same set. The definition, by tying consistency to the rules of classical logic, declares the set inconsistent — a verdict that the paraconsistent logician would firmly reject. What the definition lacks is the recognition that consistency is relative to a logical framework. A set of propositions may be consistent relative to one system of rules and inconsistent relative to another, and the question of which framework is the correct one is not settled by stipulating that classical logic is authoritative.

definition: A concept C is definable within a formal language L if and only if (i) there exists a formula F in L such that F applies to all and only the instances of C, (ii) F is finitely expressible, and (iii) F employs only primitive terms and logical constants of L.
conditions: There exists a formula in L applying to all and only instances of C
The formula is finitely expressible
The formula employs only primitive terms and logical constants of L
counterexample: Consider the concept of a well-ordering of the natural numbers. In first-order arithmetic, no finitely expressible formula employing only the primitive terms and logical constants of the language captures all and only the well-orderings, because the concept of well-ordering requires quantification over subsets, which is not available in first-order logic. Yet the concept is perfectly definable in second-order logic. The concept is definable — but not within the language L specified. The definition conflates the definability of a concept with its definability in a particular formal language.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between absolute indefinability and indefinability relative to a particular formal language, giving the misleading impression that a concept which escapes formulation in one language is thereby indefinable simpliciter.
passage: One might propose that a concept is definable within a formal language when it can be captured by a finitely expressible formula employing only the language's primitives and logical constants. This account is faithful to the practice of formal definition in mathematical logic: one specifies the language, identifies its primitives, and seeks a formula that applies to all and only the relevant instances. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis emerges from the well-known limitations of first-order languages. Consider the concept of a well-ordering of the natural numbers — that is, an ordering in which every non-empty subset has a least element. In first-order arithmetic, no finitely expressible formula captures this concept, because the language lacks the resources to quantify over arbitrary subsets of the natural numbers. By the lights of the definition, the concept is not definable. Yet the concept is perfectly definable in second-order logic, which permits quantification over sets. The concept is thus definable in one language and not in another. The definition obscures this by speaking of definability within a language without making clear that the verdict is relative to the expressive resources of the language chosen. What is needed is a recognition that definability is not an absolute property of concepts but a relational property that obtains between a concept and a formal language.

definition: A modal argument is valid if and only if (i) the premises and conclusion are well-formed formulae of a modal language, (ii) there is no interpretation in which the premises are all true and the conclusion false, and (iii) the modal operators in the argument are interpreted according to the standard Kripke semantics with the S5 accessibility relation.
conditions: Premises and conclusion are well-formed modal formulae
No interpretation makes all premises true and the conclusion false
Modal operators are interpreted via S5 Kripke semantics
counterexample: Consider a modal argument concerning the deontic notion of obligation, where the box operator is read as 'it is obligatory that.' The argument may be truth-preserving under a serial but non-euclidean accessibility relation — that is, under deontic semantics. Yet the argument would fail under S5, which validates principles inappropriate for deontic logic. The definition declares the argument invalid because it fails under S5, even though it is valid under the correct semantics for the modal notions it employs.
missing_condition: The definition fixes the accessibility relation at S5 for all modal arguments, but fails to recognise that different modal notions — necessity-related, deontic, temporal — require different accessibility relations, and that validity must be assessed relative to the appropriate semantics for the modal operators in play.
passage: One might define the validity of a modal argument by requiring well-formedness, the absence of any falsifying interpretation, and the use of S5 Kripke semantics. S5 is a natural choice for arguments about metaphysical necessity and possibility, since its accessibility relation — universally reflexive, symmetric, and transitive — captures the intuition that what is possibly necessary is necessary. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we turn to other species of modality. Consider an argument involving the deontic notion of obligation: from the premises that it is obligatory to keep one's promises and that one has promised to attend a meeting, it is concluded that it is obligatory to attend the meeting. The argument may well be truth-preserving under the appropriate deontic semantics, which employs a serial but not necessarily euclidean accessibility relation. Under S5, however, additional principles are validated — such as the thesis that what is possibly obligatory is obligatory — which are inappropriate for deontic reasoning. An argument that is valid under deontic semantics may fail under S5, and the definition would declare it invalid. The error lies in fixing the accessibility relation once and for all, when the correct semantics depends upon the species of modality in question. Necessity-related, deontic, temporal, and other modalities each require their own accessibility conditions.

definition: A self-referential sentence S is paradoxical if and only if (i) S refers to itself, (ii) the assumption that S is true leads to the conclusion that S is false, and (iii) the assumption that S is false leads to the conclusion that S is true.
conditions: S refers to itself
Assuming S true yields S false
Assuming S false yields S true
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'This sentence is not provable.' If it is true, then it is not provable, which is consistent — the sentence is true but unprovable. If it is false, then it is provable, and since provable sentences are presumably true, it is true after all, which yields a contradiction only on one direction. The sentence is self-referential and generates deep logical difficulties, yet it does not produce a symmetric oscillation between truth and falsity. Condition (ii) does not hold — the assumption of truth does not yield falsity. Yet the sentence is widely regarded as paradoxical or at least as the source of a profound logical puzzle.
missing_condition: The definition requires a symmetric oscillation between truth and falsity, but fails to accommodate sentences that generate paradoxical or logically intractable consequences through asymmetric self-reference — cases where only one direction of the biconditional yields a problematic conclusion.
passage: A familiar characterisation of self-referential paradoxes holds that a sentence is paradoxical if it refers to itself and generates a symmetric oscillation: assuming it true yields it false, and assuming it false yields it true. This characterisation fits the Liar Paradox admirably. 'This sentence is false' is true if and only if it is false, producing the classic oscillation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is provided by sentences of the kind studied by Goedel. Consider the sentence 'This sentence is not provable in the present formal system.' If the sentence is true, then it is not provable — which is entirely consistent. There is no passage from truth to falsity; the sentence may simply be a true but unprovable proposition. If the sentence is false, then it is provable, and since we expect provable sentences to be true, we obtain a contradiction — but only in this one direction. The oscillation is not symmetric. Condition (ii) is not met: the assumption of truth does not yield falsity. Yet this sentence has generated one of the deepest results in the history of logic and is widely regarded as paradoxical in character. The definition's insistence on symmetric oscillation excludes a significant class of self-referential sentences that are logically problematic in ways that do not conform to the Liar pattern.

definition: A set of premises P1, ..., Pn jointly entail a conclusion Q if and only if (i) the conjunction of P1, ..., Pn is logically incompatible with the negation of Q, (ii) each premise contributes to the derivation of Q — that is, the removal of any premise would render Q underivable, and (iii) the entailment holds in virtue of the logical form of the propositions involved.
conditions: The conjunction of the premises is logically incompatible with the negation of Q
Each premise is essential to the derivation — removal of any one renders Q underivable
The entailment holds in virtue of logical form
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'All Greeks are mortal; all Spartans are Greek; all Athenians are Greek; therefore all Spartans are mortal.' The conjunction of the premises is incompatible with the negation of the conclusion, and the entailment holds in virtue of logical form. But the third premise — that all Athenians are Greek — is not needed for the derivation. Its removal does not render the conclusion underivable. The second condition is violated, yet the premises plainly entail the conclusion.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a minimality condition — that every premise be essential — which is not a requirement of entailment but of what might be called efficient or non-redundant proof.
passage: One might propose that a set of premises jointly entails a conclusion when the premises are logically incompatible with the conclusion's negation, each premise is essential to the derivation, and the entailment holds in virtue of logical form. The essentiality condition is intended to exclude cases in which irrelevant propositions are smuggled into the premise-set. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this requirement is easily constructed. Consider the argument whose premises are: all Greeks are mortal; all Spartans are Greek; all Athenians are Greek. The conclusion is that all Spartans are mortal. The conjunction of the premises is logically incompatible with the negation of the conclusion, and the entailment holds in virtue of the logical form of the universal categorical propositions involved. But the third premise — that all Athenians are Greek — plays no role in the derivation. Remove it, and the conclusion follows just as before from the remaining two premises. The second condition is violated. Yet the three premises, taken together, certainly entail the conclusion. The presence of a redundant premise does not destroy the entailment; it merely renders the argument inelegant. The definition confuses entailment with what one might call non-redundant proof, imposing a parsimony constraint that has no bearing on whether the truth of the premises guarantees the truth of the conclusion.

definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A consists of a set of premisses and a conclusion, (ii) the conclusion follows from the premisses by the application of recognised rules of inference, and (iii) the premisses are consistent with one another.
conditions: A consists of premisses and a conclusion
The conclusion follows by recognised rules of inference
The premisses are consistent
