counterexample: Consider the argument: 'All mammals are warm-blooded; all dogs are mammals; therefore all dogs are warm-blooded.' This is valid by any standard. Now consider the argument: 'Either it is raining or it is not raining; it is not the case that it is both raining and not raining; therefore the moon is made of cheese.' The premisses are consistent (indeed, they are logical truths), but the conclusion does not follow by any recognised rule of inference. Yet in classical logic, any argument whose premisses are tautologies and whose conclusion is any proposition whatsoever can be shown to be truth-preserving, since the premisses are true in every valuation. The definition's reliance on 'recognised rules of inference' rather than on truth-preservation produces a notion of validity that is narrower than the logical concept it purports to capture.
missing_condition: The definition identifies validity with derivability by recognised rules rather than with the impossibility of true premisses and a false conclusion, and thereby fails to capture arguments that are valid in the semantic sense but not derivable by any of the rules the practitioner happens to recognise.
passage: One might propose that an argument is valid when it consists of premisses and a conclusion, the conclusion follows from the premisses by recognised rules of inference, and the premisses are consistent. This proposal has an attractive simplicity: it ties validity to the rules of inference that logicians actually employ and adds the sensible requirement that the premisses not contradict one another. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises from the gap between derivability and truth-preservation. Suppose one is given an argument whose premisses are logical truths — tautologies that hold in every possible valuation. In classical logic, any proposition whatever follows from a set of tautological premisses, because there is no valuation in which the premisses are true and the conclusion false. Such an argument is valid in the semantic sense — it is truth-preserving — even if the conclusion has no apparent connection to the premisses and no recognised rule of inference links them. The definition, by requiring that the conclusion follow by recognised rules, makes validity depend upon which rules happen to have been formulated and accepted, rather than upon the logical relationship between premisses and conclusion. One might observe that the semantic notion of validity — the impossibility of true premisses and a false conclusion — is the more fundamental concept, and that recognised rules of inference are merely the devices by which we attempt to capture it, not the thing itself.

definition: A conclusion C is deductively established from a set of premisses P if and only if (i) every premiss in P is explicitly stated, (ii) C follows from P by a finite chain of inference steps, and (iii) each step in the chain is an application of a formally specified rule.
conditions: Every premiss is explicitly stated
C follows by a finite chain of inference steps
Each step applies a formally specified rule
counterexample: Consider a proof in elementary arithmetic: from the Peano axioms, one derives that 2 + 3 = 5. The derivation is finite and each step applies a formally specified rule. But the derivation tacitly relies upon the principle of mathematical induction as a rule of inference, and in many presentations this principle is not stated as a premiss but as a rule governing the permissible forms of argument. If we insist that every premiss be explicitly stated, we must either count every rule of inference as a premiss — which conflates rules and propositions — or acknowledge that the derivation violates condition (i). Yet the proof is a paradigm of deductive reasoning.
missing_condition: The definition does not distinguish between premisses (propositions assumed as true) and rules of inference (permissions to pass from one proposition to another), and thereby requires that items of fundamentally different logical character be treated uniformly as explicit premisses.
passage: One might define a deductive establishment as one in which every premiss is explicitly stated, the conclusion follows by a finite chain of steps, and each step applies a formally specified rule. This proposal makes deduction maximally transparent: nothing is assumed silently, every step is formally sanctioned, and the chain terminates after finitely many applications. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises from the role of rules of inference themselves. Consider a derivation in elementary arithmetic from the Peano axioms. The axioms are explicitly stated as premisses, and each step — the application of the successor function, the use of the recursion equations for addition — applies a formally specified rule. But the derivation relies upon mathematical induction, which is not a premiss but a rule: it licenses a certain form of argument (if a property holds of zero and is preserved by the successor function, then it holds of every natural number). If we require that every assumption be stated as an explicit premiss, we must either treat induction as a premiss — which confuses a permission to infer with a proposition asserted as true — or concede that the derivation does not satisfy condition (i). One might observe that the definition fails to accommodate the distinction between premisses and rules, a distinction that is fundamental to the logical enterprise, and that a satisfactory account of deduction must recognise that the machinery of inference is not itself among the propositions from which the conclusion is drawn.

definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) it is impossible for P to be true and Q false, (ii) P and Q share subject matter — that is, Q concerns the same domain as P, and (iii) the truth of P provides a reason for the truth of Q.
conditions: It is impossible for P to be true and Q false
P and Q share subject matter
The truth of P provides a reason for the truth of Q
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'It is raining and it is Tuesday.' This proposition entails, in the standard logical sense, the proposition 'It is raining.' The entailment is clear: it is impossible for the conjunction to be true while one of its conjuncts is false. The propositions share subject matter — both concern the weather and the day. But does the truth of the conjunction provide a reason for the truth of 'It is raining'? It appears that the conjunction is true because it is raining, not the other way around. The direction of reason runs from conjunct to conjunction, not from conjunction to conjunct. Condition (iii) imposes a directionality requirement that the logical relation of entailment does not possess.
missing_condition: The definition adds a requirement of reason-giving directionality that is foreign to the logical concept of entailment, which is a relation of truth-preservation and has no inherent direction.
passage: One might propose that entailment is a relation between propositions such that the first cannot be true while the second is false, the two share subject matter, and the truth of the first provides a reason for the truth of the second. This proposal is richer than the bare modal condition — it adds relevance (shared subject matter) and a reason-giving requirement. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the simplest logical operations. Consider the proposition 'It is raining and it is Tuesday.' By any standard account, this conjunction entails the proposition 'It is raining' — for the conjunction cannot be true unless each conjunct is true. The propositions share subject matter, so condition (ii) is satisfied. But does the truth of the conjunction provide a reason for the truth of 'It is raining'? It appears that the explanatory relation runs in the opposite direction: the conjunction is true because it is raining and because it is Tuesday, not the other way around. The truth of each conjunct is the reason for the truth of the conjunction, not a consequence of it. The definition imposes a directionality upon entailment that the logical relation does not possess. One might observe that entailment is a structural relation of truth-preservation — wherever the premiss is true, the conclusion must be true — and that the reason-giving condition introduces an asymmetry that belongs not to logic but to the theory of explanation.

definition: An argument commits a fallacy if and only if (i) the argument purports to be valid, (ii) the argument is in fact invalid — that is, the conclusion does not follow from the premisses, and (iii) the argument is persuasive to an untrained audience by virtue of its superficial resemblance to a valid form.
conditions: The argument purports to be valid
The argument is in fact invalid
The argument is persuasive due to superficial resemblance to a valid form
counterexample: Consider a circular argument in which the conclusion is identical to one of the premisses. Such an argument is deductively valid in the strictest sense — it is impossible for the premiss to be true and the conclusion false, since they are the same proposition. Condition (ii) is therefore not satisfied. Yet the argument is universally recognised as fallacious, precisely because it fails to provide any independent support for its conclusion. The fallacy of begging the question is a fallacy not because the argument is invalid but because it is trivially valid in a way that renders it dialectically worthless.
missing_condition: The definition identifies fallaciousness with invalidity, but some of the most important fallacies — notably begging the question — are deductively valid arguments that fail in some other respect, such as providing no independent reason for the conclusion.
passage: It appears natural to define a fallacy as an argument that purports to be valid, is in fact invalid, and is persuasive to an untrained audience by virtue of its resemblance to a valid form. This definition captures a wide range of familiar fallacies — affirming the consequent, for instance, resembles modus ponens but is invalid, and the undistributed middle resembles a valid syllogism but fails to distribute its middle term. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the fallacy of begging the question. When one argues 'God exists because the Bible says so, and the Bible is authoritative because it is the word of God,' the argument may be reconstructed as a valid chain of inferences — each step follows from what precedes it. Indeed, in its simplest form, a circular argument in which the conclusion is identical to a premiss is trivially valid: it is impossible for the premiss to be true and the conclusion false. Condition (ii) is therefore not met. Yet the argument is universally recognised as fallacious. Its fault lies not in invalidity but in the failure to provide any independent support for the conclusion — it assumes precisely what it purports to establish. One might observe that the definition's identification of fallaciousness with invalidity captures only one dimension of argumentative failure, and that an argument may be deductively impeccable yet dialectically worthless if its validity is purchased at the cost of circularity.

definition: A sentence S expresses a proposition if and only if (i) S is grammatically well-formed, (ii) S is either true or false, and (iii) S has a determinate meaning that is fixed by the conventional meanings of its constituent words and their syntactic arrangement.
conditions: S is grammatically well-formed
S is either true or false
S has determinate meaning fixed by conventional word meanings and syntax
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'I am tired.' It is grammatically well-formed and is either true or false (at any given moment, the speaker is or is not tired). Its meaning appears to be fixed by the conventional meanings of its words. Yet the proposition it expresses varies with the speaker and the time of utterance: uttered by Jones at noon, it expresses the proposition that Jones is tired at noon; uttered by Smith at midnight, it expresses a wholly different proposition. The meaning of the sentence is not sufficient to determine a unique proposition — contextual factors are required. Condition (iii) is satisfied by the sentence, but the proposition expressed is not fixed by that satisfaction alone.
missing_condition: The definition neglects the role of context — speaker, time, place, and other circumstances of utterance — in determining which proposition a sentence expresses, and thus conflates sentence meaning with propositional content.
passage: One might propose that a sentence expresses a proposition when it is grammatically well-formed, is either true or false, and has a determinate meaning fixed by the conventional meanings of its words and their syntactic arrangement. This proposal has the merit of tying propositional content to the publicly available features of language — grammar, word meaning, syntax — and excluding from the category of propositions such items as exclamations, questions, and fragments. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises with sentences containing indexical expressions. The sentence 'I am tired' is grammatically well-formed, is either true or false on any occasion of utterance, and appears to have a meaning fixed by the conventional meanings of its constituent words. Yet the proposition it expresses varies with the speaker and the time: when Jones utters it at noon, it expresses the proposition that Jones is tired at noon; when Smith utters it at midnight, it expresses the proposition that Smith is tired at midnight. The conventional meanings of the words and their syntactic arrangement are identical in both cases, yet the propositions expressed are wholly distinct. One might observe that the definition conflates the meaning of a sentence — which is indeed fixed by convention and syntax — with the proposition it expresses on a particular occasion, which depends upon contextual factors that the sentence's conventional meaning does not determine.

definition: A proposition P is a tautology if and only if (i) P is true in all possible circumstances, (ii) P's truth can be established by examining its logical form alone, without knowledge of the truth-values of its components, and (iii) P is expressible using only the standard truth-functional connectives.
conditions: P is true in all possible circumstances
P's truth is established by logical form alone
P is expressible using only standard truth-functional connectives
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'If something is red, then it is coloured.' This proposition is true in all possible circumstances — nothing could be red without being coloured. Yet its truth cannot be established by examining its logical form alone; it has the form 'If Fx then Gx,' which is not a truth-functional tautology. Its truth depends upon the meanings of 'red' and 'coloured,' not upon the arrangement of connectives. Furthermore, it involves predicate structure rather than being expressible using only truth-functional connectives. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are not met, yet the proposition is necessarily true.
missing_condition: The definition restricts tautology to truth-functional form, thereby excluding necessary truths whose necessity arises from the meanings of their constituent terms or from the structure of predicate logic rather than from truth-functional logic.
passage: One might define a tautology as a proposition that is true in all possible circumstances, whose truth can be established by examining its logical form alone, and which is expressible using only the standard truth-functional connectives. This definition accommodates the paradigmatic tautologies — 'P or not-P,' 'If P and Q then P' — with admirable precision. These propositions are true regardless of what P and Q happen to be, and their truth is visible from their form. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by propositions whose necessity arises not from truth-functional form but from the meanings of their terms. The proposition 'If something is red, then it is coloured' is true in every possible circumstance: nothing could be red without being coloured. Its truth is as inescapable as that of any truth-functional tautology. Yet it cannot be shown to be true by examining its logical form, for its form — 'If Fx then Gx' — is not a truth-functional tautology (there are innumerable false propositions of this form). Its necessity rests upon the semantic relationship between 'red' and 'coloured,' a relationship that truth-functional analysis cannot capture. One might observe that the definition has been constructed to fit the resources of propositional logic and excludes necessary truths that depend upon predicate structure or upon meaning-relations between terms — a restriction that reflects the limitations of a particular formal apparatus rather than the boundaries of the concept of tautology.

definition: A set of propositions S is consistent if and only if (i) no proposition in S contradicts any other proposition in S, (ii) no proposition derivable from S contradicts any proposition in S, and (iii) every proposition in S can be simultaneously true.
conditions: No proposition in S contradicts another
No derivable proposition contradicts any in S
All propositions in S can be simultaneously be true
counterexample: Consider the set consisting of the three propositions: 'This barber shaves all and only those who do not shave themselves,' 'The barber is a person,' and 'Every person either shaves himself or is shaved by the barber.' No proposition in the set explicitly contradicts another — they appear to be compatible descriptions of a village arrangement. Yet when one attempts to determine whether the barber shaves himself, a contradiction is derivable: if he does, then he does not (since he shaves only those who do not shave themselves), and if he does not, then he does (since all who do not shave themselves are shaved by him). Condition (i) is satisfied, but condition (ii) is violated — though this violation is far from obvious upon inspection of the propositions themselves.
passage: One might propose that a set of propositions is consistent when no member contradicts another, no derivable consequence contradicts any member, and all members can be simultaneously true. This threefold condition appears to provide a thorough guarantee of consistency: it checks the propositions against one another, checks the consequences against the propositions, and insists upon the possibility of joint truth. Yet the difficulty with this analysis lies not in what it requires but in the ease with which its satisfaction can be assessed. Consider a set of propositions describing a village barber: 'This barber shaves all and only those who do not shave themselves,' 'The barber is a person,' and 'Every person either shaves himself or is shaved by the barber.' Taken individually, these propositions appear innocuous; taken pairwise, no obvious contradiction emerges. Condition (i) is satisfied upon inspection. Yet when one asks whether the barber shaves himself, a contradiction is immediately derivable, and condition (ii) is violated. The case illustrates that the consistency of a set of propositions may be undetectable from their surface form and may require extensive logical analysis to determine. One might observe that the definition, while extensionally correct, provides no guidance as to how consistency is to be verified, and that in practice the most dangerous inconsistencies are precisely those that are not apparent from the propositions themselves but emerge only when their consequences are traced through chains of inference.

definition: A conditional proposition 'If P then Q' is true if and only if (i) whenever P is true, Q is also true, (ii) there is a connection of relevance between P and Q such that P's being true is pertinent to Q's being true, and (iii) the conditional is assertible — that is, a competent speaker would be willing to assert it.
conditions: Whenever P is true, Q is also true
There is a relevance connection between P and Q
A competent speaker would assert it
counterexample: Consider the conditional 'If 2 + 2 = 5, then London is the capital of France.' The antecedent is necessarily false, so condition (i) is vacuously satisfied — there is no circumstance in which 2 + 2 = 5, and hence no circumstance in which the antecedent is true and the consequent false. But there is no connection of relevance between arithmetic and the capitals of European nations, so condition (ii) is violated. According to the definition, the conditional is false. Yet in classical logic, the conditional is true precisely because its antecedent is false, and this material reading of the conditional is the foundation upon which a great deal of mathematical and logical reasoning rests. The definition's relevance condition excludes a class of conditionals that standard logic counts as true.
missing_condition: The definition adds a relevance requirement that is foreign to the material conditional of classical logic, and in doing so excludes vacuously true conditionals that are essential to standard logical and mathematical reasoning.
passage: One might propose that a conditional 'If P then Q' is true when P's being true is always accompanied by Q's being true, when there is a connection of relevance between P and Q, and when a competent speaker would be willing to assert the conditional. This proposal is motivated by a natural intuition: we do not ordinarily assert conditionals whose antecedent and consequent are entirely unrelated, even when the material conditional happens to be true. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by conditionals with necessarily false antecedents. The conditional 'If 2 + 2 = 5, then London is the capital of France' has a necessarily false antecedent, and since there is no circumstance in which 2 + 2 = 5, there is no circumstance in which the antecedent is true and the consequent false. Condition (i) is vacuously satisfied. But arithmetic and European geography have no connection of relevance, so condition (ii) is violated, and no competent speaker would assert the conditional, so condition (iii) is also doubtful. According to the definition, the conditional is false. Yet in classical logic it is true, and the principle that a false antecedent renders a material conditional true is not a curiosity but a load-bearing principle of mathematical reasoning. One might observe that the definition has attempted to capture the ordinary-language conditional, with its implicit relevance requirements, but in doing so has produced an account that is incompatible with the material conditional upon which formal logic and mathematics depend.

definition: An argument A is sound if and only if (i) A is deductively valid, (ii) all premisses of A are true, and (iii) A is informative — that is, the conclusion of A conveys something that is not already explicitly contained in the premisses.
conditions: A is deductively valid
All premisses are true
A is informative — the conclusion conveys something not explicit in the premisses
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'All bachelors are unmarried men; therefore all bachelors are unmarried.' The argument is valid — the conclusion follows from the premiss. The premiss is true — it states a definitional truth. But the conclusion merely restates part of what the premiss already says; condition (iii) is violated. Yet the argument is, by the standard logical definition, perfectly sound: it is valid and its premiss is true. The informativeness condition excludes arguments that the logical tradition has always counted as sound.
missing_condition: The definition adds an informativeness requirement that goes beyond the standard logical notion of soundness, which requires only validity and truth of premisses, and is indifferent to whether the conclusion is informative.
passage: One might define soundness as the conjunction of deductive validity, truth of all premisses, and informativeness — the requirement that the conclusion convey something not already explicit in the premisses. This last condition is motivated by the sense that a sound argument ought to accomplish something: it ought to establish its conclusion in a way that extends our understanding. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this enriched definition arises from the simplest of valid arguments. Consider: 'All bachelors are unmarried men; therefore all bachelors are unmarried.' The argument is valid — the conclusion is a logical consequence of the premiss. The premiss is true — it is definitionally so. But the conclusion merely repeats part of the information already contained in the premiss; it is uninformative in the strictest sense. Condition (iii) is violated. Yet the argument is sound by the standard logical definition, which requires only validity and truth of premisses and says nothing about informativeness. One might observe that the definition has conflated two distinct virtues of arguments — logical soundness and dialectical usefulness — and that an argument may be logically impeccable yet dialectically pointless. The standard notion of soundness is deliberately silent on the question of informativeness, and the addition of this condition produces a concept that is narrower than the one the logical tradition has employed.

definition: A proposition P is logically necessary if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) the denial of P leads to a contradiction, and (iii) P can be established by purely logical means — that is, without appeal to observation or empirical evidence.
conditions: P is true
The denial of P leads to a contradiction
P can be established by purely logical means
counterexample: Consider the proposition that the axiom of choice is true. In standard set theory (ZFC), the axiom of choice is accepted, and many important results depend upon it. But the denial of the axiom of choice does not lead to a contradiction — it has been shown that both the axiom and its negation are consistent with the remaining axioms of set theory. The axiom cannot be established by purely logical means; it is adopted as a postulate. Condition (ii) is not met. Yet many mathematicians regard the axiom of choice as a necessary truth of mathematics, not a contingent one. The case reveals that some propositions regarded as mathematically necessary cannot be shown to be necessary by the definition's criterion of contradiction upon denial.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that all logical or mathematical necessities can be demonstrated by showing that their denial leads to a contradiction, but some necessary truths — particularly axioms and independent postulates — are necessary in a different sense that the definition does not capture.
passage: One might propose that a proposition is logically necessary when it is true, its denial leads to a contradiction, and it can be established without empirical evidence. This definition is attractive in its austerity: it confines necessity to the realm of the a priori and ties it to the strong criterion of contradiction upon denial. It accommodates the truths of propositional logic — 'P or not-P,' 'not both P and not-P' — and many theorems of mathematics. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the axiom of choice. This axiom — that for any collection of non-empty sets, there exists a function selecting one element from each — is accepted in standard set theory and is indispensable for a vast range of mathematical results. Many mathematicians regard it as a necessary truth of mathematics, not a convenient assumption. But the denial of the axiom of choice does not lead to a contradiction; it has been shown that both the axiom and its negation are consistent with the other axioms of set theory. The axiom cannot be established by purely logical means — it is adopted as a postulate. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are both violated. One might observe that the definition captures a species of logical necessity — the necessity of theorems — but not the necessity of axioms, whose status as necessary truths rests upon considerations of fruitfulness, naturalness, and systematic coherence rather than upon the impossibility of their denial.

definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A consists of a set of premisses and a conclusion, (ii) the conclusion follows from the premisses by the rules of a recognised formal system, and (iii) each step in the derivation of the conclusion is sanctioned by an explicitly stated rule of inference.
conditions: A consists of a set of premisses and a conclusion
The conclusion follows from the premisses by the rules of a recognised formal system
Each step in the derivation is sanctioned by an explicitly stated rule of inference
counterexample: Consider the argument: all men are mortal; Socrates is a man; therefore, Socrates is mortal. This argument was recognised as valid by Aristotle and by every subsequent logician. Yet when it was first formulated, there existed no recognised formal system with explicitly stated rules of inference from which the conclusion could be derived step by step. The argument's validity was grasped through the intuitive apprehension of its logical structure, not through its conformity to a formal system. Conditions (ii) and (iii) were not satisfied, yet the argument was no less valid for that.
missing_condition: The definition conflates validity with formal derivability, failing to recognise that an argument may be valid in virtue of its logical structure even when no formal system has been devised to codify that structure.
passage: One might propose that an argument is valid when it consists of premisses and a conclusion, when the conclusion follows from the premisses by the rules of a recognised formal system, and when each step in the derivation is sanctioned by an explicitly stated rule. This proposal has the merit of precision and of connecting validity to the rigorous standards of modern logic. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider the history of logical reasoning. The argument from the mortality of all men and the manhood of Socrates to the mortality of Socrates was recognised as valid by Aristotle in the fourth century before Christ. Every subsequent logician has concurred. Yet at the time of its original formulation, there existed no formal system with explicitly stated inference rules from which the conclusion could be derived in the manner the definition requires. Aristotle's syllogistic was itself the first attempt to codify such reasoning, and before that codification the argument's validity was grasped through an intuitive apprehension of its logical structure. One might observe that the argument was no less valid before the formal system was devised than after. It appears that the definition, by identifying validity with derivability within a recognised formal system, mistakes a method of demonstrating validity for the ground of validity itself.

definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) it is impossible for P to be true and Q false, (ii) the impossibility in question is logical rather than merely physical or causal, and (iii) the entailment holds in virtue of the meanings of the terms occurring in P and Q.
conditions: It is impossible for P to be true and Q false
The impossibility is logical rather than merely physical or causal
The entailment holds in virtue of the meanings of the terms in P and Q
counterexample: Consider the proposition that this figure is a triangle and the proposition that its interior angles sum to one hundred and eighty degrees. In Euclidean geometry, the former entails the latter: it is impossible for the first to be true and the second false. Yet this impossibility does not hold in virtue of the meanings of the terms alone — it depends upon the axioms of Euclidean geometry, which are substantive mathematical postulates rather than consequences of meaning. In non-Euclidean geometries, the sum of the interior angles of a triangle is not one hundred and eighty degrees. Condition (iii) is not satisfied, yet the entailment within Euclidean geometry is genuine.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring that entailment hold in virtue of meaning alone, thereby excluding mathematical and geometrical entailments that depend upon substantive axioms rather than upon the definitions of terms.
passage: One might define entailment as the relation between propositions such that the first cannot be true and the second false, where this impossibility is logical rather than physical and holds in virtue of the meanings of the terms involved. This definition captures a central class of entailments — those that are, as one might say, analytic. The proposition that John is a bachelor entails the proposition that John is unmarried, and this holds in virtue of what the terms mean. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the domain of mathematics. The proposition that a figure is a triangle entails, within Euclidean geometry, that the sum of its interior angles is one hundred and eighty degrees. The impossibility of the first being true and the second false is not merely physical — it is mathematical and, in a broad sense, logical. Yet it does not hold in virtue of the meanings of the terms alone. The word 'triangle' means a three-sided closed plane figure, and nothing in this definition determines the sum of the interior angles. The entailment depends upon the fifth postulate of Euclid, which is a substantive geometrical axiom. In non-Euclidean geometries, the sum differs. One might observe that the definition, by insisting that entailment hold in virtue of meaning, fails to accommodate the large class of mathematical entailments that depend upon axioms whose content goes beyond the definitions of the terms they employ.

definition: A sentence S expresses a proposition if and only if (i) S is either true or false, (ii) S has a determinate meaning that is invariant across contexts of utterance, and (iii) S can serve as a premiss or conclusion in a deductive argument.
conditions: S is either true or false
S has a determinate meaning that is invariant across contexts of utterance
S can serve as a premiss or conclusion in a deductive argument
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'It is raining.' This sentence is either true or false on any given occasion of utterance, and it can serve as a premiss in a deductive argument. Yet its meaning is not invariant across contexts: uttered in London on Monday it expresses one proposition, and uttered in Paris on Tuesday it expresses another. The sentence satisfies conditions (i) and (iii) but not condition (ii), yet it plainly expresses a proposition on each occasion of its use.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between the sentence, which may vary in content across contexts, and the proposition it expresses on a particular occasion, which is determined jointly by the sentence's linguistic meaning and the context of utterance.
passage: One might propose that a sentence expresses a proposition when it is either true or false, possesses a determinate meaning invariant across contexts, and can serve as a premiss or conclusion in deductive reasoning. This proposal captures the case of sentences in mathematics and formal logic, whose meaning does not vary with the occasion of utterance. The sentence that seven is a prime number expresses the same proposition regardless of who utters it, where, or when. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition arises with sentences whose content depends upon the circumstances of utterance. The sentence 'It is raining' is either true or false whenever it is uttered, and it can certainly serve as a premiss in a deductive argument. Yet its meaning is not invariant across contexts. Uttered in London on a wet Monday, it expresses a proposition about weather conditions in London on that Monday. Uttered in Paris on a dry Tuesday, it expresses a different proposition — one that happens to be false. The sentence changes its propositional content with each change in context. One might observe that the definition, by requiring invariance of meaning across contexts, either excludes such sentences from the class of proposition-expressing sentences — which would be absurd — or conflates the sentence with the proposition it expresses. It appears that a satisfactory account must distinguish between the linguistic meaning of a sentence and the proposition it expresses on a given occasion.

definition: A piece of reasoning R is a deduction if and only if (i) R proceeds from premisses to a conclusion, (ii) the conclusion of R is claimed to follow necessarily from the premisses, and (iii) R is expressible in the notation of a formal logical calculus.
conditions: R proceeds from premisses to a conclusion
The conclusion is claimed to follow necessarily from the premisses
R is expressible in the notation of a formal logical calculus
counterexample: Consider a piece of legal reasoning in which a barrister argues that because the defendant signed a valid contract and failed to perform, the defendant is liable for damages. The reasoning proceeds from premisses to a conclusion, and the conclusion is claimed to follow necessarily given the applicable law. Yet the reasoning is not expressible in the notation of any standard formal calculus, for it involves the interpretation of legal terms — 'valid contract,' 'performance,' 'liability' — whose application depends upon a body of case law and statutory interpretation that resists formal codification. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met, but condition (iii) is not, and yet the reasoning is recognisably deductive.
missing_condition: The definition is too narrow in requiring expressibility in formal notation, failing to recognise that deductive reasoning may occur in domains — law, ordinary discourse, informal mathematics — where the reasoning is genuinely necessary but resists complete formalisation.
passage: It is tempting to characterise deduction as reasoning that proceeds from premisses to a conclusion, where the conclusion is claimed to follow necessarily, and where the reasoning is expressible in the notation of a formal logical calculus. This characterisation has the advantage of tying deduction to the standards of formal rigour that have proved so fruitful in modern logic. Yet one should hesitate to regard it as fully adequate. A case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the domain of legal reasoning. A barrister who argues that because the defendant signed a valid contract and failed to perform, the defendant is liable for damages, is engaged in reasoning that proceeds from premisses to a conclusion and claims the conclusion follows necessarily given the applicable legal principles. Yet the reasoning involves the interpretation of terms — 'valid contract,' 'performance,' 'liability for damages' — whose application depends upon an intricate body of case law, statutory provisions, and judicial interpretation that resists codification in any formal calculus. One might observe that the barrister's reasoning is none the less deductive: the conclusion is presented as following necessarily from the premisses, and any competent lawyer would recognise the necessity. It appears that the definition, by requiring expressibility in formal notation, mistakes a particularly perspicuous representation of deduction for a necessary condition of its occurrence.

definition: Two propositions P and Q are contradictories if and only if (i) P and Q cannot both be true, (ii) P and Q cannot both be false, and (iii) P and Q differ only in that one is the negation of the other.
conditions: P and Q cannot both be true
P and Q cannot both be false
P and Q differ only in that one is the negation of the other
counterexample: Consider the propositions 'Every swan is white' and 'Some swan is not white.' These are contradictories in the classical sense: they cannot both be true and they cannot both be false. Yet they do not differ merely in that one is the negation of the other. The first is a universal affirmative and the second is a particular negative; they differ in both quantity and quality. One is not simply the result of prefixing 'it is not the case that' to the other, for 'it is not the case that every swan is white' is logically equivalent to but not identical in form with 'some swan is not white.'
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring that contradictories differ only by negation, failing to recognise that propositions may stand in the contradictory relation by virtue of their quantificational structure without one being the syntactic negation of the other.
passage: One might define two propositions as contradictories when they cannot both be true, cannot both be false, and differ only in that one is the negation of the other. This definition captures the simplest and most perspicuous cases of contradiction — the propositions 'It is raining' and 'It is not raining' differ only by negation and plainly satisfy the first two conditions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises within the traditional logic of categorical propositions. The propositions 'Every swan is white' and 'Some swan is not white' are contradictories: they cannot both be true and they cannot both be false. Yet they do not differ merely in that one is the negation of the other. The first is a universal affirmative proposition and the second is a particular negative. They differ in both quantity and quality. One is not simply the result of prefixing 'it is not the case that' to the other, for although 'it is not the case that every swan is white' is logically equivalent to 'some swan is not white,' the two sentences differ in their logical form. One might observe that the definition, by insisting upon a purely negational relation between contradictories, fails to accommodate the contradictory pairs that arise naturally within the logic of quantified propositions, where the relation depends upon the interplay of quantifiers and predicates rather than upon simple negation.

definition: A piece of reasoning R commits a fallacy if and only if (i) R appears to be a valid argument, (ii) R is in fact invalid, and (iii) the appearance of validity arises from an identifiable error in logical form.
conditions: R appears to be a valid argument
R is in fact invalid
The appearance of validity arises from an identifiable error in logical form
counterexample: Consider the gambler's fallacy, in which a person reasons that because a fair coin has landed heads ten times in succession, it is now more likely to land tails. This reasoning appears persuasive, and it is in fact erroneous. Yet the error does not arise from a defect in logical form — the reasoning involves no formal structure that can be identified as invalid by the canons of deductive logic. The error is a confusion about the nature of probabilistic independence, not a mistake in the formal structure of an argument.
missing_condition: The definition restricts fallacies to errors in logical form, failing to recognise that some of the most important fallacies — those involving probability, causation, or statistical reasoning — arise from substantive conceptual confusions rather than from defects in formal structure.
passage: It is common to define a fallacy as a piece of reasoning that appears to be a valid argument, is in fact invalid, and derives its specious appearance of validity from an identifiable error in logical form. This definition captures the traditional formal fallacies — affirming the consequent, denying the antecedent, undistributed middle — with admirable precision. Yet one should hesitate to regard it as exhaustive. A case which reveals its insufficiency is the gambler's fallacy. A person who reasons that because a fair coin has landed heads ten consecutive times, the next toss is more likely to produce tails, commits a well-recognised error. The reasoning has a specious persuasiveness — it seems as though the coin is 'due' for a tails. Yet the error does not reside in any defect of logical form. The reasoning involves no deductive structure whose invalidity can be exhibited by the methods of formal logic. The mistake is a substantive confusion about the nature of probabilistic independence: the person treats each toss as though it were influenced by the outcomes of prior tosses, when in fact the tosses are independent. One might observe that this fallacy, which is among the most practically important of all errors in reasoning, is entirely invisible to the definition as stated, since the definition looks only for errors in logical form and not for the conceptual confusions that underlie informal fallacies.

definition: A proposition P is a tautology if and only if (i) P is true under every possible assignment of truth-values to its component propositions, (ii) the truth of P can be established by truth-table methods, and (iii) P contains at least one logical connective.
conditions: P is true under every possible assignment of truth-values to its component propositions
The truth of P can be established by truth-table methods
P contains at least one logical connective
counterexample: Consider the proposition that for all x, if x is identical with x, then x is identical with x. This proposition is true under every possible interpretation and is a logical truth in the fullest sense. Yet it involves quantifiers and identity rather than propositional connectives, and its truth cannot be established by truth-table methods, which apply only to propositional logic. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are not satisfied in the manner the definition requires, yet the proposition is a logical truth of the same fundamental character as any propositional tautology.
missing_condition: The definition conflates tautology with truth-functional tautology, failing to recognise that there exist logical truths — particularly in predicate logic — that are true under every interpretation but that involve quantifiers and predicates rather than propositional connectives and cannot be verified by truth-table methods.
passage: One might define a tautology as a proposition that is true under every possible assignment of truth-values to its components, whose truth is establishable by truth-table methods, and that contains at least one logical connective. This definition captures the tautologies of propositional logic with precision — for instance, the law of excluded middle, which states that any proposition either holds or does not hold, is true under every assignment and is readily verified by a two-row truth table. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one passes beyond propositional logic to the logic of quantification and identity. The proposition that for all x, if x is identical with x then x is identical with x, is true under every possible interpretation and is a logical truth of the most fundamental kind. Yet it involves quantifiers and the identity relation rather than propositional connectives, and its truth cannot be established by truth-table methods, which are applicable only within the propositional calculus. One might observe that the logical truths of predicate logic are no less tautologous in the relevant sense — they hold in every possible interpretation, not in virtue of any particular fact about the world, but in virtue of their logical structure alone. The definition, by restricting its attention to truth-functional connectives and truth-table methods, fails to accommodate these broader logical truths.

definition: A conditional statement 'if P then Q' is true if and only if (i) whenever P is true, Q is also true, (ii) the truth of Q when P is true is not a matter of coincidence, and (iii) there exists an intelligible connection between the state of affairs described by P and that described by Q.
conditions: Whenever P is true, Q is also true
The truth of Q when P is true is not a matter of coincidence
There exists an intelligible connection between the state of affairs described by P and that described by Q
counterexample: Consider the conditional: if two plus two equals five, then the moon is made of green cheese. In classical logic this conditional is true, since the antecedent is false. The conditional is a logical truth and is emphatically not a matter of coincidence. Yet there is no intelligible connection between the state of affairs described by the antecedent and that described by the consequent. Condition (iii) fails, yet classical logic treats the conditional as true. Conversely, if we insist upon condition (iii), we are forced to reject a fundamental principle of classical truth-functional logic.
missing_condition: The definition conflates the material conditional with a stronger notion of connection, failing to accommodate the fact that in standard logic a conditional with a false antecedent is true regardless of any connection between antecedent and consequent.
passage: One might propose that a conditional statement 'if P then Q' is true when P's being true is always accompanied by Q's being true, when this accompaniment is not coincidental, and when there is an intelligible connection between the state of affairs described by P and that described by Q. This proposal reflects an intuition that conditionals express genuine connections rather than merely recording patterns of truth and falsity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation — or, rather, its tension with the standard treatment — arises with conditionals whose antecedents are false. Consider the conditional: if two plus two equals five, then the moon is made of green cheese. In classical logic, this conditional is true, for the material conditional is true whenever its antecedent is false. Its truth is not a matter of coincidence; it follows from a fundamental logical principle. Yet there is plainly no intelligible connection between arithmetic and the composition of the moon. Condition (iii) is not satisfied. If one insists upon the definition as stated, one must reject the classical truth-functional account of the conditional, which has served as the foundation of modern logic for more than a century. One might observe that the definition captures an important feature of the conditionals we employ in ordinary reasoning, but it does not capture the conditional as it functions in formal logic, where truth-functional considerations take precedence over considerations of intelligible connection.

definition: A statement S is paradoxical if and only if (i) S is derived from apparently unexceptionable premisses by apparently valid reasoning, (ii) S is self-contradictory or absurd, and (iii) the contradiction or absurdity arises from S referring to itself.
conditions: S is derived from apparently unexceptionable premisses by apparently valid reasoning
S is self-contradictory or absurd
The contradiction or absurdity arises from S referring to itself
counterexample: Consider the Sorites paradox. One grain of sand is not a heap. Adding one grain to something that is not a heap does not make it a heap. By repeated application of this principle, one concludes that ten thousand grains of sand do not constitute a heap — which is absurd. The premisses appear unexceptionable, the reasoning appears valid, and the conclusion is absurd. Yet no self-reference is involved. The paradox arises from the vagueness of the predicate 'heap,' not from any statement referring to itself.
missing_condition: The definition restricts paradox to cases of self-reference, failing to recognise that some of the most important paradoxes — including those arising from vagueness — involve no self-reference whatever but arise from defects in our concepts or in the apparently valid principles we apply.
passage: One might define a paradox as a statement derived from apparently unexceptionable premisses by apparently valid reasoning, that is self-contradictory or absurd, and whose contradiction arises from self-reference. This definition captures the semantic paradoxes with admirable precision — the Liar paradox, for instance, derives its contradiction from the self-referential statement 'This sentence is false,' and Russell's paradox in set theory arises from the set that refers to itself by purporting to contain all and only those sets that do not contain themselves. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition arises with the Sorites paradox, which has troubled logicians since antiquity. One grain of sand is plainly not a heap. Adding a single grain to something that is not a heap does not, it appears, make it a heap. Yet by repeated application of this inductive step, one arrives at the conclusion that ten thousand grains of sand do not constitute a heap — a conclusion that is manifestly absurd. The premisses appear unexceptionable, the reasoning appears valid, and the conclusion is absurd. All the conditions of a genuine paradox are met save one: there is no self-reference whatever. The paradox arises from the vagueness of the predicate 'heap' and from the apparently valid principle that one grain cannot make the difference between a heap and a non-heap. It appears that the definition, by requiring self-reference, excludes an entire family of paradoxes whose source lies not in self-reference but in the vagueness or defectiveness of the concepts we employ.

definition: A piece of reasoning R constitutes a valid induction if and only if (i) R proceeds from observed instances to a general conclusion, (ii) the observed instances are numerous and varied, and (iii) no observed instance contradicts the general conclusion.
conditions: R proceeds from observed instances to a general conclusion
The observed instances are numerous and varied
No observed instance contradicts the general conclusion
counterexample: Consider the reasoning from the observation that every emerald so far examined has been green to the conclusion that all emeralds are green. Now consider the rival conclusion that all emeralds are grue — that is, green if examined before a certain future date and blue otherwise. Both conclusions satisfy all three conditions equally: the observed instances are the same, they are numerous and varied, and none contradicts either conclusion. Yet one of these inductions strikes us as legitimate and the other as absurd. The definition cannot distinguish between them.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the general conclusion employ only projectible predicates — that is, predicates whose past instances provide genuine grounds for expecting similar future instances — and thus cannot discriminate between legitimate generalisations and gerrymandered ones.
passage: One might propose that a valid induction is one that proceeds from observed instances to a general conclusion, where the instances are numerous and varied and none contradicts the conclusion. This account captures the surface structure of inductive reasoning: the naturalist who has examined thousands of emeralds, drawn from many locations and periods, and found every one to be green, concludes that all emeralds are green. The instances are numerous, varied, and uniformly favourable. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one introduces what has been called a 'grue'-type predicate. Define an object as grue if it is green when examined before a certain future date and blue otherwise. Every emerald examined to date is green, and therefore every emerald examined to date is also grue. The instances supporting the conclusion that all emeralds are grue are precisely as numerous and varied as those supporting the conclusion that all emeralds are green, and no observed instance contradicts either conclusion. Yet one of these generalisations is plainly legitimate and the other is not. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to the number, variety, and consistency of observed instances, cannot distinguish between generalisations that employ legitimate predicates and those that employ gerrymandered ones. It appears that a satisfactory account of induction must impose some constraint upon the predicates that figure in the general conclusion.

definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A consists of a finite sequence of declarative sentences, the last of which is designated the conclusion and the remainder the premises, (ii) there is no possible assignment of truth-values to the non-logical vocabulary in which all premises are true and the conclusion false, and (iii) the conclusion is obtained from the premises by the application of recognised rules of inference.
conditions: A consists of a finite sequence of declarative sentences, the last of which is designated the conclusion and the remainder the premises
There is no possible assignment of truth-values to the non-logical vocabulary in which all premises are true and the conclusion false
The conclusion is obtained from the premises by the application of recognised rules of inference
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'All mammals are animals; all dogs are mammals; therefore all dogs are animals.' This argument is valid in the standard semantic sense — no truth-value assignment makes the premises true and the conclusion false. Yet suppose we present it without any derivation, simply asserting the premises and the conclusion. Condition (iii) requires that the conclusion be obtained by application of recognised rules of inference, yet no such application has been exhibited. The argument satisfies (i) and (ii) but fails (iii), even though it is plainly valid.
missing_condition: The definition conflates semantic validity (the impossibility of true premises and a false conclusion) with the syntactic requirement that a derivation be exhibited, and thereby classifies as invalid arguments that are semantically valid but for which no derivation has been produced.
passage: One might define validity as follows: an argument is valid if it consists of premises and a conclusion, no truth-value assignment makes the premises true and the conclusion false, and the conclusion is obtained from the premises by recognised rules of inference. This definition appears to capture two central aspects of validity — the semantic (truth-preservation under all assignments) and the syntactic (derivability by rules). Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when we consider an argument that is semantically valid but for which no derivation has been given. Take the syllogism: all mammals are animals; all dogs are mammals; therefore all dogs are animals. No truth-value assignment falsifies the conclusion whilst verifying the premises. Yet if the argument is simply stated — premises followed by conclusion — without any application of inference rules, condition (iii) is not satisfied. The definition would classify this argument as not valid until a derivation is produced. One might observe that this conflates two distinct notions: the semantic property of truth-preservation and the syntactic property of derivability. It appears that the definition, by requiring both simultaneously, cannot accommodate the common practice of recognising an argument as valid prior to — or independently of — exhibiting a formal derivation.

definition: A deductive system S is sound if and only if (i) every formula derivable in S is a logical truth, (ii) every rule of inference in S preserves truth under every interpretation, and (iii) S contains no axiom that is false under any interpretation.
conditions: Every formula derivable in S is a logical truth
Every rule of inference in S preserves truth under every interpretation
S contains no axiom that is false under any interpretation
counterexample: Consider a deductive system that contains as an axiom the formula 'p implies p' and as its sole rule of inference modus ponens, but which also includes a derived rule — substitution of equivalents — that, whilst truth-preserving for all standard interpretations, fails to preserve truth in certain non-standard interpretations involving intensional contexts. Every axiom is true under every interpretation, and modus ponens preserves truth, yet the derived rule introduces the possibility of deriving non-truths. The system satisfies conditions (ii) and (iii) for its primitive rules and axioms, but fails condition (i) because the derived rule can produce a formula that is not a logical truth.
missing_condition: The definition requires that every rule of inference preserve truth but does not explicitly require this of derived rules, so that a system whose primitive rules are truth-preserving may still be unsound if its derived rules fail to preserve truth under all interpretations.
passage: A natural characterisation of soundness for a deductive system holds that every derivable formula is a logical truth, every rule of inference preserves truth under every interpretation, and no axiom is false under any interpretation. The second and third conditions are meant to guarantee the first: if we begin with truths and apply truth-preserving rules, we should derive only truths. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we distinguish primitive from derived rules. Consider a system whose axioms are all logical truths, whose sole primitive rule is modus ponens — a paradigmatically truth-preserving rule — but which also includes a derived rule, the substitution of equivalents, that preserves truth in all extensional contexts but fails in intensional ones. If the language of the system includes intensional operators, the derived rule may carry us from logical truths to formulas that are false under certain interpretations. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied by the primitive apparatus, yet the system is unsound. One might observe that the definition, by requiring truth-preservation of the rules of inference without specifying whether this requirement extends to derived rules, leaves open a gap through which unsoundness may enter by way of derived procedures that the primitive apparatus does not directly sanction.

definition: A sentence C is a logical consequence of a set of sentences S if and only if (i) there is no model in which every member of S is true and C is false, (ii) the truth of C follows from S in virtue of the logical form of the sentences alone, and (iii) the connection between S and C can be recognised a priori, without empirical investigation.
conditions: There is no model in which every member of S is true and C is false
The truth of C follows from S in virtue of the logical form of the sentences alone
The connection between S and C can be recognised a priori, without empirical investigation
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'Water is H2O' and the set containing the single sentence 'This glass contains water.' The sentence 'This glass contains H2O' is true in every model in which 'This glass contains water' is true, for the terms are necessarily co-extensional. Yet the connection between the premise and the conclusion does not hold in virtue of logical form — it depends upon the chemical identity of water and H2O, which is an empirical discovery. All three conditions cannot be simultaneously satisfied: (i) holds, but (iii) does not.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish logical consequence, which holds in virtue of form, from metaphysical consequence, which holds in virtue of necessary connections between concepts that are discoverable only by empirical investigation.
passage: One might characterise logical consequence as follows: a sentence is a logical consequence of a set of sentences if no model makes the premises true and the conclusion false, if the connection holds in virtue of logical form alone, and if the connection can be recognised a priori. This analysis aims to capture the distinctive necessity and formality of logical entailment. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with necessary a posteriori truths. Consider the sentences 'This glass contains water' and 'This glass contains H2O.' Since water is necessarily identical to H2O, there is no model — in the metaphysically relevant sense — in which the first is true and the second false. Condition (i) is satisfied. But the connection between the two sentences does not hold in virtue of their logical form; it depends upon the empirical discovery that water is H2O. Condition (iii) is not satisfied, for the connection cannot be recognised a priori. One might observe that the definition, by requiring both model-theoretic truth-preservation and a priori recognisability, cannot accommodate cases of metaphysical necessity that are discovered empirically. It appears that the definition conflates logical consequence with a broader notion of necessary consequence, or else imposes an a priori constraint that is too demanding for necessary truths discovered through scientific investigation.

definition: A set of propositions constitutes a genuine paradox if and only if (i) each proposition in the set is individually plausible, (ii) the propositions are jointly inconsistent, and (iii) no proposition in the set can be rejected without significant theoretical cost.
conditions: Each proposition in the set is individually plausible
The propositions are jointly inconsistent
No proposition in the set can be rejected without significant theoretical cost
counterexample: Consider the sorites paradox: (a) a heap of sand with one grain removed remains a heap, (b) repeated application of this principle to a heap of ten thousand grains will eventually yield a single grain, and (c) a single grain of sand is not a heap. Each proposition is individually plausible, and they are jointly inconsistent. Yet proposition (a) can be rejected — by adopting a sharp cutoff for 'heap' — without any theoretical cost beyond the abandonment of an intuition about vagueness. Whether this constitutes 'significant theoretical cost' is precisely what is in dispute. The definition provides no criterion for assessing the significance of theoretical cost.
missing_condition: The definition fails to provide any criterion for evaluating when the cost of rejecting a proposition is 'significant,' so that the classification of a set of propositions as a genuine paradox depends upon a judgement of significance that the definition itself cannot ground.
passage: A standard characterisation of paradox holds that a genuine paradox is a set of individually plausible propositions that are jointly inconsistent and none of which can be rejected without significant theoretical cost. This characterisation has the merit of distinguishing paradoxes from mere fallacies (where one proposition is not genuinely plausible) and from trivial inconsistencies (where one proposition can be painlessly rejected). Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the sorites paradox. The three propositions — that removing one grain from a heap leaves a heap, that iterated removal reduces a heap to a single grain, and that a single grain is not a heap — are each individually plausible, and they are jointly inconsistent. But whether rejecting the first proposition carries significant theoretical cost is precisely the question at issue. Some philosophers hold that adopting a sharp cutoff for 'heap' is a minor price to pay; others regard it as a fundamental distortion of the semantics of vague predicates. The definition provides no principled way of adjudicating this dispute. One might observe that the notion of 'significant theoretical cost' is itself vague and contested, so that the definition, in relying upon it, defers the difficult question rather than resolving it. It appears that the analysis trades one unclarity — what makes something a paradox — for another — what makes a theoretical cost significant.

definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) it is impossible for P to be true and Q false, (ii) the impossibility in (i) holds in virtue of the meanings of the terms in P and Q, and (iii) P and Q share at least one non-logical term.
conditions: It is impossible for P to be true and Q false
The impossibility in (i) holds in virtue of the meanings of the terms in P and Q
P and Q share at least one non-logical term
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'It is raining' and the proposition 'Either it is raining or it is not raining.' The first entails the second: it is impossible for 'It is raining' to be true and 'Either it is raining or it is not raining' to be false. The impossibility holds in virtue of the logical structure of the disjunction and the meaning of negation. And they share the non-logical term 'it is raining.' All three conditions are met. Yet one might hesitate to say that 'It is raining' entails the logical truth in any informative sense, for the logical truth holds regardless of whether it is raining.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the truth of Q depend in some substantive way upon the truth of P, so that entailments to logical truths — which hold no matter what — are counted as genuine even though the conclusion is independent of the premise.
passage: One might define entailment as the impossibility of a proposition being true whilst another is false, where this impossibility holds in virtue of meaning and the propositions share at least one non-logical term. The sharing requirement is meant to ensure a relevant connection between the propositions, excluding purely formal entailments. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when we consider entailments to logical truths. The proposition 'It is raining' entails 'Either it is raining or it is not raining.' The impossibility holds — the disjunction is a logical truth and cannot be false. The propositions share the non-logical content 'it is raining.' And the impossibility is grounded in the meanings of the logical constants. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that this entailment is trivial in a way that the definition fails to capture: the truth of the conclusion is wholly independent of the truth of the premise. The conclusion would be true even if the premise were false. The sharing of a non-logical term creates the appearance of relevance, but the connection is illusory. It appears that the definition, by requiring only term-sharing, secures a merely syntactic connection where a genuine semantic dependence is wanted.

definition: A set of sentences T is consistent if and only if (i) no contradiction can be derived from T using the standard rules of inference, (ii) every sentence in T is individually satisfiable, and (iii) T is closed under logical consequence — that is, every sentence entailed by T is already a member of T.
conditions: No contradiction can be derived from T using the standard rules of inference
Every sentence in T is individually satisfiable
T is closed under logical consequence — that is, every sentence entailed by T is already a member of T
counterexample: Consider the set containing 'p' and 'not-p.' Each sentence is individually satisfiable — 'p' is true in some models, 'not-p' in others. The set is not closed under logical consequence, for it does not contain the conjunction 'p and not-p.' Yet a contradiction can be derived from this set, so condition (i) is violated. Conversely, consider a consistent set such as {'p'}. This set satisfies (i) and (ii), but it fails (iii), for it does not contain 'p or q,' which is entailed by 'p.' The closure requirement disqualifies virtually every finite consistent set.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a closure condition that is far too strong, requiring that the set already contain every logical consequence, which disqualifies all finite sets of axioms and conflates the consistency of a set with the maximality of a theory.
passage: One might define the consistency of a set of sentences by requiring that no contradiction be derivable, that each sentence be individually satisfiable, and that the set be closed under logical consequence. The first condition captures the core intuition: a consistent set is one from which no contradiction follows. The second adds a further safeguard. The third aims to ensure completeness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider ordinary axiomatic sets. Take the set consisting of the single sentence 'p.' No contradiction can be derived from it. The sentence is individually satisfiable. Yet the set is not closed under logical consequence: 'p or q' is a consequence of 'p' but is not a member of the set. By condition (iii), the set fails to be consistent — an absurd result, for a set containing a single atomic sentence is paradigmatically consistent. One might observe that the closure condition conflates two distinct properties: consistency, which concerns the absence of contradiction, and deductive closure, which concerns the completeness of a theory. The definition, by requiring both, classifies virtually every finite set of sentences as inconsistent, which is plainly too restrictive.

definition: A concept C is definable within a formal language L if and only if (i) there exists a formula F of L such that C applies to an object just in case F is satisfied by that object, (ii) F employs only the primitive vocabulary of L, and (iii) the equivalence between C and F is provable within the deductive system associated with L.
conditions: There exists a formula F of L such that C applies to an object just in case F is satisfied by that object
F employs only the primitive vocabulary of L
The equivalence between C and F is provable within the deductive system associated with L
counterexample: Consider the concept of truth for sentences of the language of arithmetic. By Tarski's theorem, there is no formula of the language of arithmetic that is satisfied by exactly the true sentences of arithmetic. Nevertheless, the concept of arithmetic truth is perfectly well-defined — it is definable in a richer metalanguage. All three conditions fail within the language of arithmetic, yet the concept is unambiguously definable in a broader setting.
missing_condition: The definition relativises definability to a single language and its associated deductive system, and thereby cannot account for concepts that are undefinable within a given language but perfectly definable in a richer metalanguage, conflating the limitations of a particular formal system with the indefinability of the concept itself.
passage: A natural analysis of definability holds that a concept is definable within a formal language if there is a formula of that language coextensive with the concept, employing only primitive vocabulary, and provably equivalent to the concept within the associated deductive system. This analysis captures the standard logical notion of explicit definition. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider Tarski's theorem on the undefinability of truth. The concept of truth for sentences of the language of arithmetic is not definable within that language — no formula of first-order arithmetic is satisfied by exactly the true sentences of arithmetic. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet the concept of arithmetic truth is perfectly well-defined: it can be characterised rigorously in a metalanguage that includes set-theoretic resources. One might observe that the definition, by relativising definability to a single language and its deductive system, conflates the limitations of a particular formal framework with the inherent indefinability of the concept. It appears that a concept may be undefinable in one language whilst being straightforwardly definable in another, and the definition provides no means of marking this distinction.

definition: An inference from premises P1, ..., Pn to conclusion C is deductively valid if and only if (i) the premises and conclusion are well-formed formulas of a given formal language, (ii) the conclusion C is true in every model in which all the premises are true, and (iii) the conclusion C contains no non-logical vocabulary not already present in the premises.
conditions: The premises and conclusion are well-formed formulas of a given formal language
The conclusion C is true in every model in which all the premises are true
The conclusion C contains no non-logical vocabulary not already present in the premises
counterexample: Consider the inference from 'All humans are mortal' and 'Socrates is a human' to 'Something is mortal.' The conclusion is true in every model in which the premises are true. The conclusion, however, contains the existential quantifier and the predicate 'mortal' but does not contain the name 'Socrates' or the predicate 'human,' which appear in the premises. The vocabulary constraint (iii) is satisfied, since the conclusion introduces no new non-logical vocabulary. But now consider the inference from 'All dogs are animals' to 'There exists something that is self-identical.' The conclusion is true in every model in which the premise is true — indeed, in every non-empty model. Yet the conclusion introduces no non-logical vocabulary at all. The inference satisfies all three conditions, yet the conclusion bears no substantive connection to the premise.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the truth of the conclusion depend in some non-trivial way upon the content of the premises, so that inferences to logical truths or near-logical truths — which hold independently of any premises — are classified as valid despite the absence of any genuine inferential connection.
passage: One might define deductive validity by requiring that the premises and conclusion be well-formed, that the conclusion be true in every model satisfying the premises, and that the conclusion contain no non-logical vocabulary absent from the premises. The third condition aims to enforce a relevance constraint: the conclusion should concern the same subject matter as the premises. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider inferences to near-logical truths. From the premise 'All dogs are animals,' one may infer 'There exists something that is self-identical.' The conclusion is true in every non-empty model and thus in every model in which the premise is true. The conclusion contains no non-logical vocabulary at all, so the vocabulary constraint is trivially satisfied. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the conclusion bears no substantive connection to the premise — it would be true regardless of whether all dogs are animals, or indeed regardless of any facts about dogs. It appears that the vocabulary constraint, by concerning itself only with the presence or absence of terms, fails to secure the kind of relevance that genuine deductive reasoning is ordinarily taken to require.
