definition: A statement S is paradoxical if and only if (i) S leads, by apparently valid reasoning, to a conclusion that contradicts S itself, (ii) no step in the reasoning from S to the contradictory conclusion involves an obvious fallacy, and (iii) S is expressible in a natural language or formal language without syntactic anomaly.
conditions: S leads, by apparently valid reasoning, to a conclusion that contradicts S itself
No step in the reasoning from S to the contradictory conclusion involves an obvious fallacy
S is expressible in a natural language or formal language without syntactic anomaly
counterexample: Consider the Banach-Tarski theorem, which shows that a solid ball can be decomposed into finitely many pieces and reassembled into two solid balls of the same size. The conclusion seems to contradict basic intuitions about volume. The reasoning is valid — it employs the axiom of choice and group-theoretic operations without any fallacy. Yet the theorem is not paradoxical in the sense of being self-contradictory; it is a provable theorem of set theory. It satisfies conditions (ii) and (iii) and appears to satisfy (i) in a loose sense, yet it is more properly described as a surprising theorem than as a genuine paradox.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between genuine logical paradoxes, in which the reasoning produces an authentic contradiction, and merely surprising or counter-intuitive theorems, in which valid reasoning leads to conclusions that conflict with pre-theoretic intuitions but do not yield actual self-contradiction.
passage: A natural characterisation of paradox holds that a statement is paradoxical if it leads by apparently valid and non-fallacious reasoning to a conclusion contradicting itself, and if it is expressible without syntactic anomaly. This analysis captures the liar paradox, Russell's paradox, and other paradigmatic cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the Banach-Tarski theorem. This theorem demonstrates that a solid ball in three-dimensional space can be decomposed into a finite number of pieces and reassembled — by rigid motions — into two balls each equal in size to the original. The conclusion appears to contradict the conservation of volume. The reasoning is impeccable — it employs only the axiom of choice and operations from group theory. No step is fallacious. The theorem is expressible in standard mathematical language. Yet one might observe that it is not a paradox in the sense of producing a genuine self-contradiction: it is a proved theorem, and what it contradicts is not itself but our pre-theoretic intuition about volume. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the reasoning lead to a conclusion that contradicts the original statement in some broad sense, cannot distinguish the genuinely paradoxical from the merely astonishing.

definition: A formal theory T is consistent if and only if (i) T does not prove both a sentence and its negation, (ii) every finite subset of the axioms of T has a model, and (iii) T has a model — that is, there exists an interpretation under which all the axioms of T are true.
conditions: T does not prove both a sentence and its negation
Every finite subset of the axioms of T has a model
T has a model — that is, there exists an interpretation under which all the axioms of T are true
counterexample: Consider a theory T formulated in second-order logic whose axioms are individually and finitely consistent but which, by Gödel's incompleteness theorem, cannot prove its own consistency. The theory satisfies conditions (i) and (ii), and by the completeness of finite subsets it appears to satisfy (iii). Yet the existence of a model for the full theory may not be provable within T itself. A defender of the definition would claim that (iii) is satisfied if there exists such a model, whether or not T can prove it, but this makes the consistency of T dependent upon a metatheoretic fact that may be unknowable from within T.
missing_condition: The definition does not address whether the existence of a model must be established from within the theory itself or from a metatheoretic standpoint, leaving the status of consistency ambiguous for theories whose consistency is unprovable from within.
passage: One might define the consistency of a formal theory by requiring that it prove no sentence together with its negation, that every finite subset of its axioms have a model, and that the full set of axioms have a model. The three conditions appear jointly exhaustive: the first captures syntactic consistency, the second captures finite semantic consistency, and the third captures full semantic consistency. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider theories whose consistency is unprovable from within. A sufficiently strong theory of arithmetic, by Gödel's second incompleteness theorem, cannot prove its own consistency. It satisfies condition (i) — it does not prove a contradiction (assuming it is consistent). Every finite subset of its axioms has a model. And if it is consistent, it has a model, by the completeness theorem for first-order logic. Yet the existence of that model cannot be established within the theory itself. One might observe that the definition presupposes a standpoint from which the existence of a model can be affirmed, but that standpoint is metatheoretic. For theories of sufficient strength, the question of whether condition (iii) is satisfied depends upon metatheoretic commitments that lie beyond the resources of the theory. It appears that the definition leaves the locus of consistency — whether internal or external — unresolved.

definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A consists of a set of premises and a conclusion, (ii) it is impossible for all the premises of A to be true and the conclusion of A to be false, and (iii) the impossibility in (ii) is due to the logical form of A rather than to the particular content of its constituent propositions.
conditions: A consists of a set of premises and a conclusion
It is impossible for all the premises of A to be true and the conclusion of A to be false
The impossibility in (ii) is due to the logical form of A rather than to the particular content of its constituent propositions
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'All bachelors are unmarried; therefore, all bachelors are unmarried.' The premise and conclusion are identical, so it is impossible for the premise to be true and the conclusion false. The impossibility appears to be due to form — the argument has the form 'P; therefore P.' But now consider: 'John is a bachelor; therefore, John is unmarried.' It is impossible for the premise to be true and the conclusion false, but this impossibility is due to the meaning of 'bachelor' rather than to the logical form of the argument, which is merely 'P; therefore Q.' Condition (iii) excludes this argument from validity, yet it is necessarily truth-preserving.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate arguments whose necessary truth-preservation arises from the meanings of their non-logical terms rather than from logical form alone, thereby excluding analytically valid arguments from the class of valid arguments.
passage: One might characterise validity as the impossibility, grounded in logical form, of true premises and a false conclusion. This characterisation captures the classical notion that validity is a matter of form rather than content: any argument of the same form will be equally valid, regardless of the subject matter. The requirement that the impossibility derive from logical form distinguishes genuinely valid arguments from those that are merely materially truth-preserving. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this requirement may be constructed from the phenomenon of analytic truth. Suppose one argues: 'John is a bachelor; therefore, John is unmarried.' It is impossible for the premise to be true and the conclusion false. But the logical form of the argument is simply 'Fa; therefore Ga,' which is not a valid form — there are substitution instances in which the premise is true and the conclusion false (for example, 'John is tall; therefore, John is happy'). The truth-preservation in the original argument derives not from form but from the meaning of the word 'bachelor,' which contains within it the concept of being unmarried. Condition (iii) therefore fails, and the definition excludes the argument from the class of valid arguments. Yet one might observe that it is, in a perfectly clear sense, impossible for anyone to be a bachelor without being unmarried. The definition, by insisting that the impossibility be formal, cannot recognise the species of necessary truth-preservation that flows from meaning rather than from structure.

definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) in every possible situation in which P is true, Q is also true, (ii) the truth of Q in those situations depends upon the truth of P rather than upon independent factors, and (iii) P and Q are logically distinct propositions.
conditions: In every possible situation in which P is true, Q is also true
The truth of Q in those situations depends upon the truth of P rather than upon independent factors
P and Q are logically distinct propositions
counterexample: Consider the propositions 'It is raining and the ground is wet' and 'The ground is wet.' In every possible situation in which the conjunction is true, the second conjunct is true. The propositions are logically distinct. But one might hesitate over condition (ii): does the truth of 'The ground is wet' depend upon the truth of 'It is raining and the ground is wet,' or does it hold independently because it is already contained within the conjunction? The dependence relation is obscure, for the truth of the whole conjunction is partly constituted by the truth of its conjunct. The definition requires a notion of dependence it does not adequately specify.
missing_condition: The definition fails to provide a clear criterion for distinguishing cases in which the truth of Q depends upon P from cases in which Q's truth is merely guaranteed by P's truth without standing in a genuine dependence relation to it.
passage: One might propose that entailment holds between two logically distinct propositions when the first cannot be true without the second being true, and when the truth of the second depends upon the truth of the first. The requirement of dependence is intended to distinguish genuine entailment from cases of mere co-occurrence: it is not enough that Q be true whenever P is true; Q must be true because P is true. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this requirement arises in the simplest logical operations. Consider the conjunction 'It is raining and the ground is wet' and its conjunct 'The ground is wet.' In every possible situation in which the conjunction holds, the conjunct holds. The propositions are logically distinct. But the dependence condition is problematic. Does the truth of the conjunct depend upon the truth of the conjunction? In one sense, plainly not: the conjunct might be true for reasons that have nothing to do with whether the conjunction is true — the ground might be wet because of a burst pipe rather than rain. In another sense, the conjunction guarantees the conjunct, but guarantee and dependence may not be the same relation. One might observe that the definition, by requiring dependence without specifying what dependence amounts to, introduces an ambiguity that undermines its usefulness. The notion of entailment was intended to be clear and formal, but the addition of a dependence requirement imports a causal or explanatory concept that the formal apparatus of logic is ill-equipped to handle.

definition: Two propositions P and Q are contradictory if and only if (i) P and Q cannot both be true, (ii) P and Q cannot both be false, and (iii) the negation of P is logically equivalent to Q.
conditions: P and Q cannot both be true
P and Q cannot both be false
The negation of P is logically equivalent to Q
counterexample: Consider the propositions 'Some swans are black' and 'No swans are black.' They cannot both be true, and they cannot both be false — one of them must hold. The negation of 'Some swans are black' is 'No swans are black,' and vice versa. All three conditions are satisfied, and we correctly call them contradictories. But now consider 'This sentence is true' and 'This sentence is false,' where each sentence refers to itself. They cannot both be true, and arguably they cannot both be false. Yet the negation of the first is not straightforwardly equivalent to the second, because both generate paradox. The definition cannot accommodate the possibility that propositions might satisfy conditions (i) and (ii) whilst failing to have well-defined negations.
missing_condition: The definition presupposes that every proposition has a well-defined negation, and thus fails to accommodate cases involving self-reference or semantic paradox in which the negation of a proposition is itself problematic.
passage: One might define two propositions as contradictory when they cannot both be true, cannot both be false, and the negation of each is logically equivalent to the other. This definition captures the classical notion of contradiction with admirable precision. The first two conditions ensure that the propositions exhaust the logical space between them: one must be true and the other false. The third condition ensures that the relationship is not merely one of incompatibility or contrariety but of genuine logical opposition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when we consider propositions that resist the ordinary operations of negation. Suppose we have a sentence that asserts its own truth and a sentence that asserts its own falsehood. There is a temptation to regard them as contradictory: they cannot both be true, and one might argue that they cannot both be false. But when we attempt to verify condition (iii), we encounter difficulty. The negation of a self-referential sentence generates paradox rather than producing a well-defined proposition that can stand in a relation of logical equivalence. It appears that the definition, by requiring logical equivalence between each proposition and the negation of the other, presupposes that negation is always a well-defined operation. One might observe that this presupposition holds for the propositions of classical logic but fails for the sentences that give rise to the semantic paradoxes. The definition, admirable within its intended domain, cannot account for the boundaries of that domain.

definition: An argument A commits a fallacy if and only if (i) A appears to be valid to a typical reasoner, (ii) A is in fact invalid — that is, it is possible for its premises to be true and its conclusion false, and (iii) the appearance of validity in A is due to a systematic and classifiable error in reasoning.
conditions: A appears to be valid to a typical reasoner
A is in fact invalid
The appearance of validity in A is due to a systematic and classifiable error in reasoning
counterexample: Consider an argument from a highly technical domain — say, an argument in modal logic employing the axiom S5 where the axiom S4 was required — that a typical reasoner would not recognise as valid or invalid, because the typical reasoner has no acquaintance with modal logic. The argument is in fact invalid, and the error is systematic and classifiable. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied. But condition (i) fails: the argument does not appear valid to a typical reasoner, because it does not appear to be anything at all to such a person. Yet logicians would rightly classify the error as a fallacy.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow that fallacies may occur in specialised reasoning where the appearance of validity is relative to an expert audience rather than to a typical reasoner, and that a systematic error in reasoning may constitute a fallacy even when the typical reasoner cannot assess the argument at all.
passage: One might characterise a fallacy as an argument that appears valid to a typical reasoner but is in fact invalid, where the appearance of validity is due to a systematic and classifiable error. This characterisation captures the traditional understanding of fallacies as deceptive arguments — arguments that ensnare the unwary by mimicking the form of valid reasoning. The requirement that the error be systematic and classifiable distinguishes fallacies from mere mistakes or slips. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when we consider arguments in highly technical domains. Suppose a logician, working in the framework of modal logic, deploys the axiom that if a proposition is possibly necessary then it is necessary, where the correct axiom for the system in question requires only that if a proposition is necessarily necessary then it is necessary. The resulting argument is invalid, and the error is both systematic and classifiable — it consists in the substitution of one modal axiom for another. Condition (ii) is satisfied, and condition (iii) is satisfied. But condition (i) fails: a typical reasoner, unacquainted with modal logic, would not recognise the argument as valid or invalid. The argument produces no appearance of validity in such a person, because it produces no appearance at all. Yet the error is, by any reasonable standard, a fallacy. It appears that the definition, by tying the concept of fallacy to the responses of a typical reasoner, cannot accommodate the fallacies that arise in specialised disciplines, where the relevant audience is not the public at large but the community of experts.

definition: A proposition P is a tautology if and only if (i) P is true under every possible assignment of truth-values to its constituent propositional variables, (ii) P is a compound proposition — that is, P contains at least one logical connective, and (iii) the truth of P is determinable by the truth-functional analysis of its connectives alone.
conditions: P is true under every possible assignment of truth-values to its constituent propositional variables
P is a compound proposition containing at least one logical connective
The truth of P is determinable by the truth-functional analysis of its connectives alone
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'For all x, if x is a bachelor then x is unmarried.' This proposition is true under every possible assignment of values to its variables — indeed, it is necessarily true. It contains logical connectives (the universal quantifier and the conditional). Yet its truth is not determinable by truth-functional analysis alone: it depends upon the meaning of 'bachelor' and the structure of quantification, neither of which can be captured by truth-tables. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but condition (iii) fails, even though the proposition is necessarily true and might reasonably be called a tautology in an extended sense.
missing_condition: The definition restricts tautology to the propositional calculus and fails to accommodate propositions whose necessary truth arises from quantificational structure or from the meanings of non-logical terms.
passage: One might propose that a tautology is a compound proposition whose truth can be determined by truth-functional analysis alone and which is true under every possible assignment of truth-values to its variables. This proposal correctly identifies the tautologies of the propositional calculus: 'P or not-P,' 'If P then P,' and so forth. The requirement of compoundness excludes atomic propositions, which may be contingently true but are not tautologous. The requirement of truth-functional determinability excludes propositions whose truth depends upon extra-logical factors. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when we consider propositions from the predicate calculus. The proposition 'For all x, if x is a bachelor then x is unmarried' is necessarily true. It is compound, containing a universal quantifier and a conditional. It is true regardless of what individuals exist or what properties they possess. Yet its truth cannot be determined by truth-functional analysis alone: the conditional 'If x is a bachelor then x is unmarried' is not a truth-function of its constituents, for its truth depends upon the meanings of the predicates involved and upon the structure of universal quantification. Condition (iii) therefore fails. One might observe that there is a natural and unobjectionable sense in which the proposition is tautologous — it is true in virtue of its logical and semantic structure, and its truth is independent of empirical fact. The definition, by restricting itself to truth-functional analysis, captures only the narrowest class of necessarily true propositions.

definition: An argument A is sound if and only if (i) A is valid — that is, the conclusion follows necessarily from the premises, (ii) all of A's premises are true, and (iii) the truth of A's premises is established independently of the truth of A's conclusion.
conditions: A is valid
All of A's premises are true
The truth of A's premises is established independently of the truth of A's conclusion
counterexample: Consider the following argument: 'If the fundamental theorem of arithmetic is true, then every integer greater than one has a unique prime factorisation. The fundamental theorem of arithmetic is true. Therefore, every integer greater than one has a unique prime factorisation.' The argument is valid. The premises are true. But the truth of the second premise — 'The fundamental theorem of arithmetic is true' — is established in part by proving the very conclusion the argument draws. The premise and the conclusion are, in the mathematical context, essentially the same assertion. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the argument is, by the standard definition of soundness, perfectly sound.
missing_condition: The definition introduces a requirement of independence that, while seemingly desirable, excludes arguments in mathematics and logic where the truth of a premise is constitutively connected to the truth of the conclusion, and which are nonetheless sound by the standard definition that requires only validity and true premises.
passage: One might characterise soundness as the conjunction of validity with the independent truth of all premises. The requirement of independence is intended to exclude circular arguments — arguments in which the premises are true only because the conclusion is assumed. This seems a reasonable refinement of the standard definition, which requires only validity and true premises without specifying how the truth of the premises is established. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the independence requirement arises in mathematical reasoning. Consider an argument whose first premise is a conditional expressing the content of a well-known theorem, whose second premise asserts the truth of that theorem, and whose conclusion simply states what the theorem guarantees. The argument is valid by modus ponens. Both premises are true. But the truth of the second premise is established by proving the conclusion — for the theorem just is the conclusion, stated in a different form. Condition (iii) therefore fails: the truth of the premise is not established independently of the truth of the conclusion. Yet one might observe that there is nothing wrong with the argument. In mathematics, it is perfectly routine to assert a theorem as a premise and draw out its consequences; the circularity, if it exists, is benign. It appears that the definition, by requiring independence, imposes a constraint that has force in empirical reasoning but that breaks down in the formal sciences, where the relations between premises and conclusions are often constitutive rather than evidential.

definition: A piece of reasoning R is a deduction if and only if (i) R proceeds from a set of premises to a conclusion, (ii) the conclusion is claimed to follow necessarily from the premises, and (iii) each step of R is justified by an explicit rule of inference.
conditions: R proceeds from a set of premises to a conclusion
The conclusion is claimed to follow necessarily from the premises
Each step of R is justified by an explicit rule of inference
counterexample: Suppose a mathematician, in the course of a proof, perceives that a certain conclusion follows necessarily from the premises but cannot identify the explicit rule of inference that justifies one of the intermediate steps. She proceeds by intuitive insight, recognising the necessity of the transition without being able to articulate the rule. The reasoning proceeds from premises to conclusion. The conclusion is claimed to follow necessarily. But one of the steps lacks justification by an explicit rule. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the reasoning may be perfectly valid, and the step may later be shown to be justified by a rule the mathematician had not yet formulated.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate deductive reasoning in which some steps are justified by intuitive logical insight rather than by explicitly formulated rules, even when those steps are in fact necessarily truth-preserving.
passage: One might propose that deduction is reasoning in which a conclusion follows necessarily from premises, with each step justified by an explicit rule of inference. This proposal captures the formal ideal of deduction as it is practised in logic textbooks: every transition is governed by a stated rule, and the correctness of the reasoning can be mechanically verified by checking that each rule has been correctly applied. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the practice of working mathematicians. Suppose a mathematician, in the midst of a lengthy proof, reaches a point at which she perceives with complete clarity that a certain proposition follows necessarily from what has been established. She cannot, however, identify the explicit rule of inference that sanctions the transition. The step is not an application of modus ponens, nor of universal instantiation, nor of any rule she can name. She proceeds by what she would describe as logical intuition — a direct apprehension of the necessity of the conclusion given the premises. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied: the reasoning proceeds from premises to a conclusion, and the conclusion is claimed to follow necessarily. But condition (iii) fails, since the step is not justified by any explicit rule. One might observe that the mathematician's reasoning is nonetheless deductive in character: the conclusion does follow necessarily, and the step may eventually be shown to instantiate a rule that was, at the time of the inference, merely implicit. It appears that the definition, by requiring explicit rules at every step, confuses the ideal of formalisation with the nature of deduction itself.

definition: A sentence S expresses a proposition if and only if (i) S has a determinate truth-value — that is, S is either true or false, (ii) the truth-value of S is independent of the context in which S is uttered, and (iii) S can serve as the premise or conclusion of a valid argument.
conditions: S has a determinate truth-value
The truth-value of S is independent of the context in which S is uttered
S can serve as the premise or conclusion of a valid argument
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'I am in pain.' It has a determinate truth-value: it is true if the speaker is in pain and false otherwise. It can serve as a premise in a valid argument ('I am in pain; anyone in pain deserves sympathy; therefore, I deserve sympathy'). Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied. But its truth-value is not independent of context: it depends upon who is speaking and when. Condition (ii) fails. Yet the sentence plainly expresses a proposition — indeed, a different proposition on each occasion of its utterance.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between the sentence and the proposition it expresses, and thereby excludes context-dependent sentences that express determinate propositions relative to each context of utterance.
passage: One might propose that a sentence expresses a proposition if and only if it has a determinate truth-value, its truth-value is context-independent, and it can serve as a premise or conclusion in a valid argument. This proposal is motivated by the desire to identify propositions with the stable, shareable contents of thought — the sort of thing that can be asserted, denied, believed, and doubted without variation across speakers and occasions. The requirement of context-independence is intended to exclude utterances whose truth-value shifts with the identity of the speaker, the time of utterance, or the location. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this requirement arises in the most ordinary uses of language. Consider a person who says, 'I am in pain.' The sentence has a determinate truth-value — it is true or false depending on the speaker's condition. It can serve as a premise in a valid argument. But its truth-value is context-dependent: if spoken by one person it may be true, if spoken by another it may be false, and if spoken by the same person at different times it may change. Condition (ii) fails. Yet one might observe that the sentence does express a proposition — a proposition about the speaker's condition at the time of utterance. What varies across contexts is not whether a proposition is expressed but which proposition is expressed. The definition, by requiring context-independence at the level of the sentence rather than at the level of the proposition, conflates the vehicle of expression with the content expressed.

definition: A proposition P is necessary if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) the denial of P leads to a contradiction, and (iii) P's truth can be established by reasoning alone, without recourse to empirical observation.
conditions: P is true
The denial of P leads to a contradiction
P's truth can be established by reasoning alone, without recourse to empirical observation
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'Water is H₂O.' It is true. And one might argue that its denial leads to a contradiction: if water is not H₂O, then the substance in our lakes and rivers is not what chemistry has determined it to be, which contradicts the established identity. Many philosophers would classify this proposition as necessary — it is true in every possible world in which water exists. Yet its truth cannot be established by reasoning alone; it required empirical investigation to discover that water has the molecular composition H₂O. Condition (iii) fails. The definition thus excludes a proposition that is, on many accounts, necessarily true.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate a posteriori necessities — propositions that are necessarily true but whose truth can only be discovered through empirical investigation — and thereby restricts the concept of necessity to the a priori.
passage: One might characterise necessity as truth whose denial leads to contradiction and which can be established by pure reasoning without recourse to empirical observation. This characterisation captures the traditional rationalist conception of necessary truth: the truths of mathematics and logic, which hold in every possible situation and which can be grasped by the intellect alone. The requirement that denial lead to contradiction ensures that the truth is not merely actual but could not be otherwise. The requirement that truth be establishable by reasoning alone ensures that necessity is a matter of rational insight rather than of empirical discovery. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when we consider identity statements involving natural kinds. The proposition that water is the compound of hydrogen and oxygen atoms bonded in a particular molecular configuration appears to be necessary — it is difficult to conceive of a possible situation in which the very substance that is water has a different molecular composition. Its denial seems to lead to something like a contradiction: it would require that the substance in our rivers and oceans be both water and not the compound we have identified. But its truth cannot be established by reasoning alone. It required centuries of empirical investigation to discover the composition of water. Condition (iii) fails. One might observe that the definition, by equating necessity with a priori knowability, forecloses the possibility that some truths are necessary yet discoverable only through experience. It appears that necessity and the a priori, though often conjoined, are not identical.

definition: A proposition P is contingent if and only if (i) P is neither necessarily true nor necessarily false, (ii) the truth-value of P is determined by the way the world actually is rather than by logic alone, and (iii) it is conceivable that the world might have been such that P had the opposite truth-value.
conditions: P is neither necessarily true nor necessarily false
The truth-value of P is determined by the way the world actually is rather than by logic alone
It is conceivable that the world might have been such that P had the opposite truth-value
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'There are exactly seven billion human beings alive at this moment.' It is neither necessarily true nor necessarily false. Its truth-value is determined by the way the world actually is. And it is conceivable that the world might have been otherwise. All three conditions are satisfied, and the proposition is indeed contingent. But now consider 'Hesperus is Phosphorus.' It is true, and many philosophers hold that it is necessarily true — true in every possible world in which the referent exists. Yet it is conceivable that Hesperus might not have been Phosphorus: one can imagine discovering that the evening star and the morning star are different celestial bodies. Condition (iii) is satisfied by conceivability, even though the proposition may be necessary. The definition thus classifies as contingent a proposition that is, on many accounts, necessary.
missing_condition: The definition relies on conceivability as a criterion for contingency, but fails to distinguish between genuine metaphysical possibility and mere epistemic conceivability, thereby misclassifying necessarily true propositions that are conceivably false.
passage: One might propose that a proposition is contingent if and only if it is neither necessarily true nor necessarily false, its truth-value is determined empirically, and it is conceivable that the world might have been otherwise. This proposal captures the intuitive notion that contingent truths are those that happen to hold but might not have. The requirement of conceivability is meant to serve as a test for possibility: if we can conceive of the world being otherwise, then the proposition's truth is not guaranteed by logic or necessity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this criterion arises in the analysis of identity statements. Consider the proposition that the celestial body visible in the evening and the celestial body visible in the morning are one and the same. It is true. Many philosophers hold that it is necessarily true — that in every possible world in which the referent exists, the identity holds. Yet it is eminently conceivable that they might have been different bodies: the astronomical discovery that established their identity was an empirical achievement, and one can without difficulty imagine a world in which the discovery had gone the other way. Condition (iii) is satisfied, and the definition classifies the proposition as contingent. One might observe that conceivability here tracks our ignorance rather than the modal status of the proposition. It appears that the definition, by employing conceivability as its test for contingency, cannot distinguish between what might genuinely have been otherwise and what merely seems, from the standpoint of our limited information, as though it might have been.

definition: An argument is valid if and only if (i) it contains at least one premise and exactly one conclusion, (ii) the truth of all premises necessitates the truth of the conclusion, and (iii) the necessitation in (ii) can be demonstrated by a finite sequence of applications of recognised inference rules.
conditions: It contains at least one premise and exactly one conclusion
The truth of all premises necessitates the truth of the conclusion
The necessitation in (ii) can be demonstrated by a finite sequence of applications of recognised inference rules
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'For every natural number n, P(n); therefore, P(0) and P(1) and P(2) and so on without end.' The truth of the premise necessitates the truth of the conclusion, for if a property holds of every natural number it holds of each one individually. Yet the conclusion is an infinite conjunction, and the derivation of the infinite conjunction from the universal premise cannot be completed in a finite sequence of inference steps. Condition (iii) fails, though the argument is plainly valid.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between the existence of a logical necessitation and the demonstrability of that necessitation by finitely many inference steps, thereby excluding valid arguments whose validity cannot be captured by any finite proof.
passage: One might characterise validity as the necessitation of a conclusion by its premises, where this necessitation is demonstrable through a finite sequence of recognised inference steps. This characterisation has practical appeal: it connects validity to the activity of proof construction, ensuring that any valid argument can, in principle, be verified step by step. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from reasoning about infinite domains. Suppose one asserts that a property P holds of every natural number and infers from this that P(0) and P(1) and P(2) and so on, for each natural number individually and collectively. The truth of the premise — that P holds universally — certainly necessitates the truth of the conclusion. If P holds of every natural number, then the conjunction of P(0), P(1), P(2), and so forth must hold. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. But condition (iii) is not, for the conclusion is an infinite conjunction and no finite sequence of inference steps can derive it. Each individual conjunct P(k) can be derived in finitely many steps by instantiation, but the conjunction of all of them requires infinitely many such instantiations. One might observe that this is no mere logical curiosity: reasoning about infinite totalities is central to mathematics and to any logic adequate to mathematical practice. The definition, by requiring finite demonstrability, imposes a constructive constraint that excludes valid arguments whose validity is grounded in the structure of infinite domains. It confuses the concept of logical necessitation with the narrower concept of finite provability.

definition: A set of propositions S entails a proposition P if and only if (i) P is a proposition distinct from any member of S, (ii) there is no possible assignment of truth-values under which every member of S is true and P is false, and (iii) the impossibility in (ii) is discoverable by inspection of the logical forms of the members of S and of P.
conditions: P is a proposition distinct from any member of S
There is no possible assignment of truth-values under which every member of S is true and P is false
The impossibility in (ii) is discoverable by inspection of the logical forms of the members of S and of P
counterexample: Consider the set S containing the single proposition 'Water is H2O' and the proposition P 'Water contains hydrogen.' There is no possible assignment of truth-values under which 'Water is H2O' is true and 'Water contains hydrogen' is false, for the chemical composition of water necessitates that it contains hydrogen. Yet this necessitation is not discoverable by inspection of logical form: the logical forms are merely 'a is F' and 'a has G,' and substitution instances of this form can have true premises and false conclusions. The entailment depends upon the content of the chemical terms, not upon their logical structure.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate entailments grounded in the necessary connections among the contents of non-logical terms, thereby excluding all cases of analytic or metaphysically necessary entailment that are not reducible to logical form.
passage: One might propose that entailment obtains when there is no possible truth-value assignment making the premises true and the conclusion false, and when this impossibility is discoverable by inspecting the logical forms of the propositions involved. This proposal captures the purely formal dimension of entailment: the argument from 'All men are mortal' and 'Socrates is a man' to 'Socrates is mortal' is valid in virtue of its quantificational structure, and this can be seen by attending to the logical forms alone. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this requirement may be drawn from the relation between scientific propositions. Suppose one takes as premise the proposition that water is H2O and asks whether it entails that water contains hydrogen. It appears evident that it does: there is no coherent scenario in which water is H2O yet fails to contain hydrogen. The molecular formula specifies the constituent elements. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, for the propositions are distinct and the truth of the premise excludes the falsity of the conclusion. But condition (iii) fails. The logical forms of the two propositions — 'a is F' and 'a has G' — do not, by themselves, reveal any entailment. One can substitute other terms to produce instances with true premises and false conclusions: 'The table is brown' does not entail 'The table has fragrance.' The entailment from 'Water is H2O' to 'Water contains hydrogen' rests not upon logical form but upon the internal structure of the chemical concepts involved. One might observe that the definition, by restricting entailment to what is discoverable through formal inspection, excludes a vast class of necessary connections among propositions — connections that are no less rigorous for being grounded in content rather than in form.

definition: Two propositions P and Q are contradictory if and only if (i) P and Q cannot both be true, (ii) P and Q cannot both be false, and (iii) P and Q differ only in that one is the negation of the other.
conditions: P and Q cannot both be true
P and Q cannot both be false
P and Q differ only in that one is the negation of the other
counterexample: Consider the propositions 'This surface is entirely red' and 'This surface is entirely green,' said of the same surface at the same time. They cannot both be true, for a surface cannot be entirely red and entirely green simultaneously. And they cannot both be false in a way that preserves their contradictory character — well, in fact they can both be false, for the surface might be blue. This shows the pair is not contradictory on the full definition. But consider instead 'Every integer is even' and 'Some integer is odd.' These cannot both be true and cannot both be false. Yet condition (iii) fails: 'Some integer is odd' is not the direct negation of 'Every integer is even' in surface form, though they are logically equivalent to a contradictory pair. The definition excludes genuine contradictories that are not expressed as direct negations.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that contradictory propositions need not be related as direct negation and its base; they may be logically distinct in surface form yet stand in the contradictory relation in virtue of their logical content.
passage: One might characterise contradiction as the relation between a proposition and its negation, where the two cannot share a truth-value. This is tidy and captures the paradigm cases: 'It is raining' and 'It is not raining' cannot both be true and cannot both be false. The requirement that the propositions differ only as negation and its base provides a simple formal criterion for identifying contradictory pairs. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this criterion may be drawn from the logic of quantified propositions. Consider the pair: 'Every integer is even' and 'Some integer is odd.' These two propositions cannot both be true, for if every integer is even then no integer is odd. And they cannot both be false, for if it is false that every integer is even then some integer is not even, and if it is also false that some integer is odd then every integer is even — a contradiction. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied: the propositions cannot share a truth-value. But condition (iii) fails, for 'Some integer is odd' is not the negation of 'Every integer is even.' The negation of 'Every integer is even' is 'Not every integer is even' or equivalently 'Some integer is not even.' The proposition 'Some integer is odd' is a different proposition that happens to be logically equivalent to the negation, in virtue of the relationship between oddness and non-evenness. One might observe that the definition, by requiring that contradictory propositions be related as a proposition and its direct negation, draws the class of contradictory pairs too narrowly. Propositions that are materially and necessarily contradictory — that cannot share a truth-value — may fail to exhibit the surface form of negation and its base.

definition: A proposition P is a tautology if and only if (i) P is true under every possible assignment of truth-values to its constituent atomic propositions, (ii) the truth of P is determinable by truth-table methods alone, and (iii) P contains at least one logical connective.
conditions: P is true under every possible assignment of truth-values to its constituent atomic propositions
The truth of P is determinable by truth-table methods alone
P contains at least one logical connective
counterexample: Consider the proposition 'For all x, either x is F or x is not F.' This is true under every interpretation: for any property F and any object x, either x has F or x lacks F. It is a logical truth of the most elementary kind. Yet condition (ii) fails, for truth-table methods operate upon propositional logic and cannot evaluate quantified propositions. And condition (iii) is arguably satisfied by the disjunction and negation, but the truth-table method cannot be applied to determine the truth of the proposition because the quantifier 'for all x' falls outside the scope of propositional connectives. The definition excludes quantified logical truths from the class of tautologies.
missing_condition: The definition fails to extend beyond propositional logic, excluding those logical truths whose truth in every interpretation depends upon quantificational structure rather than upon truth-functional composition alone.
passage: One might characterise a tautology as a proposition that is true under every possible assignment of truth-values to its atoms, where this universal truth is determinable by the method of truth-tables. This characterisation is clean and effective within propositional logic: 'P or not-P' is a tautology because every row of its truth-table yields the value true, and this can be mechanically verified. The requirement of at least one logical connective excludes atomic propositions, which are not logical truths. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from quantificational logic. Consider the proposition that for every object x, either x has the property F or x lacks F. This is a logical truth of the most fundamental kind — it is an instance of the law of excluded middle generalised to arbitrary properties and objects. It is true in every interpretation, on every domain, for every assignment of extensions to the predicate F. If any proposition deserves to be called a tautology, this one does. But the truth-table method cannot evaluate it. The quantifier 'for all x' ranges over a domain of objects, and the truth or falsity of the proposition depends upon the structure of this domain and the extension of the predicate — matters that truth-tables, which operate exclusively upon atomic propositional variables and truth-functional connectives, cannot address. One might observe that the definition, by tying tautologousness to the truth-table method, restricts the concept to the propositional fragment of logic and thereby excludes a vast class of logical truths whose universal truth rests upon quantificational rather than truth-functional structure.

definition: A statement S constitutes a paradox if and only if (i) S is derived from apparently acceptable premises by apparently valid reasoning, (ii) the conclusion of S is absurd or self-contradictory, and (iii) S admits of no resolution that preserves all the premises and all the reasoning steps.
conditions: S is derived from apparently acceptable premises by apparently valid reasoning
The conclusion of S is absurd or self-contradictory
S admits of no resolution that preserves all the premises and all the reasoning steps
counterexample: Consider Zeno's paradox of Achilles and the tortoise. From apparently acceptable premises — that Achilles must first reach the point where the tortoise began, then the point the tortoise has moved to, and so on — and by apparently valid reasoning, we reach the conclusion that Achilles can never overtake the tortoise. The conclusion is absurd. Yet the paradox does admit of resolution: the infinite series of distances Achilles must traverse converges to a finite sum, and the theory of convergent series shows that Achilles overtakes the tortoise in finite time. This resolution preserves all the premises and all the reasoning steps; it merely shows that the conclusion does not follow, because the infinite sequence of tasks can be completed in finite time. Condition (iii) fails, for the paradox is resolvable, yet it remains a paradigmatic paradox.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that a statement may constitute a genuine paradox even when it admits of resolution, provided that the resolution requires a conceptual advance or the introduction of theoretical apparatus that was not available when the paradox was first formulated.
passage: One might define a paradox as an argument that proceeds from acceptable premises by valid reasoning to an absurd conclusion, where no resolution is available that preserves the premises and the reasoning. This definition captures the sense of intellectual crisis that paradoxes occasion: we are compelled to accept something unacceptable, and there appears to be no escape. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the history of reasoning about infinity. Consider the paradox attributed to Zeno, in which Achilles races a tortoise that has been given a head start. Before Achilles can overtake the tortoise, he must first reach the point where the tortoise started. But by the time he reaches that point, the tortoise has moved ahead. He must then reach this new point, by which time the tortoise has moved again. The sequence of points Achilles must reach is infinite, and the conclusion appears to be that Achilles can never overtake the tortoise — a manifest absurdity, since fast runners do overtake slow ones. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied: the premises appear acceptable, the reasoning appears valid, and the conclusion is absurd. But condition (iii) fails. The paradox admits of a resolution that preserves all the premises and all the reasoning: the theory of infinite series shows that the sum of the diminishing distances converges to a finite quantity, and Achilles traverses this finite distance in finite time. The resolution does not reject any premise or any inference step; it shows that the absurd conclusion was an illusion generated by unfamiliarity with infinite convergence. Yet Zeno's argument is, by universal consent, a paradox — indeed, among the most celebrated paradoxes in the history of thought. One might observe that the definition mistakes the current state of resolution for the defining feature of paradox. What makes an argument paradoxical is not that it is permanently irresolvable but that it reveals a gap in our conceptual apparatus that demands a theoretical advance.

definition: A piece of reasoning R is a deduction if and only if (i) R proceeds from premises to a conclusion, (ii) the conclusion of R is claimed to follow necessarily from the premises, and (iii) R introduces no information in the conclusion that is not already contained in the premises.
conditions: R proceeds from premises to a conclusion
The conclusion of R is claimed to follow necessarily from the premises
R introduces no information in the conclusion that is not already contained in the premises
counterexample: Consider mathematical reasoning by reductio ad absurdum: one assumes the negation of the proposition to be proved, derives a contradiction, and concludes that the original proposition is true. The conclusion follows necessarily from the premises. Yet condition (iii) is problematic: the conclusion — that the original proposition holds — introduces a positive assertion that was not contained in the premises; the premises contained only the assumption of the negation and the derivation of an absurdity. The information that the proposition positively holds is, in a natural sense, new. Nevertheless, reductio reasoning is paradigmatically deductive.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that deductive reasoning may yield conclusions whose informational content, whilst logically implicit in the premises, is not explicitly contained therein and may require creative insight to extract.
passage: One might characterise deduction as reasoning in which the conclusion follows necessarily from the premises and adds no information beyond what the premises already contain. This characterisation captures the traditional contrast between deduction and other forms of inference: deduction is said to be explicative rather than ampliative, drawing out what is implicit rather than venturing beyond it. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this requirement may be drawn from mathematical practice. Consider a proof by reductio ad absurdum. The mathematician wishes to establish that a certain proposition — say, that the square root of two is irrational — is true. She assumes the negation: that the square root of two is rational, expressible as a fraction in lowest terms. From this assumption she derives, by a chain of necessary inferences, a contradiction: that the fraction both is and is not in lowest terms. She then concludes that the original proposition is true. This reasoning is deductive in the clearest possible sense: every step is necessary, and the conclusion could not be false if the axioms of arithmetic are true. But does the conclusion introduce no information not already contained in the premises? The premises contain an assumption of rationality and a derivation of absurdity. The conclusion — that the square root of two is irrational — is a positive assertion of a quite different character. One might observe that the information was, in some sense, implicit in the axioms all along, but the word 'contained' in condition (iii) is strained by this usage. The conclusion represents a genuine discovery, requiring considerable ingenuity to extract. The definition, by requiring that the conclusion contain no new information, either trivialises the concept of containment or excludes paradigmatic cases of deductive reasoning.

definition: An utterance U expresses a proposition if and only if (i) U is either true or false, (ii) U has a determinate content that is independent of the context in which it is uttered, and (iii) U can serve as a premise or conclusion in an argument.
conditions: U is either true or false
U has a determinate content that is independent of the context in which it is uttered
U can serve as a premise or conclusion in an argument
counterexample: Consider the utterance 'I am hungry,' spoken by a particular person at a particular time. It is either true or false — the speaker is either hungry or not. It can serve as a premise in an argument — 'I am hungry; if I am hungry I should eat; therefore I should eat.' But its content is not independent of context: what proposition it expresses depends upon who utters it and when. Yet 'I am hungry' plainly expresses a proposition — a definite claim about the world that is true or false. Condition (ii) excludes it, but no reasonable account of propositions should do so.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate propositions whose content is partially determined by the context of utterance, thereby excluding all indexical and demonstrative propositions from the class of genuine propositions.
passage: One might define a proposition as what is expressed by an utterance that is either true or false, has a determinate context-independent content, and can figure in argument. This definition aims to distinguish propositions from questions, commands, and exclamations on the one hand, and from vague or incomplete expressions on the other. The requirement of context-independence ensures that a proposition has a fixed content that can be assessed, debated, and transmitted. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this requirement may be drawn from the most ordinary uses of language. Suppose a person says, at noon on Tuesday, 'I am hungry.' This utterance is either true or false — the person is either in the state of hunger or not. It can serve as a premise: one might reason from 'I am hungry' and 'If I am hungry, I ought to eat' to 'I ought to eat.' Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied. But condition (ii) fails, for the content of the utterance depends essentially upon who produces it and when. The same sentence, spoken by a different person or at a different time, would express a different proposition. The word 'I' picks out the speaker; the tense locates the claim in time. Remove the context and the utterance has no determinate content at all. Yet it plainly expresses a proposition — a claim about the world that is definitely true or definitely false. One might observe that an enormous proportion of our propositional discourse employs indexical expressions of this kind: 'here,' 'now,' 'this,' 'that.' A definition that excludes context-dependent utterances from the class of proposition-expressing utterances excludes the greater part of ordinary rational discourse. The definition mistakes one species of proposition — the context-independent, eternal proposition — for the genus.

definition: An argument A commits a fallacy if and only if (i) A appears, on casual inspection, to be valid, (ii) A is in fact invalid — the conclusion does not follow necessarily from the premises, and (iii) the appearance of validity in A is due to a specifiable error in reasoning that can be identified and named.
conditions: A appears, on casual inspection, to be valid
A is in fact invalid — the conclusion does not follow necessarily from the premises
The appearance of validity in A is due to a specifiable error in reasoning that can be identified and named
counterexample: Consider the gambler's fallacy: 'The roulette wheel has landed on red ten times in succession; therefore it is more likely to land on black next.' This argument appears persuasive to many people. But its persuasiveness does not arise from an appearance of formal validity — no one would claim that the conclusion follows necessarily from the premise. The error is not one of deductive invalidity but of probabilistic reasoning: confusing the independence of trials with a supposed tendency toward balance. Condition (ii) is satisfied only trivially — the argument was never presented as deductively valid — and the argument's defectiveness lies in a domain that the definition does not address.
missing_condition: The definition fails to encompass fallacies of probabilistic, inductive, and informal reasoning, in which the error is not a matter of deductive invalidity but of misapplied principles of non-deductive inference.
passage: One might characterise a fallacy as an argument that appears valid but is not, where the spurious appearance of validity arises from a specifiable error. This characterisation captures the classical fallacies well: affirming the consequent appears valid because it resembles modus ponens, but the error — confusing the direction of the conditional — can be precisely identified. The requirement that the error be specifiable distinguishes genuine fallacies from mere bad reasoning. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from probabilistic reasoning. Consider a gambler who has observed a roulette wheel land on red ten consecutive times and concludes that black is now more likely. This reasoning is defective, and it is so in a way that merits the name 'fallacy.' It is widely committed, it has a recognisable structure, and it has a name — the gambler's fallacy. But the definition cannot accommodate it. The gambler's argument does not appear deductively valid; no one supposes that ten reds necessitate a black. The argument's appeal lies in a misunderstanding of probability: the intuition that a series of outcomes must, over time, balance out. The error is not a matter of deductive invalidity but of misapplied probabilistic reasoning. One might observe that many of the most practically important fallacies are of this character — errors in inductive reasoning, in statistical inference, in causal reasoning. The appeal to authority, the post hoc fallacy, the base rate neglect — none of these involves an argument that appears deductively valid. The definition, by restricting fallacy to cases of spurious deductive validity, captures only the formal fallacies and leaves the informal ones — which are far more prevalent and far more dangerous — outside its scope.

definition: A conditional statement 'If P then Q' is true if and only if (i) it is not the case that P is true and Q is false, (ii) the truth-value of the conditional is wholly determined by the truth-values of P and Q, and (iii) the conditional asserts a connection between the content of P and the content of Q.
conditions: It is not the case that P is true and Q is false
The truth-value of the conditional is wholly determined by the truth-values of P and Q
The conditional asserts a connection between the content of P and the content of Q
counterexample: Consider the conditional 'If Paris is the capital of France, then 2 + 2 = 4.' On the material conditional analysis, this is true: the antecedent is true and the consequent is true, so condition (i) is satisfied, and the truth-value is determined by the components, satisfying condition (ii). But condition (iii) fails, for there is no connection whatever between the content of the antecedent and the content of the consequent. The geography of France has nothing to do with arithmetic. Yet on the material analysis the conditional is straightforwardly true. The definition cannot simultaneously uphold (ii) and (iii).
missing_condition: The definition incorporates two incompatible requirements: truth-functionality, which determines truth-value solely from component truth-values, and relevance, which demands a connection of content — and no single analysis of the conditional can satisfy both.
passage: One might propose that a conditional is true when the antecedent is not true whilst the consequent is false, that its truth-value is determined by the truth-values of its components, and that it asserts a connection between the content of its antecedent and its consequent. This tripartite proposal attempts to combine the simplicity of the material conditional with the relevance that ordinary conditional reasoning seems to require. The first two conditions give the material conditional: a truth-function that is false only when the antecedent is true and the consequent false. The third condition adds a relevance requirement, ensuring that the conditional is not merely a truth-functional compound but expresses a genuine connection. Yet a case which reveals that these conditions cannot be jointly satisfied may be constructed from propositions that are both true but entirely unrelated. Suppose one asserts: 'If Paris is the capital of France, then two and two make four.' The antecedent is true. The consequent is true. Condition (i) is satisfied: it is not the case that the antecedent is true whilst the consequent is false. Condition (ii) is satisfied: the truth-value of the conditional, on any truth-functional analysis, is determined by the truth-values of its components. But condition (iii) fails entirely, for the political geography of France has no connection whatever with elementary arithmetic. One might observe that this is not merely a peripheral difficulty but a fundamental tension at the heart of the analysis. If the conditional is truth-functional, then its truth-value is fixed by its components regardless of content, and the relevance condition (iii) will inevitably be violated by pairs of true propositions drawn from unrelated domains. The definition asks for a conditional that is at once truth-functional and relevant, but these two requirements are in conflict.

definition: An inference from particular instances to a general conclusion is inductively strong if and only if (i) the observed instances are numerous, (ii) the observed instances are varied in their circumstances, and (iii) no observed instance contradicts the general conclusion.
conditions: The observed instances are numerous
The observed instances are varied in their circumstances
No observed instance contradicts the general conclusion
counterexample: Consider a scientist who has observed ten thousand swans, all in different regions of Europe, at different seasons, in different habitats — lakes, rivers, estuaries, and marshes. All are white. The instances are numerous, varied, and none contradicts the conclusion that all swans are white. All three conditions are satisfied, and the induction appears maximally strong. Yet the conclusion is false, for there exist black swans in Australia. The conditions fail to ensure inductive strength because they take no account of whether the variation in circumstances extends to the relevant variable — in this case, geographical region — that would expose the generalisation's limits.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the variation in observed instances be relevant variation — that it extend to those dimensions along which exceptions to the generalisation, if any exist, would be found.
passage: One might characterise a strong inductive inference as one supported by numerous, varied, and uncontradicted instances. This characterisation has intuitive force: we feel more confident in a generalisation when it has been confirmed many times, in many different circumstances, with no exceptions. The conditions seem to guard against both insufficient evidence and biased sampling. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of these safeguards may be drawn from natural history. Suppose a naturalist surveys the swans of Europe over many years. She observes them in England and in France, in Sweden and in Italy, in winter and in summer, on lakes and on rivers and in coastal estuaries. Her sample runs to many thousands. Every swan she observes is white. The instances are numerous, satisfying condition (i). They are varied in season, habitat, and locality, satisfying condition (ii). And none contradicts the generalisation, satisfying condition (iii). The induction to 'All swans are white' appears, on this definition, to be maximally strong. Yet the generalisation is false: swans in Australia are black. The variation in the naturalist's sample, extensive as it is, fails to extend to the dimension that matters — geographical range beyond Europe. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only that instances be varied without specifying in what respects they must vary, permits a sample to be extensively varied along irrelevant dimensions whilst remaining uniform along the dimension that would reveal the generalisation's falsity. The appearance of inductive strength is an artefact of provincial sampling. What the definition lacks is a requirement that the variation be relevant — that it probe precisely those circumstances under which the generalisation might fail.

definition: An argument A is logically valid if and only if (i) A has at least one premise and a conclusion, (ii) the conclusion follows from the premises by the rules of a recognised formal system, and (iii) no substitution instance of the argument form yields true premises and a false conclusion.
conditions: A has at least one premise and a conclusion
The conclusion follows from the premises by the rules of a recognised formal system
No substitution instance of the argument form yields true premises and a false conclusion
counterexample: Consider the argument: All horses are animals; therefore, all heads of horses are heads of animals. The conclusion follows necessarily from the premise, and no substitution of terms can produce a true premise with a false conclusion. Yet the inference cannot be derived within standard propositional logic or monadic predicate logic — it requires a logic of relations that was not always recognised as a formal system. The argument is valid, but condition (ii) is not satisfied relative to the systems available.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between validity as a property of the argument itself — the impossibility of true premises and false conclusion — and derivability within a particular formal system, which may be incomplete with respect to the full range of valid inferences.
passage: One might define logical validity as the property of an argument that has premises and a conclusion, whose conclusion follows from its premises by the rules of a recognised formal system, and whose argument form admits no substitution instance with true premises and a false conclusion. This characterisation seems to unite the syntactic and semantic aspects of validity in a satisfying manner. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from the history of logic itself. Consider the argument: all horses are animals; therefore, all heads of horses are heads of animals. The conclusion follows necessarily from the premise; no substitution of terms can produce a case in which the premise is true and the conclusion false. The semantic condition is satisfied. Yet the inference cannot be derived within standard propositional logic, nor within monadic predicate logic — it requires a logic of relations, a system whose formal recognition came comparatively late in the history of the subject. Before the development of relational logic, one might have acknowledged the argument's intuitive cogency whilst denying that it met condition (ii). It appears that the definition, by requiring derivability within a recognised formal system, confuses validity — an objective property of the argument — with our current capacity to formalise it. The semantic condition may be met whilst the syntactic condition remains unsatisfied, revealing a gap between validity and derivability that the definition cannot accommodate.

definition: An argument A is sound if and only if (i) A is logically valid, (ii) all of A's premises are true, and (iii) A's conclusion is informative — that is, not a trivial logical truth.
conditions: A is logically valid
All of A's premises are true
A's conclusion is informative — not a trivial logical truth
counterexample: Consider the argument: water is H₂O; all H₂O is composed of hydrogen and oxygen; therefore, water is composed of hydrogen and oxygen. The argument is logically valid, its premises are true, and — at least to one who does not already know the chemical composition of water — its conclusion is informative. Now consider: it is raining or it is not raining; the sky is blue; therefore, it is raining or it is not raining. This argument is valid, its premises are true, and its conclusion is a logical truth. The third condition excludes it. Yet the classical definition of soundness makes no reference to informativeness, and one should hesitate to revise the established concept merely to exclude trivial conclusions.
missing_condition: The definition imposes an informativeness requirement that is foreign to the classical concept of soundness, which concerns only the truth-preserving character of a valid argument with true premises and is properly indifferent to whether the conclusion tells us anything new.
passage: One might characterise a sound argument as one that is logically valid, has true premises, and whose conclusion is informative rather than a trivial logical truth. The addition of the third condition reflects a natural dissatisfaction with arguments that are technically impeccable but intellectually vacuous. Yet a case which tells against this amended definition may be constructed straightforwardly. Consider the argument: it is raining or it is not raining; the sky is blue; therefore, it is raining or it is not raining. The argument is valid, for the conclusion is a logical truth derivable from any premises whatsoever. Its premises are true. But its conclusion — a tautology — is by no means informative. The third condition excludes this argument from the class of sound arguments. Yet the classical concept of soundness, as it has been understood since antiquity, makes no reference to informativeness. Soundness concerns the truth-preserving character of a valid argument with true premises; it is properly indifferent to whether the conclusion tells us anything new. The proposed definition, by introducing an informativeness requirement, has subtly altered the concept it purports to analyse. It appears that one should distinguish between soundness, which is a logical property, and value or interest, which are pragmatic properties — and that the definition errs in conflating the two.

definition: A proposition Q is a logical consequence of a set of propositions P₁, ..., Pₙ if and only if (i) Q can be derived from P₁, ..., Pₙ by a finite sequence of rule applications, (ii) the derivation employs only rules that are truth-preserving, and (iii) Q cannot be derived from any proper subset of P₁, ..., Pₙ.
conditions: Q can be derived from P₁, ..., Pₙ by a finite sequence of rule applications
The derivation employs only rules that are truth-preserving
Q cannot be derived from any proper subset of P₁, ..., Pₙ
counterexample: Consider the propositions: (P₁) all men are mortal, (P₂) Socrates is a man, and (P₃) Athens is in Greece. From P₁ and P₂ alone, one may derive that Socrates is mortal. Thus the conclusion can be derived from the full set but also from the proper subset {P₁, P₂}. The third condition is violated. Yet Socrates is mortal is unquestionably a logical consequence of the full set — the addition of an irrelevant premise does not cancel the consequence relation.
missing_condition: The definition conflates logical consequence with a notion of minimal or non-redundant derivation; it fails to recognise that the consequence relation is monotonic — adding premises to a set from which Q follows cannot destroy Q's status as a consequence.
passage: One might define logical consequence as the relation that holds when a conclusion can be derived from a set of premises by truth-preserving rules and cannot be derived from any proper subset of those premises. The third condition reflects a desire to exclude redundant premises — to ensure that each premise contributes to the derivation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be readily constructed. Consider three propositions: all men are mortal, Socrates is a man, and Athens is in Greece. From the first two alone one may derive that Socrates is mortal. The conclusion is thus derivable from a proper subset of the original set, and the third condition is violated. Yet we should not hesitate to affirm that Socrates is mortal is a logical consequence of the full set. The presence of the irrelevant premise about Athens does not cancel the consequence relation. It appears that the definition confuses logical consequence with a notion of minimal or non-redundant derivation. Logical consequence, as classically understood, is monotonic: if Q follows from a set S, then Q follows from any superset of S. The proposed definition, by requiring non-derivability from proper subsets, violates this monotonicity and thereby mischaracterises the fundamental logical relation it seeks to capture.

definition: A statement S is paradoxical if and only if (i) S is self-referential, (ii) S leads to a contradiction when one attempts to assign it a truth-value, and (iii) S cannot be dismissed as merely ill-formed or meaningless.
conditions: S is self-referential
S leads to a contradiction when one attempts to assign it a truth-value
S cannot be dismissed as merely ill-formed or meaningless
counterexample: Consider the paradox of the heap (sorites): if one grain of sand does not make a heap, and adding a single grain to a non-heap never makes a heap, then no finite collection of grains can constitute a heap — yet heaps manifestly exist. This is a genuine paradox: it is not self-referential, yet it leads to a contradiction between its premises and an obvious truth, and it cannot be dismissed as ill-formed.
missing_condition: The definition restricts paradox to cases involving self-reference and thereby fails to accommodate paradoxes arising from vagueness, unexpected inferences, or the application of individually plausible principles that jointly yield an absurd conclusion.
passage: One might characterise a paradox as a self-referential statement that leads to contradiction upon the assignment of a truth-value and that cannot be dismissed as ill-formed. This account is well suited to the liar paradox and its relatives — sentences that speak of their own truth or falsity and thereby generate contradictions. Yet a case which tells against this restricted characterisation may be found in the ancient problem of the heap. If one grain of sand does not constitute a heap, and if adding a single grain to a collection that is not a heap never produces a heap, then it follows by repeated application that no finite collection of grains can constitute a heap. Yet heaps manifestly exist. The reasoning is paradoxical in the fullest sense: each premise is individually plausible, the logical steps are valid, and the conclusion is plainly false. But there is no self-reference anywhere in the argument. The paradox arises from the interaction of vagueness with classical logic rather than from a sentence referring to itself. It appears that the definition, by making self-reference a necessary condition of paradox, is too narrow. It excludes not only the sorites but also numerous other paradoxes — the paradox of the preface, the lottery puzzle, the surprise examination — in which self-reference plays no role whatsoever.
