definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) it is impossible for P to be true and Q false, (ii) P and Q share at least one non-logical term, and (iii) the truth of Q is not independently guaranteed regardless of P.
conditions: It is impossible for P to be true and Q false
P and Q share at least one non-logical term
The truth of Q is not independently guaranteed regardless of P
counterexample: Consider the proposition P: all emeralds are green, and the proposition Q: either something is green or nothing is green. Q is a logical truth — its truth is guaranteed regardless of P — violating condition (iii). Yet there is a clear sense in which P entails Q: it is impossible for P to be true and Q false, precisely because Q cannot be false at all. Classical accounts of entailment do not exclude logical truths from the class of entailed propositions.
missing_condition: The definition improperly excludes logically necessary propositions from the class of propositions that can be entailed, failing to recognise that the impossibility of combined truth-and-falsity holds trivially when the consequent is necessarily true.
passage: One might define entailment as a relation requiring the impossibility of true antecedent with false consequent, the sharing of a non-logical term, and the non-trivial status of the consequent. The second and third conditions are introduced to avoid the so-called paradoxes of material implication and strict implication. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this more restrictive account may be constructed. Consider the proposition that all emeralds are green, and the proposition that either something is green or nothing is green. The latter is a logical truth: its truth is guaranteed regardless of any contingent proposition. Thus condition (iii) is violated. Yet there is a perfectly clear sense in which the first proposition entails the second. It is impossible for the first to be true and the second false — precisely because the second cannot be false at all. Classical accounts of entailment, from strict implication to model-theoretic consequence, do not exclude logical truths from the class of propositions that may be entailed. The definition, by requiring that the consequent not be independently guaranteed, restricts entailment in a way that conflicts with its established logical usage and excludes a large class of valid entailment claims involving necessary truths.

definition: A set of propositions S is consistent if and only if (i) no proposition in S contradicts any other proposition in S, (ii) S does not contain both a proposition and its negation, and (iii) there exists a possible situation in which all members of S are true together.
conditions: No proposition in S contradicts any other proposition in S
S does not contain both a proposition and its negation
There exists a possible situation in which all members of S are true together
counterexample: Consider the set containing: all cats are mammals, all mammals are warm-blooded, and no cats are warm-blooded. No proposition directly contradicts any other in the sense of being its explicit negation, and the set does not contain a proposition together with its negation. Yet there is no possible situation in which all three are true, for together they entail both that all cats are warm-blooded and that no cats are warm-blooded. The first two conditions are met, but the third is violated.
missing_condition: The definition treats the first two conditions as if they were equivalent to the third, but they capture only explicit or surface-level contradiction; they fail to detect implicit contradictions that emerge only when the logical consequences of the propositions are drawn out.
passage: One might define the consistency of a set of propositions as the absence of contradiction between members, the non-inclusion of a proposition together with its negation, and the existence of a possible situation in which all members are jointly true. The third condition is, in fact, the standard model-theoretic criterion of consistency. Yet the first two conditions, which appear to be intended as equivalent or supplementary, fall short. Consider a set containing three propositions: all cats are mammals, all mammals are warm-blooded, and no cats are warm-blooded. No member of the set is the explicit negation of any other. No proposition directly contradicts another in the sense of flatly denying what another affirms. The first two conditions are satisfied. Yet the three propositions are jointly inconsistent: together they entail that all cats are warm-blooded and that no cats are warm-blooded. There is no possible situation in which all three hold. The third condition is violated. It appears that the first two conditions, by attending only to surface-level or explicit contradiction, fail to detect the implicit contradictions that emerge when the logical consequences of the propositions are drawn out. The definition would have been adequate had it relied solely on the third condition; the first two, far from strengthening it, actually weaken the account by suggesting that consistency can be assessed by inspection of the propositions themselves rather than by consideration of their joint satisfiability.

definition: A concept C is definable within a formal language L if and only if (i) there exists a formula φ in L such that C applies to exactly those objects satisfying φ, (ii) φ employs only the primitive symbols of L, and (iii) φ is finitely long.
conditions: There exists a formula φ in L such that C applies to exactly those objects satisfying φ
φ employs only the primitive symbols of L
φ is finitely long
counterexample: Consider the concept of a well-ordering of the natural numbers within the language of first-order arithmetic. No finitely long first-order formula can define the class of well-orderings, because first-order logic lacks the expressive resources to distinguish well-orderings from non-standard orderings. The concept is perfectly determinate and mathematically precise, yet it is not definable within first-order arithmetic. All three conditions are satisfiable only within a language of greater expressive power — second-order logic, for example — yet the concept's mathematical determinacy is not in question.
missing_condition: The definition conflates the determinacy of a concept with its expressibility in a particular formal language, failing to recognise that the definability of a concept is relative to the expressive power of the language and that a concept may be perfectly determinate yet undefinable in a given formalism.
passage: One might propose that a concept is definable within a formal language when there exists a finitely long formula in that language, employing only its primitive symbols, that applies to exactly the objects falling under the concept. This characterisation captures a natural idea: definability as the existence of an explicit linguistic specification. Yet a case which reveals its limitation may be drawn from mathematical logic. Consider the concept of a well-ordering of the natural numbers — that is, an ordering in which every non-empty subset has a least element. This is a perfectly determinate mathematical concept. Yet within the language of first-order arithmetic, no finitely long formula can define the class of well-orderings. First-order logic lacks the expressive resources to distinguish well-orderings from certain non-standard orderings that satisfy every first-order specification a well-ordering satisfies. All three conditions of the proposed definition fail to be jointly satisfiable within this language, yet the concept's mathematical determinacy is not in doubt. It is definable in second-order logic, which has greater expressive power. It appears that the definition conflates the determinacy of a concept with its expressibility in a particular formal language, overlooking the fact that definability is relative to the expressive resources available and that a concept may be perfectly clear yet beyond the reach of a given formalism.

definition: An inference rule R is valid if and only if (i) R preserves truth from premises to conclusion in every application, (ii) R is syntactically specifiable as a finite schema, and (iii) R has been accepted by the community of logicians as a standard rule.
conditions: R preserves truth from premises to conclusion in every application
R is syntactically specifiable as a finite schema
R has been accepted by the community of logicians as a standard rule
counterexample: Consider the omega-rule in arithmetic, which allows one to infer a universal statement 'for all n, P(n)' from the infinite set of premises P(0), P(1), P(2), and so on for every natural number. This rule preserves truth in every application and can be specified as a schema. But it has not been widely accepted as a standard rule by the community of logicians, precisely because it requires infinitely many premises and is thus not finitary. Yet the rule is truth-preserving and captures genuine logical relationships.
missing_condition: The definition makes validity depend upon sociological acceptance by a community rather than upon the intrinsic truth-preserving character of the rule, thereby confusing the logic of discovery with the logic of justification.
passage: One might define a valid inference rule as one that preserves truth in every application, is syntactically specifiable as a finite schema, and has been accepted by the community of logicians. The third condition is intended to exclude eccentric or non-standard rules from the canon. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this sociological amendment may be constructed from proof theory. Consider the omega-rule in arithmetic: from the infinite set of premises P(0), P(1), P(2), ..., for every natural number, one may infer the universal statement that for all n, P(n). This rule preserves truth in every application; whenever all of its infinitely many premises are true, its conclusion is true. It is specifiable as a schema. But it has not been widely accepted as a standard rule by the community of logicians, for the very good reason that it requires infinitely many premises and thus cannot be applied in finite proofs. The third condition is violated. Yet the omega-rule is truth-preserving and captures genuine logical relationships that finitary rules cannot reach. It appears that the definition, by making validity contingent upon community acceptance, confuses an intrinsic logical property — truth-preservation — with a sociological fact about the current state of logical practice.

definition: A logical paradox P is resolved if and only if (i) an error is identified in the reasoning leading to the contradictory conclusion, (ii) the identified error is correctable without abandoning any established logical principle, and (iii) the corrected reasoning yields no further contradictions.
conditions: An error is identified in the reasoning leading to the contradictory conclusion
The identified error is correctable without abandoning any established logical principle
The corrected reasoning yields no further contradictions
counterexample: Consider Russell's paradox concerning the set of all sets that do not contain themselves. The resolution — restricting the axiom of unrestricted comprehension — does require abandoning an established logical principle, namely the principle that for every property there exists a set of exactly those objects possessing it. The second condition is violated. Yet Russell's paradox is widely regarded as resolved, precisely because the abandoned principle was shown to be untenable.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for resolutions that proceed by revising or abandoning a previously established principle shown to be defective — it assumes that all paradoxes arise from errors in reasoning rather than from faulty principles.
passage: One might propose that a logical paradox is resolved when an error in the reasoning is identified, the error is correctable without abandoning any established logical principle, and the corrected reasoning yields no further contradictions. This account presupposes that paradoxes arise from mistakes in reasoning within an otherwise sound logical framework. Yet a case which tells against it may be drawn from the foundations of set theory. Russell's paradox — concerning the set of all sets that do not contain themselves — was resolved not by identifying a fallacious step in otherwise correct reasoning but by restricting the axiom of unrestricted comprehension. This axiom was an established logical principle: for every property, there exists a set of exactly those objects possessing that property. Its restriction — or outright abandonment in favour of more cautious axioms of set formation — was precisely what resolved the paradox. The second condition is thus violated: the resolution required abandoning an established principle. Yet Russell's paradox is widely regarded as resolved, and the resulting set theories have proved extraordinarily fruitful. It appears that the definition, by assuming that resolution must leave all established principles intact, cannot accommodate cases in which the paradox reveals that one of those very principles is defective and must be revised.

definition: A formal theory T is consistent if and only if (i) T does not prove a proposition and its negation, (ii) every theorem of T is true under the intended interpretation, and (iii) T has been in use for a sufficient period without any contradiction being discovered.
conditions: T does not prove a proposition and its negation
Every theorem of T is true under the intended interpretation
T has been in use for a sufficient period without any contradiction being discovered
counterexample: Consider Frege's Grundgesetze der Arithmetik, which was in use for several years and whose theorems appeared true under the intended interpretation of its logical axioms. All three conditions were satisfied until Russell's letter of 1902 revealed the inconsistency. The theory was in fact inconsistent from the moment of its formulation; the third condition — absence of discovered contradiction — was met only through ignorance.
missing_condition: The definition confuses our evidence for consistency with consistency itself; that no contradiction has yet been found is an inductive ground for confidence but not a constitutive condition of the logical property, which holds or fails independently of our awareness.
passage: One might define the consistency of a formal theory as the non-derivability of a proposition together with its negation, the truth of every theorem under the intended interpretation, and a sufficient period of use without the discovery of contradiction. The third condition introduces a pragmatic element: if a theory has survived prolonged scrutiny, it is more likely to be consistent. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this pragmatic amendment may be drawn from the history of logic itself. Consider Frege's Grundgesetze der Arithmetik, published in 1893 and supplemented in 1903. For several years the theory was in use, its theorems appeared true under the intended interpretation, and no contradiction had been found. All three conditions were satisfied. Then Russell, in a letter of 1902, revealed that the theory was inconsistent: its axiom of unrestricted comprehension permitted the derivation of a contradiction. The theory had been inconsistent from the moment of its formulation; the third condition was met only through ignorance. It appears that the definition, by including a requirement of sustained use without discovered contradiction, confuses our evidence for consistency with consistency itself. Consistency is a logical property that holds or fails independently of whether anyone has noticed.

definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A consists of a set of premises and a conclusion, (ii) the conclusion of A follows from the premises by recognised rules of inference, and (iii) the premises of A do not contradict one another.
conditions: A consists of a set of premises and a conclusion
The conclusion of A follows from the premises by recognised rules of inference
The premises of A do not contradict one another
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'All mammals are warm-blooded; all whales are mammals; therefore, all whales are warm-blooded.' This has premises and a conclusion, the conclusion follows by the recognised rule of the hypothetical syllogism, and the premises are mutually consistent. All three conditions are satisfied, and the argument is indeed valid. Now consider the argument: 'All cats are animals; therefore, all cats are animals or all fish can fly.' The conclusion follows from the premise by the rule of disjunction introduction, the premise set is consistent, and the argument has the required structure. All three conditions are met. Yet the conclusion introduces a disjunct — 'all fish can fly' — that is entirely unrelated to the premise and that most reasoners would regard as an objectionable dilution of the argument's content, raising the question of whether formal validity alone captures what we mean by a conclusion genuinely following from premises.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the conclusion be relevant to the premises in a substantive sense — that the inferential connection reflect a genuine logical dependence of the conclusion's content upon the content of the premises, rather than a merely formal derivability that permits the introduction of arbitrary material through rules like disjunction introduction.
passage: One might define a valid argument as one whose conclusion follows from its premises by recognised rules of inference, where the premises form a consistent set. This formulation captures the central idea of deductive logic: that the conclusion is guaranteed by the premises, and that the premises are not internally defective. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed by exploiting a familiar rule of inference. Let us consider the argument whose sole premise is 'All cats are animals' and whose conclusion is 'All cats are animals or all fish can fly.' The conclusion follows from the premise by the rule of disjunction introduction, which permits one to derive a disjunction from any established proposition by appending an arbitrary disjunct. The premise set is trivially consistent, containing only one member. The argument possesses the requisite structure of premises and conclusion. All three conditions of the proposed definition are thus satisfied. Yet one might observe that the argument, though formally valid, introduces into the conclusion a claim — that all fish can fly — which has no connection whatever to the premise. The conclusion is weaker than, and in part entirely alien to, the content of the premise from which it is derived. It appears that the definition, by requiring only structural integrity, consistency, and derivability by recognised rules, fails to demand that the conclusion bear a substantive relevance to the premises — that the logical connection reflect a genuine dependence of the conclusion's content upon the content from which it is drawn.

definition: An argument A is sound if and only if (i) A is deductively valid, (ii) all premises of A are true, and (iii) the conclusion of A is distinct from any of the premises.
conditions: A is deductively valid
All premises of A are true
The conclusion of A is distinct from any of the premises
counterexample: Consider the argument: 'All terrestrial vertebrates breathe air; all horses are terrestrial vertebrates; therefore, all horses breathe air.' This argument is valid, its premises are true, and the conclusion is distinct from both premises. All three conditions are satisfied, and the argument is sound in the fullest sense. Now consider the argument: 'All objects with mass attract other objects with mass; the Earth has mass; therefore, some object with mass attracts other objects with mass.' The argument is valid, the premises are true, and the conclusion is distinct from either premise. All conditions are met. Yet the conclusion is a trivial weakening of the first premise, deducible from it alone without the second premise; the second premise is entirely idle.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that all premises be necessary for the derivation of the conclusion — that each premise make an indispensable contribution to the inferential support of the conclusion, so that the argument is not padded with premises that, though true, play no role in establishing the result.
passage: One might propose that a sound argument is a deductively valid argument with true premises and a conclusion distinct from any premise. This definition unites the logical virtues of validity and truth while excluding trivial cases in which the conclusion merely restates a premise. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed as follows. Consider the argument: 'All objects with mass attract other objects with mass; the Earth has mass; therefore, some object with mass attracts other objects with mass.' The argument is valid: the conclusion follows from the premises. The premises are true. And the conclusion, being an existential claim, is distinct from both the universal first premise and the singular second premise. Each condition is satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the second premise — that the Earth has mass — is entirely idle. The conclusion follows from the first premise alone, by instantiation to any object with mass. The argument contains a premise that, though true, makes no contribution whatever to the derivation of the conclusion. We should hesitate to call such an argument sound in the fullest sense, for soundness, properly understood, should require not merely that the premises be true and the conclusion follow, but that the premises collectively and individually contribute to establishing the conclusion. It appears that the definition, by requiring only validity, truth, and distinctness, fails to demand that each premise be indispensable to the derivation.

definition: A sentence S is paradoxical if and only if (i) S appears to be well-formed and meaningful, (ii) S leads, by apparently sound reasoning, to a contradiction, and (iii) S does not involve any obvious equivocation or ambiguity.
conditions: S appears to be well-formed and meaningful
S leads, by apparently sound reasoning, to a contradiction
S does not involve any obvious equivocation or ambiguity
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'This sentence has five words.' It is well-formed and meaningful. By counting its words — 'This,' 'sentence,' 'has,' 'five,' 'words' — we find that it has five words, so it is true. Now consider 'This sentence has four words.' It is equally well-formed and meaningful, free of equivocation. By counting, the sentence has five words, not four, so it is false. Now consider a sentence that says of itself that it has a prime number of words and also a composite number of words: 'This sentence simultaneously has exactly seven words and exactly six words.' The sentence is well-formed and appears meaningful. It leads, by straightforward counting, to the conclusion that it is false in both conjuncts, which is a contradiction with the appearance of meaningfulness. There is no equivocation. Yet the sentence is simply self-contradictory rather than paradoxical — it is a straightforward falsehood dressed up in self-referential clothing, not a genuine paradox that challenges our logical principles.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the contradiction be genuinely surprising or resistant to resolution — that the reasoning which produces it expose a deep tension in our accepted logical principles or conceptual framework, rather than merely exhibiting an obvious self-contradiction that can be dismissed as a straightforward error.
passage: One might define a paradoxical sentence as one that is well-formed and meaningful, leads by apparently sound reasoning to a contradiction, and involves no obvious equivocation or ambiguity. This characterisation captures the discomfiting quality that we associate with genuine paradoxes: they present us with an apparently impeccable chain of reasoning that terminates in absurdity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed without great difficulty. Consider the sentence 'This sentence simultaneously has exactly seven words and exactly six words.' The sentence is grammatically well-formed and appears meaningful — it makes a claim about its own word-count. It involves no equivocation; each word is used in its standard sense. By straightforward counting, the sentence has neither seven words nor six words (it has, in fact, nine), so both conjuncts are false. The appearance of meaningfulness combined with this result produces something that mimics the form of a contradiction arising from apparently sound reasoning: the sentence seems to say something determinate, yet what it says cannot be the case. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet the sentence is not genuinely paradoxical. It is merely self-contradictory in an obvious and unremarkable way. A genuine paradox — such as the liar or Russell's class — challenges our fundamental logical principles and resists easy resolution. It appears that the definition, by requiring only well-formedness, the derivation of contradiction, and the absence of equivocation, fails to require that the contradiction be genuinely surprising and resistant to resolution, exposing a deep tension in our conceptual framework rather than a trivial self-defeat.

definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) it is impossible for P to be true and Q to be false, (ii) P and Q are both meaningful propositions, and (iii) Q is not a logical truth.
conditions: It is impossible for P to be true and Q to be false
P and Q are both meaningful propositions
Q is not a logical truth
counterexample: Let P be the proposition 'It is raining in London' and let Q be the disjunction 'It is raining in London or the Eiffel Tower is in Paris.' It is impossible for P to be true and Q to be false, since P is a disjunct of Q and the truth of a disjunct guarantees the truth of the disjunction. Both P and Q are meaningful propositions. And Q is not a logical truth — there are possible circumstances in which it does not rain in London and the Eiffel Tower is not in Paris, so that Q would be false. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might hesitate to say that P entails Q, since Q contains a component — that the Eiffel Tower is in Paris — which is entirely irrelevant to the content of P, and the connection between P and Q is merely a by-product of the logical structure of disjunction rather than a reflection of any substantive relation between the contents of the two propositions.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the content of Q be wholly contained in or derivable from the content of P — that the entailment relation reflect a genuine connection between the subject-matters of the two propositions, rather than a formal guarantee that happens to obtain through the structural properties of logical connectives.
passage: One might define entailment by saying that P entails Q when it is impossible for P to be true and Q false, where both are meaningful propositions and Q is not a logical truth. The exclusion of logical truths from Q is meant to ensure that entailment is not trivialised by the fact that necessary truths are entailed by everything. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed from ordinary propositions. Let P be the proposition that it is raining in London, and let Q be the disjunction that either it is raining in London or the Eiffel Tower is in Paris. It is impossible for P to be true and Q to be false, since P is a disjunct of Q and the truth of any disjunct guarantees the truth of the disjunction. Both propositions are meaningful. And Q is not a logical truth, for there are possible circumstances in which neither disjunct obtains. Each of the three conditions of the definition is thus satisfied. Yet one might observe that there is something objectionable about saying that the fact that it is raining in London entails a proposition partly about the location of the Eiffel Tower. The second disjunct of Q is entirely irrelevant to the content of P. The formal guarantee that Q cannot be false when P is true is merely a by-product of the logical structure of disjunction, not a reflection of any genuine content-connection between the two propositions. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the impossibility of the relevant truth-value combination together with meaningfulness and the non-logical-truth status of Q, fails to demand that the entailment reflect a substantive relation between the subject-matters of the propositions involved.

definition: A set of propositions S is consistent if and only if (i) no proposition in S is the negation of another proposition in S, (ii) S contains no proposition that is itself a contradiction, and (iii) each proposition in S is individually possible.
conditions: No proposition in S is the negation of another proposition in S
S contains no proposition that is itself a contradiction
Each proposition in S is individually possible
counterexample: Consider the set S containing three propositions: 'All swans in this lake are white,' 'All swans in this lake are black,' and 'There is at least one swan in this lake.' No proposition in S is the explicit negation of another — 'All swans are white' is not the negation of 'All swans are black.' No proposition is itself a contradiction; each is individually possible. All three conditions are met. Yet S is inconsistent, for the three propositions together entail that some swan is both white and black, which is impossible.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the propositions in S be jointly satisfiable — that there exist a possible state of affairs in which all propositions in S are simultaneously true — rather than merely individually possible and free from explicit contradictions and direct negation pairs.
passage: One might propose that a set of propositions is consistent when no member is the negation of another, no member is itself a contradiction, and each member is individually possible. This formulation captures three natural requirements: the absence of direct negation pairs, the absence of self-contradictory elements, and the individual coherence of each proposition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be readily constructed. Consider a set S comprising three propositions: that all swans in a certain lake are white, that all swans in that same lake are black, and that there is at least one swan in the lake. No proposition in S is the negation of another — the universal claim that all swans are white is not the negation of the universal claim that all swans are black, since both could be vacuously true were the lake empty of swans. No proposition in S is a contradiction in itself; each is perfectly intelligible and individually possible. The three conditions of the proposed definition are thus satisfied. Nevertheless, the set is manifestly inconsistent. If there is at least one swan in the lake, and all swans in the lake are white, and all swans in the lake are black, then some swan must be simultaneously white and black, which is impossible. It appears that the definition, by attending only to direct negation, self-contradiction, and individual possibility, fails to require what is essential to consistency — namely, that the propositions be jointly satisfiable, that there exist some possible state of affairs in which all members of the set are true at once.

definition: A proposition Q is a logical consequence of a set of propositions S if and only if (i) every model in which all members of S are true is a model in which Q is true, (ii) Q is expressible in the same formal language as the members of S, and (iii) the derivation of Q from S requires no more than finitely many steps.
conditions: Every model in which all members of S are true is a model in which Q is true
Q is expressible in the same formal language as the members of S
The derivation of Q from S requires no more than finitely many steps
counterexample: Suppose S contains a single proposition expressed in the language of arithmetic: 'For every natural number n, if n is greater than 2, then n can be expressed as the sum of two prime numbers.' Let Q be the proposition 'Zero equals zero,' expressed in the same language. Every model in which S is true is trivially a model in which Q is true, since Q is true in every model of arithmetic whatsoever. Q is expressible in the language of arithmetic. And Q can be derived in a single step, as a theorem of identity. All three conditions are met. Yet it seems absurd to say that the proposition that zero equals zero is a logical consequence of the Goldbach conjecture; the two propositions have no connection in content.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that Q's truth in those models depend upon the truth of the members of S — that the members of S be responsible for Q's holding, so that the consequence relation reflects a genuine logical dependence rather than the trivial fact that Q happens to be true in every model regardless of S.
passage: One might define logical consequence by saying that Q is a logical consequence of a set S when every model satisfying S satisfies Q, when Q is expressible in the same formal language as S, and when the derivation proceeds in finitely many steps. This characterisation captures the model-theoretic core of the consequence relation and adds requirements of linguistic uniformity and finitary derivability. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from elementary arithmetic. Let S contain the single proposition that every natural number greater than two can be expressed as the sum of two primes — a proposition whose truth-value remains, for present purposes, beside the point. Let Q be the trivial identity that zero equals zero. Every model in which S is true is a model in which Q is true, because Q is true in every model of arithmetic whatsoever. Q is expressed in the same formal language. And Q can be derived in a single step as an instance of the reflexivity of identity. Each of the three conditions is met. Yet it would be absurd to say that the identity of zero with itself is a logical consequence of the Goldbach conjecture. The two propositions are entirely unrelated; Q's truth in every model has nothing to do with S. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that Q hold in every S-model, linguistic uniformity, and finite derivability, fails to require that Q's truth depend upon the members of S — that S be responsible for Q's holding, rather than Q being a proposition that would hold regardless.

definition: A concept C is formally definable within a system T if and only if (i) there exists a formula F in the language of T that captures all and only the instances of C, (ii) F is finitely expressible, and (iii) F does not employ C itself or any synonym of C.
conditions: There exists a formula F in the language of T that captures all and only the instances of C
F is finitely expressible
F does not employ C itself or any synonym of C
counterexample: Consider the concept of a prime number. Suppose a system T contains the formula 'n is a natural number greater than one such that for every pair of natural numbers a and b, if n equals a times b, then a equals one or b equals one.' This formula captures all and only the prime numbers, is finitely expressible, and does not use the term 'prime' or any synonym. All three conditions are satisfied. But now consider a pathological variant: let T be a system in which the quantifiers range over a non-standard model of arithmetic containing elements that are not genuine natural numbers. In such a model, the formula may capture entities that are not genuinely prime. The definition would count the concept as formally defined within T, even though the formula's extension in T does not correspond to the intended concept.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the models of T over which the formula is evaluated be the intended models of the subject-matter — that the formula capture the instances of C not merely relative to some model of T but relative to the intended interpretation, so that the formal definability is not an artefact of an unintended model.
passage: One might propose that a concept is formally definable within a system when some formula in the language of that system captures all and only the concept's instances, is finitely expressible, and avoids circularity by not employing the concept or any synonym. This characterisation captures the central desiderata of formal definition: extensional adequacy, finite expressibility, and non-circularity. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the theory of numbers. Consider the concept of a prime number and the formula 'n is a natural number greater than one such that for every pair of natural numbers a and b, if n equals a times b, then a equals one or b equals one.' This formula captures all and only the primes in the standard model of arithmetic, is finitely expressed, and contains neither the word 'prime' nor any synonym. Each of the three conditions is satisfied. However, suppose the system T in question admits non-standard models of arithmetic — models containing elements that satisfy the axioms but are not genuine natural numbers. In such a model, the formula may capture entities that are not genuinely prime, because the non-standard elements can satisfy the formal conditions without corresponding to any actual prime number. The definition would count the concept as formally defined within T, even though the formula's extension in a non-standard model fails to correspond to the intended concept. It appears that the definition, by requiring only extensional adequacy, finite expressibility, and non-circularity, fails to require that the formula capture the instances of the concept relative to the intended interpretation of the system, rather than relative to some arbitrary model.

definition: A rule of inference R is valid if and only if (i) R preserves truth from premises to conclusion in every possible case, (ii) R can be stated as a finite schema, and (iii) R has been accepted in standard logical practice.
conditions: R preserves truth from premises to conclusion in every possible case
R can be stated as a finite schema
R has been accepted in standard logical practice
counterexample: Consider the rule of inference known as explosion or ex falso quodlibet: from a contradiction, anything follows. This rule preserves truth in the vacuous sense that no case exists in which the premises (a contradiction) are all true, so the condition of truth-preservation is trivially met. The rule can be stated as a finite schema. And it has long been accepted in standard classical logical practice. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet many logicians have argued that a rule which permits the derivation of any conclusion whatever from contradictory premises is not valid in any robust sense, since it licenses conclusions that bear no connection to the premises.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that R preserve truth in a non-vacuous manner — that there exist genuine cases in which the premises are true and the rule delivers a true conclusion, so that the truth-preservation is substantive rather than trivially guaranteed by the impossibility of the premises being jointly true.
passage: One might define a valid rule of inference as one that preserves truth in every possible case, can be stated as a finite schema, and has been accepted in standard logical practice. This characterisation captures the thought that a valid rule must be truth-preserving, formally specifiable, and endorsed by the logical community. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from a rule that has generated considerable controversy. The rule of explosion, or ex falso quodlibet, permits the derivation of any proposition whatever from a contradiction. Since no possible case exists in which the premises of a contradictory pair are jointly true, the condition of truth-preservation is trivially satisfied: there is no case in which the premises are true and the conclusion false, because there is no case in which the premises are true at all. The rule can be stated finitely as a schema. And it has been accepted in standard classical logic for centuries. Each of the three conditions is thus met. Nevertheless, many careful logicians have questioned the validity of this rule. It permits the derivation of utterly irrelevant conclusions from contradictory premises; from the conjunction of 'it is raining' and 'it is not raining,' one may derive 'the moon is made of cheese.' The truth-preservation is real but vacuous, resting entirely on the impossibility of the premises being true. It appears that the definition, by requiring only truth-preservation, finite stateability, and acceptance in practice, fails to require that the truth-preservation be non-vacuous — that there exist genuine cases in which the premises hold and the rule delivers a substantive result.

definition: A set of propositions constitutes a paradox if and only if (i) each proposition in the set is individually plausible, (ii) the propositions are jointly inconsistent, and (iii) the set contains at least three propositions.
conditions: Each proposition in the set is individually plausible
The propositions are jointly inconsistent
The set contains at least three propositions
counterexample: Consider the following three propositions: 'London is in England,' 'Paris is in France,' and 'No city is in both England and France.' Each proposition is individually plausible — indeed, each is true. The propositions are jointly inconsistent only if one interprets them as requiring some city to be in both countries, but in fact they are perfectly consistent. Let us adjust: consider 'Every integer is either even or odd,' 'The number two is even,' and 'The number two is odd.' Each proposition, taken in isolation, might seem individually plausible to a careless reasoner. They are jointly inconsistent. The set contains three propositions. All conditions are met. Yet this is not a paradox; it is simply a set containing one false proposition whose falsity is immediately apparent upon minimal reflection.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the inconsistency be non-obvious and resistant to easy resolution — that no single proposition in the set be identifiable, upon ordinary reflection, as the clearly false member, so that the inconsistency generates genuine perplexity rather than a trivially defective set of claims.
passage: One might propose that a paradox is constituted by a set of at least three individually plausible propositions that are jointly inconsistent. This formulation captures the idea that paradoxes arise from the collision of separately reasonable commitments. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed from arithmetic. Consider the set containing the propositions that every integer is either even or odd, that the number two is even, and that the number two is odd. A careless reasoner might find each proposition individually plausible — the first is a familiar principle, and the second and third each predicate a common property of a familiar number. The propositions are jointly inconsistent, since the first and second together entail that two is not odd, contradicting the third. The set contains three members. All conditions of the definition are thus satisfied. Yet we should not regard this as a paradox. The third proposition — that two is odd — is immediately recognisable as false upon the slightest reflection. The inconsistency generates no genuine perplexity; it is resolved the moment one identifies the obviously false member. A genuine paradox, by contrast, is one in which the inconsistency resists easy resolution because no single proposition stands out as the clearly defective one. It appears that the definition, by requiring only individual plausibility, joint inconsistency, and a minimum of three propositions, fails to demand that the inconsistency be non-obvious and resistant to facile resolution.

definition: A set of axioms S entails a theorem T if and only if (i) T is derivable from S using the inference rules of the system, (ii) S is consistent, and (iii) T is not already an axiom of S.
conditions: T is derivable from S using the inference rules of the system
S is consistent
T is not already an axiom of S
counterexample: Consider a set of axioms S for elementary group theory, and let T be a trivial theorem derivable in a single step by substituting the identity element into one of the axioms. T is derivable from S, S is consistent, and T is not itself an axiom. All three conditions are met. Yet the derivation is so immediate and the theorem so trivially related to the axioms that it seems misleading to say S entails T in a substantively informative sense; T is merely a notational variant of an axiom obtained by a mechanical substitution, conveying no new mathematical content.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that T convey content that goes beyond what is immediately apparent from the axioms by trivial substitution or instantiation — that the entailment involve a genuine logical step yielding a proposition whose truth is not transparently obvious from the axioms themselves.
passage: One might define axiom-to-theorem entailment by requiring that the theorem be derivable from the axioms, that the axiom set be consistent, and that the theorem not already be an axiom. This formulation ensures derivability, non-triviality in the sense of excluding mere repetition, and the coherence of the axiom base. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from elementary algebra. Consider a standard axiomatisation of group theory, and let T be the proposition obtained by substituting the identity element for the variable in the axiom that every element has an inverse. The result states that the identity element has an inverse, which is the identity element itself. T is derivable in a single mechanical step. The axiom set is consistent. And T is not itself an axiom — it is a substitution instance. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that T conveys no genuinely new mathematical content. It is a notational variant of the axiom, obtained by the most trivial of operations, and its truth is transparently obvious to anyone who has read the axioms. To say that the axiom set entails this theorem in a substantively informative sense would be misleading. It appears that the definition, by requiring only derivability, consistency, and non-identity with an axiom, fails to demand that the theorem represent a genuine logical advance — that it convey content whose truth is not immediately transparent from the axioms by trivial substitution.

definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A consists of a set of premises and a conclusion, (ii) the conclusion of A follows from the premises by the application of accepted rules of inference, and (iii) A contains no informal fallacies.
conditions: A consists of a set of premises and a conclusion
The conclusion of A follows from the premises by the application of accepted rules of inference
A contains no informal fallacies
counterexample: Consider a case in which an argument proceeds as follows: from the premise that all mammals are warm-blooded and the premise that all warm-blooded creatures are mammals, one infers by two applications of universal instantiation and hypothetical syllogism that all mammals are mammals. The argument consists of premises and a conclusion; the conclusion follows from the premises by accepted rules of inference; and the argument contains no informal fallacy. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the conclusion is a trivial tautology that conveys no information, and the second premise is false. The argument is valid in the technical sense that the conclusion follows from the premises, but the definition fails to distinguish between substantive and trivially circular valid arguments.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the conclusion of A be non-trivially derivable from the premises — that the derivation not reduce to a mere restatement of what is already contained, explicitly or by immediate implication, in the premises themselves.
passage: One might propose that an argument is valid when it consists of premises and a conclusion, the conclusion follows from the premises by accepted rules of inference, and the argument contains no informal fallacies. This characterisation captures the structural, inferential, and non-fallacious character that we associate with valid reasoning. Yet a case which tells against the proposed definition may be constructed from elementary syllogistic logic. Suppose one begins with the premise that all mammals are warm-blooded and the premise that all warm-blooded creatures are mammals. From these two premises, by universal instantiation and hypothetical syllogism, one may derive the conclusion that all mammals are mammals. The argument consists of a set of premises and a conclusion. The conclusion follows from the premises by the application of accepted rules of inference. And the argument contains no informal fallacy — it commits neither equivocation nor appeal to authority nor any other recognised informal error. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the conclusion is a trivial logical truth that conveys no information beyond what is already implicit in the concept of identity. The argument is, in a sense, vacuously valid: its conclusion cannot fail to follow from any set of premises whatsoever, and the derivation adds nothing to our understanding. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the conclusion follow by accepted rules and that no informal fallacy be present, fails to distinguish between arguments whose conclusions are substantively derived from the premises and arguments whose conclusions are trivially guaranteed regardless of what the premises assert.

definition: A statement S is paradoxical if and only if (i) S is constructed from well-formed expressions of a given language, (ii) S gives rise to a contradiction when evaluated according to the standard rules of that language, and (iii) S does not depend upon any ambiguity or equivocation for its contradictory character.
conditions: S is constructed from well-formed expressions of a given language
S gives rise to a contradiction when evaluated according to the standard rules of that language
S does not depend upon any ambiguity or equivocation for its contradictory character
counterexample: Suppose we consider the statement 'This sentence has exactly five words.' The statement is well-formed, and when evaluated according to the standard rules of English, it gives rise to a peculiar situation: if it is true, then it must have exactly five words, but upon counting we find it has six, so it is false; yet nothing in the falsity generates a contradiction in the logical sense, for the statement is simply and determinately false. It is not paradoxical. Now consider the strengthened version: 'This sentence is not true.' Evaluated by standard semantic rules, it yields a contradiction without equivocation. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that many theorists would not regard this as a genuine paradox but rather as evidence that the standard semantic rules themselves require revision — the contradiction signals a defect in the rules, not a genuinely paradoxical statement.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the contradiction generated by S cannot be dissolved by a principled revision of the rules of the language, thereby failing to distinguish between statements that reveal genuine logical paradox and statements that merely expose defects in a particular set of semantic rules.
passage: One might analyse the concept of paradox by proposing that a statement is paradoxical when it is well-formed, gives rise to a contradiction under the standard rules of its language, and does not depend upon ambiguity for its contradictory character. This account captures the formal, rule-governed, and non-equivocal aspects of what we ordinarily regard as paradoxical. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by attending to the liar-type sentences that have exercised logicians since antiquity. Suppose we consider the statement 'This sentence is not true.' The statement is constructed from well-formed expressions of English. When evaluated according to the standard semantic rules — rules which assign truth-values to declarative sentences and allow self-reference — it generates a contradiction: if it is true, then it is not true, and if it is not true, then it is true. And this contradiction does not depend upon any ambiguity or equivocation in the terms employed. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that a substantial tradition in logic and the philosophy of language regards this not as a genuine paradox but as evidence that the standard semantic rules are themselves defective — that unrestricted self-reference, or the assumption that every well-formed sentence must be either true or not true, ought to be abandoned or restricted. On this view, the contradiction signals not a paradoxical statement but an inadequate set of rules, and once the rules are suitably revised, the contradiction dissolves. It appears that the definition, by requiring only well-formedness, contradiction under standard rules, and non-equivocation, cannot distinguish between statements that are genuinely and irreducibly paradoxical and those whose contradictory character merely indicates that the prevailing rules of the language stand in need of principled revision.

definition: An argument A is sound if and only if (i) A is deductively valid, (ii) all premises of A are true, and (iii) A employs no suppressed premises that are themselves unwarranted.
conditions: A is deductively valid
All premises of A are true
A employs no suppressed premises that are themselves unwarranted
counterexample: Suppose one constructs the following argument: Premise 1, all things identical to Socrates are mortal; Premise 2, Socrates is identical to Socrates; Conclusion, Socrates is mortal. The argument is deductively valid. Both premises are true. And the argument employs no suppressed premises whatsoever. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the argument is not sound in any philosophically interesting sense: its first premise is merely a disguised restatement of its conclusion in universally quantified form, and the argument moves from a particular truth about Socrates to itself by a trivially circular route. It establishes nothing that was not already assumed.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the premises of A be inferentially independent of the conclusion — that the truth of the premises be establishable without presupposing or merely restating the conclusion, thereby excluding arguments that are trivially circular despite being technically valid and having true premises.
passage: One might propose that an argument is sound when it is deductively valid, all its premises are true, and it employs no suppressed premises that are unwarranted. This characterisation unites logical rigour with factual accuracy and transparency of inferential structure. Yet a case which tells against the proposed definition may be constructed from an argument about mortality. Suppose one argues as follows: all things identical to Socrates are mortal; Socrates is identical to Socrates; therefore, Socrates is mortal. The argument is deductively valid, for the conclusion follows necessarily from the premises by universal instantiation. Both premises are true — everything identical to Socrates is indeed mortal, and Socrates is certainly identical to herself. No suppressed premises are in play; the argument is entirely explicit. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the argument is not sound in any philosophically illuminating sense. The first premise — that all things identical to Socrates are mortal — is nothing more than a universally quantified restatement of the very conclusion it is meant to support. The argument moves from a disguised form of its conclusion to the conclusion itself by a trivially circular route. While it satisfies the formal requirements of validity and the material requirement of true premises, it fails to establish anything that was not already presupposed in the act of affirming its first premise. It appears that the definition, by requiring only validity, truth of premises, and absence of unwarranted suppressed premises, cannot exclude arguments whose premises are inferentially dependent upon the conclusion — arguments in which the truth of a premise can be recognised only by one who already accepts the very proposition that the argument purports to demonstrate.

definition: A proposition Q is a logical consequence of a set of propositions P1, P2, ... Pn if and only if (i) there is no possible interpretation under which all of P1 through Pn are true and Q is false, (ii) the derivation of Q from P1 through Pn can be carried out within a formal system with specified rules, and (iii) the derivation does not require the introduction of any propositions not among P1 through Pn or the logical axioms of the system.
conditions: There is no possible interpretation under which all of P1 through Pn are true and Q is false
The derivation of Q from P1 through Pn can be carried out within a formal system with specified rules
The derivation does not require the introduction of any propositions not among P1 through Pn or the logical axioms of the system
counterexample: Let us imagine a case involving a first-order formal system whose axioms include a sentence that is in fact logically true but whose truth is not recognisable from the axioms of the system alone. Suppose Q is a proposition of such complexity that its truth under every interpretation can be established only by semantic reasoning that outstrips the derivational resources of the formal system — that is, Q is logically true and hence a consequence of any set of premises, yet no derivation of Q from any set of premises can be carried out within the system because the system is incomplete with respect to Q. Condition (i) is satisfied, since there is no interpretation under which Q is false. Yet condition (ii) fails. The definition thus rules that Q is not a logical consequence of P1 through Pn, despite the fact that, semantically, Q follows from every set of premises.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that the semantic notion of logical consequence and the syntactic notion of formal derivability may diverge in systems that are not complete — it conflates consequence with derivability and thereby excludes genuine consequences that are not derivable within a particular formal system.
passage: One might propose that a proposition is a logical consequence of a set of premises when there is no possible interpretation under which the premises are all true and the conclusion false, when the conclusion can be derived within a formal system, and when the derivation introduces no propositions beyond the premises and the system's logical axioms. This characterisation attempts to unite the semantic and syntactic conceptions of consequence into a single account. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the study of incomplete formal systems. Suppose we are working within a first-order formal system that is sound but not complete — a system in which every derivable sentence is logically true, but not every logically true sentence is derivable. Let Q be a proposition of sufficient complexity that its truth under every interpretation can be established only by semantic reasoning that outstrips the derivational resources of the system. Then Q is logically true: there is no interpretation under which Q is false, and hence there is no interpretation under which any set of premises P1 through Pn are all true and Q is false. Condition (i) is satisfied. Yet condition (ii) fails, for no derivation of Q from P1 through Pn can be carried out within the system. The definition thus pronounces that Q is not a logical consequence of the premises, despite the fact that, on the semantic conception, Q follows from every set of premises whatsoever. One might observe that the definition, by requiring both semantic and syntactic conditions simultaneously, conflates two notions of consequence that may diverge whenever the formal system in question is incomplete. It appears that a satisfactory account must either adopt a purely semantic criterion, a purely syntactic one, or explicitly address the relationship between the two.

definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) it is impossible for P to be true and Q to be false, (ii) the impossibility in (i) is a matter of the logical form of P and Q rather than of their particular content, and (iii) Q is not itself a necessary truth.
conditions: It is impossible for P to be true and Q to be false
The impossibility in (i) is a matter of the logical form of P and Q rather than of their particular content
Q is not itself a necessary truth
counterexample: Suppose P is the proposition that it is raining and it is not raining. P is a logical contradiction and hence necessarily false. Since it is impossible for P to be true at all, it is a fortiori impossible for P to be true and Q to be false, regardless of what Q is. Let Q be any contingent proposition — say, that there are exactly seven planets. Condition (i) is satisfied. The impossibility is a matter of the logical form of P (a conjunction of a proposition with its negation), so condition (ii) is satisfied. And Q is not a necessary truth, so condition (iii) is satisfied. All three conditions are met. Yet it seems wrong to say that a contradiction entails an arbitrary contingent proposition, for there is no intelligible connection between the content of P and the content of Q.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that there be a relevant connection between the content or subject matter of P and the content of Q, thereby admitting the so-called paradox of entailment in which contradictions entail arbitrary propositions with which they have no substantive relation.
passage: One might analyse entailment by proposing that P entails Q when it is impossible for P to be true and Q false, when this impossibility derives from logical form rather than particular content, and when Q is not itself a necessary truth. This account captures the modal force, formal character, and non-triviality that we associate with genuine entailment. Yet a case which tells against the proposed definition may be constructed from the behaviour of contradictory premises. Suppose P is the proposition that it is raining and it is not raining. P is a logical contradiction, necessarily false by virtue of its form. Since P cannot be true under any circumstances, it is impossible for P to be true and any proposition Q to be false. Let Q be the contingent proposition that there are exactly seven planets. Condition (i) is satisfied, for the impossibility is guaranteed. The impossibility arises from the logical form of P — a conjunction of a proposition with its own negation — so condition (ii) is satisfied. And Q, being a contingent proposition about the number of planets, is certainly not a necessary truth, so condition (iii) is satisfied. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to affirm that the contradiction entails the proposition about the number of planets. There is no intelligible connection between P and Q; the entailment holds solely because the premise is impossible, not because the conclusion bears any relevant relation to the premise. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to the impossibility of joint truth and falsity, the formal ground of that impossibility, and the contingency of the conclusion, fails to require any substantive connection between the premise and the conclusion. It appears that a satisfactory account of entailment must demand that the content or subject matter of the conclusion be relevantly connected to that of the premise.

definition: A set of propositions S is consistent if and only if (i) no proposition in S is the negation of another proposition in S, (ii) no proposition in S is self-contradictory, and (iii) S contains only well-formed propositions of the language in question.
conditions: No proposition in S is the negation of another proposition in S
No proposition in S is self-contradictory
S contains only well-formed propositions of the language in question
counterexample: Consider the set S containing three propositions: (a) all swans are white, (b) all white things are visible, and (c) some swans are not visible. No proposition in S is the direct negation of another; no proposition is self-contradictory; and all three are well-formed. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet the set is inconsistent, for the conjunction of (a) and (b) entails that all swans are visible, which contradicts (c). The inconsistency arises not from any direct negation or self-contradiction but from the logical interrelations among the three propositions taken jointly.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that no contradiction be derivable from the propositions in S taken jointly — it checks only for direct negation pairs and individual self-contradictions, overlooking inconsistencies that emerge from the logical interrelations among three or more propositions.
passage: One might propose that a set of propositions is consistent when no proposition in the set is the negation of another, no proposition is self-contradictory, and all propositions are well-formed. This characterisation captures the idea that consistency requires the absence of overt conflict between the members of the set. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from elementary syllogistic reasoning. Suppose we consider a set S containing three propositions: first, that all swans are white; second, that all white things are visible; and third, that some swans are not visible. No proposition in S is the direct negation of another — none says that not all swans are white, or that not all white things are visible, or that all swans are visible. No proposition in S is self-contradictory: each is a perfectly coherent universal or particular claim. And all three propositions are well-formed sentences of English with determinate logical structure. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the set is not consistent. The conjunction of the first two propositions logically entails that all swans are visible, which stands in direct contradiction with the third proposition that some swans are not visible. The inconsistency arises not from any pairwise negation or from self-contradiction within an individual proposition, but from the logical interrelations among the three propositions taken jointly. It appears that the definition, by checking only for direct negation pairs and individual self-contradictions, fails to require that no contradiction be derivable from the members of the set considered as a whole. A satisfactory account of consistency must demand that the propositions be jointly satisfiable, not merely free of overt pairwise conflict.

definition: A concept C is definable within a formal language L if and only if (i) there exists a formula F in L such that F applies to all and only those objects falling under C, (ii) F is finitely expressible in L, and (iii) the extension of C is determinate — for every object, it is settled whether the object falls under C or not.
conditions: There exists a formula F in L such that F applies to all and only those objects falling under C
F is finitely expressible in L
The extension of C is determinate
counterexample: Let us imagine the concept of a natural number that is not a member of any set definable by a formula of L. Suppose L is a first-order language of arithmetic with standard syntax. The concept has a determinate extension: for each natural number, it is settled whether it falls under the concept (assuming a fixed model). And if a formula F existed in L capturing the concept, it would be finitely expressible. Yet the concept cannot be defined in L, because any formula of L that purported to capture it would itself be a formula of L, and the number picked out by that formula would then need to both satisfy and fail to satisfy the defining condition. All three conditions appear satisfiable in principle, yet the concept resists definition due to a self-referential obstacle invisible to the stated requirements.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the concept C be free from self-referential entanglement with the defining resources of L itself — that the characterisation of C not presuppose or quantify over the very formulae of L by means of which definitions are constructed.
passage: One might propose that a concept is definable within a formal language when there exists a formula in that language applying to all and only the objects falling under the concept, the formula is finitely expressible, and the extension of the concept is determinate. This characterisation captures the extensional adequacy, finite character, and determinacy that we associate with successful formal definition. Yet a case which tells against the proposed analysis may be drawn from the study of self-referential concepts in arithmetic. Suppose we work within a first-order language of arithmetic with the usual expressive resources. Consider the concept of a natural number that is not a member of any set definable by a formula of this language. The extension of this concept is determinate relative to a fixed model: for each natural number, it is settled whether it belongs to a set definable by some formula or not. And if a formula capturing this concept existed, it would certainly be finitely expressible. One might therefore suppose that all three conditions are satisfiable. Yet the concept cannot be defined within the language, for any formula of the language that purported to capture it would itself be a formula of the language, and the number picked out would then need to both satisfy and fail to satisfy the defining condition. The obstacle is invisible to the three stated requirements: it arises not from indeterminacy of extension, nor from infinite complexity, nor from the absence of an extensionally adequate formula, but from a self-referential entanglement between the concept and the defining resources of the language. It appears that the definition, by requiring only extensional adequacy, finite expressibility, and determinacy, fails to demand that the concept be free from self-referential involvement with the very formal apparatus by means of which definitions are constructed.

definition: A set of axioms X entails a theorem T if and only if (i) T can be derived from X by a finite sequence of applications of the rules of inference of the system, (ii) each step in the derivation is mechanically checkable, and (iii) the derivation does not appeal to any principle not explicitly stated in X or in the rules of the system.
conditions: T can be derived from X by a finite sequence of applications of the rules of inference of the system
Each step in the derivation is mechanically checkable
The derivation does not appeal to any principle not explicitly stated in X or in the rules of the system
counterexample: Suppose T is a true arithmetical sentence — say, a particular instance of the consistency of the axioms themselves — that cannot be derived from X within the system, despite being true in every model of X. No finite sequence of rule applications yields T; the system, though sound, is incomplete with respect to T. Condition (i) fails. Yet T is entailed by X in the semantic sense: every structure that satisfies X also satisfies T. The definition, by requiring derivability, excludes truths that are semantic consequences of the axioms but lie beyond the reach of the formal derivation apparatus.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between syntactic derivability and semantic entailment, and by requiring a finite derivation within the system it excludes propositions that are true in every model of the axioms yet unprovable within the system due to its incompleteness.
passage: One might characterise axiomatic entailment by proposing that a set of axioms entails a theorem when the theorem can be derived by a finite sequence of rule applications, each step is mechanically checkable, and no appeal is made to principles outside the system. This formulation captures the constructive, verifiable, and self-contained character of formal derivation. Yet a case which tells against the proposed definition may be constructed from the study of incomplete formal systems. Suppose we work within a formal system of arithmetic whose axioms are consistent and whose rules of inference are sound. There exist arithmetical sentences that are true in every model satisfying the axioms — semantic consequences of the axioms in the fullest sense — yet which cannot be derived within the system by any finite sequence of rule applications. Such sentences satisfy the semantic criterion for entailment: every structure that makes the axioms true also makes the sentence true. Yet they fail condition (i) of the proposed definition, for no derivation of the required kind exists. The definition thus pronounces that the axioms do not entail the sentence, despite the fact that the sentence holds in every model of the axioms. One might observe that the definition, by identifying entailment with derivability, conflates two notions that are known to diverge in systems of sufficient expressive power. A satisfactory account must either restrict itself to complete systems, in which derivability and semantic consequence coincide, or explicitly address the gap between the two notions rather than defining the one in terms of the other.

definition: An inference from P to Q is valid if and only if (i) Q is true whenever P is true, (ii) the connection between P and Q can be expressed as a formal rule of a logical system, and (iii) the inference preserves certainty — if P is certain, then Q is certain.
conditions: Q is true whenever P is true
The connection between P and Q can be expressed as a formal rule of a logical system
The inference preserves certainty
counterexample: Consider the inference from the proposition that the number of planets is nine to the proposition that the number of planets is greater than seven. In the actual world (at the time the claim was accepted), Q is true whenever P is true. The connection can be expressed as a formal rule involving the greater-than relation in arithmetic. And the inference preserves certainty: if it is certain that the number is nine, it is certain that it is greater than seven. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the inference is not logically valid in the strictest sense, for its truth-preservation depends upon the specific arithmetical content of the propositions rather than upon their logical form alone. Under a different interpretation of the terms, the inference could fail.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the truth-preservation in condition (i) hold under every possible interpretation of the non-logical terms — that the inference be valid in virtue of its logical form rather than in virtue of the particular content of the propositions involved.
passage: One might characterise valid inference by proposing that Q follows validly from P when Q is true whenever P is true, the connection can be expressed as a formal rule, and the inference preserves certainty. This formulation captures the truth-preserving, formalisable, and certainty-transmitting character of deductive reasoning. Yet a case which tells against this analysis may be drawn from inferences involving specific content. Consider the inference from the proposition that the number of planets is nine to the proposition that the number of planets is greater than seven. In the state of affairs in which the premise is true, the conclusion is also true: nine is indeed greater than seven. The connection between premise and conclusion can be expressed as a formal rule within arithmetic: for all n, if n equals nine, then n is greater than seven. And the inference preserves certainty: anyone who is certain that the number is nine is thereby certain that it is greater than seven. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the inference is not logically valid in the philosophically important sense. Its truth-preservation depends upon the specific arithmetical content of the propositions — upon the fact that nine is greater than seven — rather than upon the logical form of the inference. If we replace 'nine' and 'seven' with different values, or if we interpret the terms under a non-standard assignment, the inference may fail. It appears that the definition, by requiring only truth-preservation, formal expressibility, and certainty transmission, fails to demand that the truth-preservation hold under every possible interpretation of the non-logical terms. A satisfactory account of logical validity must require that the inference be valid in virtue of its form, not merely in virtue of the particular content of the propositions it connects.

definition: A formal theory T is consistent if and only if (i) T does not derive both a sentence and its negation, (ii) T has been formalised in a language with well-defined formation rules, and (iii) the rules of inference of T preserve truth under the intended interpretation.
conditions: T does not derive both a sentence and its negation
T has been formalised in a language with well-defined formation rules
The rules of inference of T preserve truth under the intended interpretation
counterexample: Consider a formal theory T that is formalised in a language with well-defined formation rules and whose rules of inference preserve truth under the intended interpretation. Suppose, however, that T is so weak that it derives almost nothing: its axioms are so carefully restricted that no sentence and its negation can be derived, but equally, almost no interesting theorem can be derived either. The theory satisfies all three conditions. Yet one might also consider a stronger theory T* that derives a vast body of interesting theorems.
