T* satisfies conditions (ii) and (iii), but we do not know whether T* satisfies condition (i): we have no proof that T* does not derive a contradiction, and indeed the consistency of T* may itself be unprovable within T*. The definition provides no guidance as to whether T* is consistent, for condition (i) requires the absence of a derivation of contradictory sentences, a property that may be unknowable in practice.
missing_condition: The definition fails to address the distinction between a theory's being consistent and our being able to establish its consistency, since for sufficiently strong theories the consistency may be unprovable within the theory itself, rendering condition (i) unverifiable by the resources of the very system it is meant to characterise.
passage: One might propose that a formal theory is consistent when it does not derive both a sentence and its negation, is formalised in a language with well-defined formation rules, and has rules of inference that preserve truth under the intended interpretation. This characterisation unites the syntactic criterion of non-contradictoriness with the well-formedness of the language and the semantic soundness of the inference rules. Yet a case which reveals a difficulty in this analysis may be drawn from the metamathematics of strong formal systems. Suppose we have a formal theory T* that has been formalised in a well-defined language and whose rules of inference are sound — they preserve truth under the intended interpretation. T* derives a rich body of theorems of considerable mathematical interest. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied. As for condition (i), we have no derivation within T* of both a sentence and its negation, and extensive investigation has uncovered no inconsistency. Yet we also have no proof that T* is consistent, and indeed, by a celebrated metamathematical result, the consistency of T* cannot be established by means available within T* itself, provided T* is sufficiently strong. The definition requires that T not derive a contradiction, but this property may be in principle unverifiable by the resources of the system in question. One might observe that the definition, by formulating consistency as the absence of a derivation of contradictory sentences, presupposes that this absence can be ascertained, yet for the most important and powerful formal theories, it cannot be so ascertained from within. It appears that a satisfactory account of consistency must reckon with the distinction between a theory's being consistent and our being in a position to establish that consistency, a distinction that the proposed definition elides.

definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A consists of a set of premisses and a conclusion, (ii) it is impossible for all the premisses of A to be true and the conclusion of A to be false, and (iii) the conclusion of A follows from the premisses by the application of recognised rules of inference.
conditions: A consists of a set of premisses and a conclusion
It is impossible for all the premisses to be true and the conclusion to be false
The conclusion follows from the premisses by recognised rules of inference
counterexample: Suppose one considers the argument whose sole premiss is a necessary truth of mathematics, say that two plus two equals four, and whose conclusion is another necessary truth, say that three is a prime number. It is impossible for the premiss to be true and the conclusion false, since both are necessarily true. The argument consists of a premiss and a conclusion, so the first condition is satisfied. The second condition is satisfied by the necessity of both propositions. Yet the conclusion does not follow from the premiss by any recognised rule of inference; the truth of the conclusion is entirely independent of the premiss. The third condition fails, yet the argument satisfies the modal characterisation of validity given by the first two conditions alone.
missing_condition: The definition conflates two distinct notions of validity: the modal notion, which requires only the impossibility of true premisses and a false conclusion, and the derivational notion, which requires that the conclusion follow from the premisses by rules of inference. These two notions come apart when premisses and conclusion are independently necessary, revealing that the definition imposes an overly restrictive conjunctive requirement.
passage: One might define the validity of an argument as consisting in its possession of premisses and a conclusion, the impossibility of the premisses being true whilst the conclusion is false, and the derivability of the conclusion from the premisses by recognised rules of inference. This characterisation attempts to unite the modal and derivational conceptions of validity in a single set of conditions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from elementary mathematics. Let us imagine an argument whose sole premiss is that two plus two equals four and whose conclusion is that three is a prime number. Both propositions are necessary truths. It is therefore impossible for the premiss to be true and the conclusion false, since both are true in every possible circumstance. The first and second conditions are thus satisfied. Yet the conclusion does not follow from the premiss by any recognised rule of inference; the primality of three is entirely independent of the arithmetic of two and two. The third condition fails. One might observe that the definition, by conjoining the modal and derivational requirements, produces an analysis under which this argument both is and is not valid, depending upon which pair of conditions one emphasises. The difficulty is that the modal and derivational conceptions of validity are not equivalent: the former is broader, classifying as valid any argument whose premisses and conclusion are independently necessary, whilst the latter demands a genuine inferential connexion. The definition, by requiring both, fails to acknowledge that these are distinct concepts that warrant separate treatment.

definition: An argument A is sound if and only if (i) A is deductively valid, (ii) all of A's premisses are true, and (iii) the conclusion of A provides new information not already contained in the premisses.
conditions: A is deductively valid
All of A's premisses are true
The conclusion of A provides new information not already contained in the premisses
counterexample: Consider the argument: All mammals are warm-blooded; all dogs are mammals; therefore, all dogs are warm-blooded. This argument is deductively valid, and both premisses are true. One might contend, however, that the conclusion provides no new information, for the fact that all dogs are warm-blooded is already implicitly contained in the premisses taken together. By the proposed definition, this argument would fail to be sound because the third condition is not met. Yet this is precisely the sort of argument that logicians have always regarded as paradigmatically sound.
missing_condition: The definition imposes an extraneous requirement of informational novelty that is not part of the standard logical concept of soundness. A deductively valid argument with true premisses is sound regardless of whether the conclusion provides information beyond what is implicitly contained in the premisses, and indeed the characteristic virtue of deduction is precisely that the conclusion is already contained in the premisses.
passage: One might define a sound argument as one that is deductively valid, has true premisses, and whose conclusion provides new information not already contained in the premisses. This characterisation appears to capture both the formal and substantive virtues we expect of a good argument: logical rigour, factual accuracy, and informational contribution. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the most elementary syllogistic reasoning. Consider the argument: all mammals are warm-blooded; all dogs are mammals; therefore all dogs are warm-blooded. The argument is deductively valid by the classical rules of the syllogism, and both premisses are true. It is, by any conventional standard, a paradigmatically sound argument. Yet one might contend that the conclusion provides no new information, for the fact that all dogs are warm-blooded is already implicitly contained in the premisses taken together. The third condition of the proposed definition is therefore not satisfied, and the argument, by this analysis, is not sound. We should hesitate to accept this result, for it conflicts with the established usage of the term in logic. It appears that the definition, by imposing a requirement of informational novelty, introduces a condition that is extraneous to the concept of soundness as logicians have traditionally understood it. Indeed, the characteristic virtue of deductive reasoning is precisely that the conclusion is already contained in the premisses; to require novelty is to misunderstand the nature of deduction itself.

definition: A proposition Q is a logical consequence of a set of propositions P if and only if (i) Q is true in every model in which all members of P are true, (ii) the truth of Q depends upon the logical form of the propositions in P rather than upon their specific content, and (iii) there exists a finite derivation of Q from P using the rules of a sound and complete deductive system.
conditions: Q is true in every model in which all members of P are true
The truth of Q depends upon the logical form of the propositions in P rather than upon their specific content
There exists a finite derivation of Q from P using the rules of a sound and complete deductive system
counterexample: Let us imagine the set P consisting of second-order axioms for arithmetic, and let Q be a proposition that is true in every model of P but that, by the incompleteness of second-order logic, cannot be derived from P in any sound and complete deductive system, for no such system exists for second-order logic. Q is true in every model in which all members of P are true, and its truth depends upon the logical form of the axioms. The first and second conditions are satisfied. Yet the third condition cannot be met, since second-order logic does not admit of a sound and complete deductive system. The proposition is a logical consequence by the semantic criterion but not by the proof-theoretic criterion, and the definition, by requiring both, wrongly excludes it.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that the semantic and proof-theoretic characterisations of logical consequence may diverge in logics beyond first-order logic, and by requiring a finite derivation in a sound and complete system, it excludes genuine semantic consequences that arise in second-order and higher-order settings where completeness does not obtain.
passage: One might define logical consequence as obtaining when a proposition is true in every model in which certain premisses are true, when this truth depends upon logical form rather than specific content, and when a finite derivation exists in a sound and complete deductive system. This characterisation attempts to unify the semantic and proof-theoretic conceptions of consequence within a single definition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from the domain of higher-order logic. Suppose that P is a set of second-order axioms for arithmetic and Q is a proposition that is true in every model of P. The truth of Q depends upon the logical form of the axioms, and the first two conditions are thus satisfied. Yet second-order logic, unlike first-order logic, does not admit of a sound and complete deductive system: there exist semantic consequences that outstrip the resources of any formal proof procedure. The third condition therefore cannot be met for Q. By the proposed definition, Q is not a logical consequence of P, even though it is true in every model in which the premisses hold. We are led to conclude that the definition, by requiring a finite derivation in a sound and complete system, conflates a feature peculiar to first-order logic with a general condition on consequence. In higher-order settings, the semantic and proof-theoretic notions come apart, and a definition that insists upon both will inevitably exclude genuine logical consequences.

definition: A proposition P is paradoxical if and only if (i) P is derived from apparently true premisses by apparently valid reasoning, (ii) P is self-contradictory or absurd, and (iii) the derivation of P cannot be blocked without abandoning at least one premiss that is independently plausible.
conditions: P is derived from apparently true premisses by apparently valid reasoning
P is self-contradictory or absurd
The derivation cannot be blocked without abandoning at least one independently plausible premiss
counterexample: Suppose one considers the sorites paradox: a heap of sand remains a heap if one grain is removed; by repeated application of this premiss, a single grain of sand is a heap. The conclusion is absurd, and it is derived from an apparently true premiss by apparently valid reasoning. Yet the derivation can be blocked without abandoning any independently plausible premiss, simply by recognising that the predicate 'is a heap' is vague and that the premiss, whilst apparently true, is not strictly true but only true for most applications. The third condition is not satisfied, for the premiss can be revised without cost to any independent body of commitments. Yet the sorites is universally regarded as a genuine paradox.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for paradoxes whose force derives from the vagueness of their constituent concepts rather than from the independent plausibility of their premisses, so that the derivation can be blocked by clarifying the meaning of a term rather than by abandoning a substantive commitment.
passage: One might propose that a proposition is paradoxical when it is derived from apparently true premisses by apparently valid reasoning, is self-contradictory or absurd, and cannot be blocked without abandoning an independently plausible premiss. This characterisation captures the sense of intellectual entrapment that paradoxes characteristically produce. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the sorites paradox. A heap of sand remains a heap if one grain is removed; by repeated application of this principle, a single grain of sand is a heap. The conclusion is manifestly absurd, and it is derived from an apparently true premiss by apparently valid instances of modus ponens. Yet it is by no means clear that the derivation cannot be blocked without cost. One might observe that the predicate 'is a heap' is vague, and that the inductive premiss, whilst appearing true, is not strictly true but only true for most applications. The premiss can therefore be rejected or qualified without abandoning any independently plausible commitment about the world. The third condition of the proposed definition is not satisfied, for the revision costs nothing beyond a more careful treatment of vagueness. Yet the sorites is universally regarded as a genuine and instructive paradox. It appears that the definition, by requiring that no premiss can be abandoned without independent cost, fails to accommodate paradoxes whose force derives not from the robustness of their premisses but from the vagueness of their constituent concepts.

definition: A proposition P entails a proposition Q if and only if (i) it is impossible for P to be true and Q false, (ii) the content of Q is relevant to the content of P, and (iii) one who asserts P and denies Q thereby contradicts themselves.
conditions: It is impossible for P to be true and Q false
The content of Q is relevant to the content of P
One who asserts P and denies Q thereby contradicts themselves
counterexample: Suppose P is the proposition that it is raining and Q is the disjunction that either it is raining or the earth is flat. It is impossible for P to be true and Q false, since the truth of a disjunct guarantees the truth of the disjunction. One who asserts that it is raining and denies that either it is raining or the earth is flat contradicts herself. The first and third conditions are satisfied. Yet the content of the second disjunct, that the earth is flat, appears wholly irrelevant to the content of P. The second condition, requiring relevance of content, is not met. Nevertheless, by the classical logic of disjunction, P does entail Q.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a relevance requirement that is not part of the classical notion of entailment, thereby excluding cases of disjunction introduction in which a proposition entails a disjunction containing an irrelevant disjunct, and conflating logical entailment with a stricter notion of relevant implication.
passage: One might define entailment as obtaining when it is impossible for one proposition to be true and another false, the content of the second is relevant to the content of the first, and anyone who asserts the first and denies the second contradicts herself. This characterisation attempts to supplement the modal conception of entailment with a requirement of relevance, thereby avoiding the so-called paradoxes of material implication. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the logic of disjunction. Suppose P is the proposition that it is raining and Q is the disjunction that either it is raining or the earth is flat. By the rule of disjunction introduction, P entails Q: it is impossible for it to be raining without it being the case that either it is raining or the earth is flat. One who asserts that it is raining and denies this disjunction contradicts herself. The first and third conditions are satisfied. Yet the content of the second disjunct, that the earth is flat, is wholly irrelevant to the content of P. The second condition is therefore not met. By the proposed definition, P does not entail Q. But this result conflicts with the classical understanding of entailment, under which disjunction introduction is a valid and entailment-preserving operation. It appears that the definition, by imposing a relevance requirement, conflates the classical notion of entailment with a stricter notion of relevant implication, and thereby excludes entailments that are recognised as legitimate by standard logic.

definition: A set of propositions S is consistent if and only if (i) no contradiction can be derived from S by the rules of classical logic, (ii) every member of S can be simultaneously true, and (iii) the members of S are jointly satisfiable in at least one interpretation that assigns determinate truth-values to all propositions.
conditions: No contradiction can be derived from S by the rules of classical logic
Every member of S can be simultaneously true
The members of S are jointly satisfiable in at least one interpretation that assigns determinate truth-values to all propositions
counterexample: Suppose S is a set of propositions that includes vague predicates, such as the proposition that a certain man is tall and the proposition that a certain man is not tall, where the man in question is a borderline case of tallness. No contradiction can be derived from S by the rules of classical logic, because the vagueness of the predicate prevents either proposition from being determinately true or determinately false. The members of S can, in a sense, be simultaneously true if truth-value gaps are admitted. Yet there is no interpretation that assigns determinate truth-values to all propositions in S and makes them all true, for the borderline case does not admit of a determinate assignment. The first and second conditions are satisfied, but the third is not, and yet the set S is not inconsistent in any ordinary sense.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate sets of propositions containing vague predicates, for which determinate truth-value assignments may be unavailable without the set's being genuinely inconsistent, revealing that the requirement of determinate satisfiability is too strong for languages that admit of vagueness.
passage: One might define the consistency of a set of propositions as consisting in the non-derivability of a contradiction, the simultaneous truth of all members, and their joint satisfiability in an interpretation that assigns determinate truth-values. This characterisation captures the classical understanding of consistency with admirable precision. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the treatment of vague predicates. Suppose S is a set containing the proposition that a certain man is tall and the proposition that the same man is not tall, where the man in question stands at a height that makes him a borderline case of tallness. No contradiction can be derived from S by the rules of classical logic, for the vagueness of the predicate prevents either proposition from being determinately true or determinately false. In a sense, both propositions can be simultaneously held without absurdity, for borderline cases are precisely those in which both predication and its denial possess some measure of plausibility. Yet there is no interpretation that assigns determinate truth-values to these propositions and makes them all true, for the borderline case does not admit of a determinate assignment. The third condition is not satisfied. By the proposed definition, S is inconsistent. But we should hesitate to accept this conclusion, for the set is not inconsistent in any ordinary sense; it merely involves vagueness. It appears that the definition, by requiring determinate satisfiability, imposes a condition that is too strong for languages containing vague predicates, and it wrongly classifies as inconsistent sets of propositions whose failure of determinate satisfiability is an artefact of vagueness rather than of genuine contradiction.

definition: A concept C is definable within a formal language L if and only if (i) there exists a formula F in L such that F applies to all and only those objects falling under C, (ii) F is finite in length, and (iii) the extension of C is invariant across all models of L.
conditions: There exists a formula F in L that applies to all and only those objects falling under C
F is finite in length
The extension of C is invariant across all models of L
counterexample: Let us imagine the concept of a natural number, and let L be the first-order language of Peano arithmetic. There is no first-order formula F in L that applies to all and only the natural numbers, for by the compactness theorem the first-order theory of arithmetic has non-standard models containing elements that satisfy every formula satisfied by the standard natural numbers but are not themselves natural numbers. The first condition fails because no finite formula can distinguish the standard naturals from non-standard elements. Yet the concept of a natural number is, in a clear intuitive sense, perfectly well defined; it is the formal language that lacks the resources to capture it. The definition wrongly locates the source of indefinability in the concept rather than in the expressive limitations of the language.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between the intrinsic definability of a concept and the expressive power of the language in which one attempts to define it, so that a concept which is perfectly clear and well understood may be classified as indefinable merely because the chosen formal language lacks the resources to capture its extension.
passage: One might define a concept as definable within a formal language when there exists a finite formula that applies to all and only the objects falling under it and when its extension is invariant across all models of the language. This characterisation captures the precision and determinacy that we associate with formal definition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the theory of arithmetic. Consider the concept of a natural number, and let L be the first-order language of Peano arithmetic. By the compactness theorem, the first-order theory of arithmetic possesses non-standard models containing elements that are not natural numbers but that satisfy every formula satisfied by the standard natural numbers. There is therefore no first-order formula in L that applies to all and only the natural numbers: the first condition fails. By the proposed definition, the concept of a natural number is not definable in first-order arithmetic. Yet the concept is, in an intuitive sense, perfectly well defined; every mathematician understands what a natural number is. The indefinability is an artefact of the expressive limitations of first-order logic, not a deficiency in the concept itself. It appears that the definition, by requiring that the extension be capturable by a formula in L, conflates the definability of a concept with the expressive power of the language in which one attempts the definition.

definition: A reasoning pattern R is paradoxical if and only if (i) R proceeds from premisses that are individually self-evident, (ii) each step of R follows from the preceding steps by a logically unimpeachable rule, and (iii) R terminates in a conclusion that contradicts one or more of its own premisses.
conditions: R proceeds from premisses that are individually self-evident
Each step follows by a logically unimpeachable rule
R terminates in a conclusion that contradicts one or more of its own premisses
counterexample: Suppose one considers the following reasoning: from the premiss that every rule has an exception, one may infer by universal instantiation that the rule 'every rule has an exception' itself has an exception. This means there exists at least one rule without an exception. The conclusion does not contradict any premiss in the formal sense; rather, it undermines the universal scope of the premiss without negating it directly. The premiss is self-evident in ordinary discourse, the reasoning step is logically unimpeachable, but the conclusion is not a contradiction of the premiss but a reductio that reveals the premiss to be self-undermining. The first and second conditions are satisfied, but the third is not, for the conclusion does not strictly contradict the premiss. Yet the reasoning pattern is widely regarded as paradoxical.
missing_condition: The definition fails to include reasoning patterns whose conclusions are self-undermining or self-defeating without being strictly contradictory, revealing that paradoxicality may consist in self-referential instability rather than in outright contradiction.
passage: One might define a paradoxical reasoning pattern as one that proceeds from self-evident premisses by unimpeachable rules to a conclusion that contradicts its own premisses. This characterisation captures the classical form of the antinomy with considerable exactitude. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from self-referential generalisations. Suppose one begins with the premiss, taken as self-evident in ordinary discourse, that every rule has an exception. By universal instantiation one may infer that this very rule, the rule that every rule has an exception, itself has an exception. This means there exists at least one rule without an exception. One might observe that the conclusion does not strictly contradict the premiss, for the premiss asserts that every rule has an exception, and the conclusion asserts that there exists a rule without one. These are indeed contraries, but the path to the conclusion is unusual: it is the premiss itself that, when applied to itself, generates its own undoing. The conclusion is not derived from the premiss by the addition of further information; it is extracted from the premiss's own self-application. The reasoning is self-undermining rather than contradictory in the standard sense. The first and second conditions are satisfied, but the third is not quite met, for the conclusion does not so much contradict the premiss as reveal it to be self-defeating. Yet the pattern is universally regarded as paradoxical. It appears that the definition, by insisting upon outright contradiction, fails to accommodate the broader phenomenon of self-referential instability.

definition: A set of premisses P entails a conclusion C if and only if (i) no possible situation exists in which all members of P are true and C is false, (ii) the entailment can be recognised by any rational agent who grasps the meanings of the terms involved, and (iii) the entailment holds in virtue of the meanings of the logical constants alone.
conditions: No possible situation exists in which all members of P are true and C is false
The entailment can be recognised by any rational agent who grasps the meanings of the terms involved
The entailment holds in virtue of the meanings of the logical constants alone
counterexample: Consider the premiss 'John is a bachelor' and the conclusion 'John is unmarried.' No possible situation exists in which the premiss is true and the conclusion false, for bachelorhood analytically involves being unmarried. A rational agent who grasps the meanings of 'bachelor' and 'unmarried' can recognise the entailment. The first and second conditions are satisfied. Yet the entailment does not hold in virtue of the meanings of the logical constants alone; it holds in virtue of the semantic connexion between the non-logical terms 'bachelor' and 'unmarried.' The third condition is not satisfied. Nevertheless, the premiss plainly entails the conclusion.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for analytic entailments that hold in virtue of the meanings of non-logical terms rather than of logical constants alone, thereby excluding a large class of genuine entailments that depend upon conceptual or definitional relations among the substantive vocabulary.
passage: One might define entailment as obtaining when no possible situation makes the premisses true and the conclusion false, when any rational agent who grasps the relevant meanings can recognise the relation, and when it holds in virtue of the meanings of the logical constants alone. This characterisation attempts to isolate entailment as a purely logical relation, free from dependence upon the specific content of the terms involved. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from analytic connexions among non-logical terms. Consider the premiss that John is a bachelor and the conclusion that John is unmarried. No possible situation exists in which John is a bachelor but not unmarried, for bachelorhood analytically involves being unmarried. A rational agent who grasps the meanings of both terms recognises the entailment immediately. The first and second conditions are satisfied. Yet the entailment does not hold in virtue of the logical constants; it holds in virtue of the semantic connexion between the non-logical terms 'bachelor' and 'unmarried.' The third condition is not met. By the proposed definition, the premiss does not entail the conclusion, a result that conflicts with our firm pre-theoretical intuitions. It appears that the definition, by restricting entailment to relations that hold in virtue of logical constants, is too narrow. It captures logical entailment in a strict sense but excludes analytic or conceptual entailments that are no less genuine.

definition: A formal theory T is consistent if and only if (i) T does not prove both a proposition and its negation, (ii) T has at least one model, and (iii) the consistency of T can be established by a finitary proof within T itself.
conditions: T does not prove both a proposition and its negation
T has at least one model
The consistency of T can be established by a finitary proof within T itself
counterexample: Consider Peano arithmetic. It does not prove both a proposition and its negation, assuming it is consistent, and it has the natural numbers as a model. The first and second conditions are satisfied. Yet, by the second incompleteness theorem, Peano arithmetic cannot prove its own consistency by finitary means from within itself. The third condition is not met. Nevertheless, Peano arithmetic is consistent, and its consistency is established by methods external to the theory, such as transfinite induction up to a certain ordinal. The definition would classify Peano arithmetic as not consistent, which is plainly incorrect.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly requires that the consistency of T be provable by finitary means within T itself, a condition that, by the second incompleteness theorem, cannot be satisfied by any sufficiently strong consistent theory, thereby misclassifying all such theories as inconsistent.
passage: One might define the consistency of a formal theory as consisting in the non-derivability of a contradiction, the existence of at least one model, and the provability of consistency by finitary means within the theory itself. This characterisation appears to capture the syntactic, semantic, and metamathematical aspects of consistency in a single set of conditions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the theory of arithmetic. Consider Peano arithmetic, a theory of the natural numbers that is widely regarded as consistent. It does not prove both a proposition and its negation; it has the natural numbers as a model. The first and second conditions are satisfied. Yet, by the second incompleteness theorem, Peano arithmetic cannot prove its own consistency by finitary means from within itself, provided it is indeed consistent. The third condition is therefore not met. By the proposed definition, Peano arithmetic is not consistent, a conclusion that is plainly incorrect. It appears that the definition, by requiring finitary self-proof of consistency, imposes a condition that cannot be satisfied by any sufficiently strong consistent theory. The incompleteness theorem shows that this requirement is too demanding, and a theory may be perfectly consistent even though its consistency must be established by methods external to the theory.

definition: An argument is valid if and only if its premises and conclusion are expressible in a formal language, no interpretation assigns truth to all premises and falsehood to the conclusion, and the argument contains at least one premise.
conditions: Its premises and conclusion are expressible in a formal language
No interpretation assigns truth to all premises and falsehood to the conclusion
The argument contains at least one premise
counterexample: Consider a case in which one constructs an argument whose sole premise is a logically true sentence and whose conclusion is a different logically true sentence that bears no conceptual connection to the premise whatsoever. Both are expressible in a formal language. Since the conclusion is true under every interpretation, no interpretation assigns truth to the premise and falsehood to the conclusion. The argument contains one premise. All three conditions are satisfied, and the argument is valid on this analysis. Yet one may feel that calling it valid misrepresents the logical situation, for there is no relation of logical support between the premise and the conclusion — the conclusion's truth is entirely independent of the premise.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between arguments whose conclusions are preserved by the premises and arguments whose conclusions happen to be logically true independently, thereby conflating validity with the mere impossibility of true-premises-and-false-conclusion.
passage: It is customary to analyse the validity of an argument in terms of the impossibility of true premises combined with a false conclusion, together with certain formal requirements. One might propose that an argument is valid when its premises and conclusion are expressible in a formal language, when no interpretation assigns truth to every premise and falsehood to the conclusion, and when the argument contains at least one premise. These conditions appear, upon initial reflection, to capture the core of what we mean by deductive validity. Yet one might construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose one forms an argument whose sole premise is a sentence that is logically true — say, a tautology of the propositional calculus — and whose conclusion is a wholly unrelated logically true sentence. Both are expressible in a formal language; the argument contains a premise; and no interpretation makes the premise true and the conclusion false, for the conclusion is true under every interpretation regardless. All three conditions are satisfied. Nevertheless, one may hesitate to regard this as a genuinely valid argument in the fullest sense, for the truth of the conclusion owes nothing to the premise. The premise provides no logical support for the conclusion; the impossibility of the critical combination of truth-values is, as it were, an artefact of the conclusion's independent logical truth rather than a consequence of any inferential connection. We are led to recognise that the analysis, as stated, conflates two quite different situations and thereby proves insufficient.

definition: An argument is sound if and only if it is deductively valid, all of its premises are true, and its conclusion follows from the premises by a recognised rule of inference.
conditions: The argument is deductively valid
All of its premises are true
Its conclusion follows from the premises by a recognised rule of inference
counterexample: Suppose that one constructs a lengthy argument with twenty true premises and a true conclusion, where each inferential step employs a recognised rule of inference such as modus ponens or universal instantiation, and the argument is deductively valid in the standard sense. However, the argument is circular: among the twenty premises, one of them is logically equivalent to the conclusion, and the chain of inference effectively presupposes what it purports to establish. All three conditions are met — validity holds, the premises are true, and each step follows by a recognised rule — yet one may question whether such an argument is genuinely sound in the sense that matters for rational persuasion, since it begs the question.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the premises be independently warranted rather than merely true — that is, it does not exclude cases of circularity in which a premise is logically equivalent to the conclusion.
passage: The standard analysis of soundness holds that an argument is sound when it is deductively valid, when all of its premises are true, and when its conclusion follows from those premises by recognised rules of inference. One might observe that this characterisation accords well with many ordinary cases. A simple syllogism with true premises and a conclusion derived by a familiar rule appears paradigmatically sound. Yet we should hesitate to accept this analysis without further scrutiny. Let us imagine a lengthy argument comprising twenty premises, all of which are true, and a true conclusion. Each step of the argument employs a recognised rule of inference — modus ponens, universal instantiation, or the like — and the argument is deductively valid by any standard test. However, upon closer examination, it emerges that one of the twenty premises is logically equivalent to the conclusion itself. The argument is, in effect, circular: it presupposes what it sets out to establish. All three conditions of the proposed analysis are satisfied. The argument is valid; its premises are true; its inferential steps are recognised. Yet it appears that something has gone wrong. We should not wish to commend this argument as sound in any robust sense, for it provides no independent grounds for its conclusion. The case reveals that the analysis omits a requirement of non-circularity or independent warrant for the premises, and is therefore insufficient as it stands.

definition: A sentence Q is a logical consequence of a set of sentences S if and only if every model that satisfies all members of S also satisfies Q, the vocabulary of Q is contained within the vocabulary of S, and S is consistent.
conditions: Every model that satisfies all members of S also satisfies Q
The vocabulary of Q is contained within the vocabulary of S
S is consistent
counterexample: Consider a case in which S is the set containing the single sentence 'All emeralds are green' and Q is the sentence 'All emeralds are green or all rubies are red.' The vocabulary of Q includes 'rubies' and 'red,' which do not appear in S. The second condition is therefore not satisfied. Yet Q is a logical consequence of S in the standard model-theoretic sense, since every model satisfying S also satisfies Q (the disjunction is true whenever its first disjunct is). The case shows that the vocabulary-containment condition is too restrictive, excluding genuine instances of logical consequence.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously imposes a vocabulary-containment requirement that rules out valid cases of logical consequence involving vocabulary not present in the premise set, such as consequences obtained by disjunction introduction.
passage: One might propose that logical consequence be analysed as follows: a sentence Q is a logical consequence of a set S just in case every model satisfying the members of S also satisfies Q, the vocabulary of Q is contained within that of S, and S is consistent. The first and third conditions are familiar and widely accepted. The second condition — that Q introduce no vocabulary beyond what appears in S — may seem a reasonable constraint, intended perhaps to ensure a genuine connection between premises and conclusion. Yet we should hesitate to accept it. Suppose that S consists of the single sentence asserting that all emeralds are green, and let Q be the disjunction asserting that all emeralds are green or all rubies are red. The vocabulary of Q includes terms — 'rubies' and 'red' — that do not appear in S, and so the second condition is violated. Nevertheless, Q follows from S in the standard model-theoretic sense without the slightest difficulty: every model in which all emeralds are green is a model in which the disjunction holds, since a disjunction is true whenever one of its disjuncts is. We are thus presented with a case in which a sentence is by all ordinary standards a logical consequence of the given set, yet the proposed analysis denies this. The vocabulary-containment requirement, far from capturing a genuine feature of logical consequence, excludes perfectly legitimate instances and must therefore be rejected as an unnecessary and overly restrictive condition.

definition: A statement is paradoxical if and only if it leads by apparently valid reasoning to a contradiction, the reasoning employs only commonly accepted principles, and the statement is expressible in ordinary language.
conditions: The statement leads by apparently valid reasoning to a contradiction
The reasoning employs only commonly accepted principles
The statement is expressible in ordinary language
counterexample: Suppose that a mathematician presents a statement within Cantor's set theory which, by apparently valid reasoning employing commonly accepted principles of set formation, leads to a contradiction — namely, that there exists a set of all sets that do not contain themselves. The statement is not readily expressible in ordinary language without considerable technical apparatus; it requires the vocabulary of set membership, comprehension, and the distinction between sets and their elements. The third condition is not satisfied. Yet it appears that this is paradigmatically paradoxical, suggesting that the requirement of expressibility in ordinary language is too narrow.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires expressibility in ordinary language; genuine paradoxes may arise in formal or technical languages and are no less paradoxical for that.
passage: An analysis of the concept of paradox might proceed as follows: a statement is paradoxical when it leads, by apparently valid reasoning employing only commonly accepted principles, to a contradiction, and when the statement is expressible in ordinary language. The first two conditions capture what seems essential to paradox — the derivation of an absurdity by means that appear, at each step, entirely legitimate. The third condition is perhaps intended to ensure that the paradox is genuinely surprising and accessible, not a mere technicality of an artificial system. Yet one might construct a case which reveals that this third condition is overly restrictive. Let us consider the statement within naive set theory that there exists a set comprising exactly those sets that do not contain themselves as members. By apparently valid reasoning — indeed, by the commonly accepted principle that for any well-defined property there exists the set of all objects satisfying it — one derives a contradiction: the set in question both does and does not contain itself. The reasoning employs a principle that was, prior to the discovery of the paradox, commonly accepted. The first two conditions are plainly met. Yet the statement resists expression in ordinary language without the introduction of considerable technical vocabulary — the notions of set membership, comprehension, and self-membership. The third condition is not satisfied. It appears, however, that this is as clear and significant a case of paradox as one could wish for. We are led to conclude that the requirement of expressibility in ordinary language is too restrictive and must be abandoned.

definition: A set of propositions P entails a proposition Q if and only if it is impossible for all members of P to be true and Q false, the entailment is recognisable by an ideally rational agent upon reflection, and Q is not itself a member of P.
conditions: It is impossible for all members of P to be true and Q false
The entailment is recognisable by an ideally rational agent upon reflection
Q is not itself a member of P
counterexample: Consider a case in which P is a set of axioms for an exceedingly complex formal system and Q is a theorem that follows from those axioms but whose proof requires millions of inferential steps. It is impossible for all members of P to be true and Q false, and Q is not a member of P. However, whether the entailment is recognisable upon reflection by an ideally rational agent is doubtful if by 'reflection' one means something short of mechanically executing an astronomically long proof. One might argue that an ideally rational agent could in principle trace through any finite proof, but the condition as stated seems to import a notion of cognitive accessibility that sits uneasily with the purely logical character of entailment.
missing_condition: The definition conflates a logical relation (the impossibility of true premises and false conclusion) with a cognitive achievement (recognisability upon reflection), importing an unnecessary condition that threatens to exclude genuine cases of entailment in highly complex formal systems.
passage: One might attempt to analyse entailment by conjoining three conditions: it is impossible for all members of a set P to be true whilst a proposition Q is false; this entailment is recognisable by an ideally rational agent upon reflection; and Q is not itself contained in P. The first condition captures the modal core of entailment. The third excludes the trivial case. The second is intended, perhaps, to distinguish genuine logical entailment from mere necessary co-occurrence of truth-values. Yet we should hesitate to accept the second condition without misgiving. Suppose that P is a set of axioms for a formal system of considerable complexity and that Q is a theorem whose derivation from those axioms requires an extraordinarily lengthy proof — millions of steps, let us say. It is impossible for all axioms to hold and Q to fail; Q is not among the axioms. The first and third conditions are met. But whether the entailment is recognisable upon reflection by an ideally rational agent depends upon what we mean by such an agent and by reflection. If the agent is truly ideal, she may in principle trace any finite proof; yet the condition as stated seems to import a notion of cognitive accessibility that is foreign to the concept of entailment as a logical relation. One may hold that Q is entailed by P regardless of whether any agent, however idealised, can recognise this upon reflection. The case suggests that the second condition conflates a logical matter with a cognitive one and is therefore not a genuine requirement of entailment.

definition: A set of sentences is consistent if and only if there exists a model that satisfies all members of the set, no sentence in the set is the negation of another, and the set is finite.
conditions: There exists a model that satisfies all members of the set
No sentence in the set is the negation of another
The set is finite
counterexample: Consider a case in which one takes the set of all sentences of first-order Peano arithmetic. This set is infinite, so the third condition is not met. Yet the set is consistent in the standard sense — there exists a model (the natural numbers under the standard interpretation) that satisfies every sentence in the set. The finitude requirement excludes a paradigmatic case of consistency and is therefore too restrictive.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires that the set be finite; infinite sets of sentences may be perfectly consistent, as exemplified by the theorems of Peano arithmetic under the standard model.
passage: An analysis of the consistency of a set of sentences might be proposed along the following lines: a set is consistent just in case there exists a model satisfying all its members, no member is the negation of another, and the set is finite. The first condition is the most familiar and the most widely endorsed. The second may be regarded as a sufficient (though perhaps not necessary) guarantee against a certain obvious form of inconsistency. The third condition — finitude — might be motivated by the thought that infinite sets introduce complications that the concept of consistency is not designed to accommodate. Yet this third requirement is plainly too restrictive. Let us consider the set of all theorems of first-order Peano arithmetic. This set is infinite, and so the proposed analysis would deny it consistency. But it is entirely consistent: the natural numbers, under their standard interpretation, constitute a model that satisfies every theorem of the system. No contradiction can be derived. The set is, by every reasonable standard, consistent. We are therefore compelled to recognise that the finitude condition is not a genuine requirement of consistency and that the analysis, in imposing it, excludes cases that manifestly ought to be included. The insufficiency of the definition is thereby established.

definition: A property is definable in a formal language if and only if there exists a formula in that language that is satisfied by exactly those objects possessing the property, the formula is finitely long, and the property is extensional.
conditions: There exists a formula satisfied by exactly those objects possessing the property
The formula is finitely long
The property is extensional
counterexample: Suppose one considers the property of being a natural number that is not the Goedel number of a provable sentence of a given formal system. This property is extensional — it depends only on which objects fall under it, not on how they are described. One can construct a finitely long formula that is satisfied by exactly those natural numbers possessing the property (indeed, the representability results of arithmetic show this). All three conditions are met and the property is definable in the formal language. Yet the property in question is not decidable — there is no effective procedure for determining, for an arbitrary number, whether it falls under the formula. One might have supposed that definability in a formal language entails some measure of algorithmic tractability, but the analysis as given does not require this, thereby counting as definable properties that are in principle unrecognisable.
missing_condition: The definition does not address the distinction between definability and decidability, and thus counts as definable certain properties for which no effective method of recognition exists — a point which, depending on one's purposes, may render the analysis insufficient.
passage: One might analyse the definability of a property in a formal language by requiring that there exist a finitely long formula satisfied by exactly those objects possessing the property and that the property be extensional — that is, that it depend solely upon which objects fall under it rather than upon any intensional or modal considerations. These conditions appear to characterise many standard cases of definability. Yet one might observe that they permit cases which sit uneasily with certain expectations one might bring to the concept. Suppose one considers the property of being a natural number that is not the Goedel number of a provable sentence of a particular formal system. This property is extensional; it depends only on which numbers possess it. Moreover, the representability results of elementary arithmetic assure us that there exists a finitely long formula of the formal language that is satisfied by exactly those numbers. All three conditions are met. The property is definable. Yet there is no effective procedure for determining, of an arbitrary natural number, whether it falls under this formula. The property is definable but not decidable. If one had supposed that definability in a formal language carried with it some measure of algorithmic tractability — some capacity, at least in principle, to recognise instances — then the analysis is shown to be insufficient, for it permits definability without any such capacity. One may, of course, accept this consequence and hold that definability and decidability are simply distinct notions, but the case at least reveals that the analysis is silent on a point that many would wish to see addressed.

definition: A reasoning puzzle is a genuine paradox if and only if it presents an argument with apparently true premises and apparently valid steps that yields an apparently false conclusion, the puzzle has resisted resolution by experts for a sustained period, and the puzzle arises within a well-defined formal framework.
conditions: It presents an argument with apparently true premises and apparently valid steps yielding an apparently false conclusion
The puzzle has resisted resolution by experts for a sustained period
The puzzle arises within a well-defined formal framework
counterexample: Consider a case in which a philosopher presents the ancient paradox of the heap — sorites — in ordinary, pre-formal terms. A single grain of sand is not a heap; adding one grain to something that is not a heap does not make it a heap; yet by repeated application of this principle, one concludes that no quantity of sand constitutes a heap, which is plainly false. The premises appear true, the steps apparently valid, and the conclusion apparently false. The paradox has resisted resolution for millennia. Yet it does not arise within a well-defined formal framework; it is stated in the imprecise vocabulary of ordinary language. The third condition fails, but the puzzle is paradigmatically paradoxical.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires that the paradox arise within a well-defined formal framework; many of the most significant paradoxes originate in ordinary language or pre-formal reasoning and are no less genuine for that.
passage: One might attempt to characterise a genuine paradox by three conditions: that it present an argument with apparently true premises and apparently valid steps leading to an apparently false conclusion, that it have resisted resolution by experts for a sustained period, and that it arise within a well-defined formal framework. The first condition captures the essential structure of paradox: the collision between plausible premises and an implausible conclusion. The second is intended to distinguish genuine paradoxes from superficial puzzles that admit of ready resolution. The third restricts the notion to the domain of formal logic or mathematics. Yet we should hesitate to accept the third condition. Consider the ancient paradox of the heap. One grain of sand, we are inclined to say, is not a heap. And if a collection of grains is not a heap, then adding a single grain does not transform it into one. These premises appear plainly true. The step from each case to the next appears valid. Yet the conclusion — that no quantity of sand constitutes a heap — is manifestly false. The paradox has resisted satisfactory resolution for over two thousand years, and distinguished philosophers continue to disagree about its treatment. The first two conditions are met. But the third is not: the paradox is stated in the imprecise vocabulary of ordinary language, not within any well-defined formal framework. It is, nevertheless, among the most celebrated and instructive of all paradoxes. We are led to conclude that the analysis is too restrictive in requiring a formal setting and must be broadened.

definition: P entails Q if and only if P and not-Q are jointly unsatisfiable, the entailment can be demonstrated by a finite proof, and P is logically simpler than P-and-Q taken together.
conditions: P and not-Q are jointly unsatisfiable
The entailment can be demonstrated by a finite proof
P is logically simpler than P-and-Q taken together
counterexample: Suppose that P is an elaborate conjunction of one hundred axioms and Q is the proposition that P is consistent. If P is indeed consistent, then P and not-Q are jointly unsatisfiable (since not-Q asserts that P is inconsistent, which ex hypothesi is false). One may further suppose that a finite proof of this entailment can be constructed. But P is not logically simpler than P-and-Q taken together, since P is already highly complex, and the conjunction of P with Q adds relatively little complexity. The third condition is ill-defined and in many cases fails to hold even where entailment plainly obtains.
missing_condition: The definition imposes an ill-motivated condition of relative logical simplicity that bears no genuine connection to entailment and excludes cases where a complex set of premises entails a proposition without any reduction in complexity.
passage: One might venture that P entails Q when three conditions hold: P and not-Q are jointly unsatisfiable, the entailment can be demonstrated by a finite proof, and P is logically simpler than the conjunction of P and Q. The first condition is standard and widely accepted. The second ensures that the entailment is, at least in principle, verifiable. The third is perhaps intended to capture the thought that entailment proceeds from simpler to more complex, or that the conclusion introduces something not already fully present in the premises. Yet this third condition is problematic. Suppose that P is an elaborate conjunction of one hundred axioms and that Q is the proposition asserting the consistency of P. If P is consistent, then P and not-Q are jointly unsatisfiable, and a finite proof may be available. But it is far from clear that P is logically simpler than P-and-Q: P is already exceedingly complex, and the addition of Q may contribute negligible further complexity. Indeed, one may question whether the notion of one proposition being logically simpler than another is sufficiently well-defined to serve as a condition on entailment. The third condition is in any case ill-motivated, for there is no evident reason why entailment should be constrained by considerations of relative simplicity. We are led to conclude that this condition is an extraneous addition that distorts the analysis of entailment.

definition: A formal theory is consistent if and only if no contradiction can be derived within it, every theorem of the theory is true under the intended interpretation, and the theory has been formalised using standard logical connectives.
conditions: No contradiction can be derived within the theory
Every theorem of the theory is true under the intended interpretation
The theory has been formalised using standard logical connectives
counterexample: Consider a case in which a logician develops a formal theory using non-standard connectives — say, connectives drawn from a many-valued logic or a relevance logic — while maintaining the usual standards of rigour. No contradiction can be derived within the theory, and every theorem is true under the intended interpretation. The first two conditions are satisfied. Yet the theory is not formalised using standard logical connectives, so the third condition fails. It appears, however, that the theory is consistent by any reasonable measure, and that the restriction to standard connectives is an unnecessary limitation.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires formalisation using standard logical connectives; consistency is a property that may hold regardless of the particular connectives employed, provided the system is well-defined.
passage: An analysis of the consistency of a formal theory might require that no contradiction be derivable within it, that every theorem be true under the intended interpretation, and that the theory employ standard logical connectives. The first two conditions capture important aspects of consistency: the absence of contradiction and the truth of the theory's theorems. The third condition is perhaps motivated by a desire to restrict attention to theories formulated in classical logic, where consistency is best understood. Yet one might construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose that a logician develops a formal theory using connectives drawn from a many-valued or relevance logic. The connectives are not those of classical logic — there may be additional truth-values or restrictions on the rule of explosion — but the system is rigorously defined. No contradiction is derivable within it; every theorem of the theory holds under the intended interpretation. The first two conditions are satisfied. But the third is not, since the theory does not employ standard logical connectives. It appears, nevertheless, that the theory is consistent in every sense that matters. The absence of contradiction and the truth of its theorems suffice to establish this. We are led to conclude that the restriction to standard connectives is an arbitrary limitation that excludes perfectly consistent theories from the extension of the concept, and that the analysis is insufficient.
